Stress roughly F over A] end ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Solution Methodology ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given: Two Bodies, Load F, Geometry R1 R2] --> B[Compute Effective Radius R
1 over R equals 1 over R1 plus 1 over R2] A --> C[Compute Effective Modulus E star
1 over E star equals...] B --> D{Type of Contact?} C --> D D -- POINT --> E1[Use Point Contact Formulas
a cubed = 3 F R over 4 E star] D -- LINE --> E2[Use Line Contact Formulas
b squared = 4 F R over pi L E star] D -- SURFACE --> E3[Switch to EHL or Asperity Model] E1 --> F[Compute p_max and delta] E2 --> F E3 --> G[Compute Real Contact Area
and Film Thickness] F --> H[Output: Stresses, Deformations, Failure Risk] G --> H ``` ## 4.4 Real-World Component Identification Table | Component | Type of Contact | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---| | Deep Groove Ball Bearing | Point Contact | Ball–race contact initially touches at a point | | Cylindrical Roller Bearing | Line Contact | Cylindrical roller–race contact is along a line | | Spherical Roller Bearing | Point Contact (conformal) | Barrel-shaped rollers contact at a point | | Tapered Roller Bearing | Line Contact | Modified line contact with crown | | Hypoid Gear Tooth | Line Contact | Conjugate action along a line | | Spur Gear Tooth | Line Contact | Involute action creates a line contact | | Journal Bearing | Surface Contact | Shaft conforms to bearing shell | | Cam-Follower (flat cam) | Line Contact | Cylindrical follower–flat cam | | Cam-Follower (knife-edge) | Line Contact | Theoretical line contact | | Ball Screw | Point Contact | Ball–screw race contact | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term "contact mechanics" and list the three fundamental types of contacts studied in tribology, giving one practical example for each. **Model Answer:** Contact mechanics is the study of the deformation and stresses that occur when two solid bodies are pressed together against each other under a normal load, without relative motion. The three fundamental types of contacts are: 1. **Point Contact** — Two convex bodies (or a convex body and a flat) touch at a single mathematical point initially. *Example:* Ball–race contact in a deep groove ball bearing. 2. **Line Contact** — Two cylindrical bodies (or a cylinder and a flat) touch along a straight line initially. *Example:* Cylindrical roller–race contact in a roller bearing. 3. **Surface Contact** — One body fits inside the other such that they touch over a finite area from the outset. *Example:* Journal–bearing shell contact in a hydrodynamic journal bearing. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the difference between conformal and non-conformal contacts? State one example each. **Model Answer:** In a **non-conformal contact**, the two bodies initially touch at a point or a line, and the contact area grows as the load increases. Hertzian contact theory applies here. *Example:* A ball pressed against a flat plate. In a **conformal contact**, the two bodies touch over a finite area even without any load, and this area may shrink slightly as the load increases due to elastic deformation. Hertzian theory does *not* apply; instead, hydrodynamic or asperity-contact models are used. *Example:* A journal rotating inside a bearing shell. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — With Internal Choice ### Question A **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expressions for the **contact radius $a$**, the **maximum contact pressure $p_{max}$**, and the **mutual approach $\delta$** for a sphere of radius $R$ pressed against a flat elastic half-space under a normal load $F$. State clearly all the assumptions of Hertzian contact theory. (10 marks) **(b)** A hardened steel ball of diameter 25 mm is pressed against a flat steel surface with a force of 1 kN. For the steel, $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate the contact radius, the maximum Hertzian contact pressure, and the mutual approach of the centres. (4 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions of Hertzian Contact Theory:** 1. The strains are small, and the material behaves as a **linear elastic** continuum (Hooke's law valid). 2. The surfaces are **continuous, smooth**, and **frictionless** (no tangential tractions). 3. The contact area is **small** compared to the radii of curvature and the dimensions of the bodies. 4. Each body can be modelled as an **elastic half-space** in the contact region. **Derivation (Outline — full steps in Section 3.1):** Assume a semi-ellipsoidal pressure distribution $p(r) = p_{max}\sqrt{1 - r^{2}/a^{2}}$. Apply: (i) **Force equilibrium:** $$F = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi a^{2} p_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{max} = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}}$$ (ii) **Elastic displacement continuity** (Boussinesq solution for a half-space): $$\delta = \dfrac{3F}{4 E^{*} a}$$ (iii) **Geometric compatibility** (gap closure by sphere geometry): $$\delta = \dfrac{a^{2}}{2R}$$ (iv) **Solving (ii) and (iii) simultaneously:** $$a^{3} = \dfrac{3 F R}{4 E^{*}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \left(\dfrac{3 F R}{4 E^{*}}\right)^{1/3}$$ Substitute back: $$\delta = \dfrac{a^{2}}{2R} = \left(\dfrac{9F^{2}}{16 R E^{*2}}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$p_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{6 F E^{*2}}{R^{2}}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** Diameter = 25 mm → $R = 12.5$ mm $= 1.25 \times 10^{-2}$ m; $F = 1000$ N; $E = 210$ GPa; $\nu = 0.3$. **Step 1:** Effective modulus: $$E^{*} = \dfrac{E}{2(1 - \nu^{2})} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^{9}}{2(1 - 0.09)} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^{9}}{1.82} \approx 115.4 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 2:** Contact radius: $$a = \left(\dfrac{3 \times 1000 \times 0.0125}{4 \times 115.4 \times 10^{9}}\right)^{1/3} = (8.12 \times 10^{-11})^{1/3} \approx 4.34 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{m} = 0.434\, \text{mm}$$ **Step 3:** Maximum pressure: $$p_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times 1000}{2\pi (4.34 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} = \dfrac{3000}{2\pi \times 1.884 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.53 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4:** Mutual approach: $$\delta = \dfrac{a^{2}}{2R} = \dfrac{(4.34 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{2 \times 0.0125} = \dfrac{1.884 \times 10^{-7}}{0.025} \approx 7.54\, \mu\text{m}$$ **Final Answer:** $a = 0.434$ mm, $p_{max} \approx 2.53$ GPa, $\delta \approx 7.54$ $\mu$m. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** With a clear sketch, explain the difference between **point contact** and **line contact** in Hertzian contact mechanics. Write the expressions for contact area, contact pressure, and mutual approach for both cases and tabulate the comparison. (7 marks) **(b)** A long steel cylinder of radius 40 mm and length 50 mm is pressed against a steel flat plate under a normal load of 2000 N. Take $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Determine (i) the contact half-width, (ii) the maximum contact pressure, and (iii) the approximate mutual approach. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Sketch and Distinction:** - In **point contact** (e.g., sphere on flat), the two bodies initially touch at a mathematical point; under load, the contact area is a **circle of radius $a$** with semi-ellipsoidal pressure distribution. The contact area scales as $F^{1/3}$. - In **line contact** (e.g., cylinder on flat), the two bodies initially touch along a straight line; under load, the contact area is a **rectangle of half-width $b$ and length $L$** with semi-elliptical pressure distribution across the width. The contact half-width scales as $F^{1/2}$. **Comparative Table:** | Parameter | Point Contact | Line Contact | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Initial touch | Point | Line | | Contact patch | Circle of radius $a$ | Rectangle $2b \times L$ | | Pressure profile | Semi-ellipsoidal (radial) | Semi-elliptical (transverse) | | Area scaling | $A \propto F^{2/3}$ | $A \propto F^{1/2}$ | | Half-dimension formula | $a = (3FR/4E^*)^{1/3}$ | $b = \sqrt{4FR/(\pi L E^*)}$ | | Max pressure | $p_{max} = 3F/(2\pi a^{2})$ | $p_{max} = 2F/(\pi b L)$ | | Approach | $\delta = a^{2}/(2R)$ | $\delta = (2F/\pi L E^*)[\ln(4R/b) - 0.5]$ | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $R = 0.040$ m, $L = 0.050$ m, $F = 2000$ N, $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. **Step 1:** Effective modulus: $$E^{*} = \dfrac{E}{2(1 - \nu^{2})} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^{9}}{1.82} \approx 115.4 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 2:** Contact half-width: $$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F R}{\pi L E^{*}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 2000 \times 0.040}{\pi \times 0.050 \times 115.4 \times 10^{9}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\dfrac{320}{\pi \times 5.77 \times 10^{9}}} = \sqrt{1.766 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.329 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{m} = 0.133\, \text{mm}$$ **Step 3:** Maximum contact pressure: $$p_{max} = \dfrac{2 F}{\pi b L} = \dfrac{2 \times 2000}{\pi \times 1.329 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.050} = \dfrac{4000}{2.087 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 191.6 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** Mutual approach: $$\delta = \dfrac{2 F}{\pi L E^{*}}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4 R}{b}\right) - 0.5\right]$$ $$= \dfrac{2 \times 2000}{\pi \times 0.050 \times 115.4 \times 10^{9}} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4 \times 0.040}{1.329 \times 10^{-4}}\right) - 0.5\right]$$ $$= (2.207 \times 10^{-7}) \times \left[\ln(1204) - 0.5\right]$$ $$= 2.207 \times 10^{-7} \times \left[7.093 - 0.5\right] = 2.207 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.593$$ $$\delta \approx 1.455 \times 10^{-6}\, \text{m} = 1.46\, \mu\text{m}$$ **Final Answer:** $b = 0.133$ mm, $p_{max} \approx 192$ MPa, $\delta \approx 1.46$ $\mu$m. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong $E^*$ formula:** Forgetting to use the factor of 2 when both bodies are of the same material. The correct form is $E^* = E/[2(1-\nu^2)]$ for identical materials — *not* $E^*$ $= E/(1-\nu^2)$. > > 2. **Unit mismatches in $a$:** Forgetting to convert mm to m, leading to answers that are off by orders of magnitude. **Always convert radii and forces to SI units before substitution.** > > 3. **Sign errors in $R$:** For convex-on-concave contact, the radius of the concave body must be entered as **negative** in $1/R = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2$. If forgotten, $p_{max}$ comes out completely wrong. > > 4. **Applying Hertz to conformal contacts:** Writing $p_{max} = 3F/(2\pi a^2)$ for a journal bearing is a guaranteed zero for that part — Hertz does NOT apply there. > > 5. **Forgetting the log term in line contact:** The line-contact approach $\delta$ contains $\ln(4R/b)$; students often write only the leading-order term and miss a full mark. > > 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Part (a) derivations *always* carry at least 2 marks for the assumptions — never skip them. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Contact Mechanics:** - **Three Contact Types:** - **Point** → circle patch, $a \propto F^{1/3}$, high $p_{max}$ - **Line** → rectangle patch, $b \propto F^{1/2}$, moderate $p_{max}$ - **Surface** → conformal, finite area from start, low $p_{max}$, NOT Hertzian - **Hertz Assumptions** (must be memorized verbatim): - Small strains, linear elasticity - Smooth, continuous, frictionless surfaces - Contact area $\ll$ body dimensions - Elastic half-space approximation valid - **Master Formulas to Memorize:** - Sphere on flat: $a^{3} = 3FR/(4E^*)$, $\delta = a^{2}/(2R)$, $p_{max} = 3F/(2\pi a^{2})$ - Cylinder on flat: $b^{2} = 4FR/(\pi L E^*)$, $p_{max} = 2F/(\pi b L)$ - For identical materials: $E^* = E/[2(1-\nu^2)]$ - Effective radius: $1/R = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2$ (with sign convention) - **Scaling Laws (Mnemonic: "P-cubed, L-squared"):** - Point: dimensions $\propto F^{1/3}$, area $\propto F^{2/3}$ - Line: dimensions $\propto F^{1/2}$, area $\propto F^{1/2}$ - **Conformal vs Non-Conformal (Mnemonic: "Fits or Bumps?"):** - If one body *fits inside* the other → conformal → no Hertz - If both bodies *bump* at a point/line → non-conformal → use Hertz - **Key Numerical Values (typical KTU given data):** - Steel: $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$ → $E^* = 115.4$ GPa - $p_{max} \sim 1$–$3$ GPa is normal for steel Hertzian contacts - Mutual approach $\delta$ is typically in micrometers - **Common Pitfalls (already covered in the warning above).** - **Engineering Insight to Quote in Answers:** *"For the same load and material, line contact gives lower $p_{max}$ than point contact — this is the fundamental reason roller bearings have higher load capacity than ball bearings of equivalent size."*

topics.title: Hertzian Contact Theory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hertzian Contact Theory

# 1. Hertzian Contact Theory — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hertzian Contact Theory** is the classical theory of non-conforming elastic bodies pressed against each other under a normal load, developed by **Heinrich Hertz (1881)**. It describes how two curved elastic solids deform locally and form a finite contact patch (a small ellipse for point contact, or a rectangular strip for line contact) when subjected to a normal force, while remaining purely elastic and frictionless at the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEMUT754, Module 1):**
> Hertzian contact is the **cornerstone of tribology** because virtually every machine element in relative motion — gear teeth, rolling-element bearings, cam-follower pairs, meshing gears, piston rings, wheel-rail contact — transmits load through a *non-conforming contact zone* whose true area is **far smaller** than the apparent geometric area. Hertz theory gives the **contact patch size, peak pressure, and elastic deflection** that govern subsurface stress, fatigue life (pitting), and lubricant film thickness in EHL (Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication) regimes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine pressing a **golf ball (small sphere)** gently against a **basketball (large sphere)**. The golf ball *seems* to touch the big ball at a single mathematical point — but in reality, both balls squish microscopically and the actual contact zone is a tiny **circular patch** with finite area.

Now imagine pressing two inflated **balloons** together. The flat-looking interface that forms is actually a small *lens-shaped* contact area, and the pressure inside that patch is **not uniform** — it follows a 3-D bell-shaped distribution (highest at the centre, zero at the edge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping for a Tribology Student:**
> - **Ball bearing ball ↔ inner/outer raceway** → a circular contact patch ("point contact", *a = b*).
> - **Roller bearing roller ↔ raceway** → a rectangular contact strip ("line contact").
> - **Spur gear tooth flank ↔ mating tooth flank** → a near-elliptical contact zone.
> - **Cam ↔ roller follower** → cylindrical (line) contact.
>
> The Hertzian framework predicts the *real contact pressure* (typically **1–3 GPa** in rolling bearings) and the *contact patch half-width* (typically **10–500 μm**) that control fatigue life, asperity deformation, and EHL film generation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Steel) |
|--------|----------|-----------------------|
| **E** | Young's Modulus | **210 GPa** |
| **ν** | Poisson's Ratio | **0.30** |
| **E\*** | Effective Elastic Modulus | **≈ 2.30 × 10¹¹ N/m²** |
| **R** | Radius of Curvature | m / mm |
| **F (or W)** | Normal Load | N |
| **p₀ (p_max)** | Maximum Hertzian Contact Pressure | **1 – 3.5 GPa** (bearings) |
| **a, b** | Semi-axes of contact ellipse | μm to mm |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **E\* (Effective Modulus)** relation:
> $$\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1-\nu_1^{2}}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^{2}}{E_2}$$
> For identical steel-on-steel: $E^* \approx 1.15 \times 10^{11}$ Pa (or **2.30 × 10¹¹ Pa** depending on the textbook convention — both are used; check your KTU module handout).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Hertzian Theory

The classical Hertz solution is valid only when **ALL** the following hold:

1. The contact area is **small** compared with the radii of curvature of the bodies.
2. Each body can be treated as a **semi-infinite elastic half-space** in the region of contact.
3. The surfaces are **smooth, continuous, and non-conforming** (they would meet at a point or line in the unloaded state).
4. Strains are **small** and the material is **linearly elastic**.
5. **No friction** is transmitted across the contact interface (purely normal load).
6. The contact patch is **free of tangential tractions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Hertz theory **fails** for conforming contacts (e.g., journal bearing), for plastic deformation (when p_max > ~1.6 × Y, the material yield), and when surface roughness is comparable to the contact patch size (then asperity/contact-mechanics theories like GW or Persson apply).

## 2.2 Reduced (Effective) Radius of Curvature

For two bodies in contact with principal radii $(R_{1x}, R_{1y})$ and $(R_{2x}, R_{2y})$, the **reduced radius of curvature** is defined as:

$$\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R_x} + \frac{1}{R_y} = \frac{1}{R_{1x} + R_{2x}} + \frac{1}{R_{1y} + R_{2y}}$$

where convex radii are taken as **positive** and concave (interior) radii as **negative**.

**Special case — Sphere on a Plane:** $R_1 = R$ (ball), $R_2 = \infty$ (plane) → $R' = R$.

**Special case — Two equal spheres:** $R' = R / 2$.

## 2.3 The Four Canonical Hertz Contact Configurations

| # | Configuration | Type | Contact Geometry | Semi-axes |
|---|---------------|------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Sphere ↔ Sphere (ball bearing) | **Point** | Circular | $a = b$ |
| 2 | Sphere ↔ Plane (indenter test) | **Point** | Circular | $a = b$ |
| 3 | Cylinder ↔ Cylinder (parallel axes) | **Line** | Rectangular strip | $2a = 2b$ (width $2b$, length $L$) |
| 4 | Cylinder ↔ Plane (roller on flat) | **Line** | Rectangular strip | $2a = 2b$ (width $2b$, length $L$) |
| 5 | Sphere ↔ Concave Sphere | **Point** | Circular | $a = b$ |
| 6 | Cylinder ↔ Crossed Cylinder (90°) | **Point** | Elliptical (sphere on sphere equivalent) | $a \ne b$ |

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Theory (Physical Reasoning)

The Hertz problem is solved by **superposing two elastic half-spaces** that are initially *just touching* at a point/line, and then forced together by a normal approach $\delta$. Mathematically:

- **Step 1 (Boussinesq/Cerruti):** A normal pressure distribution $p(x,y)$ acting on the surface of an elastic half-space produces a surface displacement $u_z = \frac{1-\nu^2}{\pi E} \iint \frac{p(x',y')}{r} \, dx'\,dy'$.
- **Step 2 (Compatibility):** The two bodies must approach each other by a constant amount $\delta$ over the contact region and by less than $\delta$ outside (no interpenetration).
- **Step 3 (Equilibrium):** $\iint p \, dA = F$ (the resultant pressure equals the applied load).
- **Step 4 (Shape):** Assumption that the contact patch is bounded by $x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 \le 1$ — an ellipse — yields the classic **Hertzian semi-ellipsoidal pressure distribution** $p(x,y) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2}$.

The half-space displacement integral is solved analytically only when the pressure distribution has this specific shape — that is the *deep reason* why Hertz theory produces clean closed-form expressions for $a$, $b$, $p_0$, and $\delta$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Legend:** $F$ = normal load (N), $R'$ = reduced radius (m), $E^*$ = effective modulus (Pa), $L$ = cylinder length (m), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio. All linear dimensions are in **metres** and pressures in **Pascals**.

### A. Effective Modulus

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{E^{*}} = \frac{1-\nu_1^{2}}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^{2}}{E_2}\;}$$

### B. Sphere-on-Sphere (Ball Bearing) — **Point Contact, Circular Patch ($a = b$)**

$$\boxed{\;a^{3} = \frac{3 F R'}{4 E^{*}}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;p_{\max} = \frac{3 F}{2 \pi a^{2}} = \left(\frac{6 F E^{*2}}{\pi^{3} R'^{2}}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \left[\frac{9 F^{2}}{16 R' E^{*2}}\right]^{1/3}\;}$$

### C. Cylinder-on-Cylinder (Parallel Axes) — **Line Contact, Strip of Width $2b$**

$$\boxed{\;b^{2} = \frac{4 F R'}{\pi L E^{*}}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;p_{\max} = \frac{2 F}{\pi b L}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \frac{2 F}{\pi L E^{*}} \left[\ln\left(\frac{2 R'}{b}\right) - 0.407\right]\;} \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

### D. Cylinder on a Flat Plane — **Line Contact**

For a cylinder of radius $R$ on a flat plate ($R' = R$):

$$b^{2} = \frac{4 F R}{\pi L E^{*}}, \qquad p_{\max} = \frac{2 F}{\pi b L}, \qquad \delta = \frac{F}{\pi L E^{*}}\left[2\ln\!\left(\frac{4R}{b}\right) - 1\right]$$

### E. General Elliptical Contact (Sphere on Sphere, Crossed Cylinders)

Let $\rho = b/a$ (axis ratio) be defined implicitly by:

$$\frac{1}{E^{*}}\!\left(\frac{3 F}{2 \pi R'} \cdot \frac{1}{k}\right)^{2/3} = \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[\frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{K(\rho) \, D(\rho) \, (\rho + 1/\rho)}{1 + \rho^2}\right]$$

where $K, D$ are complete elliptic integrals. For most KTU problems this is **not required** — only the circular ($a=b$) or strip ($a\to\infty$) limits appear.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Hertzian Theory

| Application | Hertzian Output Used | Why It Matters |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Ball/roller bearing design | $p_{\max}$, contact patch size | Fatigue (pitting) life, lubricant film |
| Gear design (AGMA, ISO 6336) | $p_{\max}$ at tooth flank | Bending + pitting + scuffing checks |
| Cam-follower design | $b$, $p_{\max}$ | Subsurface shear stress, wear |
| Wheel/Rail contact | $a,b$ | Rolling contact fatigue, wear |
| Brinell / Vickers / Rockwell indentation | $p_{\max}$, $\delta$ | Hardness measurement |
| EHL film thickness (Dowson-Higginson) | $a, R', \delta$ | Minimum lubricant film thickness |
| MEMS / micro-contacts | $a, p_{\max}$ | Adhesion, stiction, wear |

> [!TIP]
> In **EHL (Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication)** of gears and rolling bearings, the Hertzian *centre* and *side* film thicknesses ($h_c$, $h_s$) are derived by feeding Hertz's $a$, $R'$, and load into the **Dowson-Higginson equation**. So Hertz is the *elastic input* to the entire modern rolling-contact tribology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sphere on a Plane: Circular Contact Patch

This is the **most-tested KTU derivation**. We start from a single sphere of radius $R$ pressed into a flat plane by a normal force $F$. The two surfaces together act as a single equivalent sphere of radius $R' = R$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Approach**

Let the sphere move towards the plane by a distance $\delta$ (the approach). The vertical separation between the two original surfaces at a horizontal distance $r$ from the contact centre is:

$$\Delta z(r) = \frac{r^{2}}{2R} + \frac{r^{2}}{2R} = \frac{r^{2}}{R}$$

(We use the small-slope expansion of the sphere's profile: $z(r) = R - \sqrt{R^2 - r^2} \approx r^2/(2R)$.)

**Step 2 — Boundary Condition for Contact**

If the contact patch is a circle of radius $a$, the two bodies must be in contact (zero separation) for $r \le a$, and strictly separated for $r > a$. The pressure distribution that does this (Boussinesq-Cerruti integral) is a **hemispherical** pressure distribution:

$$p(r) = p_{0} \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}}, \qquad 0 \le r \le a$$

This is the **Hertz pressure profile** — semi-ellipsoidal (in 2-D axisymmetric) — with $p(r) = p_0$ at the centre and $p(a) = 0$ at the edge.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium (Load-Pressure Relation)**

The integral of the pressure over the contact area must equal the applied force:

$$F = \int_{0}^{a} p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi p_{0} \int_{0}^{a} r \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}} \, dr$$

Substitute $u = r^{2}/a^{2}$ so that $du = 2r \, dr/a^{2}$, $r\,dr = a^{2} du / 2$:

$$F = 2\pi p_{0} \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{1 - u} \cdot \frac{a^{2}}{2} \, du = \pi p_{0} a^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{1 - u} \, du$$

The remaining integral is the standard Beta function $\int_0^1 (1-u)^{1/2} du = 2/3$, so:

$$\boxed{\;F = \frac{2}{3}\,\pi\, p_{0}\, a^{2}\;} \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_{0} = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Elastic Displacement Compatibility (The Key Equation)**

For an elastic half-space under a circular Hertzian pressure, the surface displacement at the centre of the loaded region is:

$$u_{z} = \frac{\pi a p_{0}}{2 E^{*}}$$

(specifically: $u_{z,\text{centre}} = \frac{\pi p_0 a (2-\nu)}{4E}$ for each body, summed using $E^*$.)

The total approach is twice this (one for each body), and equals the geometric approach $\delta = a^2/R$. Therefore:

$$\frac{a^{2}}{R} = \frac{\pi a p_{0}}{E^{*}}$$

Substitute $p_0 = 3F/(2\pi a^{2})$:

$$\frac{a^{2}}{R} = \frac{\pi a}{E^{*}} \cdot \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} = \frac{3F}{2 E^{*} a}$$

Solve for $a$:

$$\boxed{\;a^{3} = \frac{3 F R}{4 E^{*}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Pressure in Terms of Load**

Insert $a^3 = 3FR/(4E^*)$ into $p_0 = 3F/(2\pi a^{2})$:

$$p_{0}^{3} = \frac{27 F^{3}}{8 \pi^{3} a^{6}} = \frac{27 F^{3}}{8 \pi^{3}} \cdot \frac{16 E^{*2}}{9 F^{2} R^{2}} = \frac{6 F E^{*2}}{\pi^{3} R^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;p_{0} = \left(\frac{6 F E^{*2}}{\pi^{3} R^{2}}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Approach $\delta$**

From $\delta = a^{2}/R$ and $a^{3} = 3FR/(4E^*)$:

$$\delta = \frac{a^{2}}{R} = \left(\frac{3 F R}{4 E^{*}}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \frac{1}{R} = \left(\frac{9 F^{2}}{16 R E^{*2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \left(\frac{9 F^{2}}{16 R E^{*2}}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder on a Plane: Strip Contact

A cylinder of radius $R$ and length $L$ pressed against a flat plane with normal force $F$ creates a contact strip of width $2b$ in the transverse direction.

**Step 1 — Geometry**

At a transverse distance $x$ from the centre, the separation is $x^2/R$ (same expansion as before, applied to the cylinder's circular profile). So the approach relation becomes $\delta = b^2/R$.

**Step 2 — Pressure Profile (Cylindrical Case)**

For a 2-D line contact, the Hertzian pressure is **semi-elliptical** in $x$:

$$p(x) = \frac{p_{0}}{\sqrt{1 - (x/b)^{2}}}, \qquad \text{but integrated, the total load becomes } F = p_{0} \cdot \frac{\pi b L}{2}$$

The 1-D integral over $[-b, b]$:

$$F = L \int_{-b}^{b} p(x) \, dx = L \int_{-b}^{b} \frac{p_{0}}{\sqrt{1-(x/b)^2}}\, dx = \pi b L \, p_{0}$$

Wait — the proper Hertz semi-elliptical profile is $p(x) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - x^2/b^2}$, giving $F = (\pi/2) b L p_0$. The **divergent** form $p_0/\sqrt{1-(x/b)^2}$ appears when solving the Boussinesq integral for a flat punch — that is the *flat-punch solution* and is the *starting* profile that gets regularised to the Hertz form. For clarity, the **Hertz pressure** is:

$$p(x) = p_{0} \sqrt{1 - \frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}}, \quad -b \le x \le b$$

Integrating:

$$F = L \int_{-b}^{b} p_{0}\sqrt{1-\frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}}\, dx = 2 p_{0} L \int_{0}^{b} \sqrt{1-\frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}}\, dx = 2 p_{0} L \cdot \frac{\pi b}{4} = \frac{\pi p_{0} b L}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;F = \frac{\pi p_{0} b L}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{0} = \frac{2F}{\pi b L}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility for Cylinder/Plane**

The approach is the sum of displacements of the two half-spaces:

$$\delta = \frac{b^{2}}{R} = \frac{2 p_{0} b}{E^{*}} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{4R}{b}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\right] = \frac{p_{0} b}{E^{*}}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4R}{b}\right) - 1\right]$$

Solving for $b$ from the leading-order (Hertz's classic) result gives the **logarithmic correction** that is usually neglected. Dropping the small log term and using the dominant Hertz relation $b^2/R = 2 p_0 b / E^*$, with $p_0 = 2F/(\pi b L)$:

$$\frac{b^{2}}{R} = \frac{2 b}{E^{*}} \cdot \frac{2F}{\pi b L} = \frac{4F}{\pi R L E^{*}}$$

$$\boxed{\;b^{2} = \frac{4 F R}{\pi L E^{*}}\;}$$

and

$$\boxed{\;p_{0} = \frac{2F}{\pi b L} = \left(\frac{4 F E^{*}}{\pi^{2} R L^{2}}\right)^{1/2} \cdot \frac{2}{\pi}\cdot \ldots \;\text{use } b\text{ formula directly.}$$

In compact form (substituting $b = \sqrt{4FR/(\pi L E^*)}$):

$$p_{0} = \frac{2F}{\pi L} \sqrt{\frac{\pi L E^{*}}{4 F R}} = \sqrt{\frac{F E^{*}}{\pi R L}}$$

$$\boxed{\;p_{0} = \left(\frac{F E^{*}}{\pi R L}\right)^{1/2}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Distribution Inside the Contact Patch

For both sphere and cylinder, the 3-D stress field is obtained by integrating Boussinesq's point-load solution over the contact region. The result (which KTU expects you to **quote**, not re-derive) is:

- **Sub-surface shear stress** peaks at a depth $\approx 0.48\,a$ (point) or $\approx 0.78\,b$ (line) below the surface.
- **Maximum shear stress** is $\tau_{\max} \approx 0.31\, p_0$ (point) or $0.30\, p_0$ (line).
- These locations are the **crack-initiation sites** for rolling contact fatigue and pitting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Why does subsurface pitting occur even when surface pressure is the design limit?"*
> Because the **maximum shear stress (octahedral) occurs below the surface** at $\tau_{\max} \approx 0.31\,p_0$. The Hertzian pressure profile concentrates deformation beneath the contact, where inclusions, voids, and decarburised layers nucleate fatigue cracks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A steel ball of radius $R = 12.5$ mm is pressed against a steel flat with a normal load $F = 500$ N. Given $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, compute the contact radius $a$, maximum contact pressure $p_0$, and approach $\delta$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Effective Modulus:

$$\frac{1}{E^{*}} = \frac{2(1-0.3^{2})}{2.10 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{2(0.91)}{2.10 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{1.82}{2.10 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$E^{*} = \frac{2.10 \times 10^{11}}{1.82} = 1.154 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$$

Step 2 — Contact Radius:

$$a^{3} = \frac{3 F R}{4 E^{*}} = \frac{3 \times 500 \times 0.0125}{4 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{0.1875}{4.616 \times 10^{11}} = 4.062 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$a = (4.062 \times 10^{-13})^{1/3} = 7.40 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = \mathbf{74.0 \,\mu m}$$

Step 3 — Maximum Pressure:

$$p_{0} = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 500}{2 \pi \times (7.40 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} = \frac{1500}{3.442 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$p_{0} = 4.36 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{43.6 \,\text{GPa}}$$

Step 4 — Approach:

$$\delta = \frac{a^{2}}{R} = \frac{(7.40 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}{0.0125} = \frac{5.476 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0125} = \mathbf{4.38 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.438 \,\mu m}$$

> [!TIP]
> A contact area of $a \approx 74 \,\mu m$ for $F = 500$ N yields $p_0 \approx 44$ GPa — well above steel yield — meaning a real bearing uses **multiple balls** and a *conforming raceway groove* to drop $p_0$ to the safe 1.5 – 2.0 GPa range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hertzian Pressure Distribution (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Normal Force F"] --> B["Elastic Deformation of Both Bodies"]
    B --> C["Geometric Approach delta equals a squared over R"]
    C --> D{"Contact Geometry"}
    D --> E["Sphere on Plane: Circular Patch radius a"]
    D --> F["Cylinder on Plane: Rectangular Strip width 2b"]
    E --> G["Pressure Profile: p of r equals p0 times sqrt of 1 minus r squared over a squared"]
    F --> H["Pressure Profile: p of x equals p0 times sqrt of 1 minus x squared over b squared"]
    G --> I["Maximum Pressure p0 at centre, zero at edge"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Subsurface Shear tau max approximately 0.31 p0 at depth 0.48 a"]
    J --> K["Fatigue Crack Initiation Site for Pitting"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hertzian Contact in a Rolling Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Radial Load F applied to Bearing"] --> M["Hertz Contact Solver Module"]
    M --> M1["Compute Effective Modulus E*"]
    M --> M2["Compute Reduced Radius R*"]
    M --> M3["Compute Contact Half-axis a or b"]
    M1 --> N["Output Block: Contact Geometry"]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    N --> O["Maximum Hertzian Pressure p0"]
    N --> P["Contact Patch Area"]
    N --> Q["Elastic Approach delta"]
    O --> R["Tribology Decision Block"]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> R1["Compare p0 with Yield: Design Safe"]
    R --> R2["Compute lambda ratio for EHL regime"]
    R --> R3["Predict Rolling Contact Fatigue Life L10"]
    R2 --> S["EHL Film Thickness h via Dowson Higginson"]
    R3 --> T["Bearing Life Output"]
    R1 --> T
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow: Point vs Line Contact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Hertzian Contact Problem Statement"] --> P1{"Are Principal Radii Different in x and y?"}
    P1 -- "No: a equals b" --> P2["Point Contact: Sphere on Sphere or Sphere on Plane"]
    P1 -- "Yes: a different from b" --> P3["Elliptical Contact: Sphere on Sphere at angle or Crossed Cylinders"]
    P1 -- "One radius tends to infinity: line contact" --> P4["Line Contact: Cylinder on Plane or Cylinder on Cylinder parallel"]

    P2a --> PEND["Yield: p0, delta, Subsurface Stress Field"]

    P3a --> PEND
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Contact Patch (Verbal Diagram)

> Since Hertzian contact involves continuous 3-D stress fields, the following **annotated description** is the canonical visual for your KTU answer sheet:

```
         z

         |   (sphere of radius R)
       __|__
      /  |   \              .--- Sphere profile z = r²/(2R)
     /   |    \          .'

     \   |    /     ◯ ← pressure peaks at centre, zero at edge
      \__|__/      p₀
     ──────────── Plane
         
  Pressure profile p(r):
  
  p₀ ●
     |╲
     | ╲
     |  ╲___

     0                  a
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the fundamental assumptions of Hertzian contact theory. Mention any two applications where it is used in tribology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The assumptions of Hertzian contact theory are: (i) the contact area is small compared to the radii of curvature; (ii) each body can be treated as a semi-infinite elastic half-space; (iii) the strains are small and the material is linearly elastic; (iv) the surfaces are smooth, continuous, and non-conforming; (v) no friction acts at the interface (purely normal load). **Applications:** (a) ball and roller bearings — to compute the Hertzian contact pressure and predict rolling contact fatigue life, and (b) gear tooth contact — to determine the contact stress on the flank of meshing teeth and check for pitting or scuffing failure.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "reduced radius of curvature" used in Hertzian contact analysis. Write the expression used for a sphere in contact with a flat plane.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *reduced (or effective) radius of curvature* $R'$ is the single equivalent radius that characterises the curvature of two bodies in contact along a particular plane. For two bodies with principal radii $R_{1x}, R_{1y}$ and $R_{2x}, R_{2y}$ (convex = +, concave = −), it is defined as

$$\frac{1}{R'} = \frac{1}{R_{1x} + R_{2x}} + \frac{1}{R_{1y} + R_{2y}}.$$

For a sphere of radius $R$ on a flat plane, $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = \infty$, so the **reduced radius is $R' = R$**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sphere on Flat (Point Contact)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum Hertzian contact pressure $p_0$ and the contact radius $a$ when a steel sphere of radius $R$ is pressed against a flat steel surface with a normal load $F$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12.5 mm radius steel ball is loaded against a flat steel plate with $F = 800$ N. Take $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$. Determine: (i) the contact patch radius $a$, (ii) the maximum contact pressure $p_0$, and (iii) the elastic approach $\delta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

1. **Assumptions (1 mark):** Small contact area, elastic half-spaces, smooth non-conforming surfaces, no tangential traction, isotropic homogeneous materials, small strains.
2. **Geometry (2 marks):** Approaching by $\delta$ gives separation $\Delta z = r^2/R$. The contact patch is a circle of radius $a$ with $p(r) = p_0 \sqrt{1-r^2/a^2}$. Equilibrium gives $F = (2/3) \pi p_0 a^2$.
3. **Compatibility (2 marks):** Half-space displacement under Hertzian pressure is $u_z = (\pi a p_0)/(2 E^*)$. Total approach $2 u_z = a^2 / R$. Substituting $p_0$ gives $a^3 = 3FR/(4E^*)$.
4. **Final expression (2 marks):** $p_0 = 3F/(2\pi a^2) = \left(6 F E^{*2}/(\pi^3 R^2)\right)^{1/3}$ and $\delta = a^2/R$.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Effective modulus (1 mark):**
$$E^{*} = \frac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{2.10\times 10^{11}}{2(1-0.09)} = \frac{2.10\times 10^{11}}{1.82} = 1.154 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Contact radius $a$ (2 marks):**
$$a^3 = \frac{3 F R}{4 E^*} = \frac{3 \times 800 \times 0.0125}{4 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}} = 6.498 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^3$$
$$a = (6.498 \times 10^{-13})^{1/3} = 8.66 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 86.6 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Maximum pressure $p_0$ (2 marks):**
$$p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^2} = \frac{3 \times 800}{2\pi \times (8.66\times 10^{-5})^2} = \frac{2400}{4.71 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 5.09 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \approx 50.9 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iv) Approach $\delta$ (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{a^2}{R} = \frac{(8.66 \times 10^{-5})^2}{0.0125} = 6.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.60 \,\mu\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Common 1–2 mark losses):**
> 1. **Mixing $E$ and $E^*$** — never plug $E = 210$ GPa directly into the Hertz formulas. Always use $E^* = E / [2(1-\nu^2)]$ for identical materials, or the full two-material form otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting to cube-root** $a^3$ to get $a$ — a frequent numerical slip.
> 3. **Wrong units** — use SI (metres, Newtons, Pascals) throughout. $a$ in **mm** and $F$ in **kN** without conversion = 0 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cylinder on Flat (Line Contact) [Internal Choice]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for (i) the half-width of contact strip $b$ and (ii) the maximum pressure $p_0$ when a long steel cylinder of radius $R$ rests on a flat steel surface under a normal load $F$ per unit length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel roller of diameter 60 mm and length 80 mm is pressed against a flat steel surface with a normal load of 25 kN. Using $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, find (i) the half-width of the contact strip, (ii) the maximum contact pressure, and (iii) the centre-line deflection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Cylinder radius $R$, length $L$, force $F$, contact strip of half-width $b$, pressure $p(x) = p_0\sqrt{1 - x^2/b^2}$.
2. **Equilibrium (2 marks):** Integrating over the strip: $F = (\pi/2) \, p_0 \, b \, L \Rightarrow p_0 = 2F/(\pi b L)$.
3. **Compatibility (2 marks):** For a half-space under line pressure, the surface displacement at $x$ is $u_z(x) = (2 p_0 b / E^*) \left[\sqrt{1-(x/b)^2} - (x/b) \arcsin(x/b)\right] \cdot (\ldots)$. At the centre, $u_z = \pi p_0 b / (2 E^*)$. Total approach $2 u_z = b^2 / R$.
4. **Combining (2 marks):** $b^2/R = 2 \cdot (\pi p_0 b)/(2E^*) = \pi p_0 b / E^*$. Substituting $p_0 = 2F/(\pi b L)$ gives $b^2 = 4 F R / (\pi L E^*)$ and $p_0 = \sqrt{F E^* / (\pi R L)}$.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Effective modulus and effective radius (1 mark):**
$$E^* = 1.154 \times 10^{11}\text{ Pa}, \quad R' = R = 0.030\text{ m}, \quad L = 0.080\text{ m}, \quad F = 25000\text{ N}$$

**(ii) Half-width of contact (2 marks):**
$$b^2 = \frac{4 F R}{\pi L E^*} = \frac{4 \times 25000 \times 0.030}{\pi \times 0.080 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{3000}{2.901 \times 10^{10}}$$
$$b^2 = 1.034 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow b = 3.216 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.322 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Maximum pressure (2 marks):**
$$p_0 = \frac{2F}{\pi b L} = \frac{2 \times 25000}{\pi \times 3.216 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.080} = \frac{50000}{8.082 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.19 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 619 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Centre-line deflection (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{b^2}{R} = \frac{1.034 \times 10^{-7}}{0.030} = 3.45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 3.45 \,\mu\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Common 1–2 mark losses):**
> 1. **Sphere vs Cylinder formula confusion** — students often plug the sphere formulas for line contact or vice versa. Memorise: sphere gives $a^3$ (cubic), cylinder gives $b^2$ (quadratic).
> 2. **Forgetting $L$ (length)** in cylinder formulas — without $L$, the pressure becomes infinite.
> 3. **Sign of $b$** — $b$ is half-width, so the **full contact width** is $2b$. The problem statement may ask for either; read carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition:** Hertzian contact theory describes the elastic deformation of two non-conforming curved bodies under a normal load, giving the size and pressure of the resulting contact patch.
- [x] **Valid when:** contact area $\ll$ body radii, elastic half-space assumption holds, small strains, smooth & dry (or simply no tangential traction) interface.
- [x] **Effective modulus:** $1/E^* = (1-\nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2)/E_2$ — for steel-on-steel, $E^* = 1.15 \times 10^{11}$ Pa.
- [x] **Reduced radius:** $1/R' = 1/(R_{1x} + R_{2x}) + 1/(R_{1y} + R_{2y})$.
- [x] **Sphere (point) formulas:** $a^3 = 3FR'/(4E^*)$, $p_0 = 3F/(2\pi a^2)$, $\delta = (9F^2/16 R' E^{*2})^{1/3}$.
- [x] **Cylinder (line) formulas:** $b^2 = 4 F R' / (\pi L E^*)$, $p_0 = 2F/(\pi b L)$, $\delta = b^2/R'$.
- [x] **Pressure profile:** semi-ellipsoidal $p(r) = p_0\sqrt{1-r^2/a^2}$ (sphere) and $p(x) = p_0\sqrt{1-x^2/b^2}$ (cylinder).
- [x] **Subsurface stress:** $\tau_{\max} \approx 0.31\, p_0$ at depth $\approx 0.48\, a$ (sphere) — this is where pitting cracks start.
- [x] **Typical $p_0$ values:** Ball bearing 1.5 – 2.5 GPa, gear tooth contact 0.8 – 1.5 GPa, cam-follower up to 1 GPa.
- [x] **Applications:** Ball/roller bearings, gears, cam-followers, wheel-rail, indentation hardness tests, EHL film thickness (Dowson-Higginson input).
- [x] **Common pitfalls:** Mixing $E$ with $E^*$, sphere vs cylinder formulas, missing $L$ for cylinders, unit errors (m vs mm, N vs kN).
- [x] **Memory hook:** "**Cubic for Sphere, Square for Strip**" — $a^3$, $b^2$. This single line can save 7 marks in a Part-B question.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** In Part B, always *quote* the four assumptions (1 mark), *write* the formula with units (1 mark), and *substitute* step-by-step with explicit numerical values (rest). Examiners reward **method visibility** — they cannot award partial marks if your work is hidden in a calculator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deformation of Solid Bodies Under Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deformation of Solid Bodies Under Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Deformation of a solid body under load* refers to the change in **shape** (geometric configuration) and/or **dimensions** of a solid when it is subjected to external mechanical forces (loads). The internal response of the body — quantified through **stress** ($\sigma$) and **strain** ($\varepsilon$) — governs whether the deformation is **recoverable (elastic)** or **permanent (plastic)**.

In Tribology, this concept is foundational because **every contacting surface deforms** before, during, and after load transmission. The nature of this deformation dictates real contact area, pressure distribution, friction, wear, and lubricant film formation.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

- **Load (P):** The external force applied on a body. SI unit: **Newton (N)**.
- **Stress ($\sigma$):** Internal resistance per unit area. $\sigma = P/A$. SI unit: **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**.
- **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Deformation per unit original dimension. Dimensionless.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Stiffness in normal loading. SI unit: **Pa** or **N/m²**.
- **Shear Modulus (G):** Stiffness in tangential loading. SI unit: **Pa**.
- **Bulk Modulus (K):** Resistance to volumetric (hydrostatic) compression. SI unit: **Pa**.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Lateral-to-axial strain ratio in uniaxial loading. Range: **$-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Rubber Ball vs. The Steel Ball:**
> Imagine pressing your thumb onto two balls of the same diameter — one made of soft rubber, the other made of hardened steel.
> - The **rubber ball** flattens noticeably under your thumb. When you remove your thumb, it *fully recovers* its original shape. This is **elastic deformation**.
> - The **steel ball** appears to barely deform. However, at the microscopic level, it *does* deform elastically over a tiny contact patch (Hertzian contact).
> - If you pressed with enormous force (or used a soft metal like lead), the ball would *permanently dent*. This is **plastic deformation**.
>
> **Tribological Insight:** Even the hardest engineering surfaces (e.g., bearing steel) deform elastically under typical contact loads. This elastic deformation is what creates the elliptical contact patch in a rolling element bearing, dictates pressure distribution, and governs lubricant film thickness.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Tribology

> [!TIP]
> In Module 1 of PEMUT754, deformation analysis is the **gateway** to understanding:
> 1. **Hertzian Contact Theory** (elliptical/circular contact patches).
> 2. **Real vs. Nominal Contact Area.**
> 3. **Asperity deformation** in surface roughness studies.
> 4. **EHL (Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication)** film formation.
>
> Without a clear grasp of elastic-plastic response of solids, *none* of the subsequent tribological models can be applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Deformation Under Load

When a load is applied to a solid body, the resulting deformation can be classified into three principal categories:

### 2.1.1 Elastic Deformation (Recoverable)
- Deformation is **fully reversible** upon load removal.
- Governed by **Hooke's Law**: stress is linearly proportional to strain.
- Atoms are displaced from equilibrium but return when the load is removed.
- **Example:** Spring stretching, ball bearing contact under normal load.

### 2.1.2 Plastic Deformation (Permanent)
- Deformation is **irreversible** upon load removal.
- Occurs when stress exceeds the **Yield Strength** ($\sigma_y$) of the material.
- Caused by dislocation motion, slip along crystallographic planes, and twinning.
- **Example:** Indentation by a hard stylus, machining, forging.

### 2.1.3 Viscoelastic Deformation (Time-Dependent)
- Material exhibits **both viscous and elastic** characteristics.
- Strain is **time-dependent** even under constant stress (creep, stress relaxation).
- **Example:** Polymers, bio-lubricants, human cartilage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently test the *boundaries* between these regimes using the **stress-strain diagram** (see SECTION 4). Memorize the **Proportional Limit (A), Elastic Limit (B), Yield Point (C), Ultimate Strength (D), and Fracture Point (E)**.

## 2.2 Stress-Strain Behaviour — The Conceptual Flow

1. **Initial Linear Region (O → A):** Hooke's law valid. $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.
2. **Proportional Limit (A):** End of strict linearity.
3. **Elastic Limit (B):** Beyond this point, some permanent deformation begins.
4. **Yield Point (C):** Material yields at yield stress $\sigma_y$. Plastic flow starts.
5. **Strain Hardening Region (C → D):** Material strengthens due to dislocation entanglement.
6. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (D):** Maximum stress the material can sustain.
7. **Necking & Fracture (D → E):** Localized cross-sectional reduction, followed by failure.

## 2.3 Elastic Constants — The Engineering Triad

Three moduli describe the elastic response under **three fundamental loading modes**:

| **Loading Mode** | **Strain Type** | **Governing Modulus** | **Constitutive Equation** | **Typical Range (Metals)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial Tension/Compression | Normal strain $\varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus **E** | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | 70–700 GPa |
| Pure Shear | Shear strain $\gamma$ | Shear Modulus **G** | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | 25–280 GPa |
| Hydrostatic Compression | Volumetric strain $\Delta$ | Bulk Modulus **K** | $p = -K \cdot \Delta$ | 70–700 GPa |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-frequency** in KTU University Examinations. The relationships between elastic constants are derived in SECTION 3.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = P/A$ | Normal stress definition | Pa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | Normal strain definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Pa |
| 4 | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | Hooke's Law (shear) | Pa |
| 5 | $p = -K \cdot (\Delta V / V)$ | Bulk modulus relation | Pa |
| 6 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Poisson's Ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | E–G–$\nu$ relationship | Pa |
| 8 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E–K–$\nu$ relationship | Pa |
| 9 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | G–K–$\nu$ relationship | Pa |
| 10 | $\sigma_y = \text{Yield Stress}$ | Boundary elastic → plastic | Pa |
| 11 | $\delta = PL / AE$ | Elongation of a bar | m |
| 12 | $U = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot V$ | Elastic strain energy density | J/m³ |

> **Note on symbols:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation has been intentionally avoided inside table cells to preserve markdown rendering integrity. The reader may interpret any vertical-bar notation in working notes as $\vert \cdot \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Bearing Design:** Hertzian contact stresses are computed using elastic deformation theory.
- **Gear Teeth:** Bending and contact (Hertzian) stresses must remain below yield.
- **Fasteners:** Bolt elongation under preload uses $\delta = PL/AE$.
- **Tribology:** Real contact area between rough surfaces depends on the elastic-plastic response of asperities.
- **Biomechanics:** Articular cartilage (joint surfaces) exhibits viscoelastic behaviour under walking loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hooke's Law from First Principles

**Goal:** Show that for an axially loaded prismatic bar, stress is proportional to strain.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry and Loading
Consider a uniform cylindrical bar of:
- Original length = $L$
- Cross-sectional area = $A$
- Applied axial tensile load at the ends = $P$

### Step 2: Define Stress
The internal force per unit area resisting the load is the **normal stress**:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

### Step 3: Define Strain
The deformation per unit original length is the **normal strain**:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}
$$

where $\delta L$ is the elongation of the bar.

### Step 4: State the Constitutive Assumption
For linearly elastic materials (within the proportional limit), experimental observation (Robert Hooke, 1676) gives:

$$
\sigma \propto \varepsilon
$$

### Step 5: Introduce the Proportionality Constant
The constant of proportionality is the **Young's Modulus (E)**:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

### Step 6: Combine All Three Equations

$$
\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}}
$$

Solving for the elongation:

$$
\boxed{\delta L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}}
$$

This is the **standard elongation formula** for an axially loaded elastic bar — used in KTU numerical problems on bolt design, spring deflection, and bar stretching.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between E, G, and $\nu$

**Goal:** Prove the identity $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$.

### Step 1: Consider a Square Element Under Pure Shear
Take a small square element of side $a$. Apply shear stress $\tau$ on the top and bottom faces. The element distorts into a parallelogram with shear strain $\gamma$.

The diagonal of the square, originally at 45°, changes length. The principal stresses on the element are:

$$
\sigma_1 = +\tau, \qquad \sigma_2 = -\tau
$$

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law in Principal Directions

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left( \sigma_1 - \nu \cdot \sigma_2 \right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} \left( \tau - \nu (-\tau) \right) = \frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E} \left( \sigma_2 - \nu \cdot \sigma_1 \right) = -\frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 3: Relate Shear Strain to Principal Strains
From Mohr's strain circle (or by geometry of the deformed parallelogram):

$$
\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E} - \left( -\frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E} \right) = \frac{2\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}
$$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions for $\gamma$

$$
\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

Cancel $\tau$ (assuming $\tau \ne 0$):

$$
\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1 + \nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 6: Invert

$$
\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}
$$

This identity is a **high-yield KTU result**. Memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between E, K, and $\nu$

**Goal:** Prove the identity $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$.

### Step 1: Apply Hydrostatic Pressure
Apply hydrostatic pressure $p$ to a cubic element. The stresses in all three principal directions are equal:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p
$$

### Step 2: Compute Volumetric Strain
Volumetric strain is the sum of normal strains:

$$
\Delta = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z
$$

By the generalized Hooke's Law:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left( \sigma_x - \nu (\sigma_y + \sigma_z) \right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left( -p - \nu (-p - p) \right) = \frac{1}{E} \left( -p + 2\nu p \right) = -\frac{p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

By symmetry: $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_x$.

### Step 3: Sum the Strains

$$
\Delta = 3 \varepsilon_x = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Bulk Modulus Definition
By definition: $p = -K \cdot \Delta \Longrightarrow \Delta = -p/K$.

### Step 5: Equate

$$
-\frac{p}{K} = -\frac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
$$

Cancel $-p$:

$$
\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

This identity is **essential for KTU problems on compressibility** of materials under high pressure (e.g., in EHL contacts).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table

| **Identity** | **Final Form** | **Proof Status** |
|---|---|---|
| $E$ – $G$ – $\nu$ | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | ✅ Derived above |
| $E$ – $K$ – $\nu$ | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | ✅ Derived above |
| $G$ – $K$ – $\nu$ | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | ✅ Follows from (7) and (8) |
| Combined | $\dfrac{E}{3G} + \dfrac{E}{9K} = 1$ | Substitute and simplify |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Types of Deformation Under Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied to Solid Body] --> B{Is load below yield stress?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Elastic Deformation]
    B -- No --> D{Is load above ultimate stress?}
    D -- No --> E[Plastic Deformation]
    D -- Yes --> F[Fracture / Failure]
    C --> C1[Reversible]
    C --> C2[Governed by Hooke's Law]
    C --> C3[Energy stored as strain energy]
    E --> E1[Permanent shape change]
    E --> E2[Dislocation motion dominant]
    E --> E3[Energy dissipated as heat]
    F --> F1[Crack initiation and propagation]
    F --> F2[Loss of load-bearing capacity]
    A --> G{Time-dependent material?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Viscoelastic Deformation]
    H --> H1[Creep under constant stress]
    H --> H2[Stress relaxation under constant strain]
    H --> H3[Typical: polymers, biological tissues]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress-Strain Response Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin O: Zero load] --> A
    A[Point A: Proportional Limit sigma equals k times epsilon valid] --> B
    B[Point B: Elastic Limit Maximum recoverable deformation] --> C
    C[Point C: Yield Point Onset of plastic flow] --> D
    D[Point D: Ultimate Tensile Strength Maximum load capacity] --> E
    E[Point E: Fracture Point Material separation]
    A --> A1[Region 1: Linear Elastic]
    B --> B1[Region 2: Non-linear Elastic]
    C --> C1[Region 3: Plastic Yielding]
    D --> D1[Region 4: Strain Hardening]
    E --> E1[Region 5: Necking and Failure]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Elastic Constants Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LO[Loading Mode] --> M1[Uniaxial Tension]
    LO --> M2[Pure Shear]
    LO --> M3[Hydrostatic Pressure]
    M1 --> E1[Young's Modulus E]
    M2 --> G1[Shear Modulus G]
    M3 --> K1[Bulk Modulus K]
    E1 --> R1[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu]
    G1 --> R1
    R1 --> P1[Poisson's Ratio nu]
    E1 --> R2[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu]
    K1 --> R2
    R2 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Real-World Deformation Behaviour (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Rough Surface Asperity 1] --> C1[Contact Point]
    S2[Rough Surface Asperity 2] --> C1
    C1 --> L1{Load Magnitude}
    L1 -- Low Load --> ED[Elastic Deformation at Asperity]
    L1 -- Medium Load --> PD[Elastic-Plastic Mixed Response]
    L1 -- High Load --> BD[Bulk Plastic Deformation and Subsurface Strain]
    ED --> H1[Hertzian Contact Patch]
    PD --> H2[Real Contact Area Approaches Nominal]
    BD --> H3[Wear Particle Generation]
    H1 --> TR1[Low Friction Regime]
    H2 --> TR2[Boundary Lubrication Regime]
    H3 --> TR3[Abrasive Wear Dominant]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
