# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT754]**

> **Q:** Define the following terms with units:
> (i) Young's Modulus
> (ii) Poisson's Ratio
> (iii) Bulk Modulus

**Model Answer:**

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Unit** | **Typical Value (Steel)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus ($E$) | Ratio of normal stress to normal strain under uniaxial loading: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Pa (or N/m²) | $\approx 210$ GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) | Negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Dimensionless | $\approx 0.30$ |
| Bulk Modulus ($K$) | Ratio of hydrostatic pressure to volumetric strain: $K = -p / (\Delta V / V)$ | Pa (or N/m²) | $\approx 170$ GPa |

> **Note:** The examiner expects *both* the definition and the SI unit. Avoid quoting values without context.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Examination]**

> **Q:** Distinguish between **elastic deformation** and **plastic deformation** of a solid body under load.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Elastic Deformation** | **Plastic Deformation** |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Fully reversible upon unloading | Permanent, irreversible |
| Stress Range | Below yield stress $\sigma_y$ | Above yield stress $\sigma_y$ |
| Mechanism | Atomic bond stretching | Dislocation motion, slip |
| Governing Law | Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) | Beyond Hooke's Law (non-linear) |
| Energy | Stored as strain energy | Dissipated as heat |
| Example | Spring stretch, bearing contact | Metal forging, indentation |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q (a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q (b)** A steel rod of length **1.5 m**, diameter **25 mm**, is subjected to an axial tensile load of **80 kN**. If $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$, find:
> (i) The elongation of the rod.
> (ii) The lateral contraction in diameter.
> (iii) The change in volume.
> (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

*This is the derivation provided in SECTION 3.2 of these notes. Examiner expects:*

1. Square element under pure shear — set up geometry (1 mark).
2. Principal stresses $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (1 mark).
3. Apply generalized Hooke's law to find $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ (2 marks).
4. Use $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ (1 mark).
5. Apply $\tau = G\gamma$ and equate to eliminate $\gamma$ (1 mark).
6. Final result $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ boxed with statement (1 mark).

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**

$$
L = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 210 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}, \quad \nu = 0.3
$$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Axial stress**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80{,}000}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.630 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 163.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 (i): Axial strain and elongation**

$$
\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{1.630 \times 10^8}{210 \times 10^9} = 7.762 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta L = \varepsilon_{axial} \cdot L = 7.762 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 1.164 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.164 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 (ii): Lateral strain and contraction**

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -0.3 \times 7.762 \times 10^{-4} = -2.329 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot d = -2.329 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 = -5.82 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta d = -5.82 \, \mu\text{m} \text{ (contraction)} }
$$

**Step 5 (iii): Volumetric strain and change in volume**

$$
\Delta = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z
$$

For uniaxial loading: $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_{axial}$ and $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_{lateral}$.

$$
\Delta = \varepsilon_{axial} + 2 \varepsilon_{lateral} = \varepsilon_{axial}(1 - 2\nu)
$$

$$
\Delta = 7.762 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 7.762 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 3.105 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 6: Original volume**

$$
V = A \cdot L = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 7.3635 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7: Change in volume**

$$
\boxed{\delta V = \Delta \cdot V = 3.105 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.3635 \times 10^{-4} = 2.286 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 = 228.6 \text{ mm}^3}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert units (mm → m, kN → N, GPa → Pa). Awarded **−1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting lateral contraction as positive. It should be **negative** (diameter decreases). Awarded **−0.5 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using $\Delta V = A \cdot \delta L$ instead of $\Delta V = \Delta \cdot V$. Awarded **−1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in volumetric strain (i.e., writing $\Delta = \varepsilon_{axial} + \varepsilon_{lateral}$). Awarded **−1 mark**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **Q (a)** Explain in detail the **stress-strain curve** for a ductile material under uniaxial tensile loading, marking all significant points and regions. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q (b)** A cylindrical brass specimen (E = 100 GPa, $\nu$ = 0.35) of length **200 mm** and diameter **15 mm** is compressed by a load of **20 kN**. Determine:
> (i) Axial stress and strain.
> (ii) Change in length.
> (iii) Change in diameter.
> (iv) Bulk modulus K using the identity $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$.
> (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

*Refer to SECTION 4.2 for the schematic. Examiner's mark-splitting:*

1. Sketch of stress-strain diagram (qualitative, with axes labelled) — **2 marks**.
2. Identification of 5 key points: Proportional Limit (A), Elastic Limit (B), Yield Point (C), Ultimate Strength (D), Fracture Point (E) — **3 marks**.
3. Brief description of each region (Linear Elastic, Yielding, Strain Hardening, Necking, Fracture) — **2 marks**.

**Key Points to Highlight (Student must mention):**
- **Point O → A (Linear Region):** Hooke's law valid; $\sigma = E\varepsilon$.
- **Point A (Proportional Limit):** End of strict proportionality.
- **Point B (Elastic Limit):** Maximum recoverable deformation.
- **Point C (Upper/Lower Yield Point):** Plastic flow begins.
- **Point C → D (Strain Hardening):** Dislocation entanglement; material strengthens.
- **Point D (Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS):** Maximum stress capacity.
- **Point D → E (Necking):** Localized cross-section reduction.
- **Point E (Fracture):** Final failure.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**

$$
L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}, \quad d = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}, \quad P = 20 \text{ kN} = 20{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
E = 100 \text{ GPa} = 100 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}, \quad \nu = 0.35
$$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 (i): Axial stress (compressive, so negative)**

$$
\sigma = -\frac{P}{A} = -\frac{20{,}000}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = -1.132 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = -113.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

Axial strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{-1.132 \times 10^8}{100 \times 10^9} = -1.132 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 (ii): Change in length**

$$
\boxed{\delta L = \varepsilon_{axial} \cdot L = -1.132 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = -2.264 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = -0.2264 \text{ mm}}
$$

(Negative sign indicates shortening, as expected under compression.)

**Step 4 (iii): Change in diameter**

Lateral strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -0.35 \times (-1.132 \times 10^{-3}) = +3.962 \times 10^{-4}
$$

(Note: Under axial compression, the specimen bulges laterally — positive lateral strain.)

$$
\delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot d = 3.962 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.015 = 5.943 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta d = +5.94 \, \mu\text{m (increase in diameter, i.e., bulging)} }
$$

**Step 5 (iv): Bulk modulus K**

Rearranging $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$
K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{100 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 0.7)} = \frac{100 \times 10^9}{3 \times 0.3} = \frac{100 \times 10^9}{0.9}
$$

$$
\boxed{K = 1.111 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 111.1 \text{ GPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using positive stress for compression without indicating sign convention. Examiner expects explicit mention of "compressive" or a negative sign. **−0.5 mark** if omitted.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing the diameter *decreases* under compression. In reality, it **increases** due to Poisson effect. **−1 mark** for wrong sign.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Substituting $1 - 2\nu = 1 - 0.35$ instead of $1 - 0.7$. Common bracket error. **−1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Reporting $K$ in Pa without converting to GPa for readability (minor — **−0.5 mark**).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deformation of Solid Bodies Under Load**

- [x] **Three Types of Deformation:** Elastic, Plastic, Viscoelastic — know triggers and reversibility.
- [x] **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (uniaxial), $\tau = G\gamma$ (shear), $p = -K\Delta$ (volumetric).
- [x] **Five Key Points on Stress-Strain Curve:** Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, UTS, Fracture Point.
- [x] **Three Elastic Constants:** $E$ (stiffness in tension), $G$ (stiffness in shear), $K$ (resistance to volume change).
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Typical range 0.2–0.35 for metals; 0.5 for incompressible materials (rubber).
- [x] **Identity 1:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ — *derived from pure shear element analysis*.
- [x] **Identity 2:** $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ — *derived from hydrostatic pressure analysis*.
- [x] **Identity 3:** $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ — *follows from identities 1 & 2*.
- [x] **Elongation Formula:** $\delta L = PL / (AE)$ — used in bolt design, bar stretching problems.
- [x] **Volumetric Strain:** $\Delta = \varepsilon_{axial}(1 - 2\nu)$ for uniaxial loading.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Tension positive, compression negative; lateral strain under tension is negative.
- [x] **Tribological Link:** Elastic deformation governs Hertzian contact, asperity flattening, and EHL film thickness — *Module 1's bridge to Module 2 (Friction) and Module 3 (Wear)*.
- [x] **Unit Vigilance:** Always convert mm → m, kN → N, GPa → Pa *before* substitution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:**
> For any 14-mark question involving *derivation of elastic constant relationships*, the examiner awards **1 mark each** for: (1) clear problem setup, (2) free body diagram / element diagram, (3) correct application of generalized Hooke's law, (4) algebraic manipulation, (5) use of shear strain definition $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$, (6) final substitution, and (7) clearly boxed final answer with physical interpretation. **Do not skip the free body diagram** — it is a guaranteed 1-mark item.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction: Laws of Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction: Laws of Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Tribology, **Friction** is formally defined as the **tangential resistance force** that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact, or the tendency of motion between two contacting bodies. It is a surface phenomenon arising from the interaction of microscopic asperities, adhesion between contact junctions, and plastic deformation of surface irregularities at the real area of contact.

Mathematically, the friction force $F$ acting tangentially at the interface of two bodies is expressed as:

$$F = \mu N$$

where $\mu$ (mu) is the **coefficient of friction** (a dimensionless scalar) and $N$ is the **normal reaction force** (in Newtons, **N**) exerted perpendicular to the contact interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme PEMUT754 syllabus, Module 1 explicitly demands the student to *identify, formulate, and apply the classical laws of friction* governing dry and lubricated contacts. Mastery of Coulomb's empirical laws is the entry point for the entire course.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden almirah (wardrobe) across a tiled floor. You push harder, and suddenly it slides. The "stickiness" that resisted your initial push is **static friction**, and the slightly smaller "dragging resistance" you feel while it slides is **kinetic (or dynamic) friction**. The microscopic peaks (asperities) on the wood and the floor interlock and deform, demanding energy to break free. Friction is the macro-scale manifestation of these micro-scale battles at the contact interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Standard gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is **dimensionless** (no units).
> - Typical dry-contact $\mu$ ranges: Metals on metals **0.15 – 0.6**, Rubber on dry concrete **0.6 – 0.85**, Teflon on steel **0.04**.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Friction (Overview)

For KTU Module 1, friction is broadly classified into:

| Category | Sub-type | Brief Description |
|----------|----------|-------------------|
| Based on State of Motion | **Static Friction ($F_s$)** | Resistance before motion initiates. |
| Based on State of Motion | **Kinetic/Sliding Friction ($F_k$)** | Resistance during relative sliding. |
| Based on Contact Type | **Dry (Coulomb) Friction** | No lubricant present. |
| Based on Contact Type | **Fluid/Lubricated Friction** | Fluid film separates the surfaces. |
| Based on Geometry | **Rolling Friction** | Resistance during rolling (e.g., ball bearings). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coulomb's Laws of Dry Friction (Empirical Foundations)

The classical laws of friction were proposed by **Charles-Augustin de Coulomb (1785)** through systematic experimentation on dry, non-lubricated surfaces. KTU examiners repeatedly test these as the foundational premise of Module 1.

**Law 1 — Proportionality to Normal Load:**
The friction force $F$ is directly proportional to the normal reaction $N$ between the contacting surfaces, provided the surfaces are in a particular condition and the normal load is within reasonable limits.

$$F \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \mu N$$

**Law 2 — Independence from Apparent Area of Contact:**
The friction force is independent of the **apparent area of contact** between the two surfaces. It depends only on the magnitude of the normal load and the nature of the materials in contact.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A short, fat block and a tall, thin block of the same mass will experience (approximately) the same friction force on the same surface — a direct consequence of asperity-based contact mechanics.

**Law 3 — Independence from Sliding Velocity:**
The kinetic friction force is independent of the relative sliding velocity between the surfaces (within a moderate velocity range). Coulomb's original claim of velocity independence was later refined; in reality, $\mu_k$ shows a mild velocity dependence, but the assumption holds for most KTU analytical problems.

**Law 4 — Direction of Friction Force:**
The direction of the friction force is always **opposite** to the direction of relative motion (or the impending motion) of the body under consideration.

**Law 5 — Limiting Equilibrium:**
A body remains in static equilibrium under applied tangential force $P$ so long as:

$$P \leq F_{s,\max} = \mu_s N$$

The instant $P$ exceeds $\mu_s N$, the body begins to slide and transitions from static to kinetic friction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** Friction is a *passive* force — it adjusts its magnitude to oppose impending motion, but never exceeds its maximum value $\mu N$.

---

### 2.2 Types of Coefficient of Friction

In KTU 2024 scheme problems, students must clearly distinguish between three coefficients:

1. **Static Coefficient ($\mu_s$):** Ratio of limiting friction to normal load before motion.
   $$\mu_s = \frac{F_{s,\max}}{N}$$

2. **Kinetic / Dynamic Coefficient ($\mu_k$):** Ratio of friction during sliding to normal load.
   $$\mu_k = \frac{F_k}{N}$$

3. **Empirical Relation:** Experimentally observed (for most engineering materials):
   $$\mu_s > \mu_k$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~90% of Part A friction questions in KTU ESE.**

| S.No. | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|:-----:|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Limiting Friction Force | $F = \mu_s N$ | $F$ [N], $\mu_s$ [–], $N$ [N] |
| 2 | Kinetic Friction Force | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | $F_k$ [N], $\mu_k$ [–], $N$ [N] |
| 3 | Coefficient of Friction | $\mu = \tan \phi$ | $\phi$ = Friction angle [°] |
| 4 | Angle of Friction | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | Output in radians for math, degrees for diagrams |
| 5 | Angle of Repose | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$ | Critical incline angle [°] |
| 6 | Body on Horizontal Plane (Pulled by $P$) | $P = \mu_s W$ | $W = mg$ [N] |
| 7 | Body on Inclined Plane | $P = W(\mu_s \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | $\theta$ = incline [°] |
| 8 | Self-Locking Condition | $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$ | For block not to slide down |
| 9 | Effort on Rough Inclined (Upward) | $P_{\text{up}} = W(\mu_s \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | To pull up the incline |
| 10 | Effort on Rough Inclined (Downward) | $P_{\text{down}} = W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)$ | If $P$ is applied to hold back |

> **Note on notation:** $\mu$ in the table refers to $\mu_s$ unless otherwise specified. Always state which coefficient you are using.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The laws of friction underpin almost every mechanical design:
- **Brake pad design** in automobiles relies on maximizing $\mu$ between pad and rotor.
- **Conveyor belt sizing** requires accurate $\mu$ between belt and pulley.
- **Belt drives and clutch plates** transmit torque via friction.
- **Foundation design** for earth-retaining walls uses soil–concrete $\mu$.
- **Tribology research** (the focus of this entire course) seeks to *minimize* $\mu$ in bearings while *maximizing* it in brakes — a fascinating duality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Angle of Friction ($\phi$)

The **angle of friction** is the angle made by the resultant reaction force $R$ with the normal reaction $N$ when the body is on the verge of sliding.

**Given:** A body of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal surface, subjected to a horizontal pull $P$ just sufficient to cause impending motion.

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the body:**
- Weight $W$ acting vertically downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ acting vertically upward.
- Applied force $P$ acting horizontally.
- Limiting friction $F = \mu_s N$ acting horizontally opposite to $P$.

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium (vertical):**

$$N - W = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = W$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium (horizontal, impending motion):**

$$P = F = \mu_s N \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \mu_s W$$

**Step 4 — Compute resultant reaction $R$:**
The total reaction $R$ at the contact is the vector sum of $N$ (vertical) and $F$ (horizontal).

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{N^2 + F^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{N^2 + (\mu_s N)^2} \\
  &= N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Define the angle of friction $\phi$:**
By definition, $\phi$ is the angle between $R$ and $N$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \frac{\text{Opposite (Friction)}}{\text{Adjacent (Normal)}} \\
          &= \frac{F}{N} \\
          &= \frac{\mu_s N}{N} \\
          &= \mu_s
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\mu_s = \tan\phi \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** When $\mu_s \leq 1$, then $\phi \leq 45°$; when $\mu_s > 1$, $\phi > 45°$. This is why rubber ($\mu_s \approx 0.8$, $\phi \approx 38.6°$) grips better than ice ($\mu_s \approx 0.05$, $\phi \approx 2.86°$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Angle of Repose ($\alpha$)

The **angle of repose** is the steepest angle $\alpha$ of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on it remains in equilibrium without sliding.

**Given:** A block of weight $W$ on an inclined plane at angle $\alpha$, on the verge of sliding down.

**Step 1 — Resolve forces along and perpendicular to the incline:**
- Component of weight along incline (down-slope): $W \sin\alpha$
- Component of weight perpendicular to incline: $W \cos\alpha$

**Step 2 — Identify normal reaction:**

$$N = W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Identify limiting friction force (acting up-slope to oppose motion):**

$$F = \mu_s N = \mu_s W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium along incline (limiting case):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W \sin\alpha &= \mu_s W \cos\alpha \\
\sin\alpha &= \mu_s \cos\alpha \\
\frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} &= \mu_s \\
\tan\alpha &= \mu_s
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

> **Key Insight for KTU:** The angle of repose $\alpha$ and the angle of friction $\phi$ are **numerically equal** for the same pair of surfaces, both equal to $\tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$. This is a favorite examiner question!

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Effort Required to Pull a Body Up a Rough Inclined Plane

**Setup:** A block of weight $W$ is pulled up an incline of angle $\theta$ by a force $P$ parallel to the incline. Coefficient of friction is $\mu_s$.

**Step 1 — Forces along incline (impending upward motion):**
Friction acts **down-slope** because the block tends to move up.

$$P = W \sin\theta + \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Factor:**

$$P = W(\sin\theta + \mu_s \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\mu_s = \tan\phi$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= W\left(\sin\theta + \tan\phi \cos\theta\right) \\
  &= W\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi}\cos\theta\right) \\
  &= W \cdot \frac{\sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\theta \sin\phi}{\cos\phi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply sine addition formula ($\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$):**

$$P = W \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{up}} = W \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$

Similarly, for a body on the verge of sliding **down** with applied force $P$ down-slope to restrain it:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{down}} = W \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta - \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$

(Valid only when $\theta > \phi$; otherwise the body is self-locking and $P_{\text{down}} = 0$.)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Scenarios

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Self-Locking Criterion |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Block on horizontal floor pulled by horizontal $P$ | $P = \mu_s W$ | — |
| Block on horizontal floor pulled at angle $\alpha$ above horizontal | $P = \frac{\mu_s W}{\cos\alpha + \mu_s \sin\alpha}$ | — |
| Block on incline (pull up, parallel to plane) | $P = W(\sin\theta + \mu_s \cos\theta)$ | $\tan\theta > \mu_s$ |
| Block on incline (gravity holds, no $P$) | Stable if $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$ | $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$ |
| Ladder against smooth wall | Slip if $\tan\theta > \frac{1}{2\mu_s}$ | $\tan\theta \leq \frac{1}{2\mu_s}$ |
| Screw jack with effort $P$ on arm $R$ | $P = \frac{W d}{2R}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | $\alpha < 90° - 2\phi$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Classification of Friction (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire Module-1 friction taxonomy, showing the logical relationships between types, governing laws, and real-world manifestations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Friction Phenomenon] --> nodeB[Based on Contact Type]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Based on State of Motion]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Based on Geometry]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Dry Coulomb Friction]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Boundary Lubrication]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Fluid Film Friction]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Static Friction]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Kinetic Sliding Friction]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Rolling Friction]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Governs F equals mu N]
    nodeH --> nodeL[F at max equals mu_s N]
    nodeI --> nodeM[F equals mu_k N where mu_k lt mu_s]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Angle of Friction phi equals tan inv mu]
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Angle of Repose alpha equals phi]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Engineering Applications]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Brake Systems]
    nodeP --> nodeR[Belt Drives]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Machine Tool Slides]
    nodeP --> nodeT[Bearing Interfaces]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **root nodeA** branches into three primary classifications of friction.
- The **dry Coulomb branch** leads to the core law $F = \mu N$.
- The **angle of friction and angle of repose nodes** converge to show their numerical equivalence — a critical KTU point.
- **Engineering applications** are mapped to demonstrate the industrial relevance of each friction subtype.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Free-Body Interaction Flow on an Inclined Plane

This second diagram illustrates the logical force-balance workflow used by KTU examiners to validate the angle-of-repose derivation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Given: Block on incline theta] --> stepB[Resolve W into W sin theta and W cos theta]
    stepB --> stepC[Identify N equals W cos theta]
    stepC --> stepD[Identify F equals mu_s times N]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply Equilibrium along incline]
    stepE --> stepF[W sin theta equals mu_s W cos theta]
    stepF --> stepG[Divide both sides by W cos theta]
    stepG --> stepH[Tan theta equals mu_s]
    stepH --> stepI[Result: alpha equals tan inv mu_s]
    stepI --> stepJ[Check: alpha equals phi for same surfaces]
```

**Reading Guide:** Each step transitions logically to the next, mirroring the derivation in Section 3.2. KTU examiners award partial marks even if students reach `stepH` — so always write the limiting equilibrium equation explicitly.

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Force Diagram on a Rough Horizontal Plane

The following free-body diagram describes the limiting case of impending motion (textual rendering, since a true sketch cannot be generated in Mermaid):

- **Block of weight $W$** rests on a rough horizontal surface.
- **Normal reaction $N$** acts vertically upward from the surface.
- **Applied force $P$** acts horizontally to the right.
- **Friction force $F$** acts horizontally to the left (opposing $P$).
- **Resultant reaction $R$** acts at angle $\phi$ from the vertical (normal), such that $\tan\phi = F/N = \mu_s$.

This is the canonical figure used in KTU Part-A 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]:**
> **Q1. State Coulomb's laws of dry friction.**
> *(Module 1 | CO1 | Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. *First Law:* The friction force is directly proportional to the normal reaction between the two surfaces, i.e., $F \propto N \Rightarrow F = \mu N$. **[1 Mark]**
2. *Second Law:* The friction force is independent of the apparent area of contact between the surfaces. **[1 Mark]**
3. *Third Law:* The kinetic friction force is independent of the relative sliding velocity of the surfaces (for moderate velocities). **[1 Mark]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]:**
> **Q2. Define the terms (i) angle of friction and (ii) angle of repose. Establish their relationship.**
> *(Module 1 | CO1 | Cognitive Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- *Angle of friction $\phi$:* It is the angle between the resultant reaction force $R$ and the normal reaction $N$ at the contact surface when a body is on the verge of sliding. Mathematically, $\tan\phi = \mu_s$. **[1 Mark]**
- *Angle of repose $\alpha$:* It is the steepest angle of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body remains in equilibrium without sliding. **[1 Mark]**
- *Relationship:* For the same pair of surfaces, $\alpha = \phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$. Both are numerically equal. **[1 Mark]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]:**
>
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the effort required to drag a body of weight $W$ up a rough inclined plane at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, with coefficient of friction $\mu_s$, when the effort is applied parallel to the plane. Also state the condition for the body to slide down on its own when released. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A block weighing **600 N** rests on a horizontal floor ($\mu_s = 0.4$). Determine: (i) the magnitude of horizontal force $P$ required to just cause motion, (ii) the angle $\phi$ of friction, and (iii) the magnitude of normal reaction at the contact. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup the free-body diagram** on the inclined plane (angle $\theta$, weight $W$, friction $\mu_s$, effort $P$ parallel to plane).

**Step 2 — Resolve forces perpendicular to incline:**

$$N - W \cos\theta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = W \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Identify friction force (acting down-slope because motion is up):**

$$F = \mu_s N = \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium along incline (limiting case):**

$$P = W \sin\theta + \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

**Step 5 — Compact form using friction angle $\phi$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= W\left(\sin\theta + \tan\phi \cos\theta\right) \\
  &= W \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Condition for self-sliding down:**
The body slides down by itself under gravity alone when the gravitational component along the incline exceeds the maximum static friction that opposes motion:

$$W \sin\theta > \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \tan\theta > \mu_s = \tan\phi$$

$$\boxed{\text{Self-sliding condition: } \tan\theta > \mu_s}$$

Otherwise, when $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$, the body is **self-locking**.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $W = 600 \text{ N}$, $\mu_s = 0.4$, horizontal floor.

**(i) Horizontal force $P$ to cause impending motion:**

$$P = \mu_s \cdot W = 0.4 \times 600 = \boxed{240 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Angle of friction $\phi$:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = \boxed{21.80°}$$

**(iii) Normal reaction $N$:**

Since no vertical component acts other than weight and reaction:

$$N = W = \boxed{600 \text{ N}}$$

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**
>
> **(a)** A block of weight **800 N** rests on a rough plane inclined at **30°** to the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction is **0.35**. Determine: (i) whether the block will remain at rest or slide down on its own, (ii) the magnitude and direction of friction force acting on the block. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain with neat derivations the terms *angle of friction* and *angle of repose*, and show that both are equal for the same contacting surfaces. Also mention their engineering significance in machine design. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Given:** $W = 800 \text{ N}$, $\theta = 30°$, $\mu_s = 0.35$.

**(i) Check for self-sliding:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \tan 30° = 0.5774 \\
\mu_s &= 0.35
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\tan\theta = 0.5774 > \mu_s = 0.35$, the block **will slide down** under gravity. **[2 Marks]**

**(ii) Magnitude of friction force:**
Normal reaction:

$$N = W \cos\theta = 800 \times \cos 30° = 800 \times 0.866 = 692.82 \text{ N}$$

Maximum static friction available:

$$F_{\max} = \mu_s \cdot N = 0.35 \times 692.82 = 242.49 \text{ N}$$

Actual gravitational pull along incline (down-slope):

$$F_g = W \sin\theta = 800 \times \sin 30° = 800 \times 0.5 = 400 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_g > F_{\max}$, the block accelerates down. Kinetic friction now acts:

$$F_k = \mu_k N \approx 0.35 \times 692.82 = 242.49 \text{ N (up-slope)}$$

Direction: **up the incline** (opposing downward motion). **[5 Marks distributed over calculations]**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Refer to Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete derivations. **[Full 7-Mark distribution: 3 marks for angle of friction derivation, 3 marks for angle of repose derivation, 1 mark for engineering significance.]**

**Engineering Significance (1 mark sample answer):**
- *Angle of friction* is used in designing brake linings, clutch plates, and bolted joints to ensure adequate gripping.
- *Angle of repose* governs bulk material storage (sand piles, grain silos, ore heaps) and ensures gravitational self-stability of embankments and slopes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Friction Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Static vs Kinetic Confusion:** Many students use $\mu_k$ when the body is still at rest, or vice versa. Always check the state of motion first. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Direction of Friction Error:** Friction opposes *impending* motion, not necessarily current motion. When a body is held by a small force $P$ from sliding *down*, friction acts *up* the incline. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Self-Locking Condition:** In incline problems, always check whether $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$ before assuming motion. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit & Notation Errors:** Writing $\mu$ without specifying $\mu_s$ or $\mu_k$ costs partial credit. Always use the subscripts explicitly.
> 5. **Angle Conversion Errors:** Calculator in *degree* vs *radian* mode is the #1 silly mistake. Always verify before finalizing the answer.
> 6. **Truncating the Derivation:** Examiners allocate marks for *each algebraic step*. Skipping the line "Apply equilibrium along incline" loses **1 mark** even if the final answer is correct.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Laws of Friction):**
> - Friction is a **tangential resistive force** at the contact interface.
> - Coulomb's law: $F = \mu N$, where $\mu$ is **dimensionless**.
> - Always distinguish **static** ($\mu_s$) from **kinetic** ($\mu_k$) coefficients; $\mu_s > \mu_k$.
> - **Angle of friction** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$; it is the inclination of the resultant reaction $R$ from the normal.
> - **Angle of repose** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$; it is the steepest self-stable incline.
> - **For the same surfaces, $\phi = \alpha$** — a high-yield KTU fact.
> - **Self-locking condition on an incline:** $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$.
> - **Pulling up the incline (parallel force):** $P = W(\sin\theta + \mu_s \cos\theta) = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$.
> - **Pulling down the incline:** $P = W(\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta) = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta - \phi)}{\cos\phi}$ (valid only if $\theta > \phi$).
> - Coulomb's friction laws are **empirical approximations** valid for dry contacts, moderate loads, and moderate sliding velocities.
> - Friction **does not depend on the apparent contact area** (Coulomb's 2nd Law).
> - Standard metric units: Force in **N**, $\mu$ dimensionless, angle in **degrees or radians** (consistent).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Friction: Static, Kinetic, and Rolling Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Friction: Static, Kinetic, and Rolling Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Friction

**Friction** is the **tangential resistive force** that opposes the relative motion (or tendency of motion) between two surfaces in contact. In the formal language of tribology — the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion — friction is the **resistance to motion** encountered when one body slides, rolls, or tends to slide over another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tribology Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> Friction is the *resistive force* acting at the interface of two contacting bodies that **opposes the initiation of motion** (static friction) or **opposes the continuation of motion** (kinetic/rolling friction). It is governed by the **friction coefficient (μ)**, the **normal load (N)**, and the **surface interaction regime**.

The three principal types of friction studied in Module 1 are:

| Type | State of Motion | Primary Resistive Mechanism |
|------|----------------|-----------------------------|
| **Static Friction** | At rest (no motion) | Microscopic asperity interlocking & adhesion |
| **Kinetic (Sliding) Friction** | Sliding in progress | Continuous asperity ploughing & adhesion-shear |
| **Rolling Friction** | Rolling in progress | Elastic hysteresis, micro-slip, and deformation losses |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden crate across a rough concrete floor:

- **Phase 1 — Static Friction:** The crate refuses to move no matter how hard you push up to a certain limit. This "stuckness" is **static friction** — like trying to peel apart two pieces of Velcro that have just been pressed together. The microscopic bumps and chemical bonds at the interface must be broken before motion begins.

- **Phase 2 — Kinetic Friction:** Once the crate finally lurches forward, you feel it becomes slightly *easier* to keep it moving (you might reduce your push). This is **kinetic friction** — the surfaces are now continuously breaking and reforming bonds as they slide past each other, much like dragging a sled across gravel.

- **Phase 3 — Rolling Friction:** Now place the crate on a **wheeled dolly**. You can push it with dramatically less effort. The rolling friction of the wheels is often **50 to 100 times smaller** than sliding friction — this is why civilizations moved from sledges to wheels, and from ball bearings to roller bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The **coefficient of static friction (μₛ) is always greater than the coefficient of kinetic friction (μₖ)**. This is why a machine suddenly "breaks free" with a jerk after a threshold force is exceeded — the friction force drops discontinuously from μₛN to μₖN.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities (Bold Highlights)

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** A dimensionless scalar quantity
- **Normal Reaction Force (N):** Measured in **Newtons (N)**
- **Friction Force (F):** Measured in **Newtons (N)**
- **Friction Angle (φ):** Measured in **degrees** or **radians**
- **Friction Cone:** A geometric construct with half-angle φ
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Amontons' Laws of Friction (Foundational Principles)

The behavior of dry (unlubricated) friction is governed by **Amontons' Three Laws**, which form the analytical backbone of every tribology problem at KTU:

1. **First Law (Load Proportionality):** The friction force is **directly proportional to the normal load** $N$ acting between the surfaces.

   $$F \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \mu N$$

2. **Second Law (Area Independence):** The friction force is **independent of the apparent area of contact**. This is paradoxical but true — it occurs because the *real* (microscopic) contact area grows with load.

3. **Third Law (Kinetic Independence):** The kinetic friction force is **independent of the sliding velocity** (approximately valid for moderate velocity ranges, $v \le 10 \text{ m/s}$).

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of the Three Friction Types

#### A. Static Friction (Self-Locking Regime)

- **Definition:** The maximum frictional force that must be overcome before motion begins.
- **Range of Values:** $0 \le F_s \le \mu_s N$
- **Critical Condition:** Motion **impends** when $F = \mu_s N$.
- **The "≈" Inequality Principle:** Static friction is *self-adjusting* — it equals the applied tangential force up to a maximum limit.

$$F_{s,\max} = \mu_s N$$

#### B. Kinetic (Sliding) Friction (Dynamic Regime)

- **Definition:** The frictional force resisting motion *after* sliding has commenced.
- **Formula:** $F_k = \mu_k N$ where typically $\mu_k < \mu_s$
- **Velocity Dependence:** For high-precision work, $\mu_k = \mu_k(v,T,p)$ where $v$ is velocity, $T$ is temperature, and $p$ is contact pressure.
- **Direction:** Always **opposes the direction of relative sliding velocity**.

#### C. Rolling Friction (Wheel-on-Surface Regime)

- **Definition:** The resistance experienced by a circular body (wheel, ball, roller) rolling over a surface.
- **Mechanism:** Caused by **elastic hysteresis** of the contacting materials and **asymmetric pressure distribution** at the contact zone.
- **Coulomb's Approximation:** $F_r = \mu_r N$ where $\mu_r$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** (typically $\mu_r \ll \mu_k$).

$$F_r = \mu_r N = \frac{C_r \cdot N}{r}$$

where $C_r$ is the **rolling resistance coefficient (N·m or mm)** and $r$ is the wheel radius.

### 2.3 The Friction Angle (φ) and Friction Cone

The **friction angle $\phi$** is the angle between the **total reaction force $R$** and the **normal reaction $N$** at the contact point.

$$\tan \phi = \frac{F}{N} = \mu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite:** The **friction cone** is a cone of half-angle $\phi$ drawn about the normal to the contact surface. If the line of action of the resultant applied force lies **inside** the cone, the body is in **self-locking (no motion)**. If it lies **outside**, motion is impending.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $F_s = \mu_s N$ | Maximum static friction force | $F_s$ in N, $\mu_s$ dimensionless, $N$ in N |
| 2 | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | Kinetic friction force | $F_k$ in N, $\mu_k$ dimensionless, $N$ in N |
| 3 | $F_r = \mu_r N$ | Rolling resistance force | $F_r$ in N, $\mu_r$ dimensionless, $N$ in N |
| 4 | $F_r = \dfrac{C_r N}{r}$ | Rolling resistance (radius dependent) | $C_r$ in mm, $r$ in mm |
| 5 | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | Friction angle relation | $\phi$ in degrees, $\mu$ dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta_{\text{crit}} = \phi$ | Self-locking angle of inclined plane | $\theta_{\text{crit}}$ in degrees |
| 7 | $\mu_e = \dfrac{\mu_s + \mu_k}{2}$ | Equivalent (mean) friction coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $W_f = F_k \cdot s$ | Work done against friction | $W_f$ in J, $s$ in m |
| 9 | $P_f = F_k \cdot v$ | Power lost to friction | $P_f$ in W, $v$ in m/s |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{F_{\text{useful}}}{F_{\text{useful}} + F_f}$ | Mechanical efficiency (with friction) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Belt Drives & Clutches:** Static-to-kinetic transition governs **engagement torque** and **engagement smoothness**.
- **Vehicle Dynamics:** $\mu_s$ determines the **maximum tractive force**; $\mu_r$ determines the **rolling energy loss** in tires.
- **Brake Systems:** Kinetic friction coefficient $\mu_k$ between pad and disc determines **stopping torque**.
- **Machine Tool Slideways:** Maintaining $\mu_k \approx 0.05$ via hydrostatic lubrication is critical for **precision positioning**.
- **Rolling Element Bearings:** Minimizing $C_r$ extends **L10 fatigue life** per ISO 281.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Coefficient Ranges (must memorize for KTU):**
> - Steel on Steel (dry): $\mu_s = 0.5$ to $0.8$, $\mu_k = 0.4$ to $0.6$
> - Rubber on Concrete: $\mu_s = 0.9$ to $1.0$
> - PTFE on Steel: $\mu_k \approx 0.04$ (self-lubricating)
> - Ball Bearing (rolling): $\mu_r \approx 0.001$ to $0.005$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Friction Angle from Force Equilibrium

**Statement to Prove:** $\tan \phi = \mu$ for a block on the verge of sliding.

**Setup:** Consider a block of weight $W$ resting on a horizontal rough surface. A horizontal force $P$ is applied. Let $N$ be the normal reaction and $F$ be the friction force opposing motion.

**Step 1 — Vertical Equilibrium:**

The vertical forces on the block are its weight $W$ (downward) and the normal reaction $N$ (upward).

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = W$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Equilibrium (impending motion):**

When the block is on the verge of sliding, the friction force reaches its maximum value $F = \mu_s N$.

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = F = \mu_s N$$

**Step 3 — Form the Resultant Reaction $R$:**

The normal reaction $N$ and friction $F$ are perpendicular. The total reaction $R$ is their vector sum, with magnitude:

$$R = \sqrt{N^2 + F^2}$$

Substituting $F = \mu_s N$:

$$R = \sqrt{N^2 + (\mu_s N)^2} = N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Friction Angle $\phi$:**

The angle $\phi$ between $R$ and $N$ satisfies:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{F}{N} = \frac{\mu_s N}{N} = \mu_s$$

$$\boxed{\tan \phi = \mu}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

The friction angle is a *material property* of the contact pair — it is the same regardless of load or contact area, as long as Amontons' laws hold.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Self-Locking Condition on an Inclined Plane

**Statement to Prove:** A block on an inclined plane of angle $\theta$ will not slide down if $\theta < \phi$.

**Setup:** Block of weight $W$ on a rough plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.

**Step 1 — Resolve the Weight $W$ into Components:**

$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin \theta \quad \text{(along the plane, down the slope)}$$

$$W_{\perp} = W \cos \theta \quad \text{(perpendicular to the plane)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Normal Reaction:**

From equilibrium perpendicular to the plane:

$$N = W \cos \theta$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Available Static Friction:**

The friction force can reach a maximum of:

$$F_{\max} = \mu_s N = \mu_s W \cos \theta$$

**Step 4 — Compare Driving Force with Maximum Friction:**

The block will remain stationary (self-locking) if the driving force along the slope is less than the maximum friction:

$$W \sin \theta \le \mu_s W \cos \theta$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Inequality:**

Dividing both sides by $W \cos \theta$:

$$\tan \theta \le \mu_s = \tan \phi$$

$$\boxed{\theta \le \phi \quad \text{: Self-Locking Condition}}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

If the inclined plane angle $\theta$ equals the friction angle $\phi$, the block is on the verge of sliding. If $\theta < \phi$, it is *self-locked* (no motion possible without external push).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rolling Resistance from Moment Equilibrium

**Statement to Prove:** $F_r = \dfrac{C_r N}{r}$ for a rigid cylinder rolling on a deformable surface.

**Setup:** A rigid cylinder of weight $W$ and radius $r$ rests on a flat deformable surface. The total normal reaction $N = W$ is **not** centered beneath the geometric contact — it is shifted forward by a small distance $\delta$ (or $C_r$) due to elastic hysteresis.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Forces:**

- Weight $W$ acting downward at the geometric center $O$.
- Normal reaction $N$ acting upward at distance $\delta$ ahead of the vertical through $O$.
- Applied horizontal force $F_r$ (rolling resistance force) at the center $O$.

**Step 2 — Sum of Vertical Forces:**

$$N = W$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Moments about the Center O:**

The normal reaction $N$ produces a moment about $O$ that resists rotation. The applied force $F_r$ also produces a moment. For equilibrium at constant velocity (no acceleration):

$$\sum M_O = 0$$

$$F_r \cdot r = N \cdot \delta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $F_r$:**

$$F_r = \frac{N \cdot \delta}{r}$$

Recognizing that $\delta$ is a characteristic of the material pair, we rename it $C_r$ (rolling resistance coefficient with units of length):

$$\boxed{F_r = \frac{C_r \cdot N}{r}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- $F_r \propto 1/r$ — larger wheels roll with less resistance. This is why **truck wheels are larger** than car wheels, and why **railway wheels are huge**.
- $C_r$ is the **horizontal offset** of the resultant normal reaction. Typical values: $C_r \approx 0.5$ to $1.5$ mm for steel on steel, much higher for rubber on asphalt (due to viscoelastic deformation).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study

| Parameter | Static Friction | Kinetic Friction | Rolling Friction |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Symbol | $F_s$ | $F_k$ | $F_r$ |
| Coefficient | $\mu_s$ | $\mu_k$ | $\mu_r$ or $C_r/r$ |
| Magnitude | Largest | Intermediate | Smallest |
| Typical Range (steel-steel) | $0.5$ to $0.8$ | $0.4$ to $0.6$ | $0.001$ to $0.005$ |
| Velocity Dependence | N/A (at rest) | Weak (Amontons) | Weak (low speed) |
| Mechanism | Asperity lock + adhesion | Asperity shear + adhesion | Elastic hysteresis + micro-slip |
| Affected by Area? | No (Amontons) | No (Amontons) | No (Amontons) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Taxonomy of Friction Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FRICTION RESISTANCE FORCE"] --> B["STATE OF CONTACT BODY"]
    B --> C["AT REST"]
    B --> D["IN MOTION"]
    C --> E["STATIC FRICTION"]
    E --> E1["Self adjusting up to Fs max"]
    E --> E2["Formula Fs equals mu s times N"]
    E --> E3["Highest value among three types"]
    D --> F["KINETIC SLIDING"]
    D --> G["ROLLING FRICTION"]
    F --> F1["Formula Fk equals mu k times N"]
    F --> F2["Direction opposes sliding velocity"]
    F --> F3["Lower than static friction"]
    G --> G1["Formula Fr equals mu r times N"]
    G --> G2["Caused by elastic hysteresis"]
    G --> G3["Smallest of the three values"]
    A --> H["FRICTION ANGLE PHI"]
    H --> H1["tan phi equals mu"]
    H --> H2["Defines friction cone"]
    H --> H3["Self locking if theta less than phi"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Operational Interactions of Friction in Tribosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["LOAD AND MOTION INPUTS"]
        L1["Normal Load N"]
        L2["Applied Tangential Force P"]
        L3["Sliding Velocity v"]
        L4["Temperature T"]
    end
    subgraph CONTACT["CONTACT INTERFACE ZONE"]
        C1["Real Contact Area Ar"]
        C2["Asperity Junction Population"]
        C3["Adhesion Energy per Junction"]
        C4["Elastic Hysteresis Loss"]
    end
    subgraph FRICTION["FRICTION GENERATION MODULE"]
        S1["Static Friction Fs less than mu s times N"]
        K1["Kinetic Friction Fk equals mu k times N"]
        R1["Rolling Friction Fr equals C r times N over r"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["TRIBOLOGICAL OUTPUTS"]
        O1["Heat Generation Q equals F times v"]
        O2["Wear Rate W dot"]
        O3["Friction Coefficient mu"]
        O4["Mechanical Efficiency eta"]
    end
    L1 --> C1
    L2 --> C2
    L3 --> C3
    L4 --> C4
    C1 --> S1
    C2 --> K1
    C4 --> R1
    S1 --> O1
    K1 --> O2
    R1 --> O3
    S1 --> O4
    K1 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Static to Kinetic Transition (Stribeck-Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Stage 1: Body at Rest"] --> P2["Stage 2: Applied Force P increases"]
    P2 --> P3["Stage 3: F equals P no motion"]
    P3 --> P4{"Stage 4: P reaches mu s times N?"}
    P4 -->|No| P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P5["Stage 5: Impending Slip Detected"]
    P5 --> P6["Stage 6: Sudden Drop from mu s to mu k"]
    P6 --> P7["Stage 7: Kinetic Friction Fk equals mu k times N"]
    P7 --> P8["Stage 8: Sustained Sliding Motion"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Stage 6 represents the *friction discontinuity* — the abrupt fall from static to kinetic friction coefficient that is responsible for the *stick-slip phenomenon* in machine tools, the *judder* in automotive drum brakes, and the *chatter* in violin bows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish clearly between static, kinetic, and rolling friction. State the empirical relationship that links the friction coefficient to the friction angle."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Static Friction | Kinetic Friction | Rolling Friction |
|--------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| State | Body at rest | Body sliding | Body rolling |
| Formula | $F_s \le \mu_s N$ | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | $F_r = \mu_r N = C_r N / r$ |
| Magnitude | Highest | Intermediate | Lowest |
| Coefficient | $\mu_s$ | $\mu_k$ | $\mu_r$ or $C_r/r$ |

**Empirical Relation:** $\tan \phi = \mu$, where $\phi$ is the friction angle and $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often incorrectly state that $F_s = \mu_s N$ is *always* the friction force. The correct statement is $F_s \le \mu_s N$ — static friction is *self-adjusting* and only reaches the maximum value of $\mu_s N$ at the verge of motion. **(−1 mark if written as equality without caveat.)**

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State and explain Amontons' laws of dry friction. Why does kinetic friction not depend on the apparent contact area?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **First Law:** Friction force is proportional to the normal load: $F = \mu N$.
2. **Second Law:** Friction is independent of the apparent area of contact.
3. **Third Law:** Kinetic friction is independent of sliding velocity (for moderate ranges).

**Apparent Area Independence — Explanation:** Although a smaller contact patch *appears* to give less friction, the *real* (microscopic) contact area at asperity junctions is proportional to the normal load, not the apparent area. Hence, friction depends on the load alone.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question B (Option A)** — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**A block of mass 50 kg rests on a horizontal plane. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.45$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction is $\mu_k = 0.35$. A horizontal force P is applied gradually from zero to 400 N.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Find the friction force acting on the block when (i) P = 100 N, (ii) P = 200 N, (iii) P = 250 N, and (iv) just before motion begins.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the applied force is then held at P = 250 N and the block begins to slide, determine (i) the new friction force, (ii) the block's acceleration, and (iii) the velocity after 4 seconds. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Static Friction Analysis:**

- **Weight:** $W = mg = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5 \text{ N}$
- **Normal reaction:** $N = W = 490.5 \text{ N}$
- **Maximum static friction:** $F_{s,\max} = \mu_s N = 0.45 \times 490.5 = 220.725 \text{ N}$

The block will remain at rest as long as $P \le F_{s,\max} = 220.725 \text{ N}$.

| Case | Applied P (N) | Status | Friction Force F (N) |
|------|---------------|--------|------------------------|
| (i) | 100 | $P < F_{s,\max}$ | $F = 100$ |
| (ii) | 200 | $P < F_{s,\max}$ | $F = 200$ |
| (iii) | 250 | $P > F_{s,\max}$ | $F = F_{s,\max} = 220.725$ |
| (iv) | Just before motion | $P = F_{s,\max}$ | $F = 220.725$ |

> In case (iii), since the applied force exceeds the maximum static friction, the block *would* slide; hence the friction force caps at $F_{s,\max}$.

**Part (b) — Kinetic Friction Analysis:**

**(i) New Friction Force (kinetic regime):**

$$F_k = \mu_k N = 0.35 \times 490.5 = 171.675 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Acceleration of the Block:**

Net force in the direction of motion:

$$F_{\text{net}} = P - F_k = 250 - 171.675 = 78.325 \text{ N}$$

By Newton's second law:

$$a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \frac{78.325}{50} = 1.5665 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(iii) Velocity after 4 Seconds:**

Using $v = u + at$ with $u = 0$:

$$v = 0 + 1.5665 \times 4 = 6.266 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 6.27 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (Option B)** — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the condition for *self-locking* of a block on an inclined plane. A block of weight 200 N is placed on an inclined plane whose angle is gradually increased. If $\mu_s = 0.35$, find the angle at which the block begins to slide.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the phenomenon of *rolling resistance* and derive the expression $F_r = \dfrac{C_r N}{r}$ for a rigid wheel rolling on a deformable surface. State two engineering applications where this principle is exploited.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Self-Locking Derivation and Critical Angle:**

**Derivation (refer to §3.2 for full derivation):**

The self-locking condition is:

$$\theta \le \phi \quad \text{where} \quad \tan \phi = \mu_s$$

The block begins to slide at the *critical angle*:

$$\theta_c = \phi = \arctan(\mu_s) = \arctan(0.35)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = 19.29^\circ}$$

**Verification of Self-Locking:** Since the typical $\mu_s$ for wood on metal is $\approx 0.3$ to $0.5$, the critical angle of $19.29^\circ$ is consistent with experimental observation.

**Part (b) — Rolling Resistance Derivation and Applications:**

**Derivation (refer to §3.3 for full derivation):**

A rigid wheel of radius $r$ on a deformable surface. The normal reaction $N$ shifts forward by distance $C_r$ due to elastic hysteresis. Moment equilibrium about the wheel center gives:

$$F_r \cdot r = N \cdot C_r$$

$$\boxed{F_r = \frac{C_r N}{r}}$$

**Engineering Applications:**

1. **Bicycle and Vehicle Tire Design:** Larger diameter wheels (e.g., trucks, locomotives) have lower rolling resistance per unit load, improving fuel efficiency.
2. **Ball and Roller Bearings:** The use of hardened, polished rolling elements (low $C_r$) and lubrication films minimizes rolling losses in high-speed machinery (e.g., spindles of CNC machines, jet engine mainshafts).
3. **Railway Engineering:** Steel wheel on steel rail has very small $C_r$ (≈ 0.1 to 0.5 mm), enabling efficient long-distance freight transport.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always state units (N, m/s, m/s², °). A dimensionless answer is *almost always* wrong.
> 2. **Using $\mu_s$ when $\mu_k$ is required (–1 mark):** When motion has already commenced, switch *immediately* to $\mu_k$ — using $\mu_s$ after motion is a classic mistake.
> 3. **Missing the Self-Locking Inequality:** Writing $\theta = \phi$ as the only condition instead of $\theta \le \phi$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the Friction Angle Definition:** In derivations, many students write $\tan \phi = \mu$ but forget to define $\phi$ as the angle between the resultant reaction and the normal. Always include the geometric definition.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] Friction is a **tangential resistive force** at the interface of two contacting bodies.
- [x] Three types: **Static**, **Kinetic (Sliding)**, and **Rolling**.
- [x] Static friction is **self-adjusting**: $0 \le F_s \le \mu_s N$.
- [x] Kinetic friction is **constant** (per Amontons): $F_k = \mu_k N$.
- [x] Rolling friction is the **smallest** in magnitude and depends on **wheel radius**: $F_r = C_r N / r$.
- [x] **Amontons' Laws:** $F \propto N$, area-independent, velocity-independent.
- [x] **Friction Angle:** $\tan \phi = \mu$ — links geometry to material property.
- [x] **Self-Locking Condition on Inclined Plane:** $\theta \le \phi$.
- [x] **Critical Angle for Slip:** $\theta_c = \arctan(\mu_s)$.
- [x] **Coefficient inequality:** $\mu_s > \mu_k \gg \mu_r$.
- [x] **Mechanisms:** Asperity interlocking (static), asperity shear (kinetic), elastic hysteresis (rolling).
- [x] **Engineering values to memorize:** Steel-steel $\mu_s \approx 0.5$–$0.8$, $\mu_k \approx 0.4$–$0.6$, $\mu_r \approx 0.001$–$0.005$.
- [x] **Stick-slip phenomenon** arises from the discontinuous drop $\mu_s \to \mu_k$.
- [x] **Larger wheel radius ⇒ smaller rolling resistance** ($F_r \propto 1/r$).
- [x] **Work lost to friction:** $W_f = F_k \cdot s$; **Power lost:** $P_f = F_k \cdot v$.
- [x] **Efficiency with friction:** $\eta = F_{\text{useful}} / (F_{\text{useful}} + F_f)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Friction** is the tangential resistance force that opposes the relative motion (or impending motion) between two contacting surfaces. In tribological systems, the **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the friction force ($F$) to the normal load ($W$) acting at the contact interface.

Mathematically:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W}$$

Friction is broadly classified into:
- **Static Friction ($F_s$)**: Resistance before motion initiates; $\mu_s = F_s / W$
- **Kinetic / Dynamic Friction ($F_k$)**: Resistance during sliding; $\mu_k = F_k / W$
- **Rolling Friction**: Resistance during rolling contact (much lower than sliding friction)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** *"Friction is the resisting force tangential to the common boundary between two bodies when, under the action of an external force, one body moves or tends to move relative to the surface of the other."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden almirah (wardrobe) across a rough cemented floor. You strain hard, the almirah *jerks* forward, and you hear a **scraping sound** as it moves. Now imagine the same almirah placed on a **polished marble floor** — it slides almost effortlessly with barely a sound.

**What changed?** The **surface condition** changed. The friction (resistance you felt) was different because the interacting factors — surface roughness, material pair, contact pressure, presence of dust or moisture — were all different.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Friction is **NOT a material constant**. It is a *system response* that depends on many interacting variables. Two identical materials can produce very different friction values under different conditions. This is the foundation of Tribology.

### 1.3 Primary Factors That Influence Friction

| S. No. | Factor | Physical Origin |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Surface Roughness / Topography** | Asperity interlocking and plastic deformation |
| 2 | **Material Pair (Compatibility)** | Adhesion tendency, atomic bonding |
| 3 | **Normal Load** | Real area of contact, asperity deformation |
| 4 | **Sliding Velocity** | Thermal softening, strain-rate effects |
| 5 | **Operating Temperature** | Surface oxidation, lubricant viscosity change |
| 6 | **Surface Films (Oxides, Coatings, Lubricants)** | Shear plane relocation, contamination layers |
| 7 | **Apparent Area of Contact** | Real vs. apparent area (Amontons' 2nd Law) |
| 8 | **Environment (Humidity, Vacuum, Contamination)** | Surface chemistry modification |
| 9 | **Hardness Ratio of Mating Surfaces** | Ploughing depth, deformation mode |

> [!NOTE]
> The **apparent area of contact** is the geometric area (visually measured), while the **real area of contact** is the sum of micro-asperity junctions — typically only **1 – 10 %** of the apparent area, but it carries 100 % of the load.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Amontons' Three Laws of Dry Friction (Foundation Theory)

Guillaume Amontons (1699) restated the classical laws of friction that form the basis of all tribological analysis:

**Law 1 — Proportionality to Normal Load:**
The friction force is directly proportional to the normal load applied perpendicular to the contact interface.

**Law 2 — Independence from Apparent Area:**
The friction force is independent of the apparent (geometric) area of contact, provided the normal load and material remain constant.

**Law 3 — Independence from Sliding Velocity (Coulomb's Addition):**
The kinetic friction force is independent of the sliding velocity between the surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Amontons' Laws are **idealizations** valid only under limited conditions (low load, dry contact, non-reactive materials). In real engineering systems, all three laws are violated to varying degrees. The deviations **are** the factors affecting friction.

### 2.2 Detailed Factor Analysis

#### Factor 1: Surface Roughness
- **Mechanism:** Asperities (microscopic peaks) on the two surfaces interlock mechanically. Sliding requires these asperities to either **plough through** the opposing surface or **shear off**.
- **Effect:** Higher roughness → higher friction (in dry sliding). However, at very high roughness, real contact area reduces and friction may decrease.
- **Quantified by:** $R_a$ (Arithmetic Mean Roughness), $R_z$ (Ten-Point Mean Roughness), RMS value.

#### Factor 2: Normal Load
- **Mechanism:** Increased load causes greater asperity deformation → larger real area of contact → higher friction.
- **Coulomb's Relation:** $F = \mu W$
- **Bowden-Tabor Model:** $A_r = W / p_y$ (real contact area is linear in load)

#### Factor 3: Sliding Velocity
- **Low velocity:** Boundary lubrication, higher μ
- **Moderate velocity:** Mixed lubrication, μ decreases
- **High velocity:** Hydrodynamic regime, μ very low (fluid shear)
- **Stick-slip phenomena** occur at low velocities in some polymer-metal pairs

#### Factor 4: Temperature
- **Surface temperature rise** softens asperities (thermal softening) → increases real area → may increase friction.
- However, temperature also softens the **subsurface material** → easier shearing of junctions → may decrease friction.
- **Net effect** depends on which mechanism dominates. For most metals, μ initially increases with temperature, then decreases near the melting point.

#### Factor 5: Surface Films and Contamination
- **Oxide layers** prevent metal-metal adhesion → reduce friction.
- **Lubricant films** relocate the shear plane from the metal interface into a weaker fluid film → drastically reduce friction.
- **Adsorbed films** (e.g., fatty acids on steel) orient molecularly to provide low-shear boundary layers.

#### Factor 6: Material Pair Compatibility
- **Metallic pairs with high mutual solubility** (e.g., Cu-Ni, Fe-Cr) → strong adhesion → high friction.
- **Dissimilar or non-metallic pairs** → low adhesion → low friction.
- This is quantified by the **ease of forming metallic bonds** between the contacting atoms.

#### Factor 7: Hardness Ratio (Ploughing Component)
- When a hard surface slides on a soft surface, asperities of the hard material **plough** grooves into the softer material, contributing an additional friction force (ploughing term).
- Ploughing becomes significant when the **hardness ratio** $H_h/H_s > 1.5$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F = \mu W$ | Coulomb's law of dry friction | $F$: N, $W$: N, $\mu$: dimensionless |
| $\mu = F / W$ | Coefficient of friction (definition) | dimensionless |
| $A_r = W / p_y$ | Real area of contact (plastic deformation) | $A_r$: m², $W$: N, $p_y$: Pa |
| $F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$ | Friction force from shear of junctions | $F$: N, $\tau_s$: Pa, $A_r$: m² |
| $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$ | Coefficient of friction (adhesion term) | dimensionless |
| $\mu = \tau_s / H$ | Coefficient of friction (using hardness) | $H$: Pa (Vickers/Brinell) |
| $F = F_{adh} + F_{plough}$ | Total friction = adhesion + ploughing | N |
| $\mu = \mu_{adh} + \mu_{plough}$ | Total μ = adhesion μ + ploughing μ | dimensionless |
| $\mu_{plough} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cot \theta$ | Ploughing μ for conical asperity (angle $\theta$) | dimensionless |
| $p_{max} \approx 3 \sigma_y$ | Maximum Hertzian contact pressure (yield onset) | Pa |
| $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Viscous shear stress (Newtonian fluid) | $\tau$: Pa, $\mu$: Pa·s, $v$: m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** The symbol $\mu$ is used for **two distinct quantities** — the dimensionless **coefficient of friction** in $F = \mu W$ and the **dynamic viscosity** (Pa·s) in fluid mechanics. In KTU answer sheets, always clarify which is meant by stating units or full name.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brake Pad Design:** Engineers tune friction by combining metal powders, ceramics, and organic fibers to balance μ (high for stopping) against wear rate.
- **Engine Bearings:** Low μ is desired; controlled by lubricant viscosity, surface finish of journals, and bearing materials.
- **Cutting Tools:** Lower tool-chip friction reduces heat, extends tool life, and improves surface finish.
- **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS):** At micro-scale, surface forces dominate and friction becomes a critical design parameter.
- **Aerospace and Vacuum Tribology:** In space, absence of oxide films increases metal-metal adhesion → cold welding → catastrophic friction rise.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coefficient of Friction from Bowden & Tabor's Adhesion Theory

The Bowden-Tabor model (1939) is the **cornerstone of modern tribology** and is heavily tested in KTU Module 1.

**Step 1 — Load Support by Asperities**
When two rough surfaces are pressed together, the normal load is supported entirely by the **real area of contact** $A_r$, which is the sum of all microscopic asperity junctions.

$$W = p_y \cdot A_r$$

where $p_y$ is the **yield pressure** of the softer material (≈ 3 × Brinell Hardness Number for metals).

**Step 2 — Solving for Real Area of Contact**

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y}$$

This shows that real area of contact is **directly proportional to the normal load** — a key result.

**Step 3 — Friction Force from Junction Shearing**
To initiate sliding, the asperity junctions must be sheared. The friction force equals the shear strength of the junctions times the real area:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

where $\tau_s$ is the **shear strength of the interface** (a property of the material pair, not of bulk material).

**Step 4 — Substitute $A_r$ into the Friction Equation**

$$F = \tau_s \cdot \frac{W}{p_y}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Coefficient of Friction**

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau_s \cdot W / p_y}{W}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The coefficient of friction equals the **ratio of interfacial shear strength to the yield pressure of the softer material**. This single equation explains why **soft, weak materials** (low $p_y$) sliding against metals tend to have **high friction** — and why **boundary lubricants** work (they reduce $\tau_s$ without changing $p_y$).

**Step 6 — When Hardness is Used Instead of Yield Pressure**
Since $p_y \approx 3 H$ (empirical relation) and in many texts $H$ is used directly:

$$\mu \approx \frac{\tau_s}{H}$$

This is the most commonly tested form in KTU.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Term Friction Model (Adhesion + Ploughing)

For surfaces of **significantly different hardness**, ploughing is non-negligible.

**Step 1 — Total Friction Force**

$$F_{total} = F_{adhesion} + F_{ploughing}$$

**Step 2 — Adhesion Component (Bowden-Tabor)**

$$F_{adhesion} = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

**Step 3 — Ploughing Component (Hard Asperity Geometry)**
For a hard conical asperity of semi-angle $\theta$ ploughing through a softer surface:

$$F_{ploughing} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_p \cdot \cot \theta$$

where $A_p$ is the projected (apparent) area of the indent and $\sigma_y$ is the yield stress of the soft material.

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Friction**

$$\mu = \frac{F_{adhesion} + F_{ploughing}}{W}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\tau_s \cdot A_r}{W} + \frac{2 \sigma_y A_p \cot \theta}{\pi W}$$

**Step 5 — Define Apparent Pressure $p = W / A_p$**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y} + \frac{2 \sigma_y \cot \theta}{\pi p}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The first term is the **adhesion contribution** (dominates in ductile metal-metal contact). The second term is the **ploughing contribution** (dominates when a very hard slider contacts a soft surface, e.g., diamond tip on lead).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Affects μ (Qualitative Summary)

| Factor | Increase in Factor | Effect on μ | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface Roughness $R_a$ | ↑ | ↑ (initially), then ↓ | Asperity interlocking & ploughing |
| Normal Load $W$ | ↑ | ≈ constant (ideal) / slight ↓ (real) | $A_r$ grows linearly with $W$ |
| Sliding Velocity $v$ | ↑ | ↓ (lubricated), mixed (dry) | Hydrodynamic film formation |
| Temperature $T$ | ↑ | ↑ then ↓ (near $T_m$) | Softening & oxidation competition |
| Hardness $H$ | ↑ | ↓ | Lower real contact area |
| Oxide Film Thickness | ↑ | ↓ | Prevents metal-metal adhesion |
| Humidity | ↑ | ↓ (for oxides), ↑ (for ceramics) | Adsorbed water films |
| Hardness Ratio $H_h/H_s$ | ↑ | ↑ | Increased ploughing term |
| Interfacial Shear Strength $\tau_s$ | ↑ | ↑ | Stronger junction adhesion |
| Yield Pressure $p_y$ | ↑ | ↓ | Smaller real contact area |

### 3.4 Numerical Illustration — μ Calculation for Steel-on-Steel

**Given:** Steel-on-steel sliding contact. Shear strength of interface $\tau_s = 80$ MPa. Yield pressure of softer steel $p_y = 800$ MPa. Normal load $W = 2000$ N. Real contact area $A_r = W/p_y$.

**Step 1 — Real Area of Contact**

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y} = \frac{2000 \text{ N}}{800 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of Friction**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{800 \times 10^6} = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Friction Force**

$$F = \mu \cdot W = 0.10 \times 2000 = 200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity Check**
A coefficient of friction of **0.10** is reasonable for lubricated steel-on-steel. Dry steel-on-steel typically gives μ ≈ 0.5 – 0.8. This validates that the assumed $\tau_s$ value represents a lubricated or oxide-film-protected interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The ratio $\tau_s / p_y$ (or $\tau_s / H$) is the **single most important derived expression** in Module 1. Memorize the derivation pathway, not just the formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
