## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Define the term "Coefficient of Friction." State its physical significance and indicate the typical range of values for (a) metals on metals, (b) polymers on metals, and (c) lubricated contacts.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **coefficient of friction (μ)** is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the friction force ($F$) opposing relative motion to the normal load ($W$) pressing the two surfaces together.

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W}$$

**Physical Significance:** It is a **system parameter**, not a material property. It quantifies how much of the applied normal force is converted into tangential resistance at the contact interface.

**Typical Ranges:**

| Contact Pair | Range of μ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Metals on Metals (dry) | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| Polymers on Metals | 0.1 – 0.4 |
| Lubricated Contacts | 0.001 – 0.1 |

> **Marking Note (1 mark each):** Definition + Significance + Typical ranges.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**State Amontons' Laws of Friction. Why is the second law (independence from apparent area) not strictly obeyed in real engineering contacts?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Amontons' Three Laws (1699):**
1. The friction force is directly proportional to the normal load: $F \propto W$
2. The friction force is **independent of the apparent area** of contact, provided the load and materials are unchanged.
3. The kinetic friction force is **independent of the sliding velocity** between the surfaces (added later by Coulomb).

**Why Law 2 Fails in Practice:**
The second law holds only when the **real area of contact** is independent of the apparent (geometric) area. In reality:
- Real contact occurs at **asperity junctions**, not across the full geometric area.
- The real area $A_r = W/p_y$ depends on **load and material yield pressure**, not on apparent area.
- However, for **non-rigid or highly deformable** materials (polymers, elastomers), larger apparent area may allow more asperity engagement, causing μ to depend weakly on apparent area.
- **Elastic vs. plastic deformation** and **wear debris entrapment** also cause deviations.

> **Marking Note (1 mark each):** Three laws stated + Reason for non-compliance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** in Part B by:
> 1. Confusing **coefficient of friction (μ, dimensionless)** with **dynamic viscosity (μ, Pa·s)** — *always specify context*.
> 2. Skipping the **derivation steps** and writing only the final formula $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$ — the **logical pathway** carries 4–5 of the 7 marks.
> 3. Mixing up **apparent area** and **real area of contact** — they differ by 1–2 orders of magnitude.
> 4. Not drawing the **asperity contact schematic** when explaining Bowden-Tabor theory — examiners expect a labeled figure (worth 1–2 marks).

---

### 5.3 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **various factors affecting friction** in dry sliding contacts, with emphasis on surface roughness, normal load, sliding velocity, and temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **coefficient of friction using Bowden and Tabor's adhesion theory**. Discuss how the presence of a thin oxide film modifies the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Surface Roughness (2 marks)**
- Real surfaces consist of asperities (peaks) and valleys at micro-scale.
- When two surfaces are loaded, contact occurs only at the **asperity tips**, not across the full geometric area.
- **High roughness** → greater mechanical interlocking → higher resistance to sliding → higher μ.
- However, **excessively rough** surfaces may reduce real contact area (fewer, larger junctions) → μ may decrease.
- Quantified using $R_a$ (arithmetic mean roughness) and $R_z$ (ten-point mean roughness).

**2. Normal Load (2 marks)**
- Per Coulomb's law, friction force is directly proportional to normal load: $F = \mu W$.
- Mechanistically: increased load → greater asperity deformation → larger real area of contact $A_r = W/p_y$ → higher $F$.
- For ideal rigid-plastic contact, μ is **constant** (Amontons' Law 1). In reality, μ shows slight load dependence.

**3. Sliding Velocity (1.5 marks)**
- In **dry sliding**, μ varies weakly with velocity. At very low speeds, **stick-slip** can occur.
- In **lubricated contacts**, increasing velocity promotes **hydrodynamic film formation** → μ drops significantly.
- Frictional heating at high velocity softens asperities, modifying μ.

**4. Temperature (1.5 marks)**
- Frictional heating raises interface temperature.
- **Softening** of asperities → larger real contact area → μ may increase initially.
- Near the **melting point**, materials lose shear strength → μ decreases sharply.
- **Oxide film growth** with temperature can act as a solid lubricant, reducing μ.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Derivation of $\mu = \tau_s / p_y$ (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Normal load is supported by real area of contact $A_r$ at the asperity junctions, each bearing the yield pressure $p_y$ of the softer material.

$$W = p_y \cdot A_r$$

**Step 2:** Real area of contact is therefore:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y}$$

**Step 3:** To initiate sliding, all junctions must be sheared. The friction force equals the interfacial shear strength $\tau_s$ times the real contact area:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $A_r$:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot \frac{W}{p_y}$$

**Step 5:** The coefficient of friction:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau_s}{p_y}$$

**Effect of Oxide Film (2 marks):**
A thin oxide layer on the metal surface (typically 1–10 nm) acts as a **solid lubricant film**. It:
- Prevents direct metal-metal bonding → reduces $\tau_s$ (interfacial shear strength).
- Does not significantly alter the substrate's $p_y$ (yield pressure).
- **Net result:** $\mu$ decreases by a factor of 2 – 10 compared to clean metal-metal contact.

For example, clean iron-on-iron in vacuum has $\mu \approx 1.0$, while oxidized iron in air has $\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.6$.

---

### 5.4 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the **effect of surface films, lubrication regime, and environmental conditions** on the friction coefficient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **ploughing component of friction**. Derive the expression for the ploughing coefficient of friction for a conical asperity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Surface Films (2.5 marks)**
- **Oxide films** form naturally on most engineering metals (Fe, Cu, Al) in air. They have low shear strength and prevent metallic adhesion → reduce μ.
- **Adsorbed films** (e.g., fatty acids, organics) form monolayers that orient under load and provide low-shear boundary layers.
- **Coatings** (DLC, TiN, MoS₂) intentionally applied to relocate the shear plane into a weak film → friction reduction of 50–90 %.
- **Contaminant films** (dust, water vapor, hydrocarbons) can either reduce or increase μ depending on chemistry.

**2. Lubrication Regime (2.5 marks)**
- **Boundary Lubrication:** Asperities still in contact; friction controlled by surface films. μ ≈ 0.05 – 0.15.
- **Mixed Lubrication:** Partial fluid film; some asperity contact. μ ≈ 0.02 – 0.08.
- **Hydrodynamic/Elastohydrodynamic:** Full fluid film separates surfaces; friction from viscous shear only. μ ≈ 0.001 – 0.01.
- **Solid Lubrication:** MoS₂, graphite, PTFE provide low-shear films. μ ≈ 0.02 – 0.1.

**3. Environmental Conditions (2 marks)**
- **Humidity:** Adsorbed water vapor reduces friction on oxide-covered metals but can increase it on ceramics.
- **Vacuum:** Absence of oxide/contaminant films → strong metal-metal adhesion → μ can exceed 1.0 (cold welding risk).
- **Inert atmospheres (Ar, N₂):** No oxide replenishment → wear accelerates.
- **Reactive atmospheres (O₂, Cl₂):** Form low-shear surface films → reduce μ.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Ploughing Concept (2 marks):**
When a hard surface slides over a softer surface, the asperities of the hard material **plough through** the softer material, creating a groove. The horizontal force required to push a hard asperity through the softer material is the **ploughing component** of friction, additional to the adhesion term.

[Schematic description for examiner: Draw a hard conical asperity with semi-apex angle $\theta$ indenting a soft surface to depth $h$, with a cross-sectional groove of projected area $A_p$]

**Derivation of Ploughing μ for Conical Asperity (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** The projected (cross-sectional) area of the conical indent:

$$A_p = \pi r^2$$

where $r$ is the radius of the indent at the surface. For a cone of semi-angle $\theta$ and depth $h$:

$$r = h \tan \theta$$

So:

$$A_p = \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta$$

**Step 2:** The vertical (normal) force to indent is the yield stress of the soft material times the projected area:

$$W = \sigma_y \cdot A_p = \sigma_y \cdot \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta$$

**Step 3:** The horizontal (ploughing) force involves a factor depending on the cone geometry. By plastic indentation analysis (Hill's solution for conical indenters):

$$F_{plough} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_p \cdot \cot \theta$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $A_p$:

$$F_{plough} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta \cdot \cot \theta = 2 \sigma_y h^2 \tan^2 \theta \cdot \cot \theta$$

Simplifying using $\tan^2 \theta \cdot \cot \theta = \tan \theta$:

$$F_{plough} = 2 \sigma_y h^2 \tan \theta$$

**Step 5:** Ploughing coefficient of friction:

$$\mu_{plough} = \frac{F_{plough}}{W} = \frac{2 \sigma_y h^2 \tan \theta}{\sigma_y \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta} = \frac{2 \tan \theta}{\pi \tan^2 \theta} = \frac{2 \cot \theta}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{plough} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cot \theta}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Smaller cone angle (sharper asperity) → larger $\cot \theta$ → higher ploughing μ. Blunter asperities give lower ploughing resistance.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Factors Affecting Friction**

- [x] **Friction is a system response, not a material property** — never say "the friction of steel."
- [x] **Coulomb's Law:** $F = \mu W$ — foundation of all dry friction analysis.
- [x] **Amontons' 3 Laws:** proportionality to load, independence from apparent area, independence from velocity — all are idealizations.
- [x] **Real vs Apparent Area:** $A_r \ll A_{apparent}$ (typically 1–10 %); all load transmitted through asperity junctions.
- [x] **Bowden-Tabor Equation:** $\mu = \tau_s / p_y \approx \tau_s / H$ — **most important derived formula** in Module 1.
- [x] **Total friction** = Adhesion term + Ploughing term.
- [x] **Ploughing μ for cone:** $\mu_{plough} = (2/\pi) \cot \theta$.
- [x] **Surface roughness** generally increases μ, but excessive roughness can reduce real contact area.
- [x] **Velocity effect:** in dry contact μ is roughly constant; in lubricated contact μ decreases with velocity (hydrodynamic regime).
- [x] **Temperature:** μ increases initially (softening) then decreases near $T_m$ (loss of shear strength).
- [x] **Oxide films** reduce μ by preventing metal-metal adhesion — explains why clean metals in vacuum have very high μ.
- [x] **Lubrication regimes** (in increasing speed order): Boundary → Mixed → EHL → Hydrodynamic.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** distinguishing **μ (coefficient of friction, dimensionless)** from **μ (dynamic viscosity, Pa·s)**.
- [x] **Unit check:** Always state units explicitly — F in N, W in N, $A_r$ in m², $\tau_s$ and $p_y$ in Pa.
- [x] **Bowden-Tabor assumption:** asperities deform plastically; $\tau_s$ is **interfacial**, not bulk, shear strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Friction: Adhesion, Deformation, and Plowing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theories of Friction: Adhesion, Deformation, and Plowing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Friction (Tribological Definition) In tribology, **friction** is defined as the **tangential resistance force** developed at the interface of two contacting bodies when one body moves or tends to move relative to the other. According to the classical **Amontons-Coulomb laws of friction (1699–1785)**, the frictional force $F$ is directly proportional to the normal load $W$ and is independent of the apparent area of contact. $$F = \mu \cdot W$$ where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction (CoF)** — a dimensionless scalar. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classical laws of friction fail to explain the *mechanisms* by which friction arises. The modern microscopic theories (Adhesion, Deformation, Plowing) explain friction as a product of **asperity-level interactions** rather than a single bulk phenomenon. ### 1.2 The Real vs. Apparent Contact Area Paradox If friction were proportional to the apparent contact area $A_a$, then a wide flat block sliding on a floor should experience more friction than a small block under the same weight. **This is experimentally false.** The resolution lies in the fact that no surface is perfectly flat at the microscopic level. - **Apparent Contact Area ($A_a$):** The geometric, macroscopic area over which two bodies appear to touch. - **Real Contact Area ($A_r$):** The sum of microscopic spots where asperities (surface peaks) of the harder body make true contact with the softer body. $$A_r \ll A_a$$ **Intuition:** Imagine pressing your palm flat against a tiled wall. Although your entire palm contacts the wall, the *actual* contact occurs only at the grout lines and tile edges — not over the full smooth surface. Similarly, at the microscopic level, the load is carried by tiny "contact spots" (junctions). ### 1.3 Intuitive Overview of the Three Theories > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Sandpaper and Clay Model** > > Imagine a rough surface (sandpaper) pressed onto soft clay: > 1. **Adhesion Theory** — The clay sticks to the sandpaper tips like glue; you must *shear* these microscopic welds to slide. > 2. **Deformation Theory** — The sandpaper tips *push down and dent* the clay (plastically deform it); the energy spent denting must be overcome. > 3. **Plowing Theory** — As the sandpaper slides, its hard, sharp tips *plow furrows* through the clay like a tiny plough; energy goes into displacing material sideways and forward. > > In real tribological contacts, **all three mechanisms act simultaneously**, but their relative dominance depends on materials, surface finish, lubrication, and contact pressure. ### 1.4 The Three Theories at a Glance | **Theory** | **Origin of Friction Force** | **Key Proponents** | |---|---|---| | **Adhesion Theory** | Shearing of microscopic adhesive junctions formed at asperity contacts | Bowden & Tabor (1950s) | | **Deformation Theory** | Resistance offered by plastic or elastic deformation of asperities | Tomlinson, Archard (1957) | | **Plowing (Grooving) Theory** | Energy required to displace softer material by hard asperities or hard particles | Bowden & Tabor, Suh (1986) | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Adhesion Theory of Friction (Bowden & Tabor, 1950) **Core Premise:** When two nominally flat surfaces are pressed together under load $W$, contact occurs only at discrete **asperity junctions**. Under sufficient contact pressure, these junctions undergo **plastic flow** and form **cold-welded adhesive bonds** ("microwelds"). The friction force is the total shear force required to rupture these bonds. #### Step-by-Step Logical Flow: 1. **Load Transmission:** The normal load $W$ is supported entirely by the real contact area $A_r$ across all junctions. 2. **Plastic Yielding of Asperities:** Asperity tips yield plastically until the contact pressure equals the **yield pressure (hardness)** of the softer material. 3. **Junction Growth:** During sliding, tangential traction enlarges the junctions — a phenomenon called **junction growth**. 4. **Microweld Formation:** Atoms of the two surfaces come into intimate contact (interatomic spacing) and form **adhesive bonds** (cold welds). 5. **Friction Mechanism:** Sliding shears these junctions at their **shear strength $\tau$**, not at the bulk material strength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Favourite Point:** The shear occurs at the **interface or within the weaker material**, *not* in the bulk. The shear strength $\tau$ used in adhesion theory is the **interfacial shear strength** of the microweld. ### 2.2 Deformation Theory of Friction **Core Premise:** Friction arises from the **resistance to deformation** of asperities (elastic or plastic) under combined normal and tangential loading. This is sometimes called the **ploughing-related deformation component**. #### Two Sub-Cases: **Case A — Plastic Deformation of Asperities (most common in metals):** When contact pressure exceeds the yield strength, asperities deform plastically. The friction force equals the shear force over the real contact area: $$F = \tau \cdot A_r$$ **Case B — Elastic Deformation of Asperities:** For very smooth, hard, or lightly loaded contacts, asperities deform elastically. The real contact area follows **Hertzian contact mechanics**. For a sphere of radius $R$ pressing on a flat with equivalent modulus $E^*$: $$A_r = \pi \cdot a^2 = \pi \left( \frac{2 \cdot W \cdot R}{\pi \cdot E^*} \right)^{2/3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The deformation theory is critical in **gear tooth contact, cam-follower systems, and rolling element bearings** where Hertzian elastic deformation governs contact stresses. It is also the foundation of **contact fatigue (pitting) analysis**. ### 2.3 Plowing (Grooving) Theory of Friction **Core Premise:** When a hard, sharp asperity (or an entrapped hard third-body particle) slides across a softer surface, it **plows a micro-groove** through the softer material. The friction force has a component equal to the **force required to displace (plow) the material out of the path**. This is sometimes called the **abrasive component** of friction, distinct from the **adhesive component**. #### Plowing Force for a Conical Asperity (Bowden & Tabor): For a hard conical asperity of semi-included angle $\theta$ sliding on a softer surface of hardness $H$: $$F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot H \cdot w^2 \cdot \tan\theta$$ For a **spherical asperity of radius $R$** with groove width $w$: $$F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot H \cdot \frac{w^3}{R}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Plowing friction is **inversely proportional to hardness** and depends on the **geometry (sharpness)** of the asperity. Sharper asperities (small $R$, small $\theta$) produce *deeper* grooves and *higher* plowing resistance. ### 2.4 The Unified Friction Model The total coefficient of friction is the **sum of contributions** from the three mechanisms: $$\mu_{total} = \mu_{adhesion} + \mu_{deformation} + \mu_{plowing}$$ For typical **metal-metal contacts in boundary lubrication**, the three terms contribute approximately: - **Adhesion:** 60–80% of total friction - **Plowing:** 15–30% of total friction - **Deformation (asperity bulk):** 5–10% of total friction ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | **Equation** | **Meaning / Use** | **Typical Units** | |---|---|---| | $F = \mu W$ | Fundamental friction law | $\text{N}$ | | $A_r = W \div \sigma_y$ | Real contact area under fully plastic asperity flow | $\text{m}^2$ | | $F = \tau A_r$ | Friction force from shearing junctions | $\text{N}$ | | $\mu = \tau \div \sigma_y$ | Coefficient of friction (adhesion theory) | dimensionless | | $\mu = \tau_s \div H$ | Coefficient of friction (using hardness) | dimensionless | | $A_r = \pi a^2$ | Real contact area for Hertzian contact | $\text{m}^2$ | | $a = \left( 3WR \div 4E^* \right)^{1/3}$ | Hertzian contact radius (sphere on flat) | $\text{m}$ | | $E^* = \left( (1-\nu_1^2) \div E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2) \div E_2 \right)^{-1}$ | Equivalent elastic modulus | $\text{Pa}$ | | $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^2 \tan\theta$ | Plowing force — conical asperity | $\text{N}$ | | $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^3 \div R$ | Plowing force — spherical asperity | $\text{N}$ | | $\mu_{total} = \mu_{adh} + \mu_{def} + \mu_{plow}$ | Unified friction model | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Key Symbol Definitions:** > - $\tau$ = interfacial shear strength (Pa) > - $\tau_s$ = shear strength of the softer material (Pa) > - $\sigma_y$ = yield pressure / hardness of softer material (Pa) > - $H$ = hardness (Pa) > - $E^*$ = combined elastic modulus (Pa) > - $\nu_1, \nu_2$ = Poisson's ratios of contacting bodies > - $w$ = groove width (m) > - $R$ = asperity tip radius (m) - $\theta$ = semi-included cone angle (radians or degrees) ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Adhesion Theory Coefficient of Friction **Goal:** Derive $\mu = \tau / \sigma_y$ starting from first principles. **Starting Premise:** Asperities deform plastically until the mean contact pressure equals the yield pressure (hardness) of the softer material. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Force balance on real contact area:} \\ & W = \sigma_y \cdot A_r \quad \text{(load supported by yielding asperities)} \\[6pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Solve for real contact area:} \\ & A_r = \frac{W}{\sigma_y} \\[6pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Friction force is the shear force to rupture junctions:} \\ & F = \tau \cdot A_r \\[6pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Substitute } A_r \text{ from Step 2 into Step 3:} \\ & F = \tau \cdot \frac{W}{\sigma_y} \\[6pt] \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Compare with Amontons' law } F = \mu W: \\ & \mu \cdot W = \tau \cdot \frac{W}{\sigma_y} \\[6pt] \text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Cancel } W \text{ from both sides:} \\ \boxed{\mu = \frac{\tau}{\sigma_y}} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A *low* coefficient of friction is obtained when the **interfacial shear strength $\tau$** is small (weak junctions) and the **hardness $\sigma_y$** is high (small real contact area). Lubrication aims to reduce $\tau$ without significantly affecting $\sigma_y$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Hertzian Real Contact Area (Sphere-on-Flat, Elastic) **Goal:** Derive $A_r = \pi a^2$ where $a$ is the contact radius. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Given: Sphere of radius } R, \text{ load } W, \text{ reduced modulus } E^* \\[4pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Hertz's classical contact radius formula:} \\ & a = \left( \frac{3 W R}{4 E^*} \right)^{1/3} \\[6pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Real contact area is the area of the circular contact patch:} \\ & A_r = \pi a^2 \\[6pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:} \\ & A_r = \pi \left( \frac{3 W R}{4 E^*} \right)^{2/3} \\[6pt] \boxed{A_r} & \boxed{= \pi \left( \frac{3 W R}{4 E^*} \right)^{2/3}} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Unlike plastic contact where $A_r \propto W$, in **elastic Hertzian contact**, $A_r \propto W^{2/3}$. This *sublinear* growth of real contact area with load is fundamental to **rolling element bearing** design and **Hertzian contact stress** analysis. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Plowing Force for a Conical Asperity **Goal:** Derive $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^2 \tan\theta$ for a hard cone sliding on a softer material. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Geometry: Conical asperity of semi-included angle } \theta \\ & \text{Cutting a groove of width } w. \\[4pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Depth of the groove (geometric relation):} \\ & h = \frac{w}{2 \tan\theta} \\[6pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{The asperity face in contact has projected area:} \\ & A_{proj} = \frac{w^2}{4 \tan\theta} \\[6pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{The vertical force resisting penetration equals the hardness times area:} \\ & N_{plow} = H \cdot A_{proj} = H \cdot \frac{w^2}{4 \tan\theta} \\[6pt] \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{The horizontal plowing force has a horizontal component given by the geometry of the cone face. The included angle between the cone face and the horizontal is } (90^\circ - \theta), \text{ so the horizontal force component is:} \\ & F_{plow} = N_{plow} \cdot \tan\theta = H \cdot \frac{w^2}{4 \tan\theta} \cdot \tan\theta \\[6pt] \text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{After accounting for the actual conical geometry and stress distribution (Bowden & Tabor's integration):} \\ \boxed{F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot H \cdot w^2 \cdot \tan\theta} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Plowing friction is **independent of load** in this model (groove width $w$ is geometric, set by asperity shape). This is a **major distinguishing feature** from adhesion friction, which scales linearly with load. In abrasive wear and grinding operations, this load-independence of plowing friction is exploited for material removal rate calculations. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Adhesion Theory** | **Deformation Theory** | **Plowing Theory** | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Shearing of microwelds | Elastic/plastic strain of asperities | Displacement of softer material | | **Dominant Regime** | Metal-metal, clean, dry | Smooth, hard, lightly loaded surfaces | Hard particle / rough surface on soft | | **Load Dependence** | $F \propto W$ | $F \propto W$ (plastic) or $F \propto W^{2/3}$ (elastic) | $F$ roughly independent of $W$ | | **Contact Area** | $A_r = W / \sigma_y$ | $A_r = \pi a^2$ (Hertz) | Geometric, asperity-controlled | | **Material Property Used** | $\tau, \sigma_y$ | $E^*, \nu$ | $H$ | | **Effect of Lubrication** | Strong reduction (lowers $\tau$) | Mild reduction | Mild reduction (still ploughs) | | **Effect of Hardness** | Inverse (high $H$ → low $\mu$) | Strong (high $E^*$ → low $A_r$) | Inverse (high $H$ → low $F_{plow}$) | | **% Contribution (typical dry metal-metal)** | 60–80% | 5–10% | 15–30% | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Real Contact & Friction Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Two Surfaces in Contact under Load W] --> B{Apparent Contact Area Aa} B --> C[Microscopic Asperity Interactions] C --> D[Real Contact Area Ar << Aa] D --> E[Mechanism 1: Adhesion] D --> F[Mechanism 2: Deformation] D --> G[Mechanism 3: Plowing] E --> E1[Microweld Formation] E1 --> E2[Shearing at Shear Strength tau] E2 --> H[Friction Force F] F --> F1[Elastic Deformation Hertzian] F --> F2[Plastic Deformation Yielding] F1 --> H F2 --> H G --> G1[Hard Asperity Grooving] G1 --> G2[Material Displacement Energy] G2 --> H H --> I[Total Friction mu_total W] E2 --> J[Component mu_adh] F1 --> K[Component mu_def] F2 --> K G2 --> L[Component mu_plow] J --> M[mu_total = mu_adh + mu_def + mu_plow] K --> M L --> M M --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top — two surfaces in contact. The load $W$ is transmitted through a *small* real contact area, which splits into three parallel friction-generating mechanisms. Each mechanism contributes a partial coefficient, and the three combine (sum) into the total observed coefficient of friction. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Three Theories of Friction (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Classical Friction F = mu W] --> B[Why does friction exist?] B --> C1[Adhesion Theory
Bowden and Tabor 1950] B --> C2[Deformation Theory
Archard 1957] B --> C3[Plowing Theory
Bowden and Tabor 1950] C1 --> D1[Shearing of cold-welded junctions] C2 --> D2[Energy to deform asperities elastically or plastically] C3 --> D3[Energy to displace softer material] D1 --> E[Total Friction mu_total W] D2 --> E D3 --> E ``` ### 4.3 Asperity Contact Schematic (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hard Surface with Asperity Peak] --> B{Contact Event at Microscopic Level} B --> C1[Real Contact Spot 1
Forms microweld] B --> C2[Real Contact Spot 2
Forms microweld] B --> C3[Real Contact Spot 3
Plastic indentation] B --> C4[Real Contact Spot 4
Plows softer material] C1 --> D[Sum of All Contact Spots = Ar] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D D --> E[Apparent Area Aa covers the full geometric footprint] E --> F[Ar is much smaller than Aa] F --> G[Friction = tau times Ar + Plowing Energy + Deformation Energy] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Real surface profiles (as measured by profilometers) show that even a finely polished surface has asperity heights of order **1–10 µm** and asperity tip radii of **10–100 µm**. The number of true contact spots for a 1 cm² apparent contact patch is typically **10–1000** — never the millions implied by simple geometrical models. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State and explain the Adhesion Theory of Friction. How does it justify the classical laws of friction?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Adhesion Theory of Friction** was proposed by **Bowden and Tabor (1950)**. It states that when two surfaces are pressed together, real contact occurs only at discrete **asperity junctions**. Under the normal load, these asperities yield plastically, and the local pressure equals the **yield pressure (hardness) $\sigma_y$** of the softer material. At these contact spots, atoms of the two surfaces come close enough to form **cold-welded microwelds** (adhesive bonds). The friction force is the force required to **shear** these junctions at their interfacial shear strength $\tau$. **Justification of classical laws:** - **$F \propto W$:** Because $A_r = W/\sigma_y$ and $F = \tau \cdot A_r$, we get $F = (\tau / \sigma_y) W = \mu W$. (1 mark for the formula) - **$F$ independent of apparent area:** Because the real contact area $A_r$ is determined by load and material hardness, not by the apparent geometry. (1 mark) - **$\mu$ independent of load (approximately):** Because $\tau$ and $\sigma_y$ are material properties, roughly constant for a given pair. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between the Adhesion Theory and the Plowing Theory of friction. Under what conditions does the plowing component become dominant?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Adhesion Theory** | **Plowing Theory** | |---|---|---| | Mechanism | Shearing of microwelds at asperity contacts | Displacement of softer material by hard asperities | | Coefficient formula | $\mu = \tau / \sigma_y$ | $F_{plow} = (2/\pi) H w^2 \tan\theta$ | | Load dependence | Linear with $W$ | Approximately independent of $W$ | (2 marks for the comparison) **Conditions for dominance of plowing component:** (1 mark) 1. When one surface is **much harder** than the other (e.g., hard abrasive particle on a soft metal). 2. When **entrapped wear debris or third-body particles** are present. 3. When the **asperity tips are very sharp** (small tip radius, small included angle). 4. In **abrasive wear** situations such as grinding, cutting, and polishing. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Students often write $F = \mu W$ as the *definition* of friction and forget that this is a *law* (Amontons' law), not a *theory*. The *mechanism* behind $\mu$ is what the examiner is testing. > 2. Confusing **hardness $H$** with **yield strength $\sigma_y$** — in many tribology texts they are equated ($H \approx 3\sigma_y$ for metals), but they are conceptually different. > 3. In plowing derivations, students forget that the **groove width $w$** is a geometric property of the asperity, *not* dependent on load — leading to wrong dimensional interpretations. > 4. Skipping units in the final answer always costs at least 0.5 mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Adhesion + Deformation Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** Derive the coefficient of friction $\mu = \tau / \sigma_y$ from the Adhesion Theory of friction, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two steel surfaces ($\sigma_y = 2.5$ GPa, $\tau = 0.8$ GPa) are in contact under a normal load of $W = 1000$ N. Calculate: (i) the real contact area $A_r$, (ii) the friction force $F$, and (iii) the coefficient of friction $\mu$. Compare the calculated $A_r$ with an apparent area of $A_a = 25 \text{ cm}^2$ and comment. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks derivation:** - State assumptions: (1) Asperities deform plastically, (2) Shear occurs at interface, (3) Real contact area is much smaller than apparent, (4) Junction growth is neglected. (2 marks) - Write force balance: $W = \sigma_y A_r \Rightarrow A_r = W/\sigma_y$ (1 mark) - Write friction as shear: $F = \tau A_r$ (1 mark) - Substitute: $F = \tau W / \sigma_y$ (1 mark) - Compare with $F = \mu W$: $\mu = \tau / \sigma_y$ (1 mark) - State conclusion / physical meaning (1 mark) **Part (b) — 7 marks numerical solution:** Given: $\sigma_y = 2.5 \text{ GPa} = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, $\tau = 0.8 \text{ GPa} = 0.8 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, $W = 1000 \text{ N}$, $A_a = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. **(i) Real contact area:** $$ A_r = \frac{W}{\sigma_y} = \frac{1000}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2 = 0.40 \text{ mm}^2 $$ (2 marks — 1 for substitution, 1 for answer with units) **(ii) Friction force:** $$ F = \tau \cdot A_r = (0.8 \times 10^9) \times (4.0 \times 10^{-7}) = 320 \text{ N} $$ (2 marks) **(iii) Coefficient of friction:** $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{\sigma_y} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^9}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.32 $$ Alternative check: $\mu = F/W = 320/1000 = 0.32$ ✓ (1 mark) **Comment on the comparison:** $A_r = 0.40 \text{ mm}^2$ vs. $A_a = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$. $$ \frac{A_r}{A_a} = \frac{0.40}{2500} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} = 0.016\% $$ **Conclusion:** The real contact area is only **0.016%** of the apparent area, confirming that contact occurs at a tiny fraction of the nominal interface. This justifies why friction is independent of apparent area. (2 marks) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Plowing + Unified Model Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Internal Choice B]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Plowing Theory of Friction**. Derive the expression for the plowing force for a hard conical asperity sliding on a softer surface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel journal ($\sigma_y = 2.0$ GPa) slides against a softer bronze bearing ($\sigma_y = 0.5$ GPa, $\tau = 0.15$ GPa). A single conical hard asperity on the steel with semi-included angle $\theta = 30^\circ$ cuts a groove of width $w = 50 \, \mu m$ into the bronze. Estimate the **plowing force** $F_{plow}$. The applied normal load is $W = 500$ N. Comment on whether the plowing force is significant compared to the adhesion force. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - Schematic description: hard asperity ploughing through softer material, displacing it sideways and forward. (1 mark) - Geometric setup: cone angle $\theta$, groove width $w$, groove depth $h = w/(2\tan\theta)$. (1 mark) - Projected contact area: $A_{proj} = w^2/(4\tan\theta)$. (1 mark) - Vertical resistance: $N_{plow} = H \cdot A_{proj}$. (1 mark) - Horizontal force via geometry: $F_{plow} = N_{plow} \cdot \tan\theta$. (1 mark) - Final result with the $\frac{2}{\pi}$ factor from Bowden & Tabor's full integration: $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^2 \tan\theta$. (1 mark) - Physical interpretation: load-independent, depends on geometry and hardness. (1 mark) **Part (b) — 7 marks numerical solution:** Given: $\theta = 30^\circ$, $w = 50 \, \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $H = 0.5 \text{ GPa} = 0.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$ (using bronze hardness). **Step 1: Compute $\tan\theta$** $$ \tan 30^\circ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774 $$ **Step 2: Compute $w^2$** $$ w^2 = (50 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2500 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3: Substitute into plowing formula** $$ F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot H \cdot w^2 \cdot \tan\theta $$ $$ F_{plow} = \frac{2}{3.1416} \cdot (0.5 \times 10^9) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.5774) $$ $$ F_{plow} = 0.6366 \cdot (0.5 \times 10^9) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 0.5774 $$ $$ F_{plow} = 0.6366 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 0.5774 = 0.6366 \cdot 0.7218 = 0.4595 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{F_{plow} \approx 0.46 \text{ N}} $$ (4 marks for the calculation) **Step 4: Adhesion force for comparison** $$ A_r = \frac{W}{\sigma_y(\text{bronze})} = \frac{500}{0.5 \times 10^9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ F_{adh} = \tau \cdot A_r = (0.15 \times 10^9) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 150 \text{ N} $$ (2 marks) **Step 5: Comment** $$ \frac{F_{plow}}{F_{adh}} = \frac{0.46}{150} \approx 0.003 = 0.3\% $$ **Conclusion:** For this asperity alone, the plowing force is only about **0.3%** of the adhesion force. However, in a real rough contact with **thousands of asperities** and possibly **entrapped hard debris particles**, the cumulative plowing contribution can rise to 15–30% of total friction. Plowing is also a **major driver of abrasive wear**, often more critical than its direct friction contribution. (1 mark) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Theories of Friction** - **Three Theories of Friction:** Adhesion, Deformation, Plowing. All three operate simultaneously in real contacts; the *dominant* one depends on materials, surface finish, and loading. - **Adhesion Theory (Bowden & Tabor, 1950):** Friction = shearing of cold-welded asperity junctions. Key formula: $\mu = \tau / \sigma_y$. Load is supported by plastically yielded asperities whose real contact area is $A_r = W / \sigma_y$. - **Deformation Theory:** Covers both **elastic (Hertzian)** and **plastic** asperity deformation. For Hertzian contact: $A_r = \pi (3WR / 4E^*)^{2/3}$. Important for bearings, gears, and cam-follower contacts. - **Plowing Theory:** Hard asperities or hard third-body particles displace softer material, creating micro-grooves. For a cone: $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^2 \tan\theta$. For a sphere: $F_{plow} = \frac{2}{\pi} H w^3 / R$. **Plowing friction is approximately load-independent.** - **Unified Model:** $\mu_{total} = \mu_{adh} + \mu_{def} + \mu_{plow}$. For dry metal-metal contacts, adhesion dominates (~60–80%). - **Real vs Apparent Area:** $A_r \ll A_a$ (typically 0.01–1% of apparent area). This is the *microscopic* explanation for why friction is independent of apparent contact area. - **Lubrication Effect:** Lubricants drastically reduce the **adhesion** component by lowering $\tau$ (forming low-shear-strength boundary films), but have **less effect** on deformation and plowing components. - **Engineering Implications:** Surface coatings (TiN, DLC) reduce $\mu$ primarily by increasing hardness $H$ → reducing $A_r$ and reducing $F_{plow}$. Lubricity additives (MoS₂, graphite) work by reducing $\tau$ at junctions. - **Standard Exam Values to Memorize:** Typical dry steel-on-steel: $\mu \approx 0.5$–$0.8$; lubricated: $\mu \approx 0.05$–$0.1$; PTFE on steel: $\mu \approx 0.04$. - **Frequently Confused Pairs (avoid losing marks):** - $\tau$ (interfacial shear strength) vs. $\tau_s$ (bulk shear strength of weaker material) - $\sigma_y$ (yield pressure) vs. $H$ (hardness) — numerically related by $H \approx 3\sigma_y$ for metals - $E^*$ (reduced modulus) vs. $E$ (Young's modulus of a single material) - Asperity *deformation* (energy-based) vs. Plowing (displacement-based) — sometimes students merge these two.

topics.title: Types of Wear: Adhesive, Abrasive, Corrosive, and Surface Fatigue Wear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Wear: Adhesive, Abrasive, Corrosive, and Surface Fatigue Wear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Wear

**Wear** is defined as the progressive loss of material from the operating surface of a solid body (typically a tribological pair) as a result of relative motion at the contact interface. It is a surface damage phenomenon that occurs due to mechanical, chemical, or thermal interactions between contacting surfaces.

According to the **ASTM G40 Standard Terminology**, wear is described as the *damage to a solid surface, generally involving progressive loss of material, due to relative motion between that surface and a contacting substance or substances*.

Mathematically, the volumetric wear rate is expressed as:

$$V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of material removed (m³)
- $K$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless)
- $W$ = Normal load (N)
- $L$ = Sliding distance (m)
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer material (Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 2 of Tribology & Lubrication focuses specifically on the **four principal modes of wear**: Adhesive, Abrasive, Corrosive, and Surface Fatigue. These are collectively responsible for over **95%** of all material loss in industrial tribosystems.

---

## 1.2 The Four Primary Types of Wear

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **four canonical wear mechanisms** that govern material degradation in mechanical systems:

### 1.2.1 Adhesive Wear
**Adhesive wear** occurs when two contacting surfaces slide against each other under sufficient load, causing **microscopic junctions** to form at asperity contacts. These junctions may be stronger than the bulk material, leading to material transfer from the softer surface to the harder one.

### 1.2.2 Abrasive Wear
**Abrasive wear** happens when hard asperities or hard particles (either embedded in one surface or present as free debris) plough, cut, or scratch the softer surface, removing material through mechanical action.

### 1.2.3 Corrosive Wear
**Corrosive (or chemical) wear** is the combined effect of **corrosion and mechanical wear**. It occurs when a chemical or electrochemical reaction forms a surface film (oxide, sulfide, etc.), which is then mechanically removed by sliding contact, exposing fresh metal to further attack.

### 1.2.4 Surface Fatigue Wear
**Surface fatigue wear** (also called **pitting** or **spalling**) results from **cyclic stress variations** at the surface or subsurface, leading to the formation and propagation of microcracks that eventually cause material detachment in the form of pits, spalls, or flakes.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway on a Bicycle" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a road:
> - **Adhesive wear** is like your **cycle tire sticking to wet road patches** and peeling off small rubber fragments when you move forward.
> - **Abrasive wear** is like riding on a **gravel road** where sharp stones scrape and scratch your tire surface.
> - **Corrosive wear** is like riding through a **salt-water puddle** — the salt chemically attacks your chain (corrosion), and then pedaling mechanically scrapes off the rusted layer, exposing fresh metal to more corrosion.
> - **Surface fatigue wear** is like the **repetitive stress on your knee joints** after millions of pedal strokes — eventually causing small cracks and pitting in the cartilage.
>
> Just as the road condition determines which "wear mode" dominates in cycling, the operating environment (load, speed, temperature, environment, and material pair) determines the dominant wear mechanism in a tribosystem.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Wear Analysis

- **Wear Rate ($\dot{W}$):** Volume of material removed per unit sliding distance (m³/m).
- **Specific Wear Rate ($K_s$):** Dimensionless coefficient relating wear volume to load and sliding distance. Typical values range from $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-2}$ depending on mechanism.
- **Wear Resistance:** Inverse of wear rate, indicating the material's ability to resist material loss.
- **Severity Index:** Quantitative measure used to classify whether wear is mild, moderate, or severe.

> [!NOTE]
> The **dimensionless wear coefficient $K$** is the most critical parameter for material selection in design. Lower $K$ values indicate better wear resistance. For unlubricated metal-metal contact, $K \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$. For well-lubricated contacts, $K$ can drop to $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-8}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Adhesive Wear — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Microscopic Process

At the microscopic level, no surface is perfectly smooth. Real surfaces have **asperities** (peaks) and **valleys**. When two surfaces come into contact under load, only the asperities actually touch, creating a very small real area of contact.

**Step-by-step adhesive wear mechanism:**

- **Step 1:** Normal load $W$ is supported by discrete asperity junctions.
- **Step 2:** Under the high contact pressure, asperities deform plastically and may **cold-weld** together (atomic-level bonding).
- **Step 3:** When tangential motion is applied, these junctions shear.
- **Step 4:** If the junction strength exceeds the cohesive strength of the softer material, a **fragment transfers** from the softer to the harder surface.
- **Step 5:** On subsequent passes, the transferred fragment may detach and become **wear debris**.

### 2.1.2 Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation

The volumetric adhesive wear is given by:

$$V = K_{ad} \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{3H}$$

For simplicity, the factor of 3 in the denominator is often absorbed into the wear coefficient:

$$V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$$

### 2.1.3 Parameters Influencing Adhesive Wear

- **Material compatibility** — Similar metals (e.g., steel on steel) suffer more adhesive wear than dissimilar pairs (e.g., steel on bronze).
- **Surface energy and crystal structure** — FCC metals tend to be more adhesive than BCC metals.
- **Load** — Higher load increases real contact area and junction size.
- **Sliding speed** — High speeds can cause frictional heating and material softening.
- **Lubrication** — Properly designed lubrication films prevent asperity contact entirely.

---

## 2.2 Abrasive Wear — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Two-Body vs. Three-Body Abrasive Wear

- **Two-Body Abrasion:** Hard asperities on one surface plough through the softer counterface (e.g., grinding, filing, cutting).
- **Three-Body Abrasion:** Hard particles trapped between the two surfaces act as the abrasive medium (e.g., contaminated lubricants with dust particles).

### 2.2.2 Mechanism of Material Removal

The abrasive particle can:
- **Plowing:** Material is displaced sideways to form ridges; minimal direct removal.
- **Cutting:** Sharp particles produce microchips, similar to machining.
- **Fracture:** Brittle materials may experience microcracking and spalling.

### 2.2.3 Abrasive Wear Equations

**Rabiowicz's Model (Cutting Action):**

$$V = \frac{W \cdot L \cdot \tan\theta}{\pi \cdot H}$$

Where $\theta$ is the attack angle of the abrasive particle.

**For general abrasive wear, the simplified form is:**

$$V = K_{ab} \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}$$

### 2.2.4 Factors Affecting Abrasive Wear

- **Hardness ratio** $H_a/H_s$ (abrasive/surface) — If $H_a > 1.2 H_s$, wear is severe.
- **Particle shape** — Angular particles cause more wear than rounded ones.
- **Attack angle** — Maximum wear occurs at $\theta \approx 90°$ for ductile materials.
- **Environment** — Corrosive media amplify wear through synergy.

---

## 2.3 Corrosive Wear (Tribochemical Wear)

### 2.3.1 Mechanism

Corrosive wear is a **synergistic process** combining:
- **Chemical/Electrochemical Reaction:** A surface film (typically oxide) forms on the metal.
- **Mechanical Removal:** Sliding contact removes the film, exposing fresh metal.
- **Repetition:** The cycle repeats, accelerating material loss.

The total wear in corrosive conditions is:

$$V_{total} = V_{mechanical} + V_{chemical} + \Delta V_{synergy}$$

The **synergy term** is often the largest contributor and represents the acceleration of corrosion by mechanical action (or vice versa).

### 2.3.2 Oxidative Wear Sub-Mode

The most common form of corrosive wear in air is **oxidative wear**, where oxide films form and are continuously removed:

$$V_{ox} = \frac{K_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L \cdot t}{H}$$

Where $t$ is the thickness of the oxide layer formed per pass.

### 2.3.3 Control Measures

- Use **corrosion-resistant alloys** (stainless steels, Inconel).
- Apply **protective coatings** (chromium, ceramic, PTFE).
- Use **inhibitor additives** in lubricants (ZDDP, Molybdenum compounds).
- Control **operating temperature and humidity**.

---

## 2.4 Surface Fatigue Wear

### 2.4.1 Mechanism

Surface fatigue occurs under **rolling or rolling-sliding contact** (e.g., bearings, gears, cam-follower pairs). Repeated cyclic stresses cause:

- **Subsurface microcrack initiation** at inclusions or voids below the surface.
- **Crack propagation** parallel to the surface.
- **Formation of pits or spalls** when cracks coalesce and reach the surface.

### 2.4.2 Pitting vs. Spalling

- **Pitting:** Small, shallow craters (typically < 1 mm depth) caused by crack initiation at or near the surface.
- **Spalling:** Larger, deeper material loss caused by subsurface crack propagation.

### 2.4.3 Hertzian Contact Stress

For a sphere on a flat surface, the maximum contact pressure is:

$$p_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{6 W E^{*2}}{R^2} \right)^{1/3}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Normal load
- $E^*$ = Effective elastic modulus
- $R$ = Radius of curvature

The maximum shear stress (which drives fatigue crack initiation) occurs at a depth of approximately $0.48a$ below the surface, where $a$ is the contact radius.

### 2.4.4 Lundberg-Palmgren Theory

The fatigue life of a rolling contact bearing is given by:

$$L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^p$$

Where:
- $L_{10}$ = Fatigue life in millions of revolutions
- $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic load
- $p$ = Exponent (3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates all critical equations for the KTU 2024 board exam. Memorize these formulas and their boundary conditions.**

| **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Application** | **Units** |
|--------------|----------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| $V = K \cdot W L / H$ | Archard's Generalized Wear Equation | All wear modes | m³ |
| $V = K_{ad} \cdot W L / 3H$ | Adhesive Wear Volume | Adhesive mode | m³ |
| $V = (W L \tan\theta)/(\pi H)$ | Abrasive Wear (Rabiowicz) | Abrasive mode | m³ |
| $p_0 = (1/\pi)(6 W E^{*2}/R^2)^{1/3}$ | Hertzian Max Contact Pressure | Fatigue wear | Pa |
| $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | Bearing Fatigue Life | Fatigue mode | 10⁶ rev |
| $\dot{W} = V / L$ | Wear Rate | All modes | m³/m |
| $K_s = V H / (W L)$ | Specific Wear Rate (dimensionless) | Material selection | — |
| $A_r = W / H$ | Real Area of Contact | Asperity contact | m² |
| $H_a > 1.2 H_s$ | Threshold for Severe Abrasion | Abrasive mode | Pa |

**Notation key:**
- $V$ = Volume of wear
- $K$ = Wear coefficient
- $W$ = Normal load
- $L$ = Sliding distance
- $H$ = Hardness
- $\theta$ = Attack angle
- $E^*$ = Effective elastic modulus
- $R$ = Radius of curvature
- $C$ = Dynamic load rating
- $P$ = Equivalent load

---

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

| **Industry** | **Critical Component** | **Dominant Wear Mode** | **Why It Matters** |
|--------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Automotive** | Camshaft-follower, piston rings | Adhesive + Abrasive | Determines engine service life |
| **Mining** | Crusher liners, drill bits | Abrasive | Equipment downtime costs |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blade roots, bearings | Surface Fatigue | Safety-critical failure mode |
| **Marine** | Propeller shafts, valve seats | Corrosive + Abrasive | Saltwater environment |
| **Manufacturing** | Cutting tools, dies | Abrasive | Tool life and surface finish |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation

Archard's classical derivation proceeds as follows. We begin with the assumption that asperities on the softer surface are hemispherical and deform plastically under load.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Real Area of Contact

The real area of contact $A_r$ for plastic deformation of asperities is given by:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

where $W$ is the normal load and $H$ is the hardness (flow pressure) of the softer material. This is a fundamental result from asperity contact mechanics: hardness $H$ is defined as the pressure required to plastically deform the material.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Asperity Junction Assumption

Assume that there are $n$ asperity junctions, each of mean diameter $d$, in contact. The real area of contact is therefore:

$$A_r = n \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Probability of Junction Failure

Archard introduced a dimensionless constant $K_s$ (the **severity of wear** or **probability of forming a wear particle**). For each asperity encounter, only a fraction $K_s$ produces a wear fragment; the remainder either detaches without forming a particle or remains attached.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Volume of Hemisphere of Diameter d

The volume of a hemisphere of diameter $d$ is:

$$V_{hemisphere} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left( \frac{d}{2} \right)^3 = \frac{\pi d^3}{12}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Wear Volume per Unit Sliding Distance

For a sliding distance $L$, each asperity has $L/d$ encounters (assuming successive encounters by asperities of mean diameter $d$).

The total wear volume is:

$$V = n \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{L}{d}$$

Simplifying:

$$V = n \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2 L}{12}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Eliminate n and d in Favour of Load

From Step 2: $n = 4A_r / (\pi d^2)$. Substituting:

$$V = \frac{4 A_r}{\pi d^2} \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2 L}{12} = \frac{K_s A_r L}{3}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7: Substitute Real Area of Contact

Substituting $A_r = W/H$ from Step 1:

$$V = \frac{K_s W L}{3H}$$

### 3.1.8 Final Result

$$\boxed{V = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}}$$

where the dimensionless wear coefficient $K = K_s/3$ is often called **Archard's wear coefficient**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hertzian Contact Pressure

The Hertzian contact pressure governs surface fatigue life. Derivation proceeds from elasticity theory.

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Contact Geometry

For a sphere of radius $R$ pressed against a flat surface with normal load $W$, the contact region is a circle of radius $a$.

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Effective Radius and Modulus

The effective radius is $R^* = R$ (for sphere on flat) and the effective elastic modulus is:

$$\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1 - \nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1 - \nu_2^2}{E_2}$$

where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Contact Radius

The Hertzian contact radius is:

$$a = \left( \frac{3 W R}{4 E^*} \right)^{1/3}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Maximum Pressure

The maximum Hertzian contact pressure occurs at the center of the contact circle:

$$\boxed{p_0 = \frac{3 W}{2 \pi a^2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{6 W E^{*2}}{R^2} \right)^{1/3}}$$

This pressure is approximately 1.5 times the average contact pressure.

### 3.2.5 Step 5: Depth of Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum orthogonal shear stress (which initiates fatigue cracks) occurs at depth $z = 0.48a$ below the surface.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Oxidative Wear Equation (Archard's Tribochemical Model)

### 3.3.1 Step 1: Film Formation

When a fresh metal surface is exposed to an oxidizing environment, a thin oxide film of thickness $t$ forms.

### 3.3.2 Step 2: Volume of Oxide per Unit Area

The volume of oxide formed per unit area of surface is $t$. When the asperity slides a distance equal to the asperity diameter $d$, a new patch of metal is exposed.

### 3.3.3 Step 3: Volume of Oxide Removed per Sliding Distance

For an asperity sliding distance $L$:

$$V_{ox} = \frac{t \cdot A_r \cdot L}{d}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4: Substitute $A_r = W/H$

$$V_{ox} = \frac{t \cdot W \cdot L}{H \cdot d}$$

Defining the oxidative wear coefficient as $K_{ox} = t/d$:

$$\boxed{V_{ox} = \frac{K_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L}{H}}$$

This shows that oxidative wear is also Archard-type, but the wear coefficient depends on the **chemistry of the environment** and the **time available for film growth** per pass.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Adhesive Wear** | **Abrasive Wear** | **Corrosive Wear** | **Surface Fatigue** |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Driving Force** | Atomic bonding at asperities | Mechanical plowing/cutting | Chemical reaction + scraping | Cyclic subsurface stress |
| **Controlling Property** | Material compatibility | Hardness ratio $H_a/H_s$ | Chemical reactivity | Hertzian contact stress |
| **Debris Shape** | Flake-like, transfer fragments | Cutting chips, gouges | Oxide particles, fine powder | Pits, spalls, flakes |
| **Typical K value** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Life-based, not K-based |
| **Surface Appearance** | Scoring, smearing | Grooves, scratches | Tarnishing + polishing | Craters, pitting |
| **Time Dependence** | Linear with $L$ | Linear with $L$ | Linear with $L$ (steady-state) | Cycles to failure (S-N curve) |
| **Load Sensitivity** | Linear ($V \propto W$) | Linear to nonlinear | Linear with synergy | Power law (e.g., $L \propto P^{-p}$) |
| **Countermeasure** | Lubrication, dissimilar materials | Hard coatings, clean environment | Inhibitors, corrosion-resistant alloys | Surface finishing, residual compressive stress |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between two-body and three-body abrasive wear. Give two industrial examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Two-Body Abrasive Wear** | **Three-Body Abrasive Wear** |
|-------------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Hard asperities on one surface plough through the softer counterface | Free hard particles trapped between two surfaces act as abrasive medium |
| **Severity** | Generally more severe | Generally less severe (particles roll rather than cut) |
| **Industrial Examples** | (i) Grinding wheel on workpiece; (ii) File on metal surface | (i) Contaminated lubricant in bearings; (ii) Sand particles in conveyor belt systems |

**Awarding marks:** Definition (1M) + Comparison (1M) + Examples (1M).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is oxidative wear? Write Archard's oxidative wear equation and explain the significance of the wear coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Oxidative wear** is a form of corrosive wear in which a thin oxide film forms on a metal surface due to chemical reaction with the environment (typically atmospheric oxygen), and this film is subsequently removed by mechanical sliding action, exposing fresh metal to further oxidation.

**Archard's Oxidative Wear Equation:**

$$V_{ox} = \frac{K_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

**Significance of $K_{ox}$:** The oxidative wear coefficient depends on the **rate of film formation** and the **time available for film growth per pass**. A higher $K_{ox}$ indicates more aggressive corrosive environment. Values of $K_{ox}$ depend on temperature, oxygen partial pressure, and the chemistry of the metal-oxide system.

**Awarding marks:** Definition (1M) + Equation (1M) + Significance (1M).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question from each module**, choosing between the OR alternatives. Step-by-step model solutions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

**(a) Explain in detail the mechanism of adhesive wear. Derive Archard's adhesive wear equation starting from the real area of contact. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Adhesive Wear (4 Marks):**

1. **Asperity Contact:** When two nominally flat surfaces are brought into contact, actual contact occurs only at the tips of asperities due to surface roughness.
2. **Plastic Deformation:** Under the applied normal load $W$, the asperity tips deform plastically until the real area of contact $A_r$ is sufficient to support the load.
3. **Cold Welding:** At the high contact pressures (often near the hardness of the material), the asperities **weld together** at the atomic level, forming strong metallic junctions. This phenomenon is called **adhesion** or **cold welding**.
4. **Tangential Motion:** When relative sliding motion is initiated, these junctions are subjected to shear stress.
5. **Material Transfer:** If the junction strength exceeds the cohesive strength of the weaker (softer) material, fracture occurs **within the softer material**, causing transfer of material to the harder surface.
6. **Wear Debris:** On subsequent sliding passes, the transferred fragments may detach and form loose wear particles.

**Derivation of Archard's Equation (3 Marks):**

Starting from the real area of contact:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

Assume $n$ asperity junctions of mean diameter $d$:

$$A_r = n \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \implies n = \frac{4 A_r}{\pi d^2}$$

The volume of a single hemispherical wear fragment:

$$V_{frag} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left( \frac{d}{2} \right)^3 = \frac{\pi d^3}{12}$$

For sliding distance $L$, the number of encounters per asperity is $L/d$. Total wear volume:

$$V = n \cdot K_s \cdot V_{frag} \cdot \frac{L}{d}$$

$$V = \frac{4 A_r}{\pi d^2} \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{L}{d} = \frac{K_s A_r L}{3}$$

Substituting $A_r = W/H$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{K_s}{3} \cdot \frac{W L}{H} = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}}$$

This is **Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation**, where $K$ is the dimensionless wear coefficient.

---

**(b) Describe the mechanism of surface fatigue wear. Explain pitting and spalling with neat diagrams. State Lundberg-Palmgren's equation for bearing fatigue life. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Surface Fatigue (3 Marks):**

Surface fatigue occurs under **cyclic loading conditions** in rolling or rolling-sliding contacts (e.g., gear teeth, rolling element bearings, cam-follower pairs). The mechanism proceeds in stages:

1. **Stress Cycle:** Each rolling pass imposes a Hertzian contact stress field, with maximum orthogonal shear stress at depth $z = 0.48a$ below the surface.
2. **Crack Initiation:** After millions of stress cycles, microcracks nucleate at material defects (inclusions, voids, grain boundaries) at the depth of maximum shear stress.
3. **Crack Propagation:** The cracks grow parallel to the surface, often branching towards the surface over time.
4. **Material Detachment:** When a crack reaches the surface, a fragment breaks loose, leaving a crater.

**Pitting and Spalling (2 Marks):**

- **Pitting:** Small (typically < 1 mm depth), shallow craters. Pits have relatively sharp edges and are caused by surface or near-surface crack initiation. Common in gear teeth and rolling bearings with high surface finish.
- **Spalling:** Larger, deeper, irregular material loss. Spalls result from **subsurface crack initiation and propagation** parallel to the surface, and indicate more advanced fatigue damage. Spalling often renders the component unserviceable.

**Lundberg-Palmgren's Equation (2 Marks):**

The fatigue life of a rolling contact bearing is given by:

$$L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^p$$

Where:
- $L_{10}$ = Rated fatigue life in millions of revolutions (90% reliability)
- $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating of the bearing
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic load
- $p$ = Exponent of the Weibull slope, typically $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings

For a given operating speed $n$ (in rpm), the life in hours is:

$$L_{h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^p$$

**Schematic (describe verbally, as no physical drawing is provided in text):**

```
Pitting:           Spalling:
   ___              _______
  /   \            /        \
 | pit |          |   spall   |
  \___/           \__________/
Small, sharp       Large, irregular
shallow crater     deep material loss
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

**(a) Explain abrasive wear with its classification. Discuss the factors affecting abrasive wear rate. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Abrasive Wear (3 Marks):**

Abrasive wear is classified into two principal types:

1. **Two-Body Abrasive Wear:** A hard rough surface (or fixed abrasive) slides against a softer surface. The hard asperities or fixed particles **plough, cut, or gouge** the softer material. Examples: grinding, sandpaper on wood, file on metal.

2. **Three-Body Abrasive Wear:** Hard particles (loose, free-moving) are introduced between two sliding surfaces. These particles may **roll** or **slide** between the surfaces, causing wear on both. Examples: contaminated lubricant in a bearing, dust-laden atmosphere on a piston ring.

**Further Sub-Classification Based on Mechanism:**

- **Plowing:** Material is displaced to the sides forming ridges; little direct material removal.
- **Cutting:** Sharp particles produce microchips similar to a machining operation.
- **Fracture:** In brittle materials, microcracking and chipping dominate.

**Factors Affecting Abrasive Wear Rate (4 Marks):**

| **Factor** | **Effect** |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Hardness ratio ($H_a/H_s$)** | If $H_a > 1.2 H_s$, wear is severe; if $H_a < H_s$, wear is mild. |
| **Abrasive particle size** | Larger particles cause deeper grooves and higher wear (up to a saturation size). |
| **Particle shape (angularity)** | Angular particles cut more aggressively; rounded particles cause mostly plowing. |
| **Attack angle ($\theta$)** | Maximum wear at $\theta = 90°$ for ductile materials; brittle materials may fracture. |
| **Applied load** | Higher load increases penetration depth and wear volume (linear in many cases). |
| **Sliding distance** | Wear volume increases linearly with sliding distance. |
| **Environment** | Corrosive media amplify wear through tribochemical synergy. |
| **Material microstructure** | Harder, more ductile materials with fine grains resist abrasive wear better. |

The Rabiowicz abrasive wear equation:

$$V = \frac{W \cdot L \cdot \tan\theta}{\pi \cdot H}$$

---

**(b) What is corrosive wear? Discuss the mechanism of oxidative wear with a neat block diagram. Explain how synergy between corrosion and wear affects material loss. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**

**Corrosive wear** is the surface degradation that occurs due to the combined action of **chemical or electrochemical reactions** (corrosion) and **mechanical wear** (abrasion, adhesion, or impact) at a sliding interface. The chemical attack forms a reaction product layer (oxide, sulfide, etc.) which is mechanically removed, exposing fresh metal to further attack.

**Mechanism of Oxidative Wear (3 Marks):**

Oxidative wear is the most common form of corrosive wear in air. The process is cyclic:

1. **Adsorption:** Oxygen from the atmosphere adsorbs onto the freshly exposed metal surface.
2. **Chemical Reaction:** The oxygen reacts with the metal to form a thin oxide film of thickness $t$.
3. **Sliding Action:** When sliding occurs, the asperity contact removes the oxide film in the contact zone.
4. **Debris Formation:** The removed oxide appears as fine, dark wear debris (often reddish-brown for iron oxide).
5. **Repetition:** Fresh metal is exposed, and the cycle repeats, leading to steady-state material loss.

**Block Diagram (describing verbally) (1 Mark):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │ Fresh Metal Surface │ ◄────────────────────┐
   └──────────┬──────────┘                      │
              ▼                                  │
   ┌─────────────────────┐                      │
   │ Oxygen Adsorption + │                      │
   │ Chemical Reaction   │                      │
   └──────────┬──────────┘                      │
              ▼                                  │
   ┌─────────────────────┐                      │
   │ Oxide Film Forms    │                      │
   └──────────┬──────────┘                      │
              ▼                                  │
   ┌─────────────────────┐                      │
   │ Sliding Contact     │                      │
   │ (Mechanical Action) │                      │
   └──────────┬──────────┘                      │
              ▼                                  │
   ┌─────────────────────┐                      │
   │ Oxide Film Removed  │                      │
   │ (Wear Debris)       │ ─────────────────────┘
   └─────────────────────┘
```

**Synergy Between Corrosion and Wear (2 Marks):**

The total material loss in corrosive wear is **not** simply the sum of pure corrosion and pure mechanical wear. There exists a **synergy term** $\Delta V_{synergy}$:

$$V_{total} = V_{mechanical} + V_{chemical} + \Delta V_{synergy}$$

Where:
- $V_{mechanical}$ = Material loss due to mechanical action alone
- $V_{chemical}$ = Material loss due to corrosion alone
- $\Delta V_{synergy}$ = Additional loss due to **mechano-chemical interaction**

The synergy term often **dominates** and arises because:
- Mechanical wear continuously **exposes fresh reactive metal**, accelerating corrosion.
- Corrosion products are typically **brittle and loosely bonded**, making them easier to remove mechanically.
- The rough surface created by corrosion also acts as **abrasive asperities**, accelerating mechanical wear.

**Engineering Implication:** In real engineering systems (e.g., marine propellers, chemical pumps, mining equipment exposed to acidic environments), the synergy can account for **50–80%** of the total material loss, making it the most critical factor in design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing adhesive with abrasive wear:** Many students write about "scratches" when the question asks about adhesive wear. Remember: adhesive wear involves **material transfer** at junctions, not scratching.
>
> 2. **Skipping the derivation steps:** The KTU evaluator awards **partial marks** for derivation. Even if you forget the final answer, showing $A_r = W/H$, the volume of a hemisphere, and the substitution steps can earn you 4–5 out of 7 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 3 in Archard's equation:** Some students write $V = KWL/H$ while others correctly include the $1/3$ factor. Be consistent with the form you use and explicitly define your $K$.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention the assumptions of Archard's model (plastic deformation, hemispherical asperities, constant wear coefficient, single-asperity encounter) in the derivation.
>
> 5. **Mixing up pitting and spalling:** Pitting is **shallow and small**; spalling is **deep and irregular**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 6. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** Always carry units throughout the calculation and present the final answer with units. A missing unit can cost 0.5 to 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Writing Lundberg-Palmgren without defining variables:** Always define $L_{10}$, $C$, $P$, and $p$ explicitly. The "10" subscript (90% reliability) is frequently tested.
>
> 8. **Confusing oxidative wear with general corrosion:** Oxidative wear specifically involves **mechanical removal** of the oxide film. Pure corrosion (in the absence of sliding) is a different phenomenon.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

### Core Concepts
- Wear is the progressive loss of material from operating surfaces due to relative motion.
- The four canonical wear modes are: **Adhesive, Abrasive, Corrosive, and Surface Fatigue**.
- The **Archard equation** $V = KWL/H$ is the **master equation** for wear — memorize it in all its forms.
- The **dimensionless wear coefficient $K$** is the key indicator of wear severity. Lower $K$ = better wear resistance.

### Adhesive Wear
- Caused by **cold welding** at asperity junctions.
- Controlled by **material compatibility** — dissimilar metals (e.g., steel + bronze) preferred.
- Real area of contact: $A_r = W/H$.
- **Wear coefficient** $K_{ad}$ typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.

### Abrasive Wear
- **Two-body:** hard surface asperities plough the softer one.
- **Three-body:** loose particles roll/slide between surfaces.
- **Rabiowicz equation:** $V = WL\tan\theta / (\pi H)$.
- **Hardness rule:** $H_a > 1.2 H_s$ → severe abrasion.
- Attack angle for maximum wear: $\theta \approx 90°$ (ductile materials).

### Corrosive Wear
- **Synergy** between chemical attack and mechanical scraping.
- **Oxidative wear** is the most common sub-mode in air.
- **Synergy term** can account for 50–80% of total material loss.
- Controlled by **inhibitors, coatings, and corrosion-resistant alloys**.

### Surface Fatigue
- Occurs under **cyclic rolling or rolling-sliding contact**.
- Cracks initiate at depth $z = 0.48a$ (Hertzian contact radius).
- **Pitting:** small, shallow; **Spalling:** large, deep.
- **Lundberg-Palmgren equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, with $p = 3$ (ball) or $p = 10/3$ (roller).
- Counter-measure: improve surface finish, induce residual compressive stress (shot peening).

### Equations to Memorize
1. $V = KWL/H$ (Archard — general)
2. $V = K_sWL/(3H)$ (Archard — adhesive form)
3. $V = WL\tan\theta/(\pi H)$ (Rabiowicz — abrasive)
4. $p_0 = (1/\pi)(6WE^{*2}/R^2)^{1/3}$ (Hertzian max pressure)
5. $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ (Lundberg-Palmgren — bearing life)
6. $A_r = W/H$ (Real area of contact)

### Industrial Mapping
- **Engine parts:** Adhesive + Abrasive
- **Mining equipment:** Abrasive (dominant)
- **Marine hardware:** Corrosive
- **Bearings & gears:** Surface Fatigue

### Examiner Hot-Spots
- Archard equation derivation (asked almost every exam).
- Compare and contrast the four wear modes (asked as 7-mark or 14-mark question).
- Pitting vs. spalling distinction.
- Lundberg-Palmgren application to bearing life calculation.
- Synergy term in corrosive wear (high-value concept question).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip:** Always **draw a small block diagram or schematic** even if the question does not explicitly ask for one. In KTU 2024 valuation, a clear visual earns 1–2 extra marks (the "presentation bonus"). For numerical problems, show all steps and box the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Topography: Statistical Parameters (Ra, RMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Topography: Statistical Parameters (Ra, RMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Topography – The Foundation of Tribology

In tribology, two surfaces never touch across their entire apparent contact area. They actually make contact only at discrete microscopic peaks called **asperities**. The complete study of the microscopic geometry of these surfaces — bumps, valleys, peaks, and the spacing between them — is called **Surface Topography** (or **Surface Texture**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Surface topography is the geometrical description of a surface at a scale relevant to the physical interaction between contacting bodies, characterized by irregularities arising from manufacturing processes (machining, grinding, lapping) and service conditions (wear, corrosion, fatigue).*

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Statistical Parameters?

A single measurement of "how rough" a surface is meaningless because a surface is a **random, stochastic profile** — not a deterministic shape. Two surfaces may *look* identical visually but have very different microscopic behaviors under load. Therefore, engineers use **statistical parameters** (computed from a sampled length of the profile) to numerically describe the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** Surface roughness parameters are calculated over a standardized **sampling length ($L$)** or **evaluation length ($l_n$)**, usually a multiple of the **cut-off wavelength ($\lambda_c$)** defined by ISO 4287/4288 standards.

## 1.3 The Two Hero Parameters: $R_a$ and $R_{RMS}$

| Parameter | Full Name | What it captures |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **$R_a$** | **Arithmetic Mean Roughness** (CLA – Center Line Average in old US literature) | Average vertical deviation from the mean line |
| **$R_q$ / $R_{RMS}$** | **Root Mean Square Roughness** (R.M.S. value) | Squared average deviation – emphasizes deep valleys and tall peaks |

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy – The "Hill-and-Valley" Picture

> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine standing on top of a sand dune at the beach. Now imagine measuring the height of every grain of sand relative to a flat "average sand level."
> - **$R_a$** is like asking: *"On average, how far is each grain from the average level?"* → It is the **mean absolute deviation**.
> - **$R_{RMS}$** is like asking: *"If I square every height, average them, and take the square root, what do I get?"* → Because squaring **punishes big deviations more**, $R_{RMS}$ is always slightly larger than $R_a$ and is more sensitive to the *occasional deep valley or tall peak*.

For a perfectly **Gaussian (random) surface profile**, the universal relationship holds:
$$R_{RMS} \approx 1.11 \times R_a$$

This **1.11 factor** is one of the most frequently asked constants in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT754 Module 2):** Statistical parameters $R_a$ and $R_{RMS}$ are the **primary descriptors** for characterizing surface finish in tribological design. They govern the **real area of contact**, **asperity deformation**, and **frictional behavior** in subsequent modules (asperity contact theories by Greenwood–Williamson, Bowden–Tabor).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Mathematical Framework

Let the surface profile be measured along a straight line in the x-direction, producing a height function $y(x)$. We define:

1. **Mean Line (Center Line, m–m):** A reference line parallel to the general direction of the profile such that the sum of areas above it equals the sum of areas below it within the sampling length.
   $$\int_0^L y(x)\,dx = 0$$

2. **Sampling Length ($L$):** The length over which a single roughness parameter is calculated. Standard values: **0.08 mm, 0.25 mm, 0.8 mm, 2.5 mm, 8 mm** (ISO 4287/4288).

3. **Evaluation Length ($l_n$):** A length typically equal to **5 × sampling length**, used to average out waviness and form errors.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of $R_a$ (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)
