Comment on the wear regime. **Model Solution for (a):** The Pin-on-Disk Tribometer consists of: 1. **A rotating disk** mounted on a spindle driven by a variable-speed motor (0–2000 rpm). 2. **A stationary pin specimen** clamped in a collet chuck and held by a loading lever arm. 3. **Dead weights** applied through the lever arm to provide normal load $W$ (typically 5–200 N). 4. **A friction force sensor** (load cell) measuring tangential friction force $F_f$. 5. **An LVDT/profilometer** for linear wear depth measurement (optional). 6. **A thermocouple** for bulk temperature monitoring. 7. **A lubricant supply system** for lubricated tests (drip feed or bath). 8. **An enclosure** to control humidity and prevent contamination. **Working Principle:** The disk rotates at preset $n$ rpm; the stationary pin is pressed against it at a fixed track radius $r$ with constant load $W$. The pin traces a circular wear track. After a set sliding distance $L = 2\pi r N$ (where $N$ is the total number of revolutions), the pin is removed and weighed. Mass loss $\Delta m$ is converted to volume loss $V = \Delta m / \rho$. The coefficient of friction $\mu = F_f/W$ is recorded continuously. **ASTM G99 Standard Parameters:** - Pin: ≤ 12.7 mm radius, ball or flat tip - Disk: 30–100 mm diameter, hardness ≥ 25 HRC above pin - Load: 1–200 N - Speed: 0.1–10 m/s sliding velocity - Temperature: 20–25 °C (or as specified) - Humidity: 50% ± 10% RH - Surface finish: $R_a \leq 0.8 \mu m$ - Duration: until desired $L$ achieved **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Sliding velocity:** $$v = \frac{2\pi n r}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 300 \cdot 0.030}{60} = \frac{56.549}{60} = 0.9425 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Sliding distance:** Total revolutions $N = 300 \times 60 = 18{,}000$ rev. $$L = 2\pi r N = 2\pi \cdot 0.030 \cdot 18{,}000 = 3392.92 \text{ m}$$ **(iii) Wear volume:** $$V = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho} = \frac{7.6 \text{ mg}}{8.5 \text{ mg/mm}^3} = 0.8941 \text{ mm}^3$$ **(iv) Specific wear rate $K$:** $$K = \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{0.8941}{30 \cdot 3392.92} = 8.781 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^3/(\text{N} \cdot \text{m})$$ **(v) Archard's wear coefficient $K_a$:** $$K_a = K \cdot H = 8.781 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1000 = 8.781 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Wear regime interpretation:** $K \approx 8.78 \times 10^{-6}$ mm³/(N·m) lies in the **mild wear** regime ($10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$). This indicates that the brass pin experiences mild, steady-state oxidative wear against the steel disk, which is acceptable for lightly loaded journal-bearing applications. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a Four Ball Tester**. Explain how the **Mean Wear Scar Diameter (MWSD)** and the **Weld Load** are determined. **(b) (7 Marks)** In a Four Ball wear test (ASTM D4172) at 40 kgf load, 1200 rpm, 75 °C, 60 minutes, the scar diameters on the three lower balls (two perpendicular measurements per ball) are: - Ball 1: 0.58, 0.61 mm - Ball 2: 0.60, 0.59 mm - Ball 3: 0.62, 0.63 mm Compute the **Mean Wear Scar Diameter** and comment on whether the lubricant passes the typical acceptance criterion. **Model Solution for (a):** The Four Ball Tester consists of: 1. **Upper spindle** carrying the top ball chuck, rotated by a motor (standard 1760 or 1200 rpm). 2. **Three lower ball clamps** arranged in a triangular ball pot, holding three identical 12.7 mm AISI 52100 steel balls. 3. **A lubricant cup** containing ~10 mL of test oil, heated by a thermostatically controlled heater. 4. **A loading lever and dead-weight stack** applying normal loads from 10 kgf to 800 kgf. 5. **A torque sensor** to monitor spindle torque (used to detect seizure/weld). 6. **An auto-stop mechanism** that halts the motor when current spikes (welding point). 7. **An optical microscope** (100×) for measuring scar diameters after the test. **Working Principle:** The upper ball rotates against three clamped lower balls submerged in the test lubricant under a controlled normal load. Sliding generates wear scars on the three lower balls. After a fixed duration (60 min for ASTM D4172), the test is stopped and the scar diameters on the three balls are measured. **MWSD determination:** - Take **two perpendicular measurements** of scar diameter on each of the three balls (6 readings total). - Compute the arithmetic mean: $d_{mw} = (d_{11} + d_{12} + d_{21} + d_{22} + d_{31} + d_{32}) / 6$. - If required, apply the Hertzian compression correction $h_c$ to obtain the **compensated MWSD**: $d_c = d_{mw} - h_c$. **Weld Load determination (ASTM D2596):** - Apply progressively increasing loads (typically in steps of 10–50 kgf) with fresh oil and balls for each load. - The **weld load** is the lowest load at which the rotating ball welds to the three stationary balls and the motor stalls within a specified time (typically 10 s). - The auto-stop circuit (current or torque sensor) registers the event. **Model Solution for (b):** All six scar measurements: - Ball 1: 0.58, 0.61 mm - Ball 2: 0.60, 0.59 mm - Ball 3: 0.62, 0.63 mm $$\begin{aligned} d_{mw} &= \frac{0.58 + 0.61 + 0.60 + 0.59 + 0.62 + 0.63}{6} \\ &= \frac{3.63}{6} \\ &= 0.605 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Comment:** The typical acceptance criterion per ASTM D4172 for an AW (anti-wear) hydraulic oil at 40 kgf is MWSD **≤ 0.50 mm**. Here, $d_{mw} = 0.605$ mm exceeds this limit by ~21%, indicating that the lubricant's anti-wear additive package is **insufficient** for AW hydraulic service. A re-formulation with higher ZDDP concentration or addition of a complementary AW additive (e.g., ashless phosphor-sulfur compounds) is recommended. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Pin-on-Disk & Four Ball):** > > 1. **Unit Blunder (2–3 marks lost):** Writing $K$ in cm³/(N·m) or mm³/(N·mm) instead of mm³/(N·m). KTU examiners are *strict* on SI units. > 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s:** Many students use $v = \pi r n / 60$ (incorrect) instead of $v = 2\pi r n / 60$. > 3. **Confusing $K$ and $K_a$:** $K$ has units mm³/(N·m); $K_a$ is dimensionless. The relationship is $K_a = K \cdot H$, **not** $K_a = K / H$. > 4. **Wrong ASTM standard:** Saying "Pin-on-Disk is ASTM D2266" will lose 1 mark instantly. Always remember: > - PoD → **ASTM G99** > - Four Ball Wear → **ASTM D2266 / D4172** > - Four Ball EP → **ASTM D2596** > 5. **Four Ball scar count:** Reporting average of 3 instead of 6 measurements loses 2 marks. *Always take 2 perpendicular measurements per ball.* > 6. **Weld load confusion:** Weld load is **NOT** the load at which seizure begins; it is the load at which the rotating ball **welds** to the three stationary balls and the motor stalls. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Pin-on-Disk & Four Ball Testers (KTU PEMUT754 Module 2)** - **Pin-on-Disk (PoD):** Rotary sliding wear rig; stationary pin against rotating disk. - ASTM standard: **G99**. - Outputs: Wear rate $\dot{W}_v$ [mm³/m], specific wear rate $K$ [mm³/(N·m)], coefficient of friction $\mu$. - **Four Ball Tester (FBT):** Top ball rotates against three stationary balls submerged in lubricant. - ASTM standards: **D2266, D4172 (Wear); D2596 (EP); D2783 (LNSL)**. - Output: **MWSD** [mm] and **Weld Load** [kgf]. - **Archard's Equation:** $V = K_a \cdot W \cdot L / H$ — fundamental sliding wear law. - **Specific Wear Rate:** $K = V / (W \cdot L)$ — independent of load/distance; compare different rigs. - **Sliding velocity:** $v = 2\pi r n / 60$. - **Sliding distance:** $L = 2\pi r N$. - **Wear volume (gravimetric):** $V = \Delta m / \rho$. - **MWSD formula:** Average of 6 scar diameter readings (2 perpendicular × 3 balls). - **Compensated MWSD:** $d_c = d_m - h_c$ (Hertzian compression correction). - **Acceptance criteria:** MWSD < 0.50 mm at 40 kgf, 1200 rpm, 75 °C, 60 min for AW oils. - **Wear regimes by $K$ [mm³/(N·m)]:** - Mild: $10^{-7}$–$10^{-6}$ - Moderate: $10^{-6}$–$10^{-4}$ - Severe: $> 10^{-4}$ - **Standard balls:** AISI 52100, 12.7 mm diameter, 64–66 HRC. - **Standard load for ASTM D4172:** 40 kgf ≈ 392 N. - **Lubricant volume in FBT:** ~10 mL. - **Temperature in FBT (D4172):** 75 °C. - **Microscope magnification for scar measurement:** **100×**. - **Coefficient of friction** $\mu = F_f / W$ — recorded continuously during PoD. - **Wear rate (volume basis)** $\dot{W}_v = V/L$ — mm³/m. - **Units memorization is critical:** mm³, N, m, kgf, mm, mm³/(N·m), dimensionless $K_a$. - **Always convert mg to mm³** using density $\rho$ when using gravimetric method. - **Weld Load** is detected via torque/current spike — **not** a friction-based measurement. > **End of Module 2 — Topic: Wear Measurement Techniques: Pin-on-Disk Tester and the Four Ball Tester** > *(Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme standards)*

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛢️ HYDRODYNAMIC LUBRICATION — Core Technical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** is a regime of full-film lubrication in which the load-carrying capacity between two relatively moving surfaces (e.g., journal and bearing) is generated entirely by the **viscous shear of the lubricant** drawn into a converging wedge-shaped clearance. The surfaces are **completely separated** by a continuous fluid film of thickness typically in the range of **1 µm to 100 µm**, and no direct asperity (solid-to-solid) contact occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):**
> Hydrodynamic Lubrication is the **cornerstone module** of Tribology. The following sub-concepts are explicitly listed in the PEMUT754 syllabus:
> 1. **Reynolds Equation** — General and simplified forms
> 2. **Petroff's Law** — Friction in concentric bearings
> 3. **Load Carrying Capacity** and **Sommerfeld Number**
> 4. **Coefficient of Friction** in hydrodynamic regime
> 5. **Pressure Distribution** in the lubricant film

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Skier & Wedge" Intuition

Imagine a person **water-skiing** behind a fast boat. The ski (a flat plate) is dragged across the water surface at a high angle of attack. As the ski moves, water is **dragged underneath** it due to viscosity, and because the ski is tilted (forming a wedge), this water is **squeezed and pressurized** between the ski and the water surface. This hydrodynamic pressure lift is what keeps the skier afloat — the ski never actually touches the water surface!

In a journal bearing, the **shaft (journal)** is the "ski," the **bearing** is the "water," and the **converging clearance** (formed because the shaft sits slightly eccentric) acts as the wedge that pumps and pressurizes the oil. **The viscosity of the lubricant does all the work** — not the supply pressure, not the surface chemistry. This is the essence of hydrodynamic lubrication.

---

## 🔑 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| $h$ | Film thickness | **1 µm to 100 µm** |
| $c$ | Radial clearance | **10 µm to 500 µm** |
| $\eta$ (or $\mu$) | Dynamic viscosity | **0.001 to 0.1 Pa·s** |
| $N$ | Journal speed | **500 to 10,000 rpm** |
| $W$ | Load on bearing | **1 kN to 1000 kN** |
| $P$ | Hydrodynamic pressure | **0.5 to 20 MPa** |
| $\mu_f$ | Coefficient of friction | **0.001 to 0.05** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this regime called "Hydrodynamic"?**
> The term *hydro* (water/fluid) + *dynamic* (motion) literally means the fluid film is **created and sustained purely by relative motion** — no external pumping pressure is required. This is what distinguishes it from **hydrostatic lubrication**, where the load is supported by an externally pressurized fluid.

---

## 📊 The Three Pillars of Hydrodynamic Lubrication

1. **Relative Motion** — Without shaft rotation, no oil is dragged into the clearance wedge, and no pressure is generated.
2. **Viscosity** — A Newtonian fluid behavior is assumed; the viscosity must be sufficient to generate shear stresses.
3. **Converging Geometry (Wedge Action)** — A non-parallel clearance (eccentricity in a journal bearing) is essential to convert shear flow into a pressure-generating flow.

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 The Physics Behind the Wedge

The hydrodynamic pressure is generated through a beautiful chain of physical events. Let's break it down step-by-step:

### Step 1 — The Shear-Drag Mechanism
When a surface moves relative to a viscous fluid, the fluid molecules **adhere** to the moving surface (no-slip condition) and are dragged along with it. This creates a velocity gradient in the fluid layer.

### Step 2 — Couette Flow (Viscous Drag Flow)
The dragged fluid moves in layers (laminar flow), with each layer moving slower than the one above. This produces a shear stress $\tau$ given by Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $u$ is the fluid velocity and $y$ is the direction normal to the surface.

### Step 3 — Wedge Convergence — The Key to Pressure!
If the clearance is **parallel**, the dragged fluid simply flows through with no pressure rise (like water between two flat parallel plates moving at constant speed). But if the clearance **converges** (gets narrower in the direction of motion), the fluid gets **squeezed**, and by the **continuity equation**, this squeezing **must generate a pressure rise**. This is the **Reynolds wedge effect**.

### Step 4 — Pressure Distribution
The pressure is **maximum** at the location of minimum film thickness (typically near the trailing edge of the wedge) and **zero** (atmospheric) at the start and end of the film. The integral of this pressure distribution over the bearing surface must equal the applied load $W$.

### Step 5 — Equilibrium and Eccentricity
The shaft automatically settles at an **equilibrium eccentricity** $\varepsilon$ (dimensionless) where the integrated pressure force exactly balances the applied load. Higher loads push the shaft closer to the bearing (higher $\varepsilon$), reducing film thickness.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover 90% of KTU exam questions on hydrodynamic lubrication.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Application |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^{3}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^{3}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \eta U \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | **General 2-D Reynolds Equation** (incompressible, isoviscous, no squeeze) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(h^{3}\dfrac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6 \eta U \dfrac{dh}{dx}$ | **1-D Reynolds Equation** (long bearing assumption, $L \gg D$) |
| 3 | $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ | **Film thickness in a journal bearing** |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dp}{dx} = 6 \eta U \dfrac{h - h^{*}}{h^{3}}$ | **Reynolds eq. integrated** — where $h^{*}$ is the film thickness at max pressure |
| 5 | $W = \eta N L D^{2} \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\psi^{2}(1-\varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}$ (approx.) | **Load-carrying capacity** (long journal bearing, full Sommerfeld) |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ (or $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\cdot\dfrac{L}{D}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\psi^{2}}$) | **Sommerfeld Number** (dimensionless bearing modulus) |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \eta N^{2} L r^{3}}{c}$ | **Petroff's Friction Torque** (concentric, no load) |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2} \eta N r}{P c}$ | **Petroff's Friction Force / Coefficient form** |
| 9 | $\mu_f = \dfrac{f}{W} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2} \eta N r^{3}}{W c}$ | **Coefficient of Friction (Petroff)** |
| 10 | $h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | **Minimum film thickness** |
| 11 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D N c}{2}(1 + 1.5\varepsilon^{2})$ | **Lubricant flow rate** through bearing |
| 12 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\mu_f W U}{Q \rho c_{p}}$ | **Temperature rise** of oil in bearing |

> ⚠️ **Notation Notes for KTU Exams:**
> - $\psi = c/r$ = **clearance ratio** (typical: 0.001 to 0.002)
> - $\varepsilon = e/c$ = **eccentricity ratio** (0 = concentric, 1 = touching)
> - Some textbooks use $\mu$ for viscosity and $f$ or $\mu_f$ for friction coefficient — KTU accepts both.
> - Never confuse **dynamic viscosity $\eta$** with **friction coefficient $\mu_f$** — they are completely different quantities!

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Journal bearings** in electric motors, turbines, pumps, IC engines, gearboxes.
- **Thrust bearings** in hydroelectric generators and marine propeller shafts.
- **Slider bearings** in machine tool slides and gibs.
- **Sleeve bearings** in automotive connecting rods and crankshafts.
- Designing to a target **Sommerfeld number** is the standard KTU approach to selecting bearing dimensions for a given load and speed.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mode — Activated.**
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped transitions.

---

## 📘 Derivation 1: Reynolds Equation from First Principles (1-D Slider)

### Setup
Consider a **rectangular slider bearing** of length $L$ (in $z$-direction, into the page), width $B$ (in $y$-direction, perpendicular to motion). A flat block of length $L$ moves at velocity $U$ over a stationary inclined surface. The film thickness varies linearly from $h_1$ at the leading edge ($x=0$) to $h_2$ at the trailing edge ($x=L$).

$$h(x) = h_1 - (h_1 - h_2)\frac{x}{L} = h_1 - \alpha x$$

where $\alpha = (h_1 - h_2)/L$ is the slope of the wedge.

### Assumptions
1. Steady-state, laminar flow.
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid, constant viscosity $\eta$.
3. Negligible inertia and body forces (creeping flow).
4. No flow in the $y$- and $z$-directions (the flow is purely in $x$).
5. No-slip at both surfaces: $u = U$ at $y = 0$ (moving block) and $u = 0$ at $y = h$ (stationary block).
6. Pressure $p$ is a function of $x$ only.

### Governing Equations

The Navier-Stokes equation in the $x$-direction, simplified under the above assumptions:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

### Step 1 — Integrate Twice with Respect to $y$

First integration:

$$\eta \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y + C_{1}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y + \frac{C_{1}}{\eta}$$

Second integration:

$$u(x, y) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y^{2} + \frac{C_{1}}{\eta} y + C_{2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions

**BC 1:** At $y = 0$, $u = U$ (block surface moving):

$$U = 0 + 0 + C_{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{2} = U$$

**BC 2:** At $y = h$, $u = 0$ (stationary surface):

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h^{2} + \frac{C_{1}}{\eta} h + U$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$\frac{C_{1}}{\eta} h = -U - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h^{2}$$

$$C_{1} = -\frac{\eta U}{h} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h$$

### Step 3 — Full Velocity Profile

Substitute $C_1$ and $C_2$ back:

$$u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y^{2} + \left(-\frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h\right) y + U$$

This can be reorganized into the **standard form**:

$$u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left(y^{2} - yh\right) + U\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

$$u = -\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} y(h - y) + \frac{U}{h}(h - y)$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The first term is the **Poiseuille (pressure-driven) flow** component, the second is the **Couette (shear-driven) flow** component.

### Step 4 — Mass Conservation (Continuity Equation)

For incompressible flow:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Integrate across the film thickness from $y = 0$ to $y = h$:

$$\int_{0}^{h} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\, dy + \int_{0}^{h} \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\, dy = 0$$

The second integral evaluates to $v \vert_{0}^{h}$. Since $v = 0$ at both $y = 0$ and $y = h$ (no flow through the walls):

$$\int_{0}^{h} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\, dy = 0$$

### Step 5 — Apply Leibniz Rule

Differentiating under the integral sign (Leibniz rule):

$$\frac{d}{dx}\int_{0}^{h} u\, dy - u \Big\vert_{y=h} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} + u \Big\vert_{y=0} \cdot 0 = 0$$

Since $u(y=h) = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\int_{0}^{h} u\, dy = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{0}^{h} u\, dy = \text{const} = Q$$

This is the **flow rate per unit width**, which is constant along the bearing (since fluid is neither created nor destroyed).

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Flow Integral

$$\int_{0}^{h} u\, dy = \int_{0}^{h}\left[\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^2 - yh) + \frac{U}{h}(h - y)\right]dy$$

Compute each part:

**Part A:**
$$\int_{0}^{h}\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^{2} - yh)\,dy = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{y^{3}}{3} - \frac{y^{2}h}{2}\right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{h^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{3}}{2}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{h^{3}}{6}\right) = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Part B:**
$$\int_{0}^{h}\frac{U}{h}(h-y)\,dy = \frac{U}{h}\left[hy - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{U}{h}\left[h^{2} - \frac{h^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{Uh}{2}$$

### Step 7 — Set Flow Rate Constant and Differentiate

$$Q = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}$$

This must hold at every $x$, so differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$0 = -\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{U}{2}\frac{dh}{dx}$$

Multiplying by $-12\eta$ and noting that $\eta = \text{const}$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(h^{3}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6\eta U\frac{dh}{dx}$$

This is the **one-dimensional Reynolds Equation** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reynolds Equation is the master equation of hydrodynamic lubrication.** It was independently derived by Osborne Reynolds in 1886 and remains the foundation of all bearing design to this day.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: Petroff's Equation (Concentric Journal Bearing)

### Setup
A shaft of radius $r$ rotates at angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s) inside a stationary bearing of radius $R = r + c$, where $c \ll r$. Assume the shaft is **perfectly concentric** (no eccentricity, $\varepsilon = 0$), and the film thickness is **uniform** at $h = c$.

### Velocity Profile
The velocity in the film at radius $\xi$ from the bearing center is linear (since $c \ll r$, the curvature is negligible):

$$u = r\omega \cdot \frac{R - \xi}{c} = r\omega\left(\frac{R - \xi}{c}\right)$$

### Shear Stress at the Shaft Surface
The shear stress on the shaft surface ($\xi = r$) is:

$$\tau = \eta \left.\frac{du}{d\xi}\right|_{\xi = r} = \eta \cdot \frac{r\omega}{c}$$

### Friction Force
The friction force on the shaft (length $L$):

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot \frac{r\omega}{c} \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{2\pi \eta r^{2} L \omega}{c}$$

### Friction Torque
Multiplying by the shaft radius $r$:

$$T = F \cdot r = \frac{2\pi \eta r^{3} L \omega}{c}$$

### Expressing in terms of RPM
Since $N$ (rpm) relates to $\omega$ by $\omega = 2\pi N$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi \eta r^{3} L (2\pi N)}{c} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{3} L}{c}$$

> **Petroff's Friction Torque Equation** ✅

### Petroff's Friction Coefficient
Divide friction force by load $W$ (where $W$ is the projected area load, $W = P \cdot 2rL$):

$$\mu_f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{2\pi \eta r^{2} L \omega / c}{P \cdot 2rL} = \frac{\pi \eta r \omega}{P c} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \eta N r}{P c}$$

> **Petroff's Coefficient of Friction** ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Petroff's equation shows that for a concentric, lightly loaded bearing, the friction coefficient is **inversely proportional to load** ($1/P$) and **directly proportional to speed and viscosity** ($N \cdot \eta$). This is the opposite of boundary lubrication, where friction is **independent of load** and **independent of speed**.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lubrication Regimes

| Property | Boundary | Mixed | Hydrodynamic |
|----------|----------|-------|--------------|
| Film thickness | $< 1\ \mu m$ | $1$–$10\ \mu m$ | $> 10\ \mu m$ |
| $\lambda = h/\sigma$ | $< 1$ | $1$–$3$ | $> 3$ |
| Asperity contact | Yes (high) | Partial | None |
| Friction coeff. $\mu_f$ | $0.05$–$0.15$ | $0.02$–$0.05$ | $0.001$–$0.01$ |
| Wear | High | Moderate | Negligible |
| Load support | Surface contact | Surface + fluid | Fluid only |
| Stribeck curve region | Plateau | Falling | Falling branch |

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define hydrodynamic lubrication. List the three essential conditions required for its existence.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hydrodynamic Lubrication** is the regime of lubrication in which the load between two relatively moving surfaces is supported entirely by a **continuous fluid film** generated by the **viscous drag of the lubricant** into a converging clearance. The surfaces are completely separated, and no direct asperity contact occurs.

**The three essential conditions are:**

1. **Relative motion** between the surfaces (shaft rotation).
2. **Sufficient viscosity** of the lubricant (Newtonian behavior).
3. **A converging (wedge-shaped) clearance** — provided by eccentricity in a journal bearing.

> **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing conditions + 1 for explaining wedge action]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**What is Petroff's equation? State its significance and the assumptions made.**

**Model Answer:**

**Petroff's equation** gives the friction torque in a **concentric, lightly loaded journal bearing** with a uniform full-film lubricant:

$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{3} L}{c}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Shaft is perfectly concentric with the bearing ($\varepsilon = 0$).
2. Film thickness is uniform ($h = c$).
3. Flow is laminar and Newtonian.
4. Side leakage is neglected.
5. Bearing is infinitely long (or end effects ignored).

**Significance:**
- It provides the **friction baseline** for a hydrodynamic bearing.
- It is used to derive the **Stribeck curve** at zero eccentricity.
- It shows friction is proportional to $\eta$, $N$, and $r^3$, and inversely proportional to clearance $c$.

> **[3 Marks: 1 for equation + 1 for assumptions + 1 for significance]**

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adaptive Model]**

**(a) Derive the one-dimensional Reynolds equation for a slider bearing starting from the Navier-Stokes equations. State all assumptions clearly.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) A 360° journal bearing has the following data:**
- Radial load $W = 50\ \text{kN}$
- Journal diameter $D = 100\ \text{mm}$, Length $L = 100\ \text{mm}$
- Speed $N = 1500\ \text{rpm}$
- Clearance $c = 0.1\ \text{mm}$
- Viscosity $\eta = 0.05\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

**Calculate:** (i) Petroff's friction torque, (ii) Petroff's coefficient of friction, (iii) Power loss in the bearing. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📐 Solution to Q-A (a)

The complete step-by-step derivation of the **1-D Reynolds Equation** is given in **SECTION 3 — Derivation 1** of these notes. For the 7-mark exam answer, the student should present:

1. **Schematic diagram** of slider bearing with coordinate axes (1 mark).
2. **List of 6 assumptions** — steady, laminar, incompressible, Newtonian, no side flow, pressure varies only in $x$ (1 mark).
3. **Starting from Navier-Stokes** simplified form: $\partial p/\partial x = \eta \partial^{2} u/\partial y^{2}$ (1 mark).
4. **Double integration** with respect to $y$ to obtain $u(x,y)$ profile (2 marks).
5. **Apply continuity**, integrate across film thickness, use Leibniz rule (1 mark).
6. **Final result** $d/dx(h^{3}\, dp/dx) = 6\eta U\, dh/dx$ with proper units and physical meaning (1 mark).

---

#### 📐 Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given Data:**
$$W = 50{,}000\ \text{N},\ D = 0.1\ \text{m},\ L = 0.1\ \text{m},\ N = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$
$$c = 0.0001\ \text{m},\ r = 0.05\ \text{m},\ \eta = 0.05\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**(i) Petroff's Friction Torque:**

$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{3} L}{c}$$

$$T = \frac{4 \times (3.1416)^{2} \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times (0.05)^{3} \times 0.1}{0.0001}$$

Computing step by step:
- $\pi^{2} = 9.8696$
- $4 \times 9.8696 = 39.478$
- $r^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$
- Numerator: $39.478 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1$
- $= 39.478 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-5}$
- $= 39.478 \times 9.375 \times 10^{-5}$
- $= 3.701 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}$

$$\boxed{T \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m} = 3.70\ \text{N}\cdot\text{mm}}$$

**(ii) Petroff's Coefficient of Friction:**

$$\mu_f = \frac{2\pi^{2} \eta N r}{P \cdot c}$$

where projected pressure $P = W / (2rL) = 50{,}000 / (2 \times 0.05 \times 0.1) = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 5\ \text{MPa}$

$$\mu_f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 0.05}{5 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0001}$$

- Numerator: $2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 0.05 = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 3.75 = 74.02$
- Denominator: $5 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-4} = 500$
- $\mu_f = 74.02 / 500 = 0.148$

$$\boxed{\mu_f \approx 0.148}$$

> ⚠️ **This high value indicates the assumption of concentricity is invalid for this load — the bearing would actually operate with significant eccentricity, lowering real friction.**

**(iii) Power Loss:**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 3.701 \times 10^{-3} \times 157.08 = 0.581\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 0.58\ \text{W}}$$

> **[7 Marks: 2 for (i) + 2 for (ii) + 1 for (iii) + 1 for units + 1 for interpretation]**

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adaptive Model]**

**(a) Explain the Sommerfeld number. Derive the expression for the load-carrying capacity of a short journal bearing using the Reynolds equation.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) A hydrodynamic bearing has the following specifications:**
- Sommerfeld number $S = 0.05$
- Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = 0.6$
- Clearance ratio $\psi = 0.001$
- Journal diameter $D = 80\ \text{mm}$

**Compute:** (i) Minimum film thickness, (ii) Load on the bearing (using the simplified Sommerfeld equation $W = \dfrac{2\pi^{2} \eta N L D^{2} \varepsilon}{\psi^{2} \cdot 2}$ for a short bearing approximation), and (iii) verify if the bearing operates in the hydrodynamic regime (assume surface roughness $\sigma = 1\ \mu m$). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### 📐 Solution to Q-B (a)

**The Sommerfeld Number (S)** is a **dimensionless bearing modulus** that characterizes the operating regime of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is defined as:

$$S = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = \frac{\mu N}{P \cdot \psi}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity
- $N$ = rotational speed
- $P$ = bearing pressure (projected)
- $\psi = c/r$ = clearance ratio

**Physical Meaning:**
- **Low S** → high load or low speed → **high eccentricity** → thin film
- **High S** → low load or high speed → **low eccentricity** → thick film

**Derivation of Load Capacity (Outline):**

For a short journal bearing, integrate the Reynolds equation in the axial direction to get an average pressure. The half-wedge solution gives:

$$W = \frac{6 \pi \eta U L r^{2} \varepsilon}{c^{2}(1-\varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}\cdot(\text{correction factor})$$

For a long journal bearing (neglecting side leakage):

$$W = \frac{2\eta N L D^{2} \varepsilon}{\psi^{2}(1-\varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}\cdot(\text{constant factor})$$

The exact form depends on the boundary conditions used (half Sommerfeld vs. full Sommerfeld).

> **[7 Marks: 2 for Sommerfeld definition + 3 for derivation steps + 2 for final expression]**

---

#### 📐 Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:**
$$\varepsilon = 0.6,\ \psi = 0.001,\ D = 0.08\ \text{m},\ S = 0.05$$

**(i) Minimum Film Thickness:**

$$h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = \psi \cdot r \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)$$

$$r = D/2 = 0.04\ \text{m}$$

$$c = \psi \cdot r = 0.001 \times 0.04 = 4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 40\ \mu\text{m}$$

$$h_{\min} = 40 \times (1 - 0.6) = 40 \times 0.4 = 16\ \mu\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\min} = 16\ \mu\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Load on the Bearing:**

Using the simplified short-bearing Sommerfeld equation:

$$W = \frac{2\pi^{2} \eta N L D^{2} \varepsilon}{2 \psi^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \eta N L D^{2} \varepsilon}{\psi^{2}}$$

> The given $\eta$ and $N$ and $L$ are not numerically specified in this sub-question — the examiner expects the student to express the answer **in terms of the unknowns** OR use the Sommerfeld number to back-calculate. From $S = \mu N / (P \psi)$:
>
> $$P = \frac{\mu N}{S \cdot \psi} = \frac{\mu N}{0.05 \times 0.001} = \frac{\mu N}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$
>
> $$W = P \cdot L \cdot D = \frac{\mu N L D}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000 \cdot \mu N L D$$

For a 50 mm length bearing with $\eta = 0.03$ Pa·s, $N = 1800$ rpm:

$$W = 20{,}000 \times 0.03 \times 1800 \times 0.05 \times 0.08 = 20{,}000 \times 0.216 = 4{,}320\ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 4.32\ \text{kN (for the assumed values)}}$$

**(iii) Verification of Hydrodynamic Regime:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{\min}}{\sigma} = \frac{16\ \mu\text{m}}{1\ \mu\text{m}} = 16$$

Since $\lambda > 3$ (the KTU standard threshold for full-film hydrodynamic lubrication):

$$\boxed{\lambda = 16 > 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HYDRODYNAMIC REGIME CONFIRMED ✅}}$$

> **[7 Marks: 2 for (i) + 2 for (ii) + 2 for (iii) + 1 for conclusion]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units!** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per quantity if units are missing or wrong. Always write **N, mm, Pa·s, W** explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Petroff's $f$ with the dimensionless coefficient of friction $\mu_f$.** Petroff's friction force equation has different units — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using $N$ as rad/s in Petroff's equation instead of rpm.** Petroff's formula requires $N$ in **revolutions per minute** (rpm), not rad/s. This is a classic trap!
> 4. **Forgetting the Sommerfeld correction factor.** The full load expression has $(1-\varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}$ in the denominator — omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not sketching the bearing geometry or film profile.** Even in a 14-mark question, a small labeled diagram of the journal, bearing, eccentricity, and wedge is worth 1–2 marks and shows understanding.
> 6. **Mixing up clearance ratio $\psi = c/r$ and $c/D$.** KTU uses $\psi = c/r$ in Sommerfeld equations. Don't substitute wrong.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** = Fluid film generated by **motion + viscosity + wedge**.

> ✅ **Reynolds Equation** (1-D) — $d/dx(h^{3}\,dp/dx) = 6\eta U\,dh/dx$ — derived from Navier-Stokes + continuity.

> ✅ **Three pillars**: Relative motion + Viscosity + Converging wedge.

> ✅ **Petroff's Torque** — $T = 4\pi^{2}\eta N r^{3} L / c$ — valid only for **concentric**, lightly loaded bearings.

> ✅ **Petroff's $\mu_f$** — Inversely proportional to load, directly proportional to $\eta N r / c$.

> ✅ **Sommerfeld Number** — $S = \mu N / (P \psi)$ — dimensionless bearing modulus. Low S = high load regime.

> ✅ **Load capacity** — proportional to $\eta N L D^{2} \varepsilon / \psi^{2}$.

> ✅ **Film thickness** — $h = c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)$ in journal bearings; $h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$.

> ✅ **Hydrodynamic regime test** — $\lambda = h_{\min}/\sigma > 3$.

> ✅ **Typical values** — $h = 1$–$100\ \mu m$, $\psi = 0.001$–$0.002$, $\mu_f = 0.001$–$0.05$.

> ✅ **Power loss** — $P = T \cdot \omega$ with $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ (rad/s).

> ✅ **Flow rate** — $Q = (\pi D N c/2)(1 + 1.5\varepsilon^{2})$ (side leakage model).

> ✅ **KTU Mantra** — Always draw the diagram, always state assumptions, always carry units, and always mention the **wedge action** as the root cause of pressure generation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Lubrication
Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** is a regime of fluid-film lubrication in which the elastic deformation of the contacting surfaces and the large pressure-induced increase in lubricant viscosity occur simultaneously, and both effects are of comparable order of magnitude to the lubricant film thickness. The hydrodynamic action of the lubricant generates a pressure distribution that is high enough to cause significant elastic flattening of the asperities (or macro-geometry), while the very same high pressure dramatically increases the lubricant viscosity according to the Barus equation, preventing film collapse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> "EHL is the lubrication regime in which the load is supported by a lubricant film whose thickness is determined by the combined action of hydrodynamic fluid pressure and the elastic deformation of the surfaces in contact. It is the dominant lubrication mechanism in highly loaded rolling-element bearings, gears, and cams."

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of EHL

For an examiner, EHL is the **convergence of three independent physical phenomena** that must be solved simultaneously:

1. **Hydrodynamics** — Reynolds equation governs the pressure build-up in the converging wedge/entrapment zone.
2. **Elasticity** — The contact pressure deforms the surfaces (Hertzian-type deflection, typically 0.1 µm to 1 µm), altering the film geometry.
3. **Rheology (Piezo-viscosity)** — Lubricant viscosity rises exponentially with pressure, characterised by the **pressure-viscosity coefficient ($\alpha$)** following Barus' law: $\eta(p) = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inflatable Air Mattress Under a Boulder"

Imagine trying to slide a **boulder** (a heavily loaded ball) across a **concrete floor** (raceway) with only a **thin water film** in between. You would expect the water to be squeezed out instantly because the load is enormous (several GPa of contact stress). However, in EHL:

- The boulder **deforms the concrete slightly** (elastically), creating a tiny **flat plateau** — this widens the gap and slows the leakage of fluid.
- The water, now trapped in this near-flat, high-pressure zone, becomes **almost glass-like in viscosity** (it refuses to be squeezed out).
- A stable, ultra-thin film (typically **0.1 – 1 µm**) survives because the surfaces have been "shaped" to retain it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Locations of EHL:**
> - Rolling-element bearings (ball/roller bearings) — most common.
> - Gear teeth contacts (involute flanks).
> - Cam–follower interfaces in IC engines.
> - Rolling/sliding contacts in metal-forming (drawing, rolling mills).

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pressure-viscosity coefficient ($\alpha$)**: typically **1.0 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹** to **2.5 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹** for mineral oils at 40 °C.
- **Reduced modulus of elasticity ($E'$)**: For steel-on-steel, $E' \approx 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ **Pa**.
- **Typical EHL film thickness ($h_c$)**: **0.1 µm to 1.0 µm** in machine elements.
- **Lambda ratio ($\lambda$)**: $\lambda = h_c / \sigma$, where $\lambda > 3$ indicates full-film EHL.
- **Dimensionless film thickness ($\bar{h}$)**: $\bar{h} = h_c / R_x$, typically **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why EHL is the "King" of Lubrication Regimes for KTU:**
> Most high-performance machine elements (gearboxes, turbomachinery bearings) operate in EHL. Every KTU question on lubrication efficiency, bearing life, or scuffing failure eventually traces back to EHL film thickness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The EHL analysis proceeds as a coupled problem. For a 2D elliptical contact (ball-on-raceway or gear-tooth), the following coupled equations must be satisfied simultaneously:

**Step 1 — Geometry of Contact (Hertzian theory):**
The undeformed contact of two elastic bodies produces either a circle (ball-on-flat) or an ellipse (general conforming contact). The peak Hertzian pressure $p_0$ can reach **1.0 – 3.0 GPa** in rolling bearings.

**Step 2 — Elastic Deformation:**
Under this pressure, the surfaces deform elastically by an amount $\delta$ that is comparable to the lubricant film thickness $h$. This is the *elasto* in EHL. The deformed shape flattens the contact, broadening the pressure distribution and creating a near-parallel film region.

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic Pressure Build-up:**
Fluid dragged into the converging inlet (approach zone) generates a pressure spike just before the Hertzian contact zone. This pressure spike is much narrower than the Hertzian width and produces a localised "horseshoe" constriction in the film.

**Step 4 — Piezo-viscous Lock-up:**
The pressure spike dramatically increases viscosity. The fluid becomes nearly solid and is carried through the contact essentially as a trapped film, with a small side-leakage.

**Step 5 — Exit Constriction:**
At the outlet, the pressure drops sharply, and the film thins to a "minimum film thickness" $h_{min}$, often the location of surface distress (e.g., micropitting).

### 2.2 The Four Classic EHL Regimes (Hamrock–Dowson Classification)

| Regime | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Isoviscous-Rigid (IVR)** | Low load, low $\alpha$, no elastic deformation | Lightly loaded slow gears |
| **Piezoviscous-Rigid (PVR)** | High $\alpha$, low $W$ | Traction drives, slow bearings |
| **Isoviscous-Elastic (IVE)** | High $W$, low $\alpha$ | Compliant bearings, rubber seals |
| **Piezoviscous-Elastic (PVE)** — *Full EHL* | Both $\alpha$ and $W$ high | **Ball/roller bearings, gear teeth** |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorise** equation set for KTU Board Examinations. Every derivation begins from one of these expressions.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barus Viscosity Law | $\eta(p)$ | $\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$ | $\eta_0$ in Pa·s, $\alpha$ in Pa⁻¹ |
| 2 | Reduced Modulus | $E'$ | $\dfrac{1}{E'} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right)$ | Pa |
| 3 | Reduced Radius (ball-on-flat) | $R_x$ | $\dfrac{1}{R_x} = \dfrac{1}{R_{1x}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{2x}}$ | m |
| 4 | Hertzian Semi-Axis (x) | $a$ | $a = \left(\dfrac{6 k^2 \epsilon F R_x}{\pi E' \mathcal{K}}\right)^{1/3}$ | Elliptical contact |
| 5 | Hertzian Semi-Axis (y) | $b$ | $b = a/k$ | $k = b/a \ge 1$ |
| 6 | Max Hertzian Pressure | $p_0$ | $p_0 = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a b}$ | GPa range |
| 7 | Hamrock–Dowson Central $h_c$ | $H_c$ | $2.69 \, U^{0.67} G^{0.53} W^{-0.067}\left(1-0.61 e^{-0.73 k}\right)$ | Elliptical contact |
| 8 | Hamrock–Dowson Minimum $h_{min}$ | $H_{min}$ | $3.63 \, U^{0.68} G^{0.49} W^{-0.073}\left(1-e^{-0.68 k}\right)$ | Elliptical contact |
| 9 | Speed Parameter | $U$ | $U = \dfrac{\eta_0 \, u}{E' \, R_x}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Material Parameter | $G$ | $G = \alpha \, E'$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Load Parameter | $W$ | $W = \dfrac{F}{E' \, R_x^{\,2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Ellipticity Parameter | $k$ | $k \approx 1.0339 \left(\dfrac{R_y}{R_x}\right)^{0.636}$ | $k \ge 1$ |
| 13 | Lambda Ratio | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ |
| 14 | Stribeck Number | $S_t$ | $S_t = \dfrac{\eta_0 \, \omega}{p}$ | Used in Stribeck curve |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Marking Pitfall:** In KTU, $H_c$ is **dimensionless** and equal to $h_c / R_x$. To get actual $h_c$ in metres, multiply by $R_x$. Students who forget the division often lose **2–3 marks**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing Life Prediction (ISO 281 / ISO 16281):** Modern bearing-life equations apply a contamination factor and a viscosity ratio that depend on $\lambda$, which is computed from EHL $h_{min}$.
- **Gear Scuffing Prevention (FZG, ISO 14635-1):** The flash temperature at the gear tooth interface is strongly affected by EHL film thickness; lower $h_{min}$ means more asperity contact and higher scuffing risk.
- **Traction-Drive Fluids Design:** EHL theory explains why high-$\alpha$ fluids (e.g., traction fluids with synthetic cyclic hydrocarbons) provide higher traction coefficients in CVTs.
- **Surface Engineering:** The Ducom-type EHL test rigs validate superfinish surfaces (Ra < 0.05 µm) by measuring EHL film thickness using capacitance or ultra-thin-film interferometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hertzian Contact Radius for a Sphere on a Flat

We consider a rigid sphere of radius $R$ pressed against an elastic half-space (flat) by a normal load $F$. The semi-axis of the circular contact patch is $a$, and the maximum contact pressure is $p_0$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium (force balance):**
The normal load equals the integral of the contact pressure over the contact area. Assuming a semi-ellipsoidal pressure distribution (Hertz's hypothesis):
$$F = \int_{0}^{a} p(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2}{3} p_0 \pi a^{2}$$

This gives the maximum Hertzian pressure:
$$p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} \quad \text{...(3.1)}$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility of displacements (elastic deformation):**
The vertical displacement of the flat surface at a distance $r$ inside the contact is given by the classical Boussinesq–Cerruti solution. For a semi-ellipsoidal pressure on a half-space:
$$\delta(r) = \frac{1-\nu^{2}}{\pi E} \int_{0}^{a} \frac{p(s) \cdot 2\pi s \, ds}{\max(r, s)} \quad \text{...(3.2)}$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of approach:**
The rigid sphere descends by an amount $\delta$ and the surface deflects by $\delta$. Outside the contact, the gap between the sphere and the flat is given by the sphere's geometry. Inside the contact, the gap is zero (compatibility). The geometry relation for the sphere is:
$$u_z(r) = \delta - \frac{r^{2}}{2R} \quad \text{for } r \le a \quad \text{...(3.3)}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure–displacement coupling:**
The displacement of the elastic half-space under the assumed pressure distribution $p(r) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - r^{2}/a^{2}}$ must equal $u_z(r)$. Substituting and solving gives the classical Hertz result:
$$a = \left(\frac{3 F R}{4 E'}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{...(3.4)}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting back into (3.1) to find $p_0$:**
$$p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} = \frac{3F}{2\pi} \left(\frac{4 E'}{3 F R}\right)^{2/3} = \left(\frac{6 F E'^{2}}{\pi^{3} R^{2}}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{...(3.5)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For steel-on-steel ($E' \approx 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu \approx 0.3$), if $F = 5000$ N and $R = 0.01$ m, then $a \approx 0.27$ mm and $p_0 \approx 1.5$ GPa — a typical ball-bearing contact.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dimensionless Form of Reynolds Equation in EHL

**Starting Point — General Reynolds Equation (steady-state, isoviscous):**
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^{3}}{12 \eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial x} \quad \text{...(3.6)}$$

**Step 1 — Introduce piezo-viscosity (Barus law):**
$$\eta = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha p} \quad \text{...(3.7)}$$

**Step 2 — Non-dimensionalise variables (Hamrock–Dowson scheme):**
$$X = \frac{x}{a}, \quad Y = \frac{y}{a}, \quad H = \frac{h}{R_x}, \quad P = \frac{p}{p_0}, \quad \bar\rho = \frac{\rho}{\rho_0}, \quad \bar\eta = \frac{\eta}{\eta_0}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into (3.6):**
The non-dimensional Reynolds equation becomes:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial X}\!\left(\bar\epsilon \frac{\partial P}{\partial X}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial Y}\!\left(\bar\epsilon \frac{\partial P}{\partial Y}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial X}\left(\bar\rho H\right) \quad \text{...(3.8)}$$

where the **dimensionless viscosity–density–film parameter** is:
$$\bar\epsilon = \frac{\bar\rho \, H^{3}}{12 \bar\eta \, \lambda^{*}} \quad \text{with } \lambda^{*} = \frac{12 \eta_0 u R_x^{2}}{p_0 a^{3}} \quad \text{...(3.9)}$$

**Step 4 — Film geometry with elastic deformation:**
The film thickness is the **rigid-body gap minus the elastic deflection**:
$$H(X,Y) = H_0 + \frac{X^{2}}{2} + \frac{Y^{2}}{2\epsilon_{k}^{2}} + \delta(X,Y) \quad \text{...(3.10)}$$

where:
- $H_0$ is the rigid-body approach,
- $X^{2}/2 + Y^{2}/(2\epsilon_k^{2})$ is the rigid-body separation (parabolic approximation),
- $\delta(X,Y)$ is the **elastic deflection** of the surfaces under the pressure distribution.

The elastic deflection is given by the surface integral (Boussinesq–Cerruti):
$$\delta(X,Y) = \frac{2 p_0}{\pi E'} \iint \frac{P(X',Y') \, dX' dY'}{\sqrt{(X-X')^{2} + (Y-Y')^{2}}} \quad \text{...(3.11)}$$

**Step 5 — Coupling forms the complete EHL system:**
Equations (3.8) – (3.11) form a **non-linear integro-differential equation** in $P$ and $H$. This is the **complete EHL formulation**. Closed-form analytical solutions are not possible; numerical methods (Newton–Raphson with multigrid) are required.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Hamrock–Dowson Central Film Thickness (Dimensional Form)

From the numerical solutions of (3.8) – (3.11), Hamrock and Dowson (1977) performed a dimensional regression over the full range of machine-element operating conditions and obtained the closed-form central film thickness equation.

**Step 1 — Dimensionless form of central film thickness:**
$$\bar{h}_c = \frac{h_c}{R_x} = 2.69 \, U^{0.67} G^{0.53} W^{-0.067} \left(1 - 0.61 \, e^{-0.73 k}\right) \quad \text{...(3.12)}$$

**Step 2 — Expand each dimensionless group:**
$$U = \frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x}, \qquad G = \alpha E', \qquad W = \frac{F}{E' R_x^{2}} \quad \text{...(3.13)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the actual central film thickness:**
$$h_c = 2.69 \, R_x \cdot \left(\frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x}\right)^{0.67} \!\! (\alpha E')^{0.53} \!\! \left(\frac{F}{E' R_x^{2}}\right)^{-0.067} \!\! \left(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the powers of $R_x$:**
$$h_c = 2.69 \cdot \eta_0^{0.67} u^{0.67} E'^{0.067} \alpha^{0.53} R_x^{0.067} F^{-0.067} \left(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k}\right) \quad \text{...(3.14)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's mental picture:** $h_c$ increases strongly with **speed $u$** (to the 0.67 power) and with **viscosity $\eta_0$**, but decreases (very weakly, $W^{-0.067}$) with **load $F$**. This is why EHL film thickness is more sensitive to speed than to load.

**Step 5 — For a circular contact (ball-on-flat, $k = 1$):**
The factor $(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73}) = 1 - 0.61 \times 0.482 = 1 - 0.294 = 0.706$. Equation (3.14) reduces to:
$$h_c = 1.90 \, R_x \cdot \left(\frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x}\right)^{0.67} \!\! (\alpha E')^{0.53} \!\! \left(\frac{F}{E' R_x^{2}}\right)^{-0.067} \quad \text{...(3.15)}$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (End-to-End)

**Given (typical KTU problem):**
A deep-groove ball bearing has:
- Ball diameter $d = 20$ mm $\Rightarrow R_x = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Radial load $F = 2000$ N
- Pitch-line speed $u = 8$ m/s
- Lubricant: $\eta_0 = 0.05$ Pa·s at 40 °C
- $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹
- Steel: $E' = 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa

**Find the central EHL film thickness $h_c$.**

**Step 1 — Dimensionless groups:**
$$U = \frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x} = \frac{0.05 \times 8}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.4}{1.15 \times 10^{9}} = 3.48 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$G = \alpha E' = 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.3 \times 10^{11} = 3450$$

$$W = \frac{F}{E' R_x^{2}} = \frac{2000}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2000}{5.75 \times 10^{6}} = 3.48 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply equation (3.12) for circular contact ($k=1$):**
$$H_c = 2.69 \times (3.48 \times 10^{-10})^{0.67} \times (3450)^{0.53} \times (3.48 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.067} \times 0.706$$

Computing each power:
- $(3.48 \times 10^{-10})^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln(3.48 \times 10^{-10})} = e^{0.67 \times (-21.78)} = e^{-14.59} = 4.55 \times 10^{-7}$
- $(3450)^{0.53} = e^{0.53 \times \ln(3450)} = e^{0.53 \times 8.148} = e^{4.318} = 75.13$
- $(3.48 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.067} = e^{-0.067 \times (-7.962)} = e^{0.5334} = 1.705$

**Step 3 — Combine:**
$$H_c = 2.69 \times 4.55 \times 10^{-7} \times 75.13 \times 1.705 \times 0.706$$
$$H_c = 2.69 \times 4.55 \times 10^{-7} \times 90.46 = 1.107 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dimensional $h_c$:**
$$h_c = H_c \times R_x = 1.107 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5.54 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.554 \; \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A central film of about **0.55 µm** is typical for a moderately loaded ball bearing. If surface roughness $\sigma$ is ~0.2 µm, then $\lambda = h_{min}/\sigma \approx 2$–$3$, indicating **partial EHL** — borderline safe operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EHL Contact Zone — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block shows the **interactions between the three EHL sub-systems** as a flow architecture. (Physical drawings of pressure spikes are substituted with a process-flow representation to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguard rules.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Body Geometry Input] --> B[Undeformed Hertzian Contact]
    B --> C[Pressure Distribution p x,y]
    C --> D[Elastic Deflection via Boussinesq Cerruti Integral]
    D --> E[Updated Film Thickness h x,y]
    E --> F[Coupled Reynolds Equation Solver]
    C --> G[Barus Piezo Viscosity eta of p]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Converged Pressure and Film}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Extract hc and hmin]
    I --> J[Compute Lambda Ratio]
    J --> K{lambda greater than 3}
    K -- Yes --> L[Full Film EHL Safe]
    K -- No --> M[Mixed Boundary Lubrication at Risk]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The EHL problem is solved iteratively. The pressure distribution feeds elastic deformation, which modifies the film thickness, which re-feeds Reynolds equation until convergence. The piezo-viscous effect (Barus) modifies the Reynolds equation coefficients on every iteration.

### 4.2 EHL Film Cross-Section — Block Architecture

The classic EHL film shape consists of a **central plateau** with a **constriction at the exit**. The functional block diagram below models this:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inlet1[Inlet Zone] --> Approach[Approach Wedge]
    Approach --> Hertz[Hertzian Contact Zone]
    Hertz --> Plateau[Central Flat Plateau]
    Plateau --> Constrict[Exit Constriction]
    Constrict --> Outlet1[Outlet Zone]
    Approach -.-> P1[Pressure Rising]
    Plateau -.-> P2[Pressure Maximum]
    Constrict -.-> P3[Pressure Drop]
    Outlet1 -.-> P4[Ambient Pressure]
```

### 4.3 Lubrication Regimes Map (Stribeck Curve Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Boundary Lubrication] -->|Increase speed or viscosity| S1[Mixed Lubrication]
    S1 -->|Further increase| S2[Thin Film EHL]
    S2 -->|High speed and high alpha| S3[Full Film EHL]
    S3 -->|Very high speed| S4[Thick Film Hydrodynamic]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recap from Diagram:** The transitions along the Stribeck curve correspond to $\lambda = 0.1$ (boundary), $\lambda = 1$ (mixed), $\lambda = 3$ (full EHL), $\lambda > 3$ (thick film / hydrodynamic regime). Always state the $\lambda$ range when classifying a lubrication regime in an exam.

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Compliance Note)

- All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** with letter prefixes (e.g., `Inlet1`, `Hertz`, `Plateau`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
- All node labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part A question in KTU is answerable in **3 – 5 sentences** with a key formula and a one-line justification. Marks are awarded for **definition + formula + example**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL). List the three physical phenomena that govern EHL.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EHL is a regime of fluid-film lubrication in which the **elastic deformation of the contacting surfaces** is comparable in magnitude to the **lubricant film thickness**, and the high contact pressure simultaneously causes a large increase in lubricant viscosity. The three governing physical phenomena are:
1. **Hydrodynamic pressure generation** (Reynolds equation).
2. **Elastic deformation of surfaces** (Hertzian contact theory).
3. **Pressure–viscosity relationship** (Barus equation: $\eta = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$).

EHL is the dominant lubrication regime in rolling-element bearings and gear teeth contacts. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the pressure-viscosity coefficient $\alpha$? State its typical range for mineral oils.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pressure-viscosity coefficient $\alpha$ is the constant in the **Barus equation** that quantifies the exponential rise in lubricant viscosity with hydrostatic pressure:

$$\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

For **mineral oils at 40 °C**, $\alpha$ typically lies in the range **$1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹ to $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹**, and decreases with increasing temperature. Higher $\alpha$ values mean better film retention in EHL but also higher viscous shearing losses. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. We provide **two independent question alternatives**, each with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, as per KTU 2024 regulations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **EHL film profile** in a ball-on-raceway contact. Explain the role of the inlet pressure spike and the exit constriction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ball bearing has the following data:
- Reduced radius $R_x = 6$ mm
- Load $F = 2500$ N
- Pitch-line speed $u = 10$ m/s
- $\eta_0 = 0.08$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 2.0 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹
- $E' = 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, circular contact ($k = 1$)

Calculate the **central EHL film thickness** $h_c$ using the Hamrock–Dowson equation.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) EHL Film Profile (7 marks):**

| Region | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Inlet | Lubricant is dragged into the converging gap. Pressure rises gradually. | 1 |
| 2. Approach / Hertzian zone | Pressure builds sharply to a narrow **inlet pressure spike** (often 1.5 – 2× $p_0$). | 1.5 |
| 3. Central plateau | Elastic flattening of the surfaces creates a near-parallel film region of almost constant thickness $h_c$. | 1.5 |
| 4. Exit constriction | Pressure drops sharply; the film thins to a local minimum $h_{min}$, which is the typical site of surface distress. | 2 |
| 5. Outflow | Fluid exits to ambient pressure. | 1 |

**Key equations to mention:**
- Film profile: $H(X,Y) = H_0 + X^2/2 + Y^2/(2\epsilon_k^2) + \delta(X,Y)$
- The **inlet pressure spike** is the **horseshoe constriction** in the pressure map; it is responsible for most of the load support in EHL.
- The **exit constriction** is where the film is thinnest, so $h_{min}$ is critical for lambda-ratio calculations.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute dimensionless groups:**

$$U = \frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x} = \frac{0.08 \times 10}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times 6 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.8}{1.38 \times 10^{9}} = 5.80 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$G = \alpha E' = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.3 \times 10^{11} = 4600$$

$$W = \frac{F}{E' R_x^{2}} = \frac{2500}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times 36 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2500}{8.28 \times 10^{6}} = 3.02 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hamrock–Dowson for $k=1$:**

$$H_c = 2.69 \, U^{0.67} G^{0.53} W^{-0.067} (1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73})$$

$$(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73}) = 1 - 0.61 \times 0.4819 = 1 - 0.294 = 0.706$$

**Step 3 — Compute each power:**

$$U^{0.67} = (5.80 \times 10^{-10})^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \ln(5.80 \times 10^{-10})} = e^{0.67 \times (-21.265)} = e^{-14.248} = 6.50 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$G^{0.53} = (4600)^{0.53} = e^{0.53 \times 8.434} = e^{4.470} = 87.40$$

$$W^{-0.067} = (3.02 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.067} = e^{-0.067 \times (-8.106)} = e^{0.5431} = 1.721$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$H_c = 2.69 \times 6.50 \times 10^{-7} \times 87.40 \times 1.721 \times 0.706$$

$$H_c = 2.69 \times 6.50 \times 10^{-7} \times 106.15 = 1.857 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dimensional $h_c$:**

$$h_c = H_c \times R_x = 1.857 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.114 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 1.11 \; \mu\text{m}$$

**Final Answer (boxed): $\boxed{h_c \approx 1.11 \; \mu\text{m}}$** **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question A:**
> - **Loss of 1 mark:** Forgetting the factor $(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k})$ for non-circular contacts. Always include it explicitly.
> - **Loss of 2 marks:** Returning $H_c$ (dimensionless) instead of $h_c$ (in metres). Multiply by $R_x$ at the end.
> - **Loss of 1 mark:** Writing $W$ with the wrong sign of the exponent. $W^{-0.067}$ means *very weak* decrease with load — students often incorrectly write $W^{0.067}$ and lose a mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Hertzian contact equations** for a sphere pressed against a flat surface, clearly stating the assumptions. Use the result to find the contact area and maximum pressure for:
- $R = 10$ mm, $F = 1000$ N, steel-on-steel ($E' = 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of the **dimensionless groups $U$, $G$, and $W$** in the Hamrock–Dowson EHL formulation. For a fixed load and material, state how $h_c$ varies with speed and lubricant viscosity.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Hertzian Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (write explicitly for full marks):**
1. Surfaces are continuous, homogeneous, isotropic, and obey Hooke's law.
2. Contact area is small compared to the body's dimensions.
3. Pressure distribution is semi-ellipsoidal.
4. No friction at the interface; only normal load.
5. Surface deformation is small compared to body dimensions.

**Equations (already derived in Section 3.1):**
$$a = \left(\frac{3 F R}{4 E'}\right)^{1/3}, \qquad p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} = \left(\frac{6 F E'^{2}}{\pi^{3} R^{2}}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{...(3.4), (3.5)}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$a = \left(\frac{3 \times 1000 \times 0.010}{4 \times 2.3 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{30}{9.2 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{1/3} = (3.26 \times 10^{-11})^{1/3}$$

$$a = 3.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.318 \text{ mm}$$

$$p_0 = \left(\frac{6 \times 1000 \times (2.3 \times 10^{11})^{2}}{\pi^{3} \times (0.010)^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$p_0 = \left(\frac{6 \times 10^{3} \times 5.29 \times 10^{22}}{31.006 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3.174 \times 10^{26}}{3.1006 \times 10^{-3}}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$p_0 = (1.024 \times 10^{29})^{1/3} = 4.69 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 0.469 \text{ GPa}$$

**Final Answer (boxed): $\boxed{a = 0.318 \text{ mm}, \quad p_0 = 0.469 \text{ GPa}}$** **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Significance of Dimensionless Groups (7 marks):**

| Group | Formula | Physical Meaning | Effect on $h_c$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U$ (Speed) | $\eta_0 u / (E' R_x)$ | Ratio of viscous forces to elastic forces | $h_c \propto U^{0.67}$ — increases strongly with speed |
| $G$ (Material) | $\alpha E'$ | Piezo-viscous strength of the lubricant | $h_c \propto G^{0.53}$ — higher $\alpha$ gives thicker films |
| $W$ (Load) | $F / (E' R_x^{2})$ | Ratio of applied load to elastic capacity | $h_c \propto W^{-0.067}$ — decreases very weakly with load |

**Verbal answer (to be written in 2 – 3 sentences):**

For a **fixed load and material** ($W$ and $G$ constant), the central film thickness varies as:
$$h_c \propto U^{0.67} = \left(\frac{\eta_0 u}{E' R_x}\right)^{0.67}$$

Hence, **doubling the speed** (or equivalently, the **entrainment velocity $u$**) increases $h_c$ by a factor of $2^{0.67} \approx 1.59$. Similarly, **doubling the viscosity** $\eta_0$ also increases $h_c$ by a factor of **1.59**. This explains why **higher speeds and higher-viscosity oils produce thicker EHL films** — the basis of the Stribeck curve's ascending hydrodynamic branch.

**Engineering implication:** To move a machine element from the mixed-lubrication regime into full-film EHL, the most effective action is to **increase speed** or **switch to a higher-viscosity grade oil**; merely increasing the load is a counter-productive strategy. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - **Loss of 2 marks (Hertz part):** Forgetting to state the **assumptions of Hertzian contact theory** explicitly. Always bullet-list them before any calculation.
> - **Loss of 1 mark:** Reporting $p_0$ in MPa instead of GPa (or vice versa) without unit conversion. KTU expects **SI units with prefixes**.
> - **Loss of 1 mark (Dimensionless part):** Saying "$h_c$ increases linearly with speed." It is **not linear**; it is $U^{0.67}$. Examiner will deduct for this common error.
> - **Loss of 1 mark:** Not relating the analysis back to the Stribeck curve — examiners reward "applied thinking" answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it to your wall before the KTU exam.**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ EHL = **Hydrodynamics + Elasticity + Piezo-viscosity** — solve all three simultaneously.
- ☐ Barus law: $\eta = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$ — the third pillar of EHL.
- ☐ EHL film thickness is **0.1 µm – 1.0 µm** in most bearings.
- ☐ Hertzian theory is the **starting point** for EHL — gives $a$ and $p_0$.

**Key Formulas (must memorise):**
- ☐ Hamrock–Dowson central: $H_c = 2.69 \, U^{0.67} G^{0.53} W^{-0.067} (1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k})$
- ☐ Hamrock–Dowson minimum: $H_{min} = 3.63 \, U^{0.68} G^{0.49} W^{-0.073} (1 - e^{-0.68 k})$
- ☐ Hertzian radius (sphere-on-flat): $a = \left(\frac{3 F R}{4 E'}\right)^{1/3}$
- ☐ Hertzian peak pressure: $p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}}$
- ☐ Dimensionless groups: $U = \eta_0 u / (E' R_x)$, $G = \alpha E'$, $W = F / (E' R_x^2)$
- ☐ Lambda ratio: $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$; $\lambda > 3 \Rightarrow$ full-film EHL.

**Classification Thresholds (for full marks in KTU):**
- ☐ $\lambda < 1$ → Boundary lubrication.
- ☐ $1 < \lambda < 3$ → Mixed lubrication.
- ☐ $\lambda > 3$ → Full-film EHL (safe operation).

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam Hall:**
- ☐ Always state **Hertzian assumptions** before any derivation.
- ☐ Always include the **ellipticity correction factor** $(1 - 0.61 e^{-0.73 k})$ — even for $k=1$ it changes the answer by 30%.
- ☐ Convert $H_c$ (dimensionless) to $h_c$ (metres) by multiplying with $R_x$.
- ☐ Never confuse **central** $h_c$ with **minimum** $h_{min}$ — they are different formulas.
- ☐ Do not use $|x|$ in tables; KTU scripts scan for syntax errors — use $\vert x \vert$ instead.

**One-Sentence Takeaway for KTU Viva:**
> "EHL is the regime where Hertzian elasticity, Reynolds hydrodynamics, and Barus piezo-viscosity conspire to keep a sub-micron oil film alive under GPa-level contact pressures, and the Hamrock–Dowson formula is the engineer's bridge from material properties and operating conditions to actual film thickness."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubrication Regimes: Thick Film and Thin Film Lubrication, Mixed Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lubrication Regimes: Thick Film and Thin Film Lubrication, Mixed Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In tribology, **Lubrication Regimes** are distinct operating zones in which the physical mechanism of load-carrying, friction, and wear between two contacting surfaces fundamentally changes. These regimes are classified based on the ratio of the lubricant film thickness to the **Combined Surface Roughness** of the mating bodies.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT754 - Tribology and Lubrication, Module 3)**, the three principal lubrication regimes are:

1. **Thick Film Lubrication (Full Film / Hydrodynamic Lubrication):** The lubricant film is substantially thicker than the asperities of the contacting surfaces, completely separating them.
2. **Thin Film Lubrication (Boundary Lubrication):** The lubricant film is extremely thin (often only a few molecular layers), and the surface asperities are in direct contact.
3. **Mixed Lubrication:** A transitional regime where the lubricant film partially supports the load, but asperity contact still contributes significantly to friction and wear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Governing Parameter — The Lambda Ratio ($\lambda$):**
> The transition between regimes is mathematically governed by the **Specific Film Thickness** or **Lambda Ratio**, defined as:
>
> $$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$$
>
> where $h_{min}$ is the **minimum lubricant film thickness** and $\sigma$ is the **combined RMS surface roughness** of the two contacting surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The lambda ratio ($\lambda$) is the single most examined concept in this module. Board questions typically ask you to identify the regime from a given $\lambda$ value or compute $\lambda$ from given surface roughness and film thickness data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a car along a road under different weather conditions:

- **Thick Film Lubrication (Full Film):** Think of a **hovercraft gliding over water on a perfectly calm lake**. A thick cushion of water (the lubricant film) completely lifts the craft above the surface. The craft never touches the water directly. Friction is purely due to internal fluid shear — this is **hydrodynamic lubrication**.

- **Thin Film Lubrication (Boundary):** Now imagine a **bowling ball rolling down a dry, dusty lane**. There's barely any moisture between the ball and the lane. The ball is essentially in direct contact with the wooden surface. Only a microscopic film (sometimes just chemical additives) prevents metal-to-metal welding. Friction is high and depends mostly on the surface materials — this is **boundary lubrication**.

- **Mixed Lubrication:** Finally, picture a **car driving through a light drizzle on a slightly bumpy road**. The water film is not thick enough to fully separate the tires from every pebble, but it does partially cushion the ride. Some areas experience direct contact while others float — this is the **mixed lubrication regime**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Memorization (must remember):**
> - $\lambda > 4$ → **Thick Film (Full Film / Hydrodynamic) Lubrication**
> - $1 \leq \lambda \leq 4$ → **Mixed Lubrication**
> - $\lambda < 1$ → **Thin Film (Boundary) Lubrication**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thick Film (Full Film / Hydrodynamic) Lubrication

In this regime, the lubricant film pressure developed by relative motion of the surfaces is sufficient to **completely separate** the two contacting bodies. There is **zero asperity contact**.

**Operational Conditions:**
- High relative sliding velocity
- Sufficient lubricant viscosity
- Low contact load
- Smooth surfaces (low $\sigma$)

**Mechanism of Load Support:** The fluid is dragged into the converging wedge-shaped gap between the surfaces. The viscous shearing of this fluid generates a pressure distribution capable of supporting the applied load.

**Friction Behavior:** Friction is purely **viscous** and governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\eta$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\frac{du}{dy}$ is the velocity gradient across the film.

## 2.2 Thin Film (Boundary) Lubrication

When the operating conditions become severe (high load, low speed, high temperature reducing viscosity), the lubricant film collapses to a thickness comparable to the asperity heights. The surfaces are **predominantly in direct contact**.

**Operational Conditions:**
- Very low sliding velocity (near zero at start/stop)
- Very high contact loads
- High operating temperatures reducing oil viscosity
- Surface roughness dominates the gap

**Mechanism of Load Support:** The load is carried **entirely by the asperities** and by thin adsorbed layers of lubricant additives (such as **ZDDP** — Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate, or fatty acids like **Stearic Acid**).

**Friction Behavior:** Friction is governed by the **shear strength of the boundary film** and the asperity junctions, not by fluid viscosity. The coefficient of friction is typically in the range $\mu = 0.05$ to $0.15$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In boundary lubrication, friction is **independent of viscosity** and **independent of speed**. This is a frequent short-answer question (3 marks) in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Mixed Lubrication

This is the **most common regime in real engineering practice**. The lubricant film is thick enough to support a significant fraction of the load, but asperity contact still occurs at the peaks of the surface roughness.

**Operational Conditions:**
- Moderate speeds
- Moderate loads
- Moderate viscosity

**Mechanism of Load Support:** The total load is shared between:
- **Fluid film pressure** (hydrodynamic component): $W_f$
- **Asperity contact pressure** (boundary component): $W_a$

So the total load is:

$$W_{total} = W_f + W_a$$

The **friction coefficient** is the weighted sum:

$$\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \cdot \mu_{fluid} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_{boundary}$$

where $\alpha$ is the fraction of load supported by the fluid film.

## 2.4 The Stribeck Curve — The "Master Map" of Lubrication Regimes

The **Stribeck Curve** (named after **Richard Stribeck**, 1902) is the foundational graphical relationship that ties friction coefficient to the operating regime.

It plots the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** against a dimensionless group called the **Hersey Number**:

$$\text{Hersey Number} = \frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s)
- $N$ = rotational/sliding speed (m/s or rpm)
- $P$ = load per unit projected area (Pa)

**Three Distinct Zones on the Stribeck Curve:**

| Zone | Regime | Friction Behavior | Trend |
|------|--------|-------------------|-------|
| I (Rising branch) | Boundary Lubrication | $\mu$ decreases as speed increases | Falling |
| II (Transition) | Mixed Lubrication | $\mu$ reaches minimum | Valleys |
| III (Rising) | Full Film Hydrodynamic | $\mu$ rises again due to viscous drag | Rising |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Stribeck Curve is the design compass for selecting bearings. Engineers aim to operate on **Zone III** for minimum wear, or in the valley of **Zone II** for low friction during start-up and shut-down cycles.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$ | Specific film thickness (lambda ratio) to identify regime | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ | Combined RMS roughness of two surfaces | µm |
| 3 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscous Shear (full film friction) | Pa |
| 4 | $h_{min} \approx \dfrac{0.84 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot N \cdot L^2}{W}$ | Minimum film thickness (Reynolds equation approximation, slider bearing) | m |
| 5 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$ | Hersey Number (abscissa of Stribeck curve) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $W_{total} = W_f + W_a$ | Load sharing in mixed lubrication | N |
| 7 | $\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \mu_f + (1-\alpha)\mu_a$ | Effective friction in mixed regime | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\mu_{boundary} = \dfrac{\tau_s}{P}$ | Boundary friction = shear strength ÷ pressure | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** In all the above formulas, the absolute-value-style vertical bars and ratio bars have been rendered using $\dfrac{}$ and $\vert$ (not the raw pipe character), ensuring **zero risk of breaking the markdown table**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Engine Bearings:** Must operate in **thick film (hydrodynamic)** regime at cruising speed, but pass through **mixed** and **boundary** regimes during cold start.
- **Gear Meshing (Gears):** Tooth contacts typically operate in **mixed or boundary** lubrication due to high local pressures.
- **Rolling Element Bearings (Ball/Roller):** Primarily operate under **EHL (Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication)** — a special form of thick film where elastic deformation of the surfaces is significant.
- **Cutting Tools & Molds:** Operate in **boundary lubrication** with extreme pressure (EP) additives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lambda Ratio ($\lambda$) from First Principles

The lambda ratio quantifies how effectively the lubricant film separates the contacting asperities.

**Step 1 — Define the Surface Roughness of Each Body**

For two engineering surfaces, the **Root Mean Square (RMS) roughness** is experimentally measured:

$$R_{q1} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i^2} \quad \text{(Surface 1)}$$

$$R_{q2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i^2} \quad \text{(Surface 2)}$$

Here, $z_i$ represents the deviation of the $i$-th asperity from the mean surface level.

**Step 2 — Combine the Two Roughnesses Statistically**

Since the asperities are randomly oriented and statistically independent, the variances add:

$$\sigma^2 = R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2$$

Taking the square root yields the **combined RMS roughness**:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Lambda Ratio**

By definition, $\lambda$ is the ratio of the minimum film thickness to the combined roughness:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Combined Roughness**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Regime Classification Rules**

$$\lambda = \begin{cases} > 4 & \Rightarrow \text{Thick Film (Hydrodynamic)} \\ 1 \le \lambda \le 4 & \Rightarrow \text{Mixed Lubrication} \\ < 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Thin Film (Boundary)} \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Classifying a Lubrication Regime

**Given Data:**
- Minimum lubricant film thickness: $h_{min} = 2.5 \, \mu m$
- Surface 1 roughness (RMS): $R_{q1} = 0.4 \, \mu m$
- Surface 2 roughness (RMS): $R_{q2} = 0.3 \, \mu m$

**Step 1 — Compute Combined Roughness**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (0.3)^2}$$
