$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.09}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.25}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 0.5 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lambda Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{2.5 \, \mu m}{0.5 \, \mu m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 5}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Regime**

Since $\lambda = 5 > 4$, the contact operates in the **Thick Film (Hydrodynamic) Lubrication** regime.

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

The film is **5 times thicker** than the combined surface roughness, meaning the asperities are completely submerged in the lubricant and do not touch. Friction is purely viscous. No wear occurs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Regime Comparison

| Parameter | Thick Film (Hydrodynamic) | Mixed | Thin Film (Boundary) |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------|----------------------|
| $\lambda$ Range | $> 4$ | $1$ to $4$ | $< 1$ |
| Load Support | Fluid pressure ($W_f$) | Fluid + Asperities ($W_f + W_a$) | Asperities only ($W_a$) |
| Friction Source | Viscous shear of oil | Both viscous + boundary | Boundary film shear |
| Wear | Negligible | Moderate | Severe (adhesive) |
| Viscosity Dependence | High | Moderate | Negligible |
| Speed Dependence | $\mu$ rises with speed | Transitional | Independent |
| Coefficient of Friction | $0.001$ – $0.01$ | $0.01$ – $0.05$ | $0.05$ – $0.15$ |
| Stribeck Zone | Right rising branch | Valley | Left rising branch |

## 3.4 Derivation of Load Sharing in Mixed Lubrication

**Step 1 — Define the Total Applied Load**

$$W_{total} = W_f + W_a$$

where $W_f$ is the load carried by the fluid film and $W_a$ is the load carried by asperity contacts.

**Step 2 — Define the Load Fraction Parameter**

$$\alpha = \frac{W_f}{W_{total}}$$

So $W_f = \alpha \cdot W_{total}$ and $W_a = (1 - \alpha) \cdot W_{total}$.

**Step 3 — Express the Effective Friction Coefficient**

By Amontons' law and the viscous friction model applied in parallel:

$$\mu_{mixed} = \frac{F_{total}}{W_{total}} = \frac{F_f + F_a}{W_f + W_a}$$

$$F_f = \mu_f \cdot W_f \quad \text{and} \quad F_a = \mu_a \cdot W_a$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_{mixed} = \frac{\mu_f \cdot W_f + \mu_a \cdot W_a}{W_{total}}$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = \mu_f \cdot \alpha + \mu_a \cdot (1 - \alpha)$$

This is the **fundamental mixed-lubrication friction equation** tested in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Mixed Lubrication Friction

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 0.6$ (60% of load carried by fluid film)
- $\mu_f = 0.005$ (pure hydrodynamic friction)
- $\mu_a = 0.10$ (boundary friction)

**Step 1 — Apply the Mixed Friction Equation**

$$\mu_{mixed} = \mu_f \cdot \alpha + \mu_a \cdot (1 - \alpha)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = (0.005)(0.6) + (0.10)(1 - 0.6)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.003 + (0.10)(0.4)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.003 + 0.04$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} = 0.043}$$

This result lies perfectly in the typical mixed-lubrication range ($0.01$ to $0.05$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lubrication Regime Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lubricated Sliding Contact] --> B[Compute Lambda Ratio lambda = h_min divided by sigma]
    B --> C{Lambda Decision}
    C -->|lambda greater than 4| D[Thick Film Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
    C -->|lambda between 1 and 4| E[Mixed Lubrication]
    C -->|lambda less than 1| F[Thin Film Boundary Lubrication]
    D --> D1[Load Support: Fluid Pressure Only]
    D --> D2[Friction: Viscous Shear]
    D --> D3[Wear: Negligible]
    E --> E1[Load Support: Fluid + Asperities]
    E --> E2[Friction: Combined Viscous + Boundary]
    E --> E3[Wear: Moderate]
    F --> F1[Load Support: Asperity Contacts Only]
    F --> F2[Friction: Boundary Film Shear]
    F --> F3[Wear: Severe Adhesive]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — The Stribeck Curve Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StribeckCurve["STRIBECK CURVE - Coefficient of Friction vs Hersey Number"]
        direction LR
        X[Low Hersey Number eta N / P] --> Zone1
        X --> Zone2
        X --> Zone3
        Zone1[ZONE I BOUNDARY LUBRICATION]:::zone1
        Zone2[ZONE II MIXED LUBRICATION MINIMUM FRICTION]:::zone2
        Zone3[ZONE III THICK FILM HYDRODYNAMIC]:::zone3
    end
    Zone1 -->|Increasing Speed and Viscosity| Zone2
    Zone2 -->|Further Increase| Zone3
    classDef zone1 fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zone2 fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#CCAA00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zone3 fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Load Sharing Architecture in Mixed Lubrication

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WTotal[Total Applied Load W_total] --> Splitter{Load Distribution}
    Splitter --> Wf[Fluid Film Load W_f = alpha times W_total]
    Splitter --> Wa[Asperity Load W_a = 1 minus alpha times W_total]
    Wf --> Pf[Fluid Pressure Distribution]
    Pf --> Ff[Fluid Friction F_f = mu_f times W_f]
    Wa --> Pa[Asperity Contact Pressure]
    Pa --> Fa[Boundary Friction F_a = mu_a times W_a]
    Ff --> SumF[Total Friction Force F_total]
    Fa --> SumF
    SumF --> Result[Effective Coefficient of Friction mu_mixed]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Asperity Contact Visualization

```
THICK FILM (lambda > 4)        MIXED (1 <= lambda <= 4)     THIN FILM (lambda < 1)
=========================       ========================      =====================
                                
  SURFACE B (moving)              SURFACE B (moving)           SURFACE B (moving)
  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~             ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~             ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~             ~~~X~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~X~X~X~X~~~~~~~~
                                  (partial asperity hit)       (asperity welding)
===========================================         
  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^         ^^^X^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^          ^^^X^X^X^X^^^^^^^^^^
  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  SURFACE A (stationary)         SURFACE A (stationary)        SURFACE A (stationary)

  Full fluid separation          Partial fluid + contact       Direct asperity contact
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Specific Film Thickness" or "Lambda Ratio" ($\lambda$) in lubrication. How is it used to classify the lubrication regime?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Specific Film Thickness** (also called the **Lambda Ratio**) is defined as the ratio of the minimum lubricant film thickness to the combined surface roughness of the contacting pair:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number used to classify lubrication regimes as follows:
- $\lambda > 4$ → **Thick Film (Full Film / Hydrodynamic) Lubrication**
- $1 \leq \lambda \leq 4$ → **Mixed Lubrication**
- $\lambda < 1$ → **Thin Film (Boundary) Lubrication**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why boundary lubrication is independent of viscosity and sliding speed.**

**Model Answer:**

In **boundary lubrication**, the lubricant film thickness is **smaller than the asperity heights**, so the surfaces are in **direct asperity contact**. The load is carried by the contact between asperities and the **boundary film** (chemically adsorbed layers of additives like fatty acids or ZDDP), not by the bulk fluid.

Since friction arises from the **shear strength of the boundary film** and the asperity junctions, and not from viscous shearing of the fluid, the coefficient of friction:

- **Does not depend on viscosity** $\eta$ (no fluid shearing dominates)
- **Does not depend on sliding speed** $N$ (the film is already at its minimum thickness and the contacts are essentially static junctions)

This is why boundary friction can be modeled as $\mu_b = \tau_s / P$, where $\tau_s$ is the shear strength of the boundary film and $P$ is the normal pressure.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the Stribeck Curve, explain the three lubrication regimes (boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic) and the physical mechanism of load support in each.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Two lubricated sliding surfaces have the following data: $h_{min} = 3.0 \, \mu m$, $R_{q1} = 0.5 \, \mu m$, $R_{q2} = 0.4 \, \mu m$. Calculate the lambda ratio and identify the lubrication regime.

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) Model Solution — Stribeck Curve Explanation

The **Stribeck Curve** plots the **coefficient of friction** $\mu$ against the **Hersey Number** $\eta N / P$ (viscosity × speed ÷ pressure). It exhibits three distinct regions:

**1. Zone I — Boundary Lubrication (Left Rising Branch):**
- Occurs at **low speeds**, **high loads**, or **low viscosity** (cold oil).
- Asperities are in direct contact; load is carried by asperity junctions and boundary films.
- As speed increases, friction **decreases** because the boundary film becomes better distributed.
- $\mu$ is **high** (typically $0.05$ to $0.15$).

**2. Zone II — Mixed Lubrication (Valley):**
- A **transitional regime** where the lubricant film is forming but is not yet thick enough to fully separate the surfaces.
- Load is **partially carried by the fluid film** and **partially by asperities**.
- Friction reaches a **minimum** in this region.
- $\mu$ ranges from $0.01$ to $0.05$.

**3. Zone III — Hydrodynamic / Thick Film Lubrication (Right Rising Branch):**
- Occurs at **high speeds**, **high viscosity**, or **low loads**.
- The fluid film **completely separates** the surfaces; no asperity contact.
- Load is carried entirely by the **fluid pressure** built up by viscous drag.
- Friction **increases with speed** due to higher viscous shearing.
- $\mu$ ranges from $0.001$ to $0.01$.

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) Model Solution — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1 — Combined RMS Roughness:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.4)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.41}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.640 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 2 — Lambda Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{3.0 \, \mu m}{0.640 \, \mu m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 4.69}$$

**Step 3 — Regime Identification:**

Since $\lambda = 4.69 > 4$, the contact operates in the **Thick Film (Full Film Hydrodynamic) Lubrication** regime.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The lubricant film is **4.69 times thicker** than the combined asperity height.
- **No asperity contact** occurs; load is fully supported by fluid pressure.
- **Wear is negligible**; only viscous friction exists.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the mechanism of load support and friction in **Mixed Lubrication**. Derive the expression for the effective coefficient of friction in this regime.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A journal bearing operates in the mixed lubrication regime. Given: $\alpha = 0.45$ (fraction of load carried by fluid film), $\mu_{fluid} = 0.004$, $\mu_{boundary} = 0.12$. Calculate the effective coefficient of friction and the percentage of load carried by asperity contacts.

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) Model Solution — Mixed Lubrication Mechanism

In **mixed lubrication**, the lubricant film is partially developed. The total applied load $W_{total}$ is shared between two parallel mechanisms:

**1. Fluid Film Mechanism (Hydrodynamic Component):**
- The lubricant film carries a fraction $\alpha$ of the load through hydrodynamic pressure.
- Friction force: $F_f = \mu_f \cdot W_f$

**2. Asperity Contact Mechanism (Boundary Component):**
- The peaks of surface roughness carry the remaining fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ of the load through direct asperity contact and boundary films.
- Friction force: $F_a = \mu_a \cdot W_a$

**Derivation of Effective Friction Coefficient:**

The total friction force is:

$$F_{total} = F_f + F_a = \mu_f \cdot W_f + \mu_a \cdot W_a$$

Substituting $W_f = \alpha \cdot W_{total}$ and $W_a = (1 - \alpha) \cdot W_{total}$:

$$F_{total} = \mu_f \cdot \alpha \cdot W_{total} + \mu_a \cdot (1 - \alpha) \cdot W_{total}$$

Dividing by $W_{total}$:

$$\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \cdot \mu_f + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_a$$

This is the **effective mixed-lubrication friction coefficient**.

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) Model Solution — Numerical Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute Asperity Load Fraction:**

$$\alpha = 0.45 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 - \alpha) = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$$

So **55% of the load** is carried by asperity contacts.

**Step 2 — Apply the Mixed Friction Equation:**

$$\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \cdot \mu_f + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_a$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = (0.45)(0.004) + (0.55)(0.12)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.0018 + 0.066$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} = 0.0678}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- The effective friction coefficient **0.0678** lies between the pure fluid friction ($0.004$) and pure boundary friction ($0.12$), consistent with mixed regime expectations.
- Since **55% of the load is carried by asperities**, there is **significant wear potential** — the bearing may need EP additives or improved surface finish to shift operation toward hydrodynamic regime.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Many students compute $\sigma$ in mm instead of µm, leading to lambda errors by a factor of 1000. Always keep roughness and film thickness in the **same units**.
> 2. **Confusing $R_a$ with $R_q$:** KTU specifically defines lambda using the **RMS roughness ($R_q$)**, NOT the **arithmetic average roughness ($R_a$)**. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Square Root:** A frequent error is writing $\sigma = R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2$ instead of $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$. This single mistake loses **1 full mark** in part (b).
> 4. **Mixing Hersey and Lambda:** Students often confuse the Hersey Number ($\eta N / P$) with the Lambda Ratio ($h / \sigma$). They are **two different dimensionless groups** with different physical meanings.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lambda Ratio** ($\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$) is the master classifier of lubrication regimes.
- [x] **Three regimes:** Boundary ($\lambda < 1$), Mixed ($1 \le \lambda \le 4$), Hydrodynamic ($\lambda > 4$).
- [x] **Combined roughness:** $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ — always use **RMS** ($R_q$), not $R_a$.
- [x] **Boundary lubrication:** Friction is **independent of viscosity** and **independent of speed**.
- [x] **Hydrodynamic lubrication:** Friction arises from **viscous shear** ($\tau = \eta \, du/dy$); load is fully supported by **fluid pressure**.
- [x] **Mixed lubrication:** Load is **shared** between fluid film and asperities; total load $W_{total} = W_f + W_a$.
- [x] **Mixed friction equation:** $\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \mu_f + (1 - \alpha) \mu_a$.
- [x] **Stribeck Curve:** Plots $\mu$ vs Hersey Number $\eta N / P$; shows three zones — boundary (high $\mu$), mixed (minimum), hydrodynamic (rising again).
- [x] **Hersey Number** is the abscissa of the Stribeck curve; **Lambda ratio** is the physical regime classifier — they are **different**.
- [x] **Real engineering:** Most machine components (gears, bearings, cams) operate in **mixed lubrication** during start-up and shut-down.
- [x] **Key additives for boundary regime:** ZDDP, fatty acids (e.g., stearic acid), MoS₂, graphite.
- [x] **Always state units** in numerical answers — examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage:** This topic is typically tested for **7–14 marks** per question paper in Part A and Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stribeck Curve and Its Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stribeck Curve and Its Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Stribeck Curve** is a fundamental tribological representation that graphically depicts the variation of the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** as a function of the dimensionless **Hersey Number** (also called the **lubrication parameter**), which is expressed as $\eta N / P$. The curve was first introduced by the German engineer **Richard Stribeck (1902)** during his experimental investigation of journal bearings.

Mathematically, the Stribeck relationship is written as:

$$\mu = f\!\left(\frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}\right)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (**Pa·s**)
- $N$ = rotational/sliding speed (**rev/s** or **rpm**)
- $P$ = load per unit projected bearing area (**Pa** or **N/m²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Stribeck curve is the single most important diagnostic tool in tribology for identifying the **mode of lubrication** operating in any machine element. Every bearing, gear, cam, and piston must be designed with the Stribeck regime in mind.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden box across three different floors at three different speeds:

1. **Slow Speed on Dry Carpet (Boundary Regime):** The box scrapes against the carpet fibers directly. You feel strong resistance — **friction is HIGH**, and it barely changes with how hard you push. This is **boundary lubrication**, where the asperities (microscopic peaks) of the two surfaces touch directly.

2. **Moderate Speed on a Slightly Wet Floor (Mixed Regime):** A thin layer of water begins to sneak between the box and the floor. You feel **less resistance**, but there are still some bumps. Friction **decreases rapidly**. This is **mixed lubrication** — partial asperity contact plus partial fluid film.

3. **High Speed on a Puddle (Hydrodynamic Regime):** Now there's a thick water layer completely separating the box from the floor. Friction **rises again** because the box is now "plowing" through fluid, and fluid viscosity generates shear. This is **full-film hydrodynamic (or elastohydrodynamic) lubrication**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Stribeck curve is essentially a **fingerprint of lubrication regimes** plotted against a single combined parameter. The deeper the "valley" (minimum $\mu$), the better the lubricant is performing its job.

---

## 1.3 Physical Meaning of the Three Distinct Zones

The Stribeck curve has **three identifiable regions** separated by two transition points. KTU examiners expect students to recognize these zones instantly:

| Regime | Hersey Number Range | Surface Contact | Friction Trend |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Boundary Lubrication** | Very low ($\eta N / P \to 0$) | Asperity-to-asperity | High, nearly constant $\mu$ |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | Intermediate | Partial film + partial contact | $\mu$ decreases sharply |
| **Hydrodynamic / EHL** | High ($\eta N / P \to \infty$) | Complete fluid film | $\mu$ rises with $\sqrt{\eta N / P}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **minimum coefficient of friction** occurs at the transition between the mixed and full-film regimes. This is the **design sweet-spot** that every tribologist targets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Stribeck curve emerges from the **competition between two opposing physical phenomena**:

1. **Asperity Load-Bearing Capacity** — solid-on-solid contact that produces friction roughly proportional to shear strength.
2. **Fluid Film Pressure Generation** — hydrodynamic wedges that lift surfaces apart, where viscous shear becomes the friction source.

As the Hersey number increases (higher speed, higher viscosity, lower load), the fluid film progressively takes over the load-carrying function.

---

### Step 1 — Boundary Regime (Low $\eta N / P$)

The lubricant film thickness $h$ is **smaller than the combined surface roughness** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$. Surfaces are essentially in direct contact, and friction is governed by:

$$\mu_b = \frac{\tau_s}{P}$$

where $\tau_s$ is the **shear strength of the boundary film** (typically formed by EP additives, fatty acids, or adsorbed molecular layers). $\mu_b$ is **independent of speed and viscosity**.

---

### Step 2 — Mixed Regime

Here the film thickness is comparable to surface roughness:

$$h \approx \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$$

Friction drops sharply because:
- A fraction of the load is carried by the fluid pressure.
- Asperity contacts become fewer and cooler.

The empirical model used by KTU textbooks is:

$$\mu_{mixed} = \mu_b \left(1 - \alpha \frac{h}{\sigma}\right) + \mu_{hd} \left(\alpha \frac{h}{\sigma}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **load fraction** supported by the fluid film.

---

### Step 3 — Hydrodynamic / Elastohydrodynamic Regime

Film thickness $h \gg \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$. The classical Petroff equation for lightly loaded bearings gives:

$$\mu_{hd} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\eta N}{P}}$$

The friction now **rises** because the fluid must be sheared continuously across a thicker film. **Energy dissipation grows** with film thickness.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU exams, you are often required to plot or identify a region on the Stribeck curve. Memorize these dimensionless parameters and their units.

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Value/Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Hersey number (dimensionless) | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| $\mu = \dfrac{F_f}{W}$ | Coefficient of friction definition | $0.001$ (ball bearing) to $0.5$ (dry) |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}}$ | Specific film thickness | $<1$ boundary, $\approx 1$ mixed, $>3$ full film |
| $h_{min} = 7.43 \, R \left(1 - 0.85 \dfrac{R_b}{R}\right) \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Minimum film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson) | Used for EHL |
| $\mu_{hd} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\eta N}{P}}$ | Hydrodynamic friction law | Increasing branch |
| $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Bearing pressure (projected area) | $1$–$20$ MPa typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the keyboard pipe `\|`) when writing absolute-value style symbols inside KTU answer sheets or markdown tables — the pipe character breaks table formatting.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Stribeck Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | Engine bearings, cam-followers | Designers target $\lambda \geq 2$ to avoid mixed regime failure |
| **Aerospace** | Turbofan turbine bearings | Must stay in EHL regime even at $-50^\circ$C viscosity spike |
| **Manufacturing** | Cold rolling mills | Mixed regime avoided — emulsion breakdown causes surface defects |
| **Wind Energy** | Gearbox bearings | Low-speed start-stop cycles push operation into boundary regime |
| **Biomedical** | Artificial hip joints | Mimic synovial fluid to keep $\lambda > 3$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Construction of the Hersey Number

The Hersey number is a **dimensionless grouping** of the three fundamental tribological variables. It is obtained by non-dimensionalizing the Reynolds equation.

**Step 1:** Start with the Reynolds equation for a journal bearing:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce dimensionless variables:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{x}{R}, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{z}{L}, \quad \bar{p} = \frac{p}{P}, \quad \bar{h} = \frac{h}{C}$$

where $C$ is the radial clearance.

**Step 3:** Substitute and rearrange. The pressure term emerges proportional to:

$$\bar{p} \propto \frac{\eta U R}{P C^2}$$

**Step 4:** Since $U = 2\pi R N$ for a journal bearing, substitute and obtain:

$$\bar{p} \propto \frac{\eta \cdot (2\pi R N) \cdot R}{P C^2} = \frac{2\pi \eta N R^2}{P C^2}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\dfrac{2\pi R^2}{C^2}$ is purely geometric (a constant for a given bearing), so the **load-bearing capacity depends only on the combined parameter**:

$$\boxed{\text{Hersey Number} = \frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}}$$

> **Physical Meaning:** Any combination of $\eta$, $N$, and $P$ that produces the **same Hersey number** will produce the **same friction coefficient**, the **same minimum film thickness ratio**, and the **same lubrication regime**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Behaviour in the Hydrodynamic Regime

Starting from the classical **Petroff's equation** for a lightly loaded concentric journal bearing:

$$F_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^3}{C}$$

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by the load $W$:

$$\mu = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^3}{C \cdot W}$$

**Step 2:** Express the load in terms of projected area: $W = P \cdot L \cdot 2R$:

$$\mu = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^3}{C \cdot P L \cdot 2R} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \eta N R^2}{C \cdot P}$$

**Step 3:** Combine constants into a geometric factor $K = \dfrac{2\pi^2 R^2}{C}$ (constant for the bearing):

$$\mu = K \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P}$$

**Step 4:** Since Petroff's equation neglects load-induced eccentricity, real bearings exhibit a stronger speed dependence. Empirical correction yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_{hd} &\propto \left(\frac{\eta N}{P}\right)^{1/2} \\[4pt]
\mu_{hd} &= k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\eta N}{P}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Verify dimensionally:

$$\left[\sqrt{\frac{\eta N}{P}}\right] = \sqrt{\frac{(\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s})(\text{1/s})}{\text{Pa}}} = \sqrt{\text{dimensionless}} = \text{dimensionless} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Identification of the Minimum Friction Point

The minimum of the Stribeck curve occurs where the **derivative of friction with respect to Hersey number vanishes**:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d(\eta N / P)} = 0$$

**Step 1:** Write a piecewise empirical form for the Stribeck curve:

$$\mu(\xi) = \begin{cases} \mu_b & \xi < \xi_1 \\[4pt] \mu_b - A(\xi - \xi_1) & \xi_1 \leq \xi \leq \xi_2 \\[4pt] \mu_{min} + B\sqrt{\xi - \xi_2} & \xi > \xi_2 \end{cases}$$

where $\xi = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$.

**Step 2:** At the boundary-mixed transition $\xi_1$:

$$\xi_1 \approx \frac{1}{\eta N_{\text{critical}}} \quad \text{(very small)}$$

**Step 3:** At the mixed-full-film transition $\xi_2$, the **minimum coefficient of friction** is reached:

$$\mu_{min} = \mu(\xi_2) \quad \text{(corresponds to } \lambda \approx 1\text{–}3\text{)}$$

**Step 4:** The minimum film thickness corresponding to $\xi_2$ is typically:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}} \approx 1 \text{ to } 3$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Transition Conditions

| Transition | Hersey Number $\xi$ | Specific Film Thickness $\lambda$ | Engineering Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Boundary $\to$ Mixed | $\xi_1 \approx 10^{-9}$ | $\lambda \approx 0.5$ | Surface roughness becomes significant |
| Mixed $\to$ Full Film | $\xi_2 \approx 10^{-7}$ | $\lambda \approx 3$ | Complete separation of asperities |
| Full Film EHL | $\xi > 10^{-6}$ | $\lambda > 4$ | Elastic deformation dominant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stribeck Regime Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision flow** that a tribologist follows when analyzing a machine element against the Stribeck curve:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Machine Element in Service] --> B{Compute Hersey Number}
    B --> C["xi = eta times N divided by P"]
    C --> D{xi less than xi1?}
    D -- Yes --> E[BOUNDARY LUBRICATION]
    D -- No --> F{xi between xi1 and xi2?}
    F -- Yes --> G[MIXED LUBRICATION]
    F -- No --> H[HYDRODYNAMIC / EHL]
    E --> I[High mu, asperity contact]
    G --> J[Falling mu, partial film]
    H --> K[Rising mu, full film]
    I --> L[Recommend EP additives or surface coating]
    J --> M[Increase viscosity or reduce load]
    K --> N[Optimize for minimum energy loss]
    L --> O[Design Solution]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Engineering Decision Tree for Stribeck Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Parameters] --> P2[Viscosity eta]
    P1 --> P3[Speed N]
    P1 --> P4[Load P]
    P2 --> P5[Compute Hersey Number xi]
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Plot on Stribeck Chart]
    P6 --> P7{Identify Regime}
    P7 --> P8[Boundary]
    P7 --> P9[Mixed]
    P7 --> P10[Full Film]
    P8 --> P11[Action: Lubricant Upgrade]
    P9 --> P12[Action: Geometry or Viscosity Change]
    P10 --> P13[Action: Validate Efficiency]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — The Three Regimes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BoundaryZone["BOUNDARY REGIME"]
        B1[Asperity 1] --- B2[Direct Solid Contact]
        B2 --- B3[Asperity 2]
        B3 --> B4[High Friction mu = 0.1 to 0.5]
    end
    subgraph MixedZone["MIXED REGIME"]
        M1[Partial Fluid Film] --> M2[Some Asperity Contact]
        M2 --> M3[mu decreasing]
    end
    subgraph FullFilmZone["HYDRODYNAMIC / EHL REGIME"]
        F1[Complete Fluid Film] --> F2[No Solid Contact]
        F2 --> F3[mu rising with sqrt xi]
    end
    BoundaryZone --> MixedZone
    MixedZone --> FullFilmZone
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Note:** When asked in exams to "sketch the Stribeck curve," always label the **three regimes**, mark the **minimum point**, indicate the **Hersey number axis** ($\eta N / P$), and show the **friction axis** ($\mu$). Also include a **logarithmic x-axis** to capture the wide dynamic range of lubrication parameters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define the Stribeck curve. What does the Hersey number represent? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stribeck curve** is a plot of the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** versus the **Hersey number** ($\eta N / P$) for a lubricated contact. It characterizes the three principal lubrication regimes: boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic.

The **Hersey number** is a dimensionless lubrication parameter combining the three governing variables:

$$\text{Hersey Number} = \frac{\eta N}{P}$$

It is significant because **any combination of $\eta$, $N$, and $P$ yielding the same Hersey number produces the same friction coefficient and the same lubrication regime** — making it a universal scaling parameter in tribology.

---

### **Question 2:** Name the three lubrication regimes identified by the Stribeck curve and state which regime has the lowest friction. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three lubrication regimes on the Stribeck curve are:

1. **Boundary Lubrication** — surfaces in direct asperity contact; lubricant additives form protective films.
2. **Mixed Lubrication** — partial fluid film with intermittent asperity contacts.
3. **Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Lubrication** — surfaces completely separated by a continuous fluid film.

The **lowest friction** occurs at the **transition between mixed and hydrodynamic lubrication**, which corresponds to the **valley (minimum)** of the Stribeck curve, typically where the specific film thickness $\lambda \approx 1$ to $3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Derive the Hersey number from the Reynolds equation for a journal bearing. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Sketch the Stribeck curve and explain the three lubrication regimes with their physical characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Reynolds equation for a journal bearing:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce dimensionless variables: $\bar{x} = x/R$, $\bar{z} = z/L$, $\bar{p} = p/P$, $\bar{h} = h/C$, where $R$ = journal radius, $L$ = bearing length, $C$ = radial clearance.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the Reynolds equation. After algebraic reduction, the dimensionless pressure term emerges:

$$\bar{p} \propto \frac{\eta U R}{P C^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute surface speed $U = 2 \pi R N$:

$$\bar{p} \propto \frac{\eta (2 \pi R N) R}{P C^2} = \frac{2 \pi \eta N R^2}{P C^2}$$

**Step 5:** Note that $\dfrac{2 \pi R^2}{C^2}$ is a geometric constant. Therefore, the **load-bearing behaviour depends only on**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\eta N}{P} \quad \text{(Hersey Number)}}$$

This non-dimensionalization proves that the Hersey number is the natural scaling parameter for journal-bearing performance. **(7 marks — full derivation chain)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Sketch (drawn in exam):** (Axes: x = Hersey number $\eta N / P$ on log scale; y = coefficient of friction $\mu$.) The curve has a characteristic **U-shape**:

- Left flat branch → high constant $\mu$
- Middle descending branch → sharply falling $\mu$
- Right ascending branch → rising $\mu \propto \sqrt{\eta N / P}$

**Explanation of three regimes:**

1. **Boundary Lubrication** ($\eta N / P < \xi_1$): Film thickness $h < \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$. Surfaces in asperity contact; friction governed by boundary film shear strength. $\mu$ is high and nearly constant.

2. **Mixed Lubrication** ($\xi_1 < \eta N / P < \xi_2$): Both fluid pressure and asperity contact share the load. As film thickness grows, the load fraction carried by fluid increases, and friction drops steeply.

3. **Hydrodynamic / EHL** ($\eta N / P > \xi_2$): Full fluid film separates surfaces. Friction rises again due to viscous shearing of the thicker fluid layer; $\mu \propto \sqrt{\eta N / P}$.

The **minimum friction** point lies at the **bottom of the U**, marking the optimal design target. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With the help of Petroff's equation, derive the relationship between coefficient of friction and Hersey number for a concentric journal bearing. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Discuss the engineering significance of the Stribeck curve in the design of rolling-element bearings and engine bearings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Petroff's equation gives the friction torque for a concentric journal bearing:

$$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^3}{C}$$

**Step 2:** Convert torque to friction force: $F_f = T_f / R$:

$$F_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{C}$$

**Step 3:** Divide by load $W = P \cdot L \cdot 2R$:

$$\mu = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L R^2}{C \cdot P L \cdot 2R} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \eta N R^2}{P C}$$

**Step 4:** Define geometric constant $K = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 R^2}{C}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu &= K \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \mu &\propto \frac{\eta N}{P}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** For real (eccentric) bearings, empirical correction modifies the exponent to $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$\mu \propto \sqrt{\frac{\eta N}{P}}$$

This confirms the **ascending branch** of the Stribeck curve in the hydrodynamic regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Significance in Rolling-Element Bearings:**

- Ball and roller bearings operate predominantly in the **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** regime, located on the **right ascending branch** of the Stribeck curve.
- Designers use the Hamrock-Dowson equation for minimum film thickness: $h_{min} = 7.43 R (1 - 0.85 R_b/R) \cdot \eta N / P$.
- The target is **$\lambda \geq 3$** to ensure the lubricant film completely separates asperities, preventing surface fatigue and prolonging fatigue life (L10 life).

**Significance in Engine Bearings:**

- Engine bearings operate across **all three regimes** during a duty cycle — boundary at cold start, mixed during warm-up, hydrodynamic at cruising speed.
- The Stribeck curve guides the **selection of multi-grade oils** with viscosity index improvers so that even at low temperature (high $\eta$) the Hersey number stays above $\xi_2$.
- Failure to stay in the hydrodynamic regime leads to **boundary/mixed regime failures** — scuffing, adhesive wear, and bearing seizure.

**Overall Design Significance:**

- The Stribeck curve is a **diagnostic map** to ensure components operate at the desired regime.
- It dictates lubricant viscosity grade, surface finish, and operating speeds.
- It underpins the entire field of **condition monitoring and predictive maintenance**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to log-scale the x-axis** in the Stribeck sketch — the wide dynamic range ($\xi$ from $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$) MUST be plotted logarithmically. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing "minimum film thickness" with "film thickness ratio $\lambda$"** — these are different; $\lambda$ is dimensionless. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Writing the Hersey number as $\eta N / P$ but forgetting to state that it is dimensionless** — KTU examiners explicitly check the dimensional analysis step. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Calling the full-film regime only "hydrodynamic"** without mentioning **EHL** for rolling-element bearings — partial credit only. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Not labelling the three regions on the curve** during sketches — uncategorized curves receive at most 50% credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exams:**

- [ ] **Stribeck curve definition:** Plot of $\mu$ vs. $\eta N / P$.
- [ ] **Hersey number formula:** $\xi = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ — dimensionless.
- [ ] **Three regimes:** Boundary, Mixed, Hydrodynamic/EHL.
- [ ] **Boundary regime:** $\mu$ high and nearly constant, governed by boundary film shear.
- [ ] **Mixed regime:** $\mu$ decreases sharply as film thickness builds up.
- [ ] **Full-film regime:** $\mu$ rises as $\mu \propto \sqrt{\eta N / P}$ (Petroff-based).
- [ ] **Minimum friction point** is the design target — corresponds to $\lambda \approx 1$–$3$.
- [ ] **Film parameter $\lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$** — universal lubrication indicator.
- [ ] **Hamrock-Dowson equation** for EHL film thickness in rolling bearings.
- [ ] **Petroff's equation** $T_f = 4\pi^2 \eta N L R^3 / C$ is the foundation for the hydrodynamic branch.
- [ ] **Logarithmic x-axis** is mandatory when plotting the Stribeck curve.
- [ ] **Cold start / warm-up** transitions in engines traverse the entire curve — multi-grade oils maintain $\xi$ above $\xi_2$.
- [ ] **Rolling bearings → EHL regime**; **Journal bearings → hydrodynamic regime**; **Engine cold start → boundary regime**.
- [ ] **Engineering significance:** lubricant selection, surface finish specification, bearing material choice, predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubricant Properties and Classification: Physical and Chemical Properties of Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lubricant Properties and Classification: Physical and Chemical Properties of Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricant:** A substance (typically a fluid, semi-fluid, solid, or gaseous medium) introduced between two relatively moving surfaces to **reduce friction, dissipate heat, minimize wear, and prevent corrosion** at the contacting interfaces. In tribological systems, the lubricant acts as a separating film whose rheological and chemical properties directly govern the load-carrying capacity and service life of machine elements.

**Physical Properties of Lubricants** are those characteristics that can be **measured without altering the chemical composition** of the lubricant — examples include viscosity, density, flash point, pour point, color, and specific gravity.

**Chemical Properties of Lubricants** are those characteristics that describe the **chemical reactivity, stability, and composition** of the lubricant — examples include oxidation stability, neutralization number, saponification value, and ash content.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Body's Blood**
> Imagine a lubricant as the **"blood" of a machine**. Just as blood:
> - Flows through arteries (lubricant circulates through bearings and gears)
> - Carries nutrients and oxygen (lubricant carries away heat and wear debris)
> - Has a certain thickness or "flowability" (viscosity)
> - Can deteriorate or thicken with age (oxidation, pour point issues)
> - Must remain chemically stable and not corrode vessels (rust prevention, neutralization)
>
> If the blood becomes too thick, the heart works harder; if too thin, the organs don't get protection. **Exactly the same trade-off exists in lubrication — viscosity is the heart of lubricant selection.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Critical "Health Parameters" of a Lubricant**
> 1. **Viscosity** → Like blood thickness — determines load-carrying ability
> 2. **Flash/Fire Point** → Like body temperature — indicates thermal safety limits
> 3. **Pour Point** → Like joint mobility in cold weather — indicates low-temperature flow

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics in KTU 2024

- **SI unit of dynamic viscosity:** **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **N·s/m²**
- **CGS unit of dynamic viscosity:** **Poise (P)** → 1 Pa·s = **10 Poise**
- **SI unit of kinematic viscosity:** **m²/s**
- **CGS unit of kinematic viscosity:** **Stoke (St)** → 1 m²/s = **10⁴ St** = **10⁶ cSt (centistokes)**
- **Viscosity Index (VI):** **dimensionless number** (higher = less viscosity change with temperature)
- **Flash point & Fire point:** measured in **°C**
- **Neutralization number:** measured in **mg KOH/g of oil**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Properties — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Viscosity (The Most Important Property)
Viscosity is the **internal resistance of a fluid to flow** under an applied shear stress. It is the single most critical property governing lubricant film formation.

- **Dynamic Viscosity (μ or η):** The tangential force per unit area required to move one horizontal plane of fluid at unit velocity past another plane at unit distance.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** The dynamic viscosity divided by the density of the fluid. Represents the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity.

#### B. Viscosity Index (VI)
A **dimensionless, empirical number** that indicates the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. Invented by **Dean and Davis (1929)**.
- **VI = 0** → Reference oil (Pennsylvanian, poor)
- **VI = 100** → Reference oil (Gulf Coastal, good)
- **VI > 100** → Excellent (synthetic or additized)

#### C. Flash Point and Fire Point
- **Flash Point:** The **lowest temperature** at which lubricant vapors ignite momentarily (flash) when a flame is brought near the surface.
- **Fire Point:** The temperature at which the vapors **sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds**.

#### D. Pour Point
The **lowest temperature** at which the lubricant will flow (or pour) under standard test conditions (ASTM D97). Below this, the oil becomes wax-like and cannot circulate.

#### E. Cloud Point
The temperature at which dissolved wax begins to **crystallize**, making the oil appear cloudy. Cloud point is **always higher than the pour point**.

#### F. Specific Gravity
Ratio of the weight of a given volume of oil to the weight of an equal volume of water at a standard temperature (usually 15.6°C / 60°F).

#### G. Neutralization Number (Acid Number)
The **mg of KOH** required to neutralize the acidic constituents in **1 gram of oil**. Indicates oil degradation / oxidation level.

#### H. Saponification Number
The **mg of KOH** required to saponify the fatty acids in **1 gram of oil**. Indicates the presence of fats/oils used as additives.

#### I. Emulsification and Demulsibility
- **Emulsification:** Tendency of oil to mix with water and form emulsion (desirable in cutting oils, undesirable in turbine oils).
- **Demulsibility:** Ability of oil to separate from water (desirable in steam turbine oils).

#### J. Carbon Residue
Residual carbon left after evaporation and pyrolysis of oil under standard conditions (Conradson or Ramsbottom test). Indicates the coke-forming tendency.

#### K. Aniline Point
The **lowest temperature** at which equal volumes of aniline and oil are completely miscible. **Lower aniline point = higher aromatic content = lower VI.**

#### L. Ash Content
Inorganic residue remaining after complete combustion of the oil. Indicates the presence of metallic additives.

### 2.2 Chemical Properties — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Oxidation Stability
Resistance of lubricant to react with atmospheric oxygen. **Oxidation produces acids, sludges, varnishes, and polymers** that degrade performance. Measured by **Rotary Bomb Oxidation Test (RBOT)** or **TAN (Total Acid Number) increase**.

#### B. Thermal Stability
Ability to resist decomposition at elevated temperatures without forming deposits, sludge, or corrosive byproducts.

#### C. Hydrolytic Stability
Resistance of the lubricant (especially synthetic esters) to react with water, forming acids and alcohols. Critical for steam turbine and marine applications.

#### D. Rust Prevention
Ability to protect ferrous surfaces from rusting in the presence of moisture. Tested by ASTM D665 (Rust Test).

#### E. Corrosion Properties
Resistance to attack on copper, bronze, and other metals (ASTM D130 Copper Strip Corrosion Test).

#### F. Additive Compatibility
Ability of lubricant to mix with various performance-enhancing additives (anti-wear, EP, anti-foam) without adverse chemical interaction.

### 2.3 Classification of Lubricants

| **Classification Basis** | **Category** | **Examples** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Physical State** | Liquid lubricants | Mineral oils, synthetic oils, vegetable oils |
| | Semi-solid lubricants | Greases (soap + oil) |
| | Solid lubricants | Graphite, MoS₂, PTFE, Talc |
| | Gaseous lubricants | Air, Nitrogen, Helium |
| **Origin** | Mineral | Petroleum-derived (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic) |
| | Vegetable | Castor oil, palm oil, rapeseed oil |
| | Animal | Lard oil, tallow, whale oil |
| | Synthetic | PAO, esters, silicones, polyglycols, PFPE |
| **Application** | Industrial | Gear oils, hydraulic oils, turbine oils |
| | Automotive | Engine oils, transmission fluids |
| | Metalworking | Cutting fluids, drawing compounds |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme — memorize units and boundary conditions.

| **Formula** | **Symbol Definition** | **Application / Boundary** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\mu = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\tau$ = shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate | Newton's law of viscosity; units: Pa·s |
| $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, $\rho$ = density | Used in Reynolds equation; units: m²/s |
| 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s = 1 cm²/s | Stoke unit conversion | CGS to SI conversion |
| 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s = 1 mm²/s | Centistoke = 1/100 Stoke | Common unit in lubricant datasheets |
| 1 P = 10⁻¹ Pa·s | Poise unit conversion | CGS to SI |
| $\tau_{oil} = \eta \cdot r \cdot \omega / h$ | Shear stress in journal bearing | Couette flow assumption |
| $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | L, U, H = viscosities of low-VI, sample, high-VI ref oils at 40°C | V.I. calculation (Dean-Davis method) |
| $h_{min} = 0.0002 \sqrt{\mu \nu}$ | $h_{min}$ = minimum film thickness | Empirical (McKee's) for lightly loaded bearings |
| $S = \rho_{oil} / \rho_{water}$ | S = specific gravity at 15.6°C | Density ratio |
| $TAN = V_{KOH} \times N \times 56.1 / W$ | TAN = Total Acid Number, V = mL KOH, N = normality, W = sample weight (g) | ASTM D974 acid number |
| $W = 0.22F - 0.72$ | W = load capacity factor; F = SAE friction coefficient | — |
| $h_c \approx 0.025 D \sqrt{\mu N / P}$ | $h_c$ = central film thickness, D = diameter, N = speed, P = load | Classical journal bearing formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In a real industrial gearbox, **60% of bearing failures** are traceable to **incorrect viscosity selection**. A lubricant with **VI > 95** ensures stable film thickness across temperature swings from -10°C (winter startup) to +90°C (continuous operation). This is why the Viscosity Index is often called the **"lubricant's temperature-resilience score."**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider two parallel plates separated by a thin lubricant film of thickness $h$. The top plate moves with velocity $U$ while the bottom plate is stationary.

**Step 2 — Observation:** A shear stress $\tau$ develops in the fluid. Experiments by Newton (1687) showed that $\tau$ is **proportional to the velocity gradient** for most lubricating oils.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Statement:**

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Introducing the Proportionality Constant (Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$):**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative Form Using Shear Rate $\dot{\gamma}$:**

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 6 — Transition to Kinematic Viscosity:**
Dividing dynamic viscosity by density gives kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

This is essential because $\nu$ appears naturally in the **Reynolds lubrication equation**, where $\rho$ also appears, allowing cancellation.

**Step 7 — Unit Verification:**

$$[\mu] = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa·s} \quad ; \quad [\nu] = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Viscosity Index (VI) Calculation

**Step 1 — Reference Standards:** Two reference oils are defined:
- **L-oil:** Viscosity at 100°C = sample, with VI = 0 (poor temperature behavior)
- **H-oil:** Viscosity at 100°C = sample, with VI = 100 (excellent temperature behavior)

**Step 2 — Standard Test Temperatures:** Viscosities of sample, L, and H are measured (or looked up in ASTM D2270 tables) at **40°C and 100°C**.

**Step 3 — Empirical Formula (when VI ≤ 100):**

$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

where:
- $L$ = viscosity of low-VI reference at 40°C
- $H$ = viscosity of high-VI reference at 40°C
- $U$ = viscosity of **sample** at 40°C

**Step 4 — For VI > 100 (ASTM D2270 Method):**

$$VI = 100 + \frac{10^{N} - 1}{0.00715} \quad ; \quad N = \frac{\log H - \log U}{\log Y}$$

where $Y$ is the kinematic viscosity of the sample at 100°C.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** An oil has kinematic viscosity 75 cSt at 40°C and 10 cSt at 100°C. From reference tables, L = 110 cSt and H = 50 cSt at 40°C (for an oil with 10 cSt at 100°C). Calculate the VI.

**Solution:**

$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 = \frac{110 - 75}{110 - 50} \times 100$$

$$VI = \frac{35}{60} \times 100 = 58.33$$

**Result:** VI ≈ **58** — This is a **paraffinic mineral oil** with **moderate** temperature-viscosity stability.

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Gravity to API Gravity Conversion (Industry Standard)

**Step 1 — Definition:** Specific gravity $S$ is the ratio of oil density to water density at 15.6°C.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to API Gravity (used in petroleum industry):**

$$^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{S} - 131.5$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:** If $S = 0.85$:

$$^\circ \text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 \approx 35^\circ \text{API}$$

A **35° API oil** is classified as a **light crude**, suitable for high-quality lubricant base stock production.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Physical vs Chemical Properties

| **Property** | **Type** | **Test Standard (ASTM)** | **Acceptable Range (Typical)** | **Failure Implication** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| Kinematic Viscosity @ 40°C | Physical | D445 | 20 – 500 cSt | Wrong film thickness |
| Viscosity Index (VI) | Physical | D2270 | 90 – 110 | Excessive viscosity variation |
| Flash Point | Physical | D92 (COC) | > 180°C | Fire hazard |
| Pour Point | Physical | D97 | < -10°C | Cold-start failure |
| Cloud Point | Physical | D2500 | 5°C above pour point | Wax crystallization |
| Specific Gravity | Physical | D1298 | 0.85 – 0.95 | Contamination, wrong grade |
| TAN (Acid Number) | Chemical | D974 | < 2.0 mg KOH/g | Acidic corrosion |
| Saponification No. | Chemical | D94 | 0.5 – 50 | Fats/oil content |
| Carbon Residue | Chemical | D189 | < 2 wt% | Coke deposits |
| Aniline Point | Physical-Chemical | D611 | 60 – 120°C | Aromatic content indicator |
| Oxidation Stability | Chemical | D943 / D2272 | > 100 hrs | Sludge, varnish, acid build-up |
| Rust Prevention | Chemical | D665 | Pass/Fail | Bearing corrosion |
| Copper Strip Corrosion | Chemical | D130 | Class 1 – 3 | Catalytic degradation |
| Ash Content | Chemical | D482 | < 0.1% | Combustion deposits |
| Emulsification | Physical | D1401 | Time to separation | Water contamination risk |
| Demulsibility | Physical | D1401 | < 30 min | Critical for turbine oils |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **TAN (Total Acid Number)** is the **"blood-test" of lubrication oil**. In service, TAN typically increases from 1.0 → 2.0 mg KOH/g over 5000 hours. A **sudden TAN spike** indicates **oxidative breakdown** — the oil must be drained immediately to prevent bearing corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Lubricants

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[LUBRICANTS] --> B[Physical State]
    A --> C[Origin Source]
    A --> D[Application Domain]

    B --> B1[Liquid Oils]
    B --> B2[Semi-Solid Greases]
    B --> B3[Solid Lubricants]
    B --> B4[Gaseous Lubricants]

    B1 --> B1a[Mineral Oil]
    B1 --> B1b[Synthetic Oil]
    B1 --> B1c[Vegetable Oil]
    B1 --> B1d[Animal Oil]

    B1b --> B1b1[PAO]
    B1b --> B1b2[Esters]
    B1b --> B1b3[Silicones]
    B1b --> B1b4[Polyglycols]
    B1b --> B1b5[PFPE]

    B3 --> B3a[Graphite]
    B3 --> B3b[Molybdenum Disulfide MoS2]
    B3 --> B3c[PTFE Teflon]
    B3 --> B3d[Talc and Mica]

    C --> C1[Mineral]
    C --> C2[Vegetable]
    C --> C3[Animal]
    C --> C4[Synthetic]

    D --> D1[Engine Oils]
    D --> D2[Gear Oils]
    D --> D3[Hydraulic Oils]
    D --> D4[Turbine Oils]
    D --> D5[Compressor Oils]
    D --> D6[Metalworking Fluids]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Lubricant Property Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Lubricant Input Sample] --> B[Physical Property Tests]
    A --> C[Chemical Property Tests]

    B --> B1[Viscosity Test D445]
    B --> B2[Flash Point D92]
    B --> B3[Pour Point D97]
    B --> B4[Specific Gravity D1298]

    C --> C1[TAN Test D974]
    C --> C2[Oxidation Stability D943]
    C --> C3[Copper Corrosion D130]
    C --> C4[Ash Content D482]

    B1 --> D{Performance Classification}
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E[Grade VG 32 or VG 46 or VG 68]
    D --> F[Service Recommendation]
    D --> G[Rejection or Approval]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Couette Flow Shear Model (Viscosity Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top Moving Plate Velocity U] --> B[Lubricant Film Thickness h]
    B --> C[Bottom Stationary Plate]
    A --> D[Velocity Profile du/dy Linear]
    D --> E[Shear Stress tau equals mu times du by dy]
    E --> F[Dynamic Viscosity mu measured in Pa s]
    F --> G[Kinematic Viscosity nu equals mu by rho]
    G --> H[Applied in Reynolds Equation]
    H --> I[Hydrodynamic Film Thickness]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Viscosity-Temperature Behavior Across Oil Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Low VI Paraffinic Oil] --> A1[Sharp Viscosity Drop with Temperature]
    B[High VI Paraffinic Oil] --> B1[Moderate Viscosity Drop with Temperature]
    C[Synthetic PAO Oil] --> C1[Minimal Viscosity Drop with Temperature]
    D[Multigrade Engine Oil 20W 50] --> D1[Polymer Additives Flatten Curve]

    A1 --> E[Poor Cold Start Poor High Temp Stability]
    B1 --> F[Adequate Performance]
    C1 --> G[Excellent Performance Wide Temperature Range]
    D1 --> H[Optimal Automotive Lubrication]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The block diagrams above map the **interactions** between physical and chemical properties, the **decision tree** for grade classification (ISO VG 32, VG 46, VG 68), and the **flow logic** from raw sample to service recommendation. This is the same workflow used in a **commercial lubricant QC laboratory**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following with their standard units:**
**(a) Dynamic Viscosity**
**(b) Kinematic Viscosity**
**(c) Viscosity Index**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** The ratio of shear stress to shear rate in a flowing fluid, representing its internal resistance to deformation.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} \quad \text{Units: Pa·s or Poise (1 Pa·s = 10 P)}$$

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{Units: m²/s or Stokes (1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s)}$$

**(c) Viscosity Index (VI):** A dimensionless empirical number indicating the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. Higher VI = less viscosity change with temperature. Established by Dean and Davis (1929), using Pennsylvanian oil (VI = 0) and Gulf Coastal oil (VI = 100) as references.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish clearly between Flash Point and Fire Point. Why is flash point a critical safety parameter in industrial lubricant selection?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Flash Point** | **Fire Point** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Lowest temperature at which oil vapors ignite **momentarily (flash)** near a flame | Temperature at which oil vapors **sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds** |
| **Magnitude** | Lower value | Always **higher** than flash point (typically 5 – 30°C higher) |
| **Test Standard** | ASTM D92 (Cleveland Open Cup) | ASTM D92 |
| **Indicates** | Volatility / flammability onset | Sustained combustion threshold |

**Critical Safety Significance:**
- Determines the **maximum safe operating temperature** in hydraulic systems, transformers, and gearboxes.
- For **turbine and transformer oils**, minimum flash point is **140°C – 160°C** to prevent fire in confined electrical equipment.
- Low flash point indicates **contamination with volatile fuels** (diesel, gasoline) — a major cause of industrial fires.
- Governs the **shipping, storage, and handling classification** under hazardous material regulations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Option Set 1`

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Classify lubricants in detail based on physical state, origin, and application. Give two examples for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the physical properties of lubricants that govern their selection for hydrodynamic journal bearings. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Classification of Lubricants:**

**(1) Based on Physical State:**

- **Liquid Lubricants** — Mineral oils (SAE 30), synthetic oils (PAO 6), vegetable oils (castor oil). Used in journal bearings, hydraulic systems, gearboxes.
- **Semi-Solid Lubricants (Greases)** — Lithium-based grease, calcium-soap grease, polyurea grease. Used in rolling element bearings, slow-speed heavily-loaded applications.
- **Solid Lubricants** — Graphite, molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂), PTFE, tungsten disulfide. Used in extreme temperatures, vacuum, and high-pressure conditions where liquid lubricants fail.
- **Gaseous Lubricants** — Air, nitrogen, helium. Used in high-speed turbomachinery, gyroscopes, precision machine tool spindles.

**(2) Based on Origin:**

- **Mineral Lubricants** — Derived from petroleum refining: paraffinic (high VI, wax-prone), naphthenic (low wax, good low-temp), aromatic (poor VI, mostly used as solvents).
- **Vegetable Lubricants** — Castor oil, palm oil, rapeseed oil, coconut oil. Renewable, biodegradable, used in environmentally sensitive applications.
- **Animal Lubricants** — Lard oil, tallow, sperm oil. Historically used; now largely replaced by synthetics.
- **Synthetic Lubricants** — PAO, esters, silicones, polyalkylene glycols, PFPE. Engineered for extreme conditions (high/low temperature, radiation, vacuum).

**(3) Based on Application:**

- **Engine Oils** — SAE 10W-30, 20W-50 (multigrade).
- **Gear Oils** — API GL-4, GL-5 with EP additives.
- **Hydraulic Oils** — ISO VG 32, 46, 68.
- **Turbine Oils** — R&O (Rust and Oxidation inhibited).
- **Compressor Oils** — For reciprocating and rotary compressors.
- **Metalworking Fluids** — Cutting oils, drawing compounds, grinding fluids.
- **Transformer Oils** — Highly refined mineral oils with high dielectric strength.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Physical Properties Governing Selection for Hydrodynamic Journal Bearings:**

**(1) Viscosity:** The most critical property. Too low → metal-to-metal contact; too high → excessive friction and heat. Selected using the **Sommerfeld number and the Hersey number** ($\mu N / P$) for bearing design.

**(2) Viscosity Index (VI):** Must be high (typically > 95) to ensure stable film thickness from cold startup to operating temperature.

**(3) Flash Point:** Must be > 180°C to prevent fire from hot bearing housing.

**(4) Pour Point:** Must be 5 – 10°C **below** the minimum ambient temperature to ensure oil circulation at startup.

**(5) Specific Gravity:** Used to compute weight of oil in the bearing housing and for oil-level sensor calibration.

**(6) Demulsibility:** Essential for bearings in steam turbines and marine applications where water contamination is possible. Quick water separation prevents rust and additive washout.

**(7) Neutralization Number (TAN):** A low initial TAN (< 0.1 mg KOH/g) is required for new oils. During service, TAN is monitored to schedule oil change.

**(8) Color:** Indicates refining quality; dark color may signal contamination or oxidation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often **forget to justify the property-to-application linkage**. Merely listing properties loses marks. You must **explicitly state WHY** each property matters for journal bearings — e.g., not just "VI = 95" but "VI = 95 ensures viscosity change < 30% across 80°C operating range." This **application reasoning** carries **3 out of 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)`

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the ASTM D445 apparatus for kinematic viscosity measurement. State the kinematic viscosity of an oil if the efflux time in a Redwood No.1 viscometer at 40°C is 350 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An oil has viscosity 75 cSt at 40°C and 9.5 cSt at 100°C. From reference tables, L = 110 cSt and H = 50 cSt at 40°C for oils having 9.5 cSt at 100°C. Calculate the Viscosity Index. Comment on its temperature-viscosity behavior. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**ASTM D445 — Kinematic Viscosity Measurement (Capillary Viscometer):**

**Apparatus:** A **calibrated glass capillary viscometer** (Ubbelohde, Cannon-Fenske, or Zeitfuchs) is mounted vertically in a constant-temperature bath maintained at 40°C (or 100°C).

**Procedure:**
1. A fixed volume of oil is charged into the upper reservoir.
2. The oil is allowed to flow under gravity through the calibrated capillary.
3. The **efflux time** ($t$ seconds) for the oil meniscus to pass between two calibrated marks is measured.
4. Kinematic viscosity is given by:

$$\nu = C \times t$$

where $C$ = viscometer constant (mm²/s²) and $t$ = efflux time in seconds.

**For Redwood No.1 Viscometer:** Conversion formula:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t} \quad (\text{cSt})$$

**Calculation:**

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 350 - \frac{1.72}{350}$$

$$\nu = 0.91 - 0.00491$$

$$\nu = 0.905 \text{ cSt}$$

> This value is unrealistically low (pure water at 40°C is ~0.65 cSt). The Redwood formula validity requires $t > 100$ s; for $t = 350$ s, a more accurate Redwood-1 formula is:

$$\nu = 0.0026 t - \frac{1.72}{t} \, (\text{valid for } t > 100 \text{ s})$$

Computing precisely:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 350 - \frac{1.72}{350} = 0.9100 - 0.00491 = 0.9051 \approx 0.905 \text{ cSt}$$

(For KTU exam, present the value as computed; in real data, Redwood No.1 constant may differ.)

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:**
- Sample viscosity at 40°C: $U = 75$ cSt
- Sample viscosity at 100°C: 9.5 cSt
- L-oil (VI = 0) viscosity at 40°C: $L = 110$ cSt
- H-oil (VI = 100) viscosity at 40°C: $H = 50$ cSt

**Applying the VI formula (Dean-Davis, valid for VI ≤ 100):**

$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

$$VI = \frac{110 - 75}{110 - 50} \times 100$$

$$VI = \frac{35}{60} \times 100$$

$$VI = 58.33 \approx 58$$

**Comment on Temperature-Viscosity Behavior:**

A VI of **58** classifies the oil as a **paraffinic mineral oil with moderate temperature stability**. Compared to a high-VI oil (VI = 95+), this oil will show a **steeper viscosity drop** as temperature rises from 40°C to 100°C.

**Implication:** Suitable for **moderate-temperature applications** such as general industrial gearboxes, plain bearings, and hydraulic systems operating in temperature-controlled environments. For **wide-temperature applications** (automotive engines, aerospace hydraulics), an oil with **VI > 95** (with VI improver additives or synthetic base stock) would be preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — VI Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong formula application** — Use $L - U$ in numerator, NOT $U - L$ (this would give negative VI — a common slip).
> 2. **Unit confusion** — Viscosity values MUST be at the **same temperature** (40°C in this case) for L, U, and H.
> 3. **No comment on behavior** — Losing 2 marks for not explaining what the VI value means in engineering terms.
> 4. **No reference to ASTM D2270** for VI > 100 case (you may get a follow-up "what if VI > 100?" sub-question).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Lubricant = substance that reduces friction, wear, and heat between moving surfaces
- ☐ Physical properties: measurable without changing composition
- ☐ Chemical properties: describe reactivity, stability, composition

**Most Exam-Critical Properties:**
- ☐ **Viscosity** (dynamic μ, kinematic ν) — *the most important property*
- ☐ **Viscosity Index (VI)** — *temperature-resilience indicator*
- ☐ **Flash Point / Fire Point** — *safety parameter*
- ☐ **Pour Point / Cloud Point** — *low-temperature flow*
- ☐ **TAN (Acid Number)** — *oxidation monitoring during service*
- ☐ **Aniline Point** — *inverse of aromatic content*

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $\mu = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ (Newton's law)
- ☐ $\nu = \mu / \rho$
- ☐ $VI = (L - U) / (L - H) \times 100$
- ☐ $^\circ \text{API} = 141.5/S - 131.5$
- ☐ $TAN = V_{KOH} \times N \times 56.1 / W$

**Unit Conversions (High-Frequency in KTU):**
- ☐ 1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s; 1 cSt = 1 mm²/s
- ☐ 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s
- ☐ 1 reyn = 6890 Pa·s (rarely used)

**Classification Traps:**
- ☐ Synthetic ≠ Vegetable ≠ Mineral (all can be "liquid" but differ in origin)
- ☐ Solid lubricants (graphite, MoS₂) are NOT classified under "grease"
- ☐ Pour point is always **lower** than cloud point

**Industrial Application Memory Hook:**
- ☐ Engine Oil → High VI (>120) + High Flash Point (>220°C)
- ☐ Hydraulic Oil → ISO VG 32/46 + Anti-wear + Demulsibility
- ☐ Gear Oil → High Viscosity (VG 220+) + EP Additives
- ☐ Turbine Oil → R&O Inhibited + Low TAN + Demulsibility < 30 min
- ☐ Transformer Oil → High Dielectric Strength + Low TAN + High Flash Point

**Mnemonic — "FVP-PCA" for Top 6 Properties:**
**F**lash point → **V**iscosity → **P**our point → **P**hysical state → **C**hemical stability (TAN) → **A**dditive compatibility
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Lubricants: Oils, Greases, and Solid Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Lubricants: Oils, Greases, and Solid Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Lubricant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A lubricant is any substance (gas, liquid, semi-solid, or solid) introduced between two contacting surfaces in relative motion to reduce friction, dissipate heat, minimize wear, and protect the surfaces from corrosion or contamination. Tribologically, it functions as an *interfacial medium* that physically separates asperities and supports load through hydrodynamic, elastohydrodynamic, or boundary film mechanisms.

In KTU Board terminology, lubricants are classified primarily by their **physical state at operating temperature** rather than by chemical origin. The three principal categories that form the backbone of Module 3 are:

1. **Liquid Lubricants (Oils):** Animal, vegetable, mineral, and synthetic oils.
2. **Semi-Solid Lubricants (Greases):** Oils thickened with metallic soaps or non-soap thickeners.
3. **Solid Lubricants:** Lamellar solids, soft metals, polymers, and chemical films.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Slippery Slide" Concept:**
> Imagine sliding down a playground slide on a hot summer day wearing a thin film of water. The water film physically separates your body from the metal slide, dramatically reducing friction and preventing the "stuck" feeling. Now, replace the water with **honey** — you move even slower because honey is thicker (higher viscosity). Replace the water with **butter** (a semi-solid), and you get a sticky, slow slide but with excellent load-bearing capacity. Finally, imagine **graphite powder** sprinkled on the slide — the layers of graphite slide over one another effortlessly, even at high speeds.
>
> This is exactly how the three classes of lubricants behave:
> - **Oils** = water-like thin films for high-speed, low-load systems (spindles, turbines).
> - **Greases** = butter-like thick films for low-speed, high-load, sealed systems (wheel bearings).
> - **Solid Lubricants** = graphite-like layered structures for extreme environments (space, vacuum, high temperature).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Measured in **mm²/s** (centistokes, cSt) at 40°C and 100°C (per **ISO 3448** and **ASTM D341**).
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (η):** Measured in **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or **cP** (centipoise).
> - **NLGI Consistency Number:** 9 standardized grades defined by **ASTM D217** (worked penetration range).
> - **Viscosity Index (VI):** A dimensionless number (0 to 100+) per **ASTM D2270**.
> - **Flash Point and Pour Point:** Measured in **°C** per **ASTM D92** (Cleveland open cup) and **ASTM D97**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Liquid Lubricants — Lubricating Oils

Lubricating oils are the most widely used class of lubricants. They function primarily through *hydrodynamic* and *elastohydrodynamic* (EHL) lubrication regimes, where the fluid film fully separates the contacting asperities.

**2.1.1 Classification by Origin:**

- **Mineral Oils:** Refined from petroleum crude. They are mixtures of hydrocarbons (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic) and constitute over **95%** of all lubricants used industrially.
- **Synthetic Oils:** Engineered from chemically synthesized base stocks. Common types include:
  - **Polyalphaolefins (PAO)** — for automotive and aerospace gearboxes.
  - **Synthetic esters** — biodegradable, used in jet engines.
  - **Polyglycols (PAG)** — high-temperature, water-soluble.
  - **Phosphate esters** — fire-resistant hydraulic fluids.
  - **Silicones** — wide temperature range (−60°C to 200°C).
- **Vegetable Oils:** Castor, rapeseed, palm — biodegradable and used in environmentally sensitive applications (loss lubrication).
- **Animal Oils:** Whale oil (sperm oil) historically; largely replaced by synthetics.

**2.1.2 Key Performance Properties:**

- **Viscosity:** The most critical property. The **dynamic viscosity η** is governed by Newton's law:
  - High viscosity → better load support, thicker film.
  - Low viscosity → easier flow, less viscous friction.
- **Viscosity Index (VI):** Measures the rate of change of viscosity with temperature. Higher VI = flatter viscosity-temperature curve.
- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which oil vapors ignite momentarily.
- **Pour Point:** The lowest temperature at which oil still flows under standard conditions.
- **Oxidation Stability:** Resistance to chemical degradation by atmospheric oxygen.
- **Demulsibility:** Ability to separate from water.

**2.1.3 Additives in Lubricating Oils:**

Modern lubricating oils are rarely pure base stocks. They contain **0.1%–30% by mass of additives** to enhance performance:

| Additive Type | Function | Typical Concentration |
|---|---|---|
| Antioxidants | Inhibit oxidation chain reactions | 0.1–1.0% |
| Anti-wear (AW) | Form sacrificial films (ZDDP) | 0.5–3.0% |
| Extreme Pressure (EP) | React with surface under load | 1.0–10% |
| Viscosity Index Improvers (VII) | Reduce viscosity-temperature dependence | 1.0–15% |
| Detergents | Neutralize acids, keep surfaces clean | 1.0–6.0% |
| Dispersants | Suspend soot and sludge | 2.0–8.0% |
| Pour Point Depressants | Modify wax crystallization | 0.1–1.0% |
| Antifoam Agents | Suppress air entrainment | 5–500 ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Additives:** A base oil alone cannot simultaneously resist oxidation, withstand high contact pressure, and remain fluid at −30°C. Additives act as **chemical modifiers** that target specific failure modes, allowing a single formulated oil to perform under diverse conditions.

### 2.2 Semi-Solid Lubricants — Greases

Grease is a **structured dispersion of a thickening agent in a lubricating fluid**. The thickener forms a 3D fibrous matrix that immobilizes the oil, giving grease its semi-solid consistency.

**2.2.1 Composition of Grease:**

- **Base Oil (75%–95% by mass):** Mineral or synthetic oil providing the lubrication action.
- **Thickener (5%–25%):** Provides the structural backbone.
- **Additives (0%–10%):** Same classes as for oils.

**2.2.2 Types of Thickeners:**

- **Metallic Soap Thickeners:** Lithium, Sodium, Calcium, Aluminum, Barium salts of fatty acids. **Lithium 12-hydroxystearate** is the most widely used (multi-purpose grease).
- **Complex Soap Thickeners:** Lithium complex, calcium complex, aluminum complex — offer higher dropping points.
- **Non-Soap Thickeners:** Polyurea (long life), bentonite clay, silica gel, PTFE.
- **Mixed Soap Thickeners:** Combinations of two soap types for special properties.

**2.2.3 Critical Grease Properties:**

- **Consistency (Worked Penetration):** Measured in tenths of a millimeter (per **ASTM D217**). A standard cone penetrates the grease under 150 g for 5 seconds. Penetration depth defines the **NLGI grade**.
- **Dropping Point:** The temperature at which grease becomes fluid enough to drip. Determines upper temperature limit.
- **Mechanical Stability:** Ability of the grease to retain consistency under prolonged shearing.
- **Oil Separation:** Tendency of oil to bleed out of the thickener matrix during storage/use.
- **Water Resistance:** Critical for applications exposed to wash-out conditions.

**2.2.4 NLGI Consistency Classification:**

The **National Lubricating Grease Institute (NLGI)** has established **9 consistency grades** based on worked penetration. This is universally tested in KTU Board questions.

| NLGI Grade | Worked Penetration (mm/10) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 000 | 445–475 | Centralized lubrication systems |
| 00 | 400–430 | Gearbox grease (semi-fluid) |
| 0 | 355–385 | Enclosed gearboxes |
| 1 | 310–340 | Low-speed rolling bearings |
| **2** | **265–295** | **Most common – general bearings** |
| 3 | 220–250 | High-speed bearings |
| 4 | 175–205 | Sealed-for-life bearings |
| 5 | 130–160 | Very stiff, slow-speed |
| 6 | 85–115 | Block grease, slow journal bearings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NLGI Grade 2** is the *workhorse* of the grease industry. Approximately 70% of all grease manufactured worldwide falls in this grade, used for general-purpose ball and roller bearings.

### 2.3 Solid Lubricants

Solid lubricants are used when liquid or semi-solid lubricants fail — typically in **extreme environments**: high temperature (>250°C), high vacuum (space), cryogenic temperatures, or in the presence of reactive chemicals.

**2.3.1 Classification of Solid Lubricants:**

- **Lamellar (Layered) Solids:**
  - **Graphite:** Inert in air up to 450°C, requires adsorbed vapors for low friction.
  - **Molybdenum Disulfide (MoS₂):** Superior to graphite in vacuum; stable from −185°C to 400°C.
  - **Tungsten Disulfide (WS₂):** Better thermal stability than MoS₂.
  - **Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN):** "White graphite" — electrically insulating.
- **Soft Metal Films:** Gold, Silver, Indium, Lead, Tin — ductile, shear easily, used in vacuum and space.
- **Polymer-Based Solid Lubricants:** PTFE (Teflon), UHMWPE, Polyimide — chemical resistance, low load.
- **Chemical Conversion Films:** Phosphate coatings, oxide layers, sulfide layers — formed *in situ* on the metal surface.
- **Glass and Ceramic Lubricants:** For very high temperatures (>500°C).

**2.3.2 Mechanism of Lamellar Lubrication:**

The lamellar solid has weak **van der Waals bonds** between layers and strong covalent bonds within each layer. Under shear, the layers slide past each other like playing cards, giving friction coefficients as low as **0.001–0.05** in ideal conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Graphite Needs Moisture:** Graphite's interlaminar bonds are inherently strong in vacuum. The presence of water vapor or oxygen **adsorbs** between layers, weakening the van der Waals bonds and enabling easy shear. This is why graphite fails in space — a critical insight for aerospace tribology.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the equations below — these are the most frequently tested items in the ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 3.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
