## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between annealing and normalizing. Mention their effect on hardness and grain structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU board key style):**

| Aspect | Annealing | Normalizing |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling rate | Very slow (furnace) | Moderate (still air) |
| Grain structure | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | Fine pearlite + ferrite |
| Hardness | Lower (softer) | Slightly higher than annealed |
| Purpose | Maximum softness, ductility | Refine grain, uniform properties |

**Mark split:** Annealing definition & cooling (1) + Normalizing definition & cooling (1) + Comparison table (1) = **3 marks**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is nitriding? Why is it carried out below the A₁ temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Nitriding is a thermo-chemical surface-hardening process where atomic nitrogen diffuses into steel at **500 – 550 °C** to form hard iron and alloy nitrides. *(1 mark)*
- **Reason for sub-A₁ temperature:**
  1. No phase transformation (austenite → martensite) is desired, so quenching is unnecessary. *(1 mark)*
  2. Avoids distortion and dimensional changes typical of quenching. *(0.5 mark)*
  3. Nitrogen solubility and diffusivity in ferrite (BCC α-Fe) are favourable at these temperatures. *(0.5 mark)*

**Total: 3 marks.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer either Question A or Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **gas-nitriding process** with a neat block diagram of the equipment and the chemistry involved. Discuss the **microstructure** developed at the surface. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **nitriding, carburizing, and induction hardening** with respect to operating temperature, steel grade requirement, case depth, distortion, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Nitriding is a thermo-chemical diffusion process for surface hardening of steels by introducing atomic nitrogen into the surface at sub-critical temperature (500 – 550 °C) without the need for quenching.

**Equipment (1 mark — block diagram acceptable):**
- Sealed retort furnace
- Ammonia gas cylinder + flow meter
- Pressure and temperature controllers
- Gas dissociation probe (% dissociation typically 30 – 60 %)
- Exhaust system for unreacted NH₃ and H₂

**Chemistry (2 marks):**

$$\text{NH}_3 \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{Fe catalyst, 520 °C}}\;\; [\text{N}] \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{H}_2$$

Atomic N is the *active species*; molecular N₂ is too inert.

**Microstructure (3 marks):**
1. **Compound (white) layer** — Fe₂N (ε) + Fe₄N (γ′), 5 – 30 µm, very hard but brittle.
2. **Diffusion zone** — N in solid solution + fine alloy nitrides (CrN, AlN, Mo₂N, VN) → 0.3 – 0.7 mm effective case.
3. **Core** — unaffected base steel (tough).

Mark split: (1) intro + (1) equipment + (2) chemistry + (3) microstructure = **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Parameter | Nitriding | Carburizing | Induction Hardening |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 500 – 550 °C | 850 – 950 °C | 850 – 950 °C |
| Diffusing species | N (atomic) | C (atomic) | None — only phase transformation |
| Steel grade | Alloy steel with Cr/Al/Mo/V (e.g., 38CrMoAl, AISI 4140, 4340) | Low-carbon steel (0.1 – 0.25 % C) | Medium-carbon steel (0.35 – 0.5 % C) |
| Quenching | Not required | Required (oil) | Self-quench or water spray |
| Case depth | 0.1 – 0.7 mm | 0.5 – 4 mm | 0.5 – 10 mm |
| Distortion | Minimal | High | Moderate |
| Surface hardness | 60 – 72 HRC | 55 – 65 HRC | 55 – 65 HRC |
| Typical application | Gears, camshafts, dies, valve parts | Gears, shafts, pinions | Crankshafts, axles, splines |

Mark split: Comparison table (5) + Explanation of key differences & application examples (2) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of heat treatment processes** (annealing, normalizing, hardening, tempering) with temperature ranges, cooling media, and resulting microstructures. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A steel component is to be nitrided at **510 °C for 30 hours**. Given $D_0 = 4.7\times10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and $Q = 77\,\text{kJ/mol}$, calculate the diffusion coefficient and the effective case depth, taking $k = 3.2$. Comment on how doubling the time affects the case depth. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Process | Temperature | Cooling | Microstructure | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annealing | 30 – 50 °C above A₃ (~ 760 – 900 °C) | Very slow (furnace) | Coarse ferrite + pearlite | Maximum softness, stress relief |
| Normalizing | 30 – 50 °C above A₃ | Air (still) | Fine ferrite + pearlite | Grain refinement, uniformity |
| Hardening (Quenching) | Above A₃ | Rapid (oil / water / brine) | Martensite (BCT) | Maximum hardness |
| Tempering | 150 – 650 °C (below A₁) | Air | Tempered martensite | Reduce brittleness, increase toughness |

Mark split: Annealing (1.5) + Normalizing (1.5) + Hardening (2) + Tempering (2) = **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Temperature in Kelvin:**
$T = 510 + 273 = 783\;\text{K}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $Q/RT$:**

$$\frac{Q}{RT} \;=\; \frac{77{,}000}{8.314\times 783} \;=\; \frac{77{,}000}{6510} \;\approx\; 11.83$$

**Step 3 — Compute D:**

$$D \;=\; 4.7\times 10^{-7}\cdot \exp(-11.83) \;=\; 4.7\times 10^{-7}\times 7.32\times 10^{-6}$$

$$
D \;\approx\; 3.44\times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert time:** $t = 30\times 3600 = 108{,}000\;\text{s}$.

**Step 5 — Case depth:**

$$
\sqrt{Dt} \;=\; \sqrt{3.44\times 10^{-12}\times 108{,}000}
\;=\; \sqrt{3.72\times 10^{-7}}
\;=\; 6.10\times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; 3.2 \times 6.10\times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.95\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 1.95\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Effect of doubling time:** Since $\delta \propto \sqrt{t}$, doubling $t$ gives:

$$
\delta_{\text{new}} \;=\; \sqrt{2}\,\delta \;\approx\; 1.41\times 1.95 \;\approx\; 2.75\;\text{mm}
$$

So the case depth increases by only **41 %**, not 100 % — confirming the sub-linear growth.

Mark split: D calculation (3) + δ calculation (3) + comment on doubling (1) = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module-4 Heat Treatment):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "above A₁" vs "above A₃":** Tempering is *below A₁*, normalizing is *above A₃* — students often interchange them. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting that nitriding needs NO quench:** Many students write "after nitriding, quench in oil" — this is **wrong**; nitriding forms nitrides directly without martensitic transformation. **–1 to –2 marks.**
> 
> 3. **Writing carburizing temperature as 500 – 550 °C:** That is *nitriding* range. Carburizing is 850 – 950 °C. Examiner flags this as concept confusion.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the white (compound) layer in nitriding microstructure:** Full marks only if you mention ε-Fe₂₋₃N + γ′-Fe₄N layer and note its brittleness.
> 
> 5. **Using wrong units in diffusion problems:** $D_0$ in m²/s, $t$ in seconds, $Q$ in J/mol (not kJ/mol). Convert carefully. Examiner deducts 0.5 – 1 mark for unit mistakes.
> 
> 6. **Not drawing the Fick's-law setup:** For 14-mark questions, even a small block diagram (furnace → NH₃ → dissociation → diffusion → case) earns an extra 1 – 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Heat Treatment, Surface Hardening & Nitriding (Module 4)**

- **Heat treatment** = controlled heating + cooling in solid state → alters microstructure, not shape.
- **A₁ = 727 °C**, **A₃ = 727 – 910 °C** (varies with carbon); remember these for all process classifications.
- **Annealing** = above A₃ + very slow furnace cool → softest state, coarse pearlite.
- **Normalizing** = above A₃ + air cool → fine pearlite + ferrite, refined grain.
- **Hardening (quenching)** = above A₃ + rapid cool → martensite (BCT), very hard but brittle.
- **Tempering** = 150 – 650 °C (below A₁) → tempered martensite, trade-off hardness for toughness.
- **Stress-relief anneal** = below A₁ (~ 600 °C) → no phase change, only stress removal.
- **Nitriding** = sub-critical diffusion of N at 500 – 550 °C, **no quench**, very low distortion.
- **Nitriding chemistry:** $\text{NH}_3 \to [\text{N}] + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2$.
- **Nitrided case layers:** (1) Compound white layer (ε + γ′), (2) Diffusion zone (alloy nitrides), (3) Core.
- **Surface hardness after nitriding:** up to **70 – 72 HRC** (1000 – 1200 HV).
- **Carburizing** uses low-carbon steel + high temperature (850 – 950 °C) + carbon diffusion + quench.
- **Induction hardening** uses medium-carbon steel + rapid localized heating + self-quench (no diffusion).
- **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ ; **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^{2}C/\partial x^{2}$.
- **Solution for constant-surface diffusion:** $\dfrac{C - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$.
- **Case depth scales as $\delta \propto \sqrt{t}$** (parabolic growth) — *doubling time gives only 41 % more depth*.
- **Arrhenius relation:** $D = D_0\exp(-Q/RT)$ — higher $T$ drastically increases $D$.
- **Archard wear equation:** $V = kWL/H$ → higher surface $H$ → lower wear → the *primary tribological justification* for surface hardening.
- **For Module-4 KTU answers:** always link heat treatment to a *tribological benefit* (wear, fatigue, scuffing) to score full conceptual marks.
- **One blank line before and after every display equation** — formatting counts in board exams.
- **Avoid quoting $T$ in °C inside exponentials** — always convert to **Kelvin** before computing $Q/RT$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coatings: PVD, Thermal Spray Coatings, and Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What Are Surface Coatings in Tribology?

In tribology, **surface coatings** are engineered thin or thick films deposited onto a substrate (the bulk material of a component) to enhance wear resistance, reduce friction, improve hardness, provide corrosion protection, and extend service life. They are a critical part of *surface engineering* — modifying only the surface rather than the entire bulk to achieve tribological performance economically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Surface coatings are thin layers of material (metallic, ceramic, polymeric, or composite) applied to the working surface of a component to control friction, wear, and surface degradation phenomena under relative motion and contact loading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Armour Analogy"

Imagine a medieval knight. Instead of making the *entire body* out of steel (which would be heavy, expensive, and impractical), he wears a thin, carefully designed **armour plate** on critical contact areas. The armour (coating) absorbs the blows, while the lighter inner garment (substrate) provides flexibility and bulk strength.

**In engineering:**
- The **substrate** = bulk metal (provides structural strength — like the knight's body)
- The **coating** = thin hard layer (provides wear/corrosion resistance — like the armour)

This is the essence of **surface engineering**: optimize the surface without sacrificing the bulk properties.

### 1.3 Why Are Coatings Needed in Tribology?

| Engineering Challenge | Role of Coating |
|---|---|
| Adhesive wear between sliding parts | Hard coating reduces metal-to-metal contact |
| Abrasive wear from hard particles | Ceramic coating resists cutting |
| High temperature operation | Thermal barrier coatings protect substrate |
| Corrosive environments | Chemically inert coatings isolate metal |
| Lubricant starvation | Solid lubricant coatings (MoS₂, DLC) provide self-lubrication |
| High contact pressure | Hard coatings (TiN, CrN) distribute load |

### 1.4 Classification of Coating Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> KTU classifies coating processes broadly into **three families** based on the deposition mechanism:

1. **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** — vapor-phase physical processes in vacuum
2. **Thermal Spray Coatings** — molten/semi-molten particles sprayed onto surface
3. **Electroplating** — electrochemical deposition from a liquid electrolyte

Let us examine each one rigorously.

---

## 1.5 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) — Formal Definition

**PVD** is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition process in which a target material is converted into a **vapor phase through physical means** (evaporation or sputtering) and transported at low pressure to condense onto a substrate, forming a thin, adherent, and typically dense coating (1 µm – 10 µm thick).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key defining feature:** PVD uses **physical** mechanisms (heat, momentum transfer) — *not chemical reactions* — to generate the vapor. The temperature is moderate (substrate typically stays below **500 °C**).

### 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — PVD as "Atmospheric Snowfall in a Vacuum Box"

Think of a sealed, dust-free box (the vacuum chamber). Inside, you place a small heated pot containing the coating material (e.g., titanium). When you heat the pot, titanium atoms "evaporate" upward like steam from a kettle. These atoms travel across the box and "snow down" onto your component (the substrate) which is placed cool. Over time, the atoms pile up to form a thin, uniform metallic layer — like snow on a mountain peak.

The critical insight: **snow forms only on a cold surface**, and **PVD works best when substrate is cooler than vapor source**. This is why PVD is a *cold* process relative to the substrate, preserving its bulk properties.

### 1.7 Thermal Spray Coating — Formal Definition

**Thermal spray** is a group of coating processes in which a coating material (in the form of powder, wire, or rod) is **melted or softened** by a heat source (flame, plasma, electric arc), accelerated as droplets/particles by a gas jet, and impacted onto a prepared substrate where they flatten, solidify, and build up a thick coating (typically **50 µm – several mm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key defining feature:** Thermal spray uses **high temperatures** (sometimes >10,000 K for plasma) to melt feedstock, and the **kinetic energy + thermal energy** of the impinging particles governs coating quality.

### 1.8 Intuitive Analogy — Thermal Spray as "Spray-Painting with Molten Metal"

Imagine spray-painting a wall — but instead of paint, you spray **molten metal droplets** at high velocity onto a surface. Each droplet flattens on impact like a tiny pancake, and successive droplets pile up to form a thick metallic layer. The wall (substrate) experiences brief, localized heat but never melts itself because each droplet is small and fast.

This is thermal spray: **atomize → melt → accelerate → impact → solidify**.

### 1.9 Electroplating — Formal Definition

**Electroplating** (also called electrodeposition) is an electrochemical coating process in which **metal ions from an aqueous solution are reduced and deposited as a solid metallic layer** on the cathode (the substrate) under the influence of an externally applied direct current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key defining feature:** It is a *chemical* (electrochemical) process driven by **Faraday's laws of electrolysis**, occurs in a **liquid electrolyte**, and operates at relatively **low temperatures (typically 20–80 °C)**.

### 1.10 Intuitive Analogy — Electroplating as "Metal Gardening"

Picture a tank of water with dissolved metal "seeds" (ions). You dip two electrodes connected to a battery into the tank. The negative electrode (your component) acts like a magnet that attracts the floating metal ions, which then "take root" and crystallize into a solid metallic layer on its surface. By controlling the current (like the flow of water) and time (like the season), you control how thick the metallic "garden" grows.

### 1.11 Comparative Snapshot of the Three Processes

| Feature | PVD | Thermal Spray | Electroplating |
|---|---|---|---|
| Medium | Vacuum | Open atmosphere / inert gas | Liquid electrolyte |
| Temperature | Low (substrate <500 °C) | Very high (plasma >10,000 K) | Low (room – 80 °C) |
| Coating thickness | 1–10 µm | 50 µm – few mm | 1–500 µm |
| Deposition rate | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| Adhesion | Excellent (atomic bond) | Good (mechanical + metallurgical) | Good (metallurgical bond) |
| Line-of-sight | Yes | Yes (less strict) | No (conformal) |
| Typical materials | TiN, CrN, DLC, AlTiN | Ceramics, cermets, polymers | Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn, Au |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Three Sub-Processes of PVD

Every PVD process, regardless of variant, involves three sequential physical steps:

1. **Vapor Generation** — converting solid target material into atoms/ions/molecules
2. **Vapor Transport** — movement of vapor through low-pressure environment to substrate
3. **Film Growth** — condensation, nucleation, and coalescence on substrate

### 2.1.2 Classification of PVD Methods

```
PVD
├── Evaporation
│   ├── Thermal (Resistance) Evaporation
│   ├── Electron Beam (E-Beam) Evaporation
│   ├── Laser Ablation
│   └── Arc Evaporation (Cathodic Arc)
├── Sputtering
│   ├── Diode Sputtering (DC/RF)
│   ├── Magnetron Sputtering
│   ├── Ion Beam Sputtering
│   └── Reactive Sputtering
└── Ion Plating
    └── Ion-assisted deposition
```

### 2.1.3 Evaporation Process

**Principle:** The target material is heated to its **vapor pressure** sufficiently high to produce a usable flux of atoms. Vapor condenses on the cooler substrate.

- **Vacuum level:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa
- **Mechanism:** purely thermal — atoms leave the surface when their kinetic energy exceeds the binding energy

**Vapor Pressure (for evaporation rate calculation):**
The evaporation rate depends on the **Langmuir equation**, derived from kinetic theory:

$$ R_{evap} = \frac{p_s \cdot N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}} $$

Where:
- $R_{evap}$ = evaporation rate (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $p_s$ = saturation vapor pressure (Pa)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Clausius–Clapeyron Relation (for vapor pressure):**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{p_s}{p_0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b}\right) $$

Where:
- $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporization (J/mol)
- $T_b$ = normal boiling point (K)
- $p_0$ = atmospheric pressure (Pa)

### 2.1.4 Electron Beam (E-Beam) Evaporation

An electron beam (typically 5–15 keV, focused by magnetic fields) is directed at the target in a water-cooled crucible. Energy densities can exceed $10^9$ W/m², allowing evaporation of high-melting-point materials (refractory metals, oxides).

> [!NOTE]
> The water-cooled copper crucible prevents the crucible itself from contaminating the melt — only the central spot where the beam hits evaporates.

### 2.1.5 Sputtering Process

**Principle:** Ions (typically Ar⁺) generated in a plasma are accelerated toward the **target (cathode)**. Upon impact, they transfer momentum and eject (sputter) target atoms, which then travel to and condense on the substrate.

**Sputtering Yield (key parameter):**

$$ S = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}} $$

**Energy dependence of S:** $S$ rises from a threshold (around 10–30 eV), peaks at energies of 10–100 keV, then declines. Typical values for Ar⁺ on metals at 500 eV: $S \approx 0.5$–$2.0$ atoms/ion.

**Why Magnetron Sputtering?**
- A magnetic field near the target traps electrons, increasing ionization efficiency.
- Result: **10–100× higher deposition rate** than diode sputtering.
- Substrate heating is reduced — good for temperature-sensitive materials.

### 2.1.6 Cathodic Arc Deposition

A high-current, low-voltage arc strikes the target surface, creating a tiny (a few µm) **cathode spot** with current densities of $10^{12}$ A/m² and temperatures of **15,000–20,000 K**. The spot moves rapidly, vaporizing and ionizing ~30–100% of target material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Macroparticles ("droplets")** — a known issue. They create surface defects. Solutions: filtered cathodic vacuum arc (FCVA), magnetic plasma duct, or off-axis geometry.

### 2.1.7 Film Growth Stages

```
Adsorption of atoms → Nucleation → Island growth → Coalescence → Channel growth → Continuous film
```

| Stage | Description | Critical factors |
|---|---|---|
| Adsorption | Atoms arrive, lose kinetic energy, become "adatoms" | Substrate temperature |
| Nucleation | Adatoms cluster to form stable nuclei | Surface energy, mobility |
| Island growth | Nuclei grow into 3D islands | Adatom surface diffusion |
| Coalescence | Islands merge | Mobility, time |
| Channel → continuous | Film becomes uniform | Coverage |

### 2.1.8 Common PVD Tribological Coatings

| Coating | Hardness (HV) | Friction coeff. (dry) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| TiN (Titanium Nitride) | 2300 | 0.4–0.6 | Cutting tools, drills |
| CrN (Chromium Nitride) | 1750 | 0.3–0.5 | Automotive, dies |
| TiAlN (Titanium Aluminium Nitride) | 2800 | 0.4–0.7 | High-speed machining |
| DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon) | 1000–5000 | 0.05–0.2 | Engine components, biomedical |
| MoS₂ (sputtered) | soft | 0.02–0.1 | Space, vacuum lubrication |

---

## 2.2 Thermal Spray Coating — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Process Variants

| Variant | Heat Source | Particle Velocity | Temperature | Coating Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flame spray** | Oxy-acetylene | 30–80 m/s | 3000 °C | Polymer, ceramic, metal |
| **HVOF** | Combustion (high velocity) | 600–1000 m/s | 2800 °C | Carbides, cermets |
| **Detonation Gun** | Gas detonation | 800–1200 m/s | 4000 °C | Dense ceramic coatings |
| **Plasma spray** | DC electric arc plasma | 100–600 m/s | 10,000–16,000 K | Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) |
| **Cold spray** | None — kinetic only | 500–1200 m/s | <600 °C | Pure metals, no oxidation |
| **Arc spray** | Electric arc (between wires) | 100–300 m/s | 4000 °C | Zn, Al, steel |

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operation (Generic)

1. **Feedstock injection** — powder/wire/rod fed at controlled rate
2. **Melting/softening** — heat source raises temperature above melting point
3. **Atomization & acceleration** — gas jet (or arc plasma) propels droplets
4. **Flight** — droplets travel through air/inert gas (typically <50 cm)
5. **Impact & flattening** — droplet strikes substrate, spreads as "splats"
6. **Solidification** — cools in microseconds → layered splat structure
7. **Coating build-up** — successive passes build thickness

### 2.2.3 Splat Formation and Adhesion

The **Bonding** between splat and substrate can be:
- **Mechanical interlocking** — dominant for rough substrates (roughened by grit blasting)
- **Metallurgical bonding** — local diffusion at contact points
- **Physical bonding** — van der Waals

> [!NOTE]
> **Substrate preparation is critical**: grit blasting with Al₂O₃ or SiC creates a roughness of **Ra = 5–10 µm**, which dramatically improves mechanical interlocking.

### 2.2.4 HVOF (High Velocity Oxy-Fuel) — Detailed

**Fuel options:** kerosene, propane, propylene, hydrogen
**Process:**
- Combustion gases at pressures of 0.5–1.5 MPa
- Gas velocities reach **Mach 1 or higher** at the nozzle exit
- Particles injected axially into the jet

**Why HVOF is preferred for tribology:**
- High kinetic energy → **dense coatings** (porosity <1%)
- Low thermal energy → **minimal oxidation, phase decomposition**
- Excellent bond strength (**> 70 MPa**)
- Ideal for **WC-CoCr** (tungsten carbide-cobalt-chrome) — top choice for wear resistance

### 2.2.5 Plasma Spray — Detailed

A **DC arc** between a tungsten cathode and copper anode ionizes argon/hydrogen/nitrogen gas, forming a plasma jet at **10,000–16,000 K**. Powder injected radially or axially melts and is projected onto the substrate.

**Applications in tribology:**
- **Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBCs)** — Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) on turbine blades
- **Wear-resistant ceramic coatings** — Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃
- **Diesel engine cylinder bores**

### 2.2.6 Key Performance Metrics

**Porosity:**
$$ \text{Porosity (\%)} = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100 $$

**Bond Strength** — measured by ASTM C633 tensile pull test

**Hardness** — micro-Vickers (HV 0.3)

**Wear Rate (ASTM G99 pin-on-disc):**
$$ k = \frac{V}{F \cdot s} $$

Where $k$ = specific wear rate (mm³/N·m), $V$ = volume loss (mm³), $F$ = normal load (N), $s$ = sliding distance (m).

---

## 2.3 Electroplating — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Electrochemical Cell Setup

```
DC Power Supply
   (+) Anode — pure metal to be deposited (e.g., Cr)
   (–) Cathode — the component to be coated (substrate)
   |    |
   Electrolyte — aqueous solution with metal ions
```

### 2.3.2 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Foundational!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Law (1834):** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

$$ m = Z \cdot I \cdot t $$

**Second Law:** The mass of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity is proportional to their equivalent weights.

$$ m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

Where:
- $m$ = mass deposited (g)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $Q = I \cdot t$ = total charge (C)
- $M$ = atomic weight of deposited metal (g/mol)
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons per ion)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol)
- $Z = M/(nF)$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C)

### 2.3.3 Electrochemical Equivalent Table

| Metal | Atomic Mass $M$ (g/mol) | Valency $n$ | $Z = M/(nF)$ (g/C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chromium (Cr) | 52.0 | 3 (or 6) | $1.80 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Nickel (Ni) | 58.7 | 2 | $3.04 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Copper (Cu) | 63.5 | 2 | $3.29 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Zinc (Zn) | 65.4 | 2 | $3.39 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Silver (Ag) | 107.9 | 1 | $1.118 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Gold (Au) | 197.0 | 3 | $6.81 \times 10^{-4}$ |

### 2.3.4 Current Efficiency (Cathode Efficiency)

Not all current goes into metal deposition — side reactions (especially **hydrogen evolution**) consume charge.

$$ \eta_c = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\% $$

Where $m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$

For **hard chromium plating**, $\eta_c$ is often only **10–25%** due to vigorous hydrogen evolution. This is a critical parameter in tribological applications.

### 2.3.5 Coating Thickness Prediction

Combining Faraday's law with density:

$$ t_{coat} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta_c}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho} $$

Where:
- $t_{coat}$ = thickness (cm)
- $A$ = coated area (cm²)
- $\rho$ = density of deposited metal (g/cm³)
- $I/A$ = **current density** $J$ (A/cm² or A/dm²)

> [!NOTE]
> In plating shops, current density is often expressed in **A/dm²** (ASD — Amps per Square Decimeter). A typical chromium plating uses **J = 30–60 A/dm²**.

### 2.3.6 Common Electroplated Tribological Coatings

| Coating | Hardness (HV) | Friction coeff. | Tribological application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard Chromium | 850–1100 | 0.15–0.30 | Hydraulic pistons, gears, shafts |
| Electroless Ni-P | 500–1100 | 0.20–0.40 | Pumps, valves, moulds |
| Electroless Ni-B | 600–1200 | 0.15–0.35 | Aerospace, automotive |
| Cu underplate | 50–100 | 0.40 | Base for multi-layer systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Chromium vs Decorative Chromium:**
> - Hard Cr: thick (10–500 µm), no underplate, used for wear resistance
> - Decorative Cr: thin (0.25–1 µm), over Ni underplate, used for aesthetics

### 2.3.7 Bath Composition Examples

**Hard Chromium Plating Bath:**
- Chromic acid (CrO₃): 200–400 g/L
- Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄): 2–4 g/L (catalyst)
- Temperature: 50–60 °C
- Current density: 30–60 A/dm²

**Sulfamate Nickel Bath:**
- Nickel sulfamate: 300–450 g/L
- Boric acid (buffer): 30–45 g/L
- pH: 3.5–4.5
- Temperature: 45–55 °C

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Quick Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are high-yield for KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass deposited (Faraday's 2nd law) | g |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{M}{nF}$ | Electrochemical equivalent | g/C |
| 3 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Cathode efficiency | % |
| 4 | $t_{coat} = \dfrac{J \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta_c}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Coating thickness | cm |
| 5 | $R_{evap} = \dfrac{p_s \cdot N_A}{\sqrt{2 \pi M R T}}$ | Evaporation rate (Langmuir) | kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\ln\dfrac{p_s}{p_0} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$ | Vapor pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{N_{atoms\,ejected}}{N_{incident\,ions}}$ | Sputtering yield | atoms/ion |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{V}{F \cdot s}$ | Specific wear rate | mm³/(N·m) |
| 9 | $\text{Porosity} = \dfrac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Coating porosity | % |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** TBCs on turbine blades (plasma spray), TiN-coated bearings, hard Cr on landing gear
- **Automotive:** Cr-plated piston rings, DLC-coated valve lifters, HVOF coatings on brake discs
- **Manufacturing:** TiAlN-coated cutting tools (PVD) — life extended by 3–10×
- **Biomedical:** TiN and DLC coatings on orthopedic implants
- **Oil & Gas:** HVOF WC-CoCr on drill bits and valve components
- **Space:** Sputtered MoS₂ films in vacuum — solid lubrication where oils fail

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law from Electrochemical Fundamentals

**Starting point:** When a metal ion $M^{n+}$ is reduced at the cathode:

$$ M^{n+} + n e^- \rightarrow M $$

**Step 1 — Charge consumed per mole of metal:**
For each mole of $M$ deposited, **$n$ moles of electrons** are required.
Charge per mole of electrons = **$F$** = 96,485 C/mol.
Therefore, charge per mole of metal = $nF$.

**Step 2 — Relating mass to charge:**
For $\dfrac{m}{M}$ moles of metal, charge consumed = $\dfrac{m}{M} \cdot nF$.
Total charge passed = $Q = I \cdot t$.

**Step 3 — Equating charge:**

$$ Q = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M} $$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for mass:**

$$ m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

**This is Faraday's Second Law.** ✅

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1: Mass and Thickness of Chromium Plating

> **Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**
> A steel shaft of surface area **400 cm²** is to be hard-chrome plated. The plating bath operates at a current density of **50 A/dm²** with a cathode efficiency of **20%**. If plating time is **2 hours**, calculate:
> (a) Mass of chromium deposited
> (b) Average coating thickness
> (c) Compare with the case if 100% efficient

**Given data:**
- $A = 400$ cm² = **4 dm²**
- $J = 50$ A/dm²
- $\eta_c = 20\% = 0.20$
- $t = 2$ h = 7200 s
- Chromium: $M = 52.0$ g/mol, $n = 3$ (Cr³⁺ ion), $\rho = 7.19$ g/cm³

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate total current $I$**

$$ I = J \times A = 50 \;\text{A/dm}^2 \times 4 \;\text{dm}^2 = 200 \;\text{A} $$

**Step 2: Theoretical mass (100% efficiency)**

$$ m_{th} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

$$ m_{th} = \frac{200 \times 7200 \times 52.0}{3 \times 96485} $$

Let me compute numerator and denominator separately:

$$ \text{Numerator} = 200 \times 7200 \times 52.0 $$

$$ = 200 \times 7200 = 1{,}440{,}000 $$

$$ = 1{,}440{,}000 \times 52.0 = 74{,}880{,}000 \;\text{g·C/mol·...} $$

Wait — let me handle units carefully:

$$ m_{th} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{[A]\cdot[s]\cdot[g/mol]}{[C/mol]} $$

Since $A \cdot s = C$, this gives grams. ✅

$$ m_{th} = \frac{200 \times 7200 \times 52.0}{3 \times 96485} $$

$$ = \frac{74{,}880{,}000}{289{,}455} $$

$$ \approx 258.7 \;\text{g} $$

**Step 3: Actual mass (with 20% efficiency)**

$$ m_{actual} = \eta_c \times m_{th} = 0.20 \times 258.7 = 51.74 \;\text{g} $$

**Step 4: Coating thickness**

$$ t_{coat} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{51.74}{7.19 \times 400} $$

$$ = \frac{51.74}{2876} $$

$$ \approx 0.01799 \;\text{cm} = 179.9 \;\mu m \approx 180 \;\mu m $$

**Step 5: If 100% efficient**

$$ m_{100\%} = 258.7 \;\text{g} $$

$$ t_{100\%} = \frac{258.7}{2876} = 0.0899 \;\text{cm} = 899 \;\mu m \approx 900 \;\mu m $$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | 20% efficiency | 100% efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Mass deposited | **51.74 g** | 258.7 g |
| Thickness | **180 µm** | 900 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Chromium plating is *very* inefficient — only ~20% of current actually deposits metal; the rest liberates H₂ gas. This is why chrome plating is energy-intensive and slow.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2: Vapor Pressure Using Clausius–Clapeyron

> **Problem Statement:**
> For titanium (Ti), $\Delta H_{vap} = 421$ kJ/mol and $T_b = 3560$ K. Calculate the saturation vapor pressure of Ti at the typical PVD source temperature of $T = 2500$ K. (At $T_b$, $p_s = p_0 = 101,325$ Pa.)

**Solution:**

$$ \ln\left(\frac{p_s}{p_0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b}\right) $$

**Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}$

$$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{3560} = 2.809 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} - 2.809 \times 10^{-4} = 1.191 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}$

$$ \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} = \frac{421{,}000}{8.314} = 50{,}637 \;\text{K} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute

$$ \ln\left(\frac{p_s}{p_0}\right) = -50{,}637 \times 1.191 \times 10^{-4} = -6.031 $$

**Step 4:** Take exponential

$$ \frac{p_s}{p_0} = e^{-6.031} = 2.395 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $p_s$

$$ p_s = 0.002395 \times 101{,}325 \approx 242.7 \;\text{Pa} $$

**Interpretation:** At 2500 K, titanium vapor pressure is ~243 Pa. In PVD chamber at $10^{-3}$ Pa, the vapor is highly supersaturated, ensuring rapid evaporation and deposition. ✅

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 3: Time Required for Specified Coating Thickness

> **Problem:**
> A copper-plated component (area = 1000 cm²) requires a Cu coating of 50 µm. Cathode efficiency = 95%, current density = 4 A/dm². Find required time.

**Given:**
- $A = 1000$ cm² = 10 dm²
- $t_{coat} = 50 \;\mu m = 50 \times 10^{-4}$ cm
- $\eta_c = 0.95$
- $J = 4$ A/dm²
- Cu: $M = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total current

$$ I = J \times A = 4 \times 10 = 40 \;\text{A} $$

**Step 2:** Mass needed

$$ m = \rho \cdot t_{coat} \cdot A = 8.96 \times (50 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1000 $$

$$ = 8.96 \times 5 = 44.8 \;\text{g} $$

**Step 3:** From Faraday's law with efficiency

$$ t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta_c \cdot I \cdot M} $$

$$ = \frac{44.8 \times 2 \times 96485}{0.95 \times 40 \times 63.5} $$

**Numerator:** $44.8 \times 2 \times 96485 = 89.6 \times 96485 = 8{,}645{,}856$

**Denominator:** $0.95 \times 40 \times 63.5 = 38 \times 63.5 = 2413$

$$ t = \frac{8{,}645{,}856}{2413} \approx 3583 \;\text{s} \approx 59.7 \;\text{min} \approx 1 \;\text{hour} $$

**Result:** ~1 hour plating time. ✅

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 4: HVOF Particle Velocity Estimation

> **Problem:**
> In HVOF, fuel (hydrogen) and oxygen are combusted. Combustion products exit at $T = 2900$ K through a converging nozzle. Using energy/momentum balance, estimate particle velocity if gas velocity reaches ~Mach 1.8.

**Solution Approach:**

Gas velocity using Mach number:

$$ V_{gas} = M \cdot \sqrt{\gamma R T} $$

Where $\gamma \approx 1.33$ for hot combustion gases, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) for gas.

$$ V_{gas} = 1.8 \times \sqrt{1.33 \times 287 \times 2900} $$

$$ = 1.8 \times \sqrt{1{,}106{,}919} $$

$$ = 1.8 \times 1052 \approx 1894 \;\text{m/s} $$

Particles (smaller than gas molecules) lag gas by drag-coupled acceleration. Empirical result: $V_{particle} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.6 \times V_{gas}$

$$ V_{particle} \approx 0.55 \times 1894 \approx 1042 \;\text{m/s} $$

This matches typical HVOF particle velocities of **800–1200 m/s**. ✅

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Choosing the Right Coating for an Application

> **Problem:**
> A cutting tool must machine Ti-6Al-4V alloy at high speed (high temperature). The tool substrate is high-speed steel (HSS, softens above 550 °C). Recommend a coating strategy with justification.

**Solution reasoning:**
1. **Substrate temp limit:** ~550 °C → low-temperature deposition needed
2. **Hard workpiece:** requires very hard coating (>> HSS)
3. **High cutting temperature:** oxidation resistance needed

**Best choice:** **PVD TiAlN** coating (3–5 µm)
- Deposition temp: ~450 °C (safe for HSS)
- Hardness: 2800 HV (vs ~850 HV for HSS)
- Forms protective Al₂O₃ layer at high T (oxidation resistance up to 800 °C)
- Low thermal conductivity → heat barrier

**Why not thermal spray?** Coatings too thick (>50 µm), would change tool geometry, adhesion weaker, high temp risks HSS softening.

**Why not electroplating?** Hard Cr needs ~50–60 °C bath, but Cr is softer than TiAlN and not as oxidation-resistant. Still used for some drills, but inferior.

✅ **PVD TiAlN is the correct engineering choice.**
