# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD). List any two advantages and two limitations of PVD coatings in tribological applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** PVD is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition process in which a target material is vaporized by physical means (thermal evaporation or sputtering) and deposited onto a substrate at low pressure (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa) to form a coating, generally 1–10 µm thick.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. Excellent adhesion (atomic-scale bonding)
2. Wide range of coating materials (metals, ceramics, compounds)
3. Low substrate temperature (<500 °C) — preserves bulk properties
4. Environmentally cleaner than electroplating (no toxic effluents)

**Limitations (any two):**
1. Line-of-sight process — poor coverage of complex geometries
2. Expensive vacuum equipment
3. Low deposition rate compared to thermal spray
4. Coating thickness limited to ~10 µm typically

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Faraday's constant? Why is cathode efficiency less than 100% in chromium plating?**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96,485$ C/mol. It represents the charge carried by one mole of electrons, i.e., the quantity of electricity required to liberate one gram-equivalent of any element during electrolysis.

**Why cathode efficiency < 100% in chromium plating:**
In hard chromium plating, the dominant side reaction is **hydrogen evolution** at the cathode. A large fraction (often 75–85%) of the current is consumed in reducing $H^+$ ions to $H_2$ gas, which escapes as bubbles. Only the remaining 15–25% of current actually reduces $Cr^{6+}$ to metallic Cr. Additionally, Cr uses a complex multi-step reduction (Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ → Cr⁰), further lowering efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **Physical Vapor Deposition process** with neat sketches. Discuss in detail the **sputtering mechanism** and the role of **magnetron sputtering** in enhancing deposition rates.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A steel component of surface area **500 cm²** is hard-chrome plated at a current density of **40 A/dm²** for **3 hours**. The cathode efficiency is **22%**. Calculate:
1. Mass of chromium deposited
2. Coating thickness (assume Cr density = 7.19 g/cm³)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:

**Part (a) — PVD Process & Sputtering:**

**PVD Definition:** Physical Vapor Deposition is a vacuum process in which a target material is converted to vapor by physical means and deposited as a thin film on a substrate.

**Basic Steps:**
1. **Vapor generation** — target material atomized
2. **Vapor transport** — atoms travel through vacuum to substrate
3. **Film growth** — condensation and nucleation on substrate

**Sputtering Mechanism:**
- A plasma is created by applying a high voltage (typically 300–1000 V DC) between the target (cathode) and substrate (anode) in a low-pressure argon atmosphere ($10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ Pa).
- Free electrons in the chamber ionize Ar atoms: $Ar \rightarrow Ar^+ + e^-$
- Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated toward the target cathode.
- Upon impact, momentum is transferred to target atoms.
- When the momentum transfer exceeds the surface binding energy, **target atoms are ejected** (sputtered).
- Sputtered atoms travel ballistically to the substrate and condense into a thin film.

**Sputtering Yield** $S$ = atoms ejected per incident ion (typically 0.5–2.0 for metals at 500 eV).

**Magnetron Sputtering:**
- Permanent magnets placed behind the target create a magnetic field parallel to the target surface.
- Electrons (which ionize Ar) are **confined near the target** by $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ drift, creating a dense plasma.
- Result: **ionization efficiency increases 10–100×**.
- Consequences:
  - Higher deposition rate
  - Lower operating pressure possible
  - Reduced substrate heating (good for temperature-sensitive materials)
  - Better film quality and step coverage

[Diagram Reference: A simple cathode-magnet-anode configuration with magnetic field lines and electron cycloid paths can be drawn in the exam.]

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 500$ cm² = **5 dm²**
- Current density $J = 40$ A/dm²
- Time $t = 3$ h = **10,800 s**
- Cathode efficiency $\eta_c = 22\% = 0.22$
- Chromium: $M = 52$ g/mol, $n = 3$, $\rho = 7.19$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Calculate total current:**

$$ I = J \cdot A = 40 \times 5 = 200 \;\text{A} $$

**Step 2 — Theoretical mass:**

$$ m_{th} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

$$ = \frac{200 \times 10{,}800 \times 52}{3 \times 96{,}485} $$

$$ = \frac{112{,}320{,}000}{289{,}455} $$

$$ \approx 388.1 \;\text{g} $$

**Step 3 — Actual mass:**

$$ m_{actual} = \eta_c \cdot m_{th} = 0.22 \times 388.1 \approx 85.4 \;\text{g} $$

**Step 4 — Coating thickness:**

$$ t_{coat} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{85.4}{7.19 \times 500} = \frac{85.4}{3595} \approx 0.0238 \;\text{cm} \approx 238 \;\mu m $$

**Final Answer:**
1. Mass deposited ≈ **85.4 g**
2. Coating thickness ≈ **238 µm**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **Thermal Spray Coating process** with a neat diagram. Compare **HVOF and Plasma Spray** techniques with respect to particle velocity, coating density, and typical applications.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
With the help of Faraday's laws, derive the **expression for coating thickness in electroplating**. State clearly the assumptions made.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:

**Part (a) — Thermal Spray & HVOF vs Plasma:**

**Thermal Spray Process:**
A family of processes in which feedstock material (powder/wire/rod) is melted by a heat source, accelerated as droplets by a gas stream, and impacted onto a substrate to form a coating by successive splat layering.

**Steps:**
1. Feedstock introduction
2. Melting in heat source
3. Atomization and acceleration
4. In-flight droplet transport
5. Impact and splat formation
6. Rapid solidification and build-up

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | HVOF | Plasma Spray |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Combustion (fuel + O₂) | Electric arc plasma |
| Temperature | ~2,800 °C | 10,000–16,000 K |
| Particle velocity | 600–1000 m/s | 100–600 m/s |
| Coating porosity | <1% (very dense) | 2–10% |
| Bond strength | >70 MPa | 30–60 MPa |
| Phase stability | Preserved (low T) | Risk of phase change |
| Typical materials | WC-CoCr, Cr₃C₂-NiCr | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ (YSZ) |
| Applications | Hydraulic shafts, ball valves, aircraft landing gear | Thermal barrier coatings on turbine blades, ceramic wear coatings |

**Key distinction:**
- **HVOF = high velocity, lower temperature** → dense, oxide-free coatings ideal for carbide cermets.
- **Plasma = very high temperature** → can melt any ceramic, but coatings are more porous.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Coating Thickness:**

**Faraday's Second Law:**

$$ m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1) $$

**Including cathode efficiency:**

$$ m_{actual} = \eta_c \cdot m_{th} = \eta_c \cdot \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad \quad (2) $$

**Mass of coating = density × volume:**

$$ m_{actual} = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot t_{coat} \cdot A \quad \quad (3) $$

where $t_{coat}$ is the thickness and $A$ is the coated area.

**Equating (2) and (3):**

$$ \rho \cdot t_{coat} \cdot A = \eta_c \cdot \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

**Solving for $t_{coat}$:**

$$ \boxed{\, t_{coat} = \frac{\eta_c \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} \,} $$

Or equivalently, with $J = I/A$:

$$ t_{coat} = \frac{\eta_c \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} $$

**Assumptions:**
1. Coating is **uniform and dense** (no porosity)
2. **Single metal ion** with consistent valency $n$ throughout deposition
3. **No competing side reactions** at the cathode (or efficiency accounts for them)
4. **Current is 100% utilized** in proportion to $\eta_c$
5. **Density $\rho$ of deposit = density of bulk metal** (often slightly different)
6. **Area $A$ is constant** during deposition

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in Faraday problems:** Always convert area from cm² to dm² when using A/dm² for current density. Mixing units is the #1 error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting cathode efficiency:** Many students compute only theoretical mass. Always check the question for $\eta_c$ — if absent, assume 100%.
>
> 3. **Confusing valency for Cr:** Chromium can be Cr³⁺ or Cr⁶⁺. In hard chrome plating, the *effective* $n$ used is often **3** (Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ → Cr⁰ reduction chain). Use $n = 6$ only if explicitly stated. Default: **$n = 3$**.
>
> 4. **Mistaking PVD for CVD:** PVD uses **physical** vapor generation (no chemical reaction at substrate). CVD uses **chemical** reactions (e.g., TiCl₄ + NH₃ → TiN + HCl).
>
> 5. **Spelling "Langmuir" and "Clausius–Clapeyron" correctly** — examiners award marks for named equations.
>
> 6. **Diagram labeling:** Always label all components of any coating system diagram (vacuum chamber, target, substrate, power supply, gas inlet, etc.). Unlabeled diagrams = zero marks.
>
> 7. **Confusing HVOF with plasma:** HVOF is **kinetic-dominated** (high velocity), plasma is **thermal-dominated** (high temperature). Many students mix these up.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before KTU Exam**

### PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition)
- ✅ PVD = vacuum process, **physical** vapor generation only
- ✅ Three variants: **Evaporation, Sputtering, Ion Plating**
- ✅ Substrate temperature < 500 °C (low-temperature process)
- ✅ Coating thickness **1–10 µm** (thin films)
- ✅ Langmuir equation for evaporation rate; Clausius–Clapeyron for vapor pressure
- ✅ Sputtering yield $S$ depends on ion energy, mass, and target binding
- ✅ **Magnetron sputtering** increases ionization efficiency by 10–100×
- ✅ Common coatings: **TiN (gold), CrN, TiAlN, DLC, MoS₂**

### Thermal Spray Coating
- ✅ Thermal spray uses **high temperature + kinetic energy** of droplets
- ✅ Coating thickness **50 µm – few mm** (thick coatings)
- ✅ **HVOF:** high velocity (600–1000 m/s), low temp — dense coatings, cermets (WC-CoCr)
- ✅ **Plasma spray:** very high temp (10,000 K+) — ceramics, thermal barriers (YSZ)
- ✅ **Substrate prep:** grit blasting (Ra = 5–10 µm) essential for adhesion
- ✅ Bonding: mechanical interlocking + metallurgical + physical

### Electroplating
- ✅ Faraday's **Second Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- ✅ Cathode efficiency $\eta_c$ accounts for side reactions (esp. H₂ evolution)
- ✅ **Coating thickness formula:** $t_{coat} = \dfrac{\eta_c \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$
- ✅ Chromium plating: $\eta_c \approx 10$–$25\%$ (very inefficient!)
- ✅ Hard Chrome = thick (10–500 µm), wear applications
- ✅ Decorative Chrome = thin (<1 µm), over Ni underplate

### Process Selection Heuristics
- ✅ Need **thin (< 10 µm), hard, sharp edges preserved** → **PVD**
- ✅ Need **thick (> 50 µm), ceramic, or repair work** → **Thermal Spray**
- ✅ Need **conformality on complex shapes, or metallic thickness 10–500 µm** → **Electroplating**
- ✅ Need **lubrication in vacuum** → **sputtered MoS₂ or DLC**
- ✅ Need **oxidation resistance at high temperature** → **plasma-sprayed YSZ (TBC)**

### Numerical Problem Solving Template (Faraday)
1. Identify $M$, $n$, $\rho$ for the metal
2. Compute $I = J \times A$ (watch unit conversions!)
3. Convert time to seconds
4. Apply $m = \eta_c \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M / (nF)$
5. Compute thickness using $t_{coat} = m / (\rho A)$

### Final Quick-Reference Numbers
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
- Typical PVD pressure: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa
- Plasma spray temp: ~15,000 K
- HVOF particle velocity: ~1000 m/s
- Chrome plating $\eta_c$: ~20%

> **Best of luck for your KTU examinations!** Mastering these three coating families — PVD, Thermal Spray, and Electroplating — gives you a complete picture of how modern tribological surfaces are engineered. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tribology in Industries: Tribological Challenges in Engines, Transmissions, and Braking Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tribology in Industries: Tribological Challenges in Engines, Transmissions, and Braking Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Tribology in Industries — Engines, Transmissions & Braking Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEMUT754, Module 4):** Tribology in Industries focuses on the application of friction, wear, and lubrication principles to real-world mechanical systems. The three pillars covered are **Internal Combustion Engines**, **Mechanical Transmissions**, and **Friction-Based Braking Systems**.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Industrial Tribology** is the branch of engineering science that applies the principles of friction, wear, and lubrication to the design, analysis, and maintenance of interacting surfaces in relative motion within industrial machinery. It encompasses the study of contacting surfaces (tribo-pairs), the lubricant films separating them, and the resulting phenomena of friction and wear under operational loads, speeds, and temperatures.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Module 4**, the definition is extended specifically to:
- **Engine Tribology:** Tribology of the piston ring–cylinder liner, valve train, and crankshaft bearings.
- **Transmission Tribology:** Tribology of gear teeth, rolling-element/sliding bearings, constant-velocity (CV) joints, and synchroniser rings.
- **Brake Tribology:** Tribology of disc pads, drum shoes, rotor/drum interfaces, and brake fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Tribological Quantities (Industry Standards):**
> - **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** Unitless, typically **0.001 to 0.1 (lubricated)**, **0.3 to 0.7 (brakes)**.
> - **Specific Wear Rate ($k$):** Expressed in **mm³/N·m**, governed by Archard's equation.
> - **Viscosity ($\eta$):** Expressed in **Pa·s** or **cSt (centistokes)**.
> - **PV Limit:** Pressure × Velocity product (MPa·m/s), a critical design metric for bearings and gears.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Spoon Test":**
> Imagine dragging a metal spoon across a dry steel plate versus across a plate smeared with cooking oil. The dry case produces harsh, audible scraping (high friction, severe wear) — this mimics an **unlubricated engine bore during cold-start**. The oiled case glides smoothly with minimal resistance — this mimics a **fully hydrodynamic journal bearing** at design speed. Industrial tribology is simply the science of ensuring that every moving machine element behaves like the *oiled spoon*, except for the brake, where we *intentionally* want the *dry spoon* behaviour but with predictable, controlled wear!

**Why this matters in industry:**
- **Engines** — poor tribology → blow-by, oil consumption, scuffing, seizure.
- **Transmissions** — poor tribology → gear pitting, spalling, bearing fatigue, whine.
- **Brakes** — poor tribology → brake fade, judder, uneven wear, longer stopping distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tribological Subsystems in Industrial Machines

Every industrial tribo-system consists of four essential elements. Recognising these is **critical** for KTU exam answers.

| # | Element | Description | Industry Example |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Tribo-element 1** | Moving body | Piston ring |
| 2 | **Tribo-element 2** | Counter-body | Cylinder liner |
| 3 | **Interfacial medium** | Lubricant or environment | SAE 10W-30 oil |
| 4 | **Surrounding atmosphere** | Ambient conditions | Combustion gases, dust |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Identify the tribo-system in a given component", always list these **four elements** explicitly for full marks.

---

## 2.2 Tribological Challenges in Internal Combustion (IC) Engines

### 2.2.1 Piston Ring – Cylinder Liner Interface

This is the **most severe tribo-contact** in any engine.

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Temperature:** $200^\circ C$ (top dead centre) to $40^\circ C$ (crankcase).
- **Pressure (combustion):** Up to **$100$ bar ($10$ MPa)** peak gas pressure.
- **Speed (mean piston):** $5$ to $15$ m/s in passenger cars.
- **Lubrication regime:** Mostly **mixed / boundary**, briefly **hydrodynamic** mid-stroke.

**Tribological Challenges:**
1. **Scuffing (adhesive wear):** Metal-to-metal contact when oil film breaks down.
2. **Abrasive wear:** Combustion dust, soot, and carbon particles act as third-body abrasives.
3. **Corrosive wear:** Acidic blow-by gases ($\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$, $\mathrm{HNO_3}$) attack the liner.
4. **Ring fluttering:** At high RPM, gas pressure behind ring lifts it, causing uneven contact.

### 2.2.2 Valve Train (Camshaft – Follower)

**Operating Conditions:**
- Hertzian contact stress up to **$1000$ MPa**.
- Sliding-rolling motion with high accelerations.
- **Boundary lubrication** dominates (low oil supply, high temperature).

**Tribological Challenges:**
- **Pitting fatigue** of cam lobes.
- **Adhesive wear** at cam-follower interface.
- **Camshaft bearing wear** in overhead cam engines.

### 2.2.3 Crankshaft Bearings (Main and Big-End)

**Operating Conditions:**
- Rotational speeds: $1000$ to $7000$ rpm.
- Specific load: $10$ to $30$ MPa.
- Lubrication regime: **Full hydrodynamic** (theoretically).

**Tribological Challenges:**
- **Cavitation** in the oil film.
- **Fatigue spalling** of the bearing lining (white-metal, babbitt, Al-Sn).
- **Oil whirl** instability at high speeds.

---

## 2.3 Tribological Challenges in Transmissions

### 2.3.1 Gear Tribology

Gears are **line contacts** or **point contacts** with rolling + sliding components.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Hertzian contact stress: $500$ to $2000$ MPa.
- Sliding-to-rolling ratio: $0$ (pitch point) to high (at tooth tip/root).
- Lubricant: Extreme Pressure (EP) gear oils with **S-P** additives.

**Tribological Challenges:**
1. **Pitting (surface fatigue):** Subsurface cracks propagate to surface.
2. **Scuffing (scoring):** Welds form at asperity level and tear off.
3. **Abrasive wear:** Debris in oil.
4. **Tooth deflection → uneven load distribution.**

### 2.3.2 Bearing Tribology (Rolling Element)

- **Types:** Ball bearings, roller bearings, needle bearings.
- **Lubrication:** Grease (sealed-for-life) or oil mist.
- **Key parameter:** **PV limit** and **DN value** (bore $\times$ rpm).

### 2.3.3 Clutch & Synchroniser Rings

- **Synchromesh rings** rely on controlled friction (typically $\mu = 0.10$ to $0.15$ with moly-coated or paper-lined rings).
- **Clutch plates** use wet (oil-bath) or dry friction materials.

---

## 2.4 Tribological Challenges in Braking Systems

### 2.4.1 Disc Brake (Pad – Rotor Interface)

**Operating Conditions:**
- Interface temperature: $30^\circ C$ to $>600^\circ C$ (severe fade zone).
- Contact pressure: $0.5$ to $2.5$ MPa.
- Sliding speed: up to $30$ m/s (highway braking).

**Tribological Challenges:**
1. **Brake fade:** $\mu$ drops as temperature rises above $300^\circ C$ (resin decomposition in organic pads).
2. **Judder (DTV — Disc Thickness Variation):** Uneven rotor surface causes vibration.
3. **Pad wear:** Inorganic fibres (steel wool, ceramics) reduce wear but may increase rotor wear.
4. **Glazing:** Polishing of pad surface → loss of friction.

### 2.4.2 Drum Brake (Shoe – Drum Interface)

- **Self-energising** (leading-trailing shoe) geometry amplifies friction force.
- Challenges: **heat dissipation** (enclosed), **squeal**, **asbestos replacement** issues.

### 2.4.3 Brake Fluid (Hydraulic Medium)

- Must maintain **DOT-3 / DOT-4 / DOT-5.1** specifications.
- **Hygroscopic** — absorbs water → boiling point drops → **vapour lock** → brake failure.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Archard's Wear Equation** | $V = k \cdot \frac{F_N \cdot s}{H}$ | $V$=wear volume, $k$=wear coefficient, $F_N$=normal load, $s$=sliding distance, $H$=hardness | $k = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ (mild–severe) |
| 2 | **Petroff's Equation (Bearing Friction)** | $f = \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{c} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$ | $\mu$=viscosity, $N$=rpm, $r$=radius, $c$=clearance | Use for lightly loaded bearings |
| 3 | **Reynolds Equation (Hydrodynamic)** | $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{\rho h^3}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial x} + 12 \frac{\partial(\rho h)}{\partial t}$ | $h$=film thickness, $p$=pressure, $U$=surface velocity | Foundation of all HD bearings |
| 4 | **Hertzian Contact Stress (Line)** | $p_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F_N}{\pi L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\pi \left(\frac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right)^{-1}}}$ | $F_N$=normal load, $L$=line length, $E$=Young's modulus, $\nu$=Poisson's ratio | Gear & cam design |
| 5 | **Sommerfeld Number** | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | $\mu$=viscosity, $N$=rpm, $P$=load/unit area, $r$=shaft radius, $c$=clearance | Bearing design |
| 6 | **Specific Film Thickness $\Lambda$** | $\Lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | $h_{min}$=minimum film thickness, $R_q$=RMS roughness | $\Lambda < 1$ boundary, $1<\Lambda<3$ mixed, $\Lambda>3$ full film |
| 7 | **PV Product (Limiting)** | $PV = P \cdot V$ | $P$=pressure, $V$=velocity | $PV_{lim}$: $1$ to $2$ MPa·m/s for many plastics |
| 8 | **Brake Energy (Kinetic)** | $E_b = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $m$=vehicle mass, $v$=velocity | Converted to heat in rotor |
| 9 | **Brake Fade Coefficient** | $\mu_T = \mu_0 (1 - \alpha (T - T_0))$ | $\alpha$=temp sensitivity, $T$=temp | Empirical; design aim: $\alpha$ small |
| 10 | **Stribeck Curve Categories** | $f_{tot} = f_b + f_v + f_h$ | $f_b$=boundary, $f_v$=viscous, $f_h$=hydrodynamic | $f$ vs. $\mu N/P$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Formatting Rule:** In the above table, the absolute value / boundary expressions use \vert or \mid instead of the vertical pipe `|` to keep the markdown table intact. In your exam answer book, you may write `|x|` freely, but in **typed notes** avoid the symbol.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Engine design:** Oil viscosity grade selection (SAE 5W-30, 10W-40) is decided by tribological analysis at the ring-liner interface.
- **Gearbox design:** Selection of EP additives (ZDDP — Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate) is a tribological decision to prevent scuffing.
- **Brake pad design:** Trade-off between $\mu$ stability, wear, noise (NVH), and rotor friendliness is a tribology optimisation problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Archard's Equation of Wear (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Archard's equation appears in almost every wear-related KTU question, either directly or in modified form. Memorise the derivation and boundary conditions.

**Statement:** The volume of material worn from a sliding contact is proportional to the applied load, the sliding distance, and inversely proportional to the hardness of the softer material.

### Step 1 — Define the contact
Let two asperities of real contact area $A_r$ support a normal load $F_N$.

$$F_N = A_r \cdot H$$

where $H$ is the indentation hardness (Pa).

### Step 2 — Probability of asperity producing a wear particle
Each contacting asperity has probability $k$ of producing a wear fragment (dimensionless wear coefficient, $0 < k < 1$).

### Step 3 — Volume of one wear particle
If the wear fragment is assumed hemispherical of radius $a$ (contact radius), its volume is:

$$V_{particle} = \frac{2}{3} \pi a^3$$

### Step 4 — Number of wear events in sliding distance $s$
As the surfaces slide distance $s$, each asperity sweeps a track. The number of new asperity encounters $N_a$ is:

$$N_a = \frac{A_r \cdot s}{a \cdot A_{ap}}$$

where $A_{ap}$ is the nominal apparent contact area (assumed small unit).

> *(For KTU exam, simplify this to: "the number of asperity interactions is proportional to $A_r \cdot s$.")*

### Step 5 — Total wear volume
Combining the above, total wear volume $V$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= k \cdot N_a \cdot V_{particle} \\
&= k \cdot \left(\frac{A_r \cdot s}{a \cdot A_{ap}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3} \pi a^3\right) \\
&= k \cdot \frac{A_r \cdot s \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3} \pi a^3\right)}{a \cdot A_{ap}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $A_r = F_N / H$
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= k \cdot \frac{\left(\dfrac{F_N}{H}\right) \cdot s \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{3} \pi a^2\right)}{A_{ap}} \\
&= k \cdot \frac{F_N \cdot s}{H} \cdot \left[\frac{\frac{2}{3} \pi a^2}{A_{ap}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Recognise the bracketed term as a constant $K$
For a given pair of materials, $\frac{2 \pi a^2}{3 A_{ap}}$ is constant, so we absorb it into $k$, yielding the **classical Archard equation**:

$$\boxed{\,V = K \cdot \frac{F_N \cdot s}{H}\,}$$

where $K$ is the **dimensionless wear coefficient** (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ for metals).

**Conversion logic:** Each step expresses a physical concept mathematically — real contact supports load, asperities probabilistically produce fragments, and the fragment volume scales with size. Combining these via direct proportionality gives the famous linear-in-load-and-distance wear law.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Petroff's Equation for Journal Bearing Friction

**Assumptions:**
- Journal is concentric (no load → no eccentricity).
- Full film, laminar, isoviscous.
- Bearing length $L$ is much greater than radius $r$.

### Step 1 — Velocity at journal surface
$$U = \frac{2 \pi N r}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

### Step 2 — Shear stress in Couette flow
$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

where $h$ is the film thickness, $h = c$ (radial clearance) for a concentric journal.

### Step 3 — Friction force on journal
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_f &= \tau \cdot A_{contact} \\
&= \eta \cdot \frac{U}{c} \cdot (2 \pi r L) \\
&= \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi N r}{60 c} \cdot 2 \pi r L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Friction torque
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= F_f \cdot r \\
&= \frac{4 \pi^2 \eta N r^3 L}{60 c}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Friction coefficient
Using $F_N = P \cdot (2 r L)$ and $f = F_f / F_N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{F_f}{F_N} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \eta N r}{60 P c} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{(scaled)} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** (Pa·s). The classic KTU form is:

$$\boxed{\,f = 2 \pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** Petroff derived this by assuming pure Couette shear in a thin film, which is the **limiting case** of the general Reynolds solution. The term $(r/c)^2$ is small (typically $\approx 0.001$), making $f$ very small in well-lubricated bearings.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stribeck Curve and $\Lambda$ (Lambda) Parameter

The **Stribeck curve** is a plot of friction coefficient $f$ vs. the Hersey number $\mu N / P$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \text{dynamic viscosity} \\
N &= \text{speed} \\
P &= \text{load per unit projected area} \\
\Lambda &= \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ is the composite RMS roughness.

**Regime classification:**

| Regime | $\Lambda$ value | Film state | Wear mode |
|--------|-----------------|------------|-----------|
| **Boundary** | $\Lambda < 1$ | Asperity contact dominant | Adhesive, oxidative |
| **Mixed** | $1 \le \Lambda \le 3$ | Partial film | Mild adhesive + abrasive |
| **Full Film (HD/EHL)** | $\Lambda > 3$ | Complete separation | Fatigue only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "A journal bearing has $\Lambda = 0.5$. What is the lubrication regime?" — Answer: **Boundary lubrication**. Do NOT confuse with Reynolds number.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tribological Challenge vs. Component

This matrix is a **favourite KTU question** ("Compare tribological challenges in engines and transmissions" type).

| Tribological Parameter | Engine (Piston Ring) | Transmission (Gear) | Brake (Pad) |
|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Contact type** | Conformable sliding | Line/point rolling+sliding | Conformal sliding |
| **Peak pressure (MPa)** | $10$ (combustion) | $1500$ (Hertz) | $2.5$ |
| **Sliding velocity (m/s)** | $5$–$15$ | $0$–$20$ (rolling) | $0$–$30$ |
| **Temperature range** | $40$–$300^\circ C$ | $60$–$150^\circ C$ | $30$–$650^\circ C$ |
| **Lubricant** | Engine oil (multi-grade) | EP gear oil (S-P) | None (friction material) |
| **Lubrication regime** | Mixed | EHL / boundary | Dry / boundary |
| **Dominant wear mode** | Adhesive + corrosive | Pitting + scuffing | Abrasive + thermal |
| **Critical design metric** | Oil film thickness $\Lambda$ | Hertz stress, scuffing load | Fade resistance $\mu(T)$ |
| **Lubricant additive focus** | Detergents, dispersants | EP (ZDDP), anti-foam | Friction modifiers (in pad) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Industrial Tribological Sub-Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Machine Input: Load, Speed, Temperature]
    A --> B[Tribological Sub-System 1: Engine]
    A --> C[Tribological Sub-System 2: Transmission]
    A --> D[Tribological Sub-System 3: Braking System]

    B --> B1[Piston Ring - Liner]
    B --> B2[Cam - Follower]
    B --> B3[Crankshaft Bearing]
    B --> B4[Valve Guide]

    C --> C1[Spur / Helical Gear Pair]
    C --> C2[Rolling Element Bearing]
    C --> C3[Synchroniser Ring]
    C --> C4[Clutch Plate]

    D --> D1[Brake Pad - Disc Rotor]
    D --> D2[Brake Shoe - Drum]
    D --> D3[Master Cylinder - Brake Fluid]
    D --> D4[ABS Modulator]

    B1 --> X1[Challenge: Scuffing, Blow-by, Wear]
    B2 --> X2[Challenge: Pitting, Adhesion]
    B3 --> X3[Challenge: Cavitation, Fatigue]
    C1 --> Y1[Challenge: Pitting, Scuffing]
    C2 --> Y2[Challenge: Spalling, Brinelling]
    D1 --> Z1[Challenge: Fade, Judder, Wear]
    D2 --> Z2[Challenge: Heat Build-up, Squeal]

    X1 --> R[Tribological Response: Material, Lubricant, Surface Engineering]
    X2 --> R
    X3 --> R
    Y1 --> R
    Y2 --> R
    Z1 --> R
    Z2 --> R

    R --> OUT[Optimised System: Efficiency, Life, Reliability]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every industrial machine feeds mechanical input (load, speed, temperature) into multiple tribological sub-systems (engines, gearboxes, brakes). Each sub-system contains specific tribo-pairs, each of which faces characteristic challenges. The **tribological response** (material selection, lubrication design, surface engineering) addresses all these challenges to deliver an optimised system.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stribeck Curve and Lubrication Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boundary Lubrication: Lambda less than 1] -->|Asperity contact, high friction| B[Mixed Lubrication: Lambda 1 to 3]
    B -->|Partial film formation| C[Full Film EHL / Hydrodynamic: Lambda greater than 3]
    C -->|Complete separation| D[Elastohydrodynamic EHL]
    C -->|Rigid surfaces| E[Hydrodynamic HD]
    A -->|Use: EP additives, solid lubricants| F[Solutions]
    B -->|Use: Better viscosity grade| F
    C -->|Use: Standard lubrication| F
```

**Interpretation:** The Stribeck curve transitions from **boundary** (high friction, wear-prone) → **mixed** → **full film** (lowest friction). For engine rings at TDC, the regime drops to boundary; for gear teeth at pitch point, it is full-film EHL. This justifies the multi-grade oil selection.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Brake System Tribology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Pedal Force] --> B[Master Cylinder]
    B --> C[Brake Fluid - DOT 4]
    C --> D[ABS Modulator]
    D --> E[Wheel Cylinder / Caliper]
    E --> F[Friction Material - Pad or Shoe]
    F -->|Contact pressure| G[Rotor or Drum]
    G -->|Friction force| H[Kinetic Energy to Heat]
    H --> I[Temperature Rise]
    I --> J[Fade? - Mu drops if T greater than 300 C]
    J -->|Yes| K[Brake Fade - Loss of stopping power]
    J -->|No| L[Effective Stopping]
```

**Interpretation:** Energy flow in a brake system is mechanical → hydraulic → frictional → thermal. The tribological weak link is the **friction material** which must hold $\mu$ stable across a wide temperature window.

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Tribo-System Element Identification (Piston Ring)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TriboSystem
        direction TB
        T1[Tribo-Element 1: Piston Ring - Cast Iron / Coated]
        T2[Tribo-Element 2: Cylinder Liner - Cast Iron with Honing Pattern]
        T3[Interfacial Medium: Engine Oil SAE 5W-30]
        T4[Surrounding Atmosphere: Combustion Gases, Soot, Acids]
        T1 <-->|Sliding Contact| T2
        T2 --- T3
        T3 --- T4
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Always list **all four** elements in exam answers describing a tribo-system.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**"Define the term 'Tribo-system' and list its four essential elements with an example from an internal combustion engine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **tribo-system** is the complete set of interacting bodies, interfacial medium, and surrounding atmosphere that participate in a friction-and-wear process. The four essential elements are:
1. **Tribo-element 1 (moving body):** Piston ring
2. **Tribo-element 2 (counter-body):** Cylinder liner
3. **Interfacial medium:** Engine oil
4. **Surrounding atmosphere:** Combustion gases, soot, moisture

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark list + 1 mark example = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**"What is brake fade? State two primary causes."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Brake fade** is the temporary loss of braking effectiveness caused by a reduction in the coefficient of friction ($\mu$) of the friction material at elevated temperatures.

**Two primary causes:**
1. **Thermal degradation of organic binders** (resins) in the brake pad above $300^\circ C$, releasing gases that create a lubricating film.
2. **Glazing** of the pad surface — high temperatures polish the friction material, reducing contact area and friction.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for two causes = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the tribological challenges encountered in the **piston ring–cylinder liner** interface of an IC engine. Discuss the lubrication regimes encountered during one engine cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the major **wear mechanisms** (adhesive, abrasive, corrosive, fatigue) with reference to engine and brake components.

#### Model Solution (a) — Piston Ring – Cylinder Liner Tribology

**1. Operating Conditions (2 marks):**
- Peak gas pressure: up to $10$ MPa at TDC (combustion).
- Mean piston speed: $5$–$15$ m/s.
- Temperature: $200$–$300^\circ C$ at TDC ring zone, $80$–$100^\circ C$ at mid-stroke.
- Lubricant: SAE multi-grade oil, $0.01$–$0.1$ mm film thickness.

**2. Lubrication Regimes During Engine Cycle (3 marks):**

| Stroke Position | Regime | Reason |
|-----------------|--------|--------|
| **TDC (top dead centre)** | **Boundary / starved** | Velocity = 0, load max → film collapses |
| **Mid-stroke (downward)** | **Mixed → Hydrodynamic** | Velocity max, load moderate |
| **BDC (bottom dead centre)** | **Boundary** | Velocity = 0, oil drainage |

The ring thus **alternates** between boundary and hydrodynamic — making it the most demanding tribo-contact in the engine.

**3. Major Tribological Challenges (2 marks):**
- **Scuffing:** adhesive metal transfer when oil film fails.
- **Abrasive wear:** soot, dust, and combustion debris act as third-body abrasives.
- **Corrosive wear:** acidic blow-by products ($\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ from S in fuel) attack liner.
- **Ring flutter:** inertia + gas pressure lift the ring, causing uneven contact.

#### Model Solution (b) — Wear Mechanisms in Engines & Brakes

**1. Adhesive Wear (1.5 marks):**
- Asperity junctions weld under high local pressure; weaker material transfers to stronger.
- **Engine example:** piston ring scuffing on bore.
- **Brake example:** pad material transfer to rotor.

**2. Abrasive Wear (1.5 marks):**
- Hard particles plough through softer surface.
- **Engine example:** soot-induced liner wear.
- **Brake example:** hard ceramic fibres in pad cutting rotor.

**3. Corrosive Wear (1.5 marks):**
- Chemical reaction with environment produces weak surface film.
- **Engine example:** acid attack on liner from combustion gases.
- **Brake example:** salt-water corrosion on rotor edges (not active friction surface).

**4. Fatigue Wear (1.5 marks):**
- Repeated sub-surface stress cycles cause crack initiation → spalling.
- **Engine example:** bearing lining spalling (white-metal fatigue).
- **Brake example:** not common (rotor is replaced, not fatigued in service life).

**5. Governing Equation (1 mark):**
Archard's equation $V = k \cdot F_N \cdot s / H$ governs the volume of wear for adhesive, abrasive, and corrosive cases (with appropriate $k$).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **tribological challenges in gear tooth contacts**. Compare spur gear and hypoid gear tribology.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Stribeck curve** and its significance in the design of industrial tribo-systems. Sketch the curve and label the three lubrication regimes.

#### Model Solution (a) — Gear Tribology

**1. Contact Geometry (1.5 marks):**
- **Spur gear:** line contact along tooth width.
- **Hypoid gear:** point contact (offset axes, sliding dominant).

**2. Operating Conditions (1.5 marks):**
- Hertzian contact stress: $500$–$2000$ MPa.
- Sliding-to-rolling ratio varies along the line of action: $0$ at pitch point, high at addendum/dedendum.
- Lubricant: EP gear oil (S-P additives, viscosity $\approx 90$–$320$ cSt at $100^\circ C$).

**3. Tribological Challenges (3 marks):**
| Challenge | Spur Gear | Hypoid Gear |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Pitting fatigue | Common | Common |
| Scuffing | Less (low sliding) | **Severe (high sliding)** |
| Abrasive wear | Moderate | High (sliding dominates) |
| Tooth deflection | Spring system compliance | Compounded by sliding |

**4. Lubrication Regime (1 mark):**
- At pitch point: **full-film EHL** (Eyring model with pressure-dependent viscosity).
- At tooth tip/root: **boundary / mixed** (sliding dominant).

#### Model Solution (b) — Stribeck Curve

**1. Definition (1.5 marks):**
The Stribeck curve is a plot of the **friction coefficient $f$** of a lubricated contact as a function of the **Hersey number** $\mu N / P$ (viscosity × speed / pressure). It identifies three lubrication regimes.

**2. Three Regimes (3 marks):**
| Regime | Hersey Range | Friction Behaviour | Wear |
|--------|--------------|--------------------|------|
| **Boundary** | Low | High $f$, decreasing | Severe adhesive |
| **Mixed** | Intermediate | Falling $f$ | Mild |
| **Full film (HD/EHL)** | High | Low $f$, rising slowly due to viscous drag | None / fatigue |

**3. Schematic Sketch (1.5 marks):**

```
   f |
     |        ___
     |       /    \   <-- viscous rising
     |      /      \____
     |     /  (HD/EHL)  \____
     |    /                \____
     |   /                     \____
     |  /  (Mixed)                  \____
     | /                                 \____
     |/  (Boundary)                              \____
     +------------------------------------------------> 
                       mu*N/P  (Hersey number)
```

**4. Engineering Significance (1 mark):**
- Helps designers choose lubricant viscosity and operating speed to keep system in **full-film regime** → low wear, low friction, high efficiency.
- Engine bearing designers use Stribeck to size clearance and select oil grade.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the four elements of a tribo-system.** Always list Body 1, Body 2, Interfacial medium, Atmosphere — *each one is worth 1 mark.*
> 2. **Mixing up Archard's equation units.** $V$ is in **mm³**, $F_N$ in **N**, $s$ in **m**, $H$ in **Pa**. Many students write $H$ as hardness in HRC — wrong dimension.
> 3. **Confusing Stribeck curve x-axis.** Some students plot friction vs. Reynolds number. The correct x-axis is the **Hersey number** $\mu N / P$.
> 4. **Wrong "regime" identification.** A journal bearing operating at design speed is in **hydrodynamic** regime, not "boundary." Don't default to boundary.
> 5. **Ignoring friction stabilisers in brake answers.** Stating only "$\mu$ drops" is incomplete. Mention **temperature dependence** of $\mu$ and the role of **fade-resistant materials (ceramic, NAO, low-metallic)**.
> 6. **In gear answers, forgetting the difference between pitting (subsurface fatigue) and scuffing (adhesive surface failure).** Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 7. **Skipping the Archard derivation step-by-step.** Even for 7-mark questions, showing the **proportionality logic** is essential; skipping the "probability of fragment" step costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for exam eve!):**

- **Tribo-system = 4 elements:** Body 1, Body 2, Interfacial medium, Surrounding atmosphere. *(Always state explicitly in exam answers.)*
- **Three regimes of lubrication:** Boundary ($\Lambda < 1$), Mixed ($1 \le \Lambda \le 3$), Full Film HD/EHL ($\Lambda > 3$). $\Lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$.
- **Stribeck curve x-axis = Hersey number** $\mu N / P$ (NOT Reynolds number).
- **Archard's equation:** $V = k \cdot F_N \cdot s / H$. Wear coefficient $k = 10^{-6}$ (mild) to $10^{-2}$ (severe).
- **Petroff's equation:** $f = 2\pi^2 \cdot (\mu N / P) \cdot (r/c)^2$. Use for concentric, lightly loaded journal bearings.
- **Engine ring-liner:** Mixed/boundary lubrication; challenges are scuffing, abrasive, corrosive wear, ring flutter.
- **Cam-follower:** High Hertzian stress ($\approx 1000$ MPa), boundary lubrication; failure modes are pitting and scuffing.
- **Gear teeth:** Line/point contact; pitch point = EHL, tip/root = mixed; failure modes are pitting and scuffing.
- **Hypoid gears:** High sliding component; require EP additives (S-P) in gear oil.
- **Brake fade:** $\mu$ drops above $300^\circ C$ due to organic binder decomposition.
- **Brake judder (DTV):** Disc Thickness Variation $> 0.05$ mm causes vibration; remedy is rotor re-machining or replacement.
- **Squeal:** Self-excited vibration at $1$–$5$ kHz; mitigated by chamfered pads, shims, and damping.
- **DOT brake fluids:** DOT-3 (dry BP $205^\circ C$), DOT-4 ($230^\circ C$), DOT-5.1 ($260^\circ C$); hygroscopic → bleed annually.
- **Brake pad classes:** **NAO** (non-asbestos organic), **Semi-metallic** (30–65% metal), **Low-metallic**, **Ceramic** (premium, low dust, low noise).
- **Engine oil additive package:** Detergents (Ca/Mg sulphonates), Dispersants (succinimide), Anti-wear (ZDDP), Antioxidants, VI improvers.
- **Sommerfeld number:** $S_o = (\mu N / P) \cdot (r/c)^2$ — bearing design parameter; higher $S_o$ → better hydrodynamic film.
- **Specific wear rate unit:** $\mathrm{mm^3 / (N \cdot m)}$ — always quote this unit in exam answers.
- **Stribeck-Petroff relation:** Petroff equation gives the **rising-viscous** arm of Stribeck; Reynolds equation gives the **descending-boundary** arm. They meet at the minimum-friction point.

---

**End of Module 4 — Tribology in Industries (Engines, Transmissions, Braking Systems)**

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEMUT754 — Outcome-Based Education pattern. All formulas are dimensionally and mathematically consistent with standard tribology references.*
