topics.title: Role of Tribology in Machining, Forming, and Finishing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Role of Tribology in Machining, Forming, and Finishing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Manufacturing Tribology** is the branch of applied tribology concerned with the study of friction, wear, and lubrication phenomena occurring at the interfaces in material-removal (machining), plastic-deformation (forming), and surface-improvement (finishing) processes. In these processes, two bodies — *tool/workpiece* or *die/workpiece* — are forced into intimate sliding contact under extremely severe conditions of **pressure (up to 2–3 GPa)**, **temperature (700 °C – 1200 °C at the tool-chip interface)**, and **relative velocity (0.5 – 3000 m/min)**. Controlling the tribological contact is therefore decisive for tool life, surface integrity, energy consumption, and product quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must understand that tribology is not an isolated chapter but a *cross-cutting discipline* that decides the success or failure of nearly every primary and secondary manufacturing process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are slicing a hot loaf of bread with a stainless-steel knife, very fast, hundreds of times a day, in a bakery.

* The **knife (tool)** repeatedly rubs against the **bread (workpiece)** — the contact is hot, sticky, and adhesive.
* The bread sticks to the blade, smears, and tears the soft crumb — this is analogous to the **built-up edge (BUE)** and **adhesive wear** in machining.
* If you oil the blade lightly, slicing becomes smoother and the bread surface looks cleaner — exactly how a **cutting fluid** improves machinability.
* If you press the bread through a die (cookie cutter) without greasing, it cracks and requires huge force — this is the role of **forming lubricants**.

In short: **manufacturing is "friction management" on an industrial scale.**

### 1.3 Scope of the Topic

| Process Family | Examples | Dominant Tribological Phenomenon |
|---|---|---|
| **Machining (Material Removal)** | Turning, Milling, Drilling, Broaching | Adhesive friction at tool–chip interface, BUE, crater wear |
| **Forming (Plastic Deformation)** | Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Wire Drawing, Deep Drawing | Mixed/Boundary lubrication, severe plastic shear |
| **Finishing (Surface Generation)** | Grinding, Honing, Lapping, Polishing, CMP | Abrasive micro-contact, tribochemical reaction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Reference Values (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **Effective coefficient of friction at tool–chip interface (μ)**: **0.5 – 2.0** (much higher than classical Coulomb values because of seizure conditions)
> - **Contact pressure at tool–chip interface**: **up to 2 GPa**
> - **Flash temperature at tool–chip contact**: **800 °C – 1000 °C**
> - **Cutting fluid cooling share of total heat removal**: **≈ 80 %**, lubrication share **≈ 15 %**, chip evacuation **≈ 5 %**
> - **Typical tool-life exponent (n) in Taylor equation**: **0.1 – 0.5** depending on tool material
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Role of Tribology in Machining

The **tool–chip interface** is the most demanding tribological contact in manufacturing. Friction here is governed by two competing phenomena:

1. **Adhesion (primary mechanism)** – Clean metallic junctions form between chip and rake face due to extreme pressure. The shear strength of these junctions can approach the shear strength of the tool material itself, leading to **crater wear** and **built-up edge (BUE)**.
2. **Abrasion (secondary mechanism)** – Hard carbides, oxides, or hard particles in the workpiece plough the rake face and flank, producing **flank wear**.

**Built-Up Edge (BUE):** A wedge of work-material welded to the cutting edge due to adhesion. It temporarily "protects" the edge but periodically fractures, taking a fragment of tool material with it, and produces poor surface finish.

**Functions of Cutting Fluids:**
- **Cooling** (dominant): removes heat from the cutting zone.
- **Lubrication**: reduces adhesion, friction, and built-up edge formation.
- **Chip evacuation**: flushes chips away from the cutting zone.
- **Corrosion protection**: prevents rust on machine and workpiece.
- **Improved surface finish**: reduces tear and smearing.

**Classification of Cutting Fluids:**
1. **Straight cutting oils** (mineral oils + extreme-pressure additives like S, Cl, P).
2. **Emulsifiable / Soluble oils** (oil-in-water emulsion; 3–10 % oil).
3. **Synthetic fluids** (chemical solutions without mineral oil).
4. **Semi-synthetic fluids** (hybrid of soluble + synthetic).
5. **Gaseous fluids** (air, CO₂, cryogenic N₂ for difficult-to-cut materials).

### 2.2 Role of Tribology in Metal Forming

In forming, large bulk plastic deformation occurs while the workpiece slides against the die. The contact is **always boundary or mixed lubrication** because hydrodynamic films cannot survive the enormous contact pressures (**≈ 1–2.5 GPa**).

Two friction laws are commonly used:

- **Coulomb's law:** τ = μ·p  (where μ is friction coefficient, p is local pressure) — valid at low pressures.
- **Constant friction factor (Schey):** τ = m·k  (where m ≤ 0.5–0.6 is a friction factor and k is the shear yield stress of the workpiece) — valid at high pressures (preferred for hot forging).

**Forming-process lubricants** include graphite, MoS₂, soap films (sodium stearate), glass coatings, phosphate + soap conversion coatings, and polymer films.

### 2.3 Role of Tribology in Finishing Processes

Finishing processes operate at the **micro- and nano-scale**:

- **Grinding**: abrasive grits (Al₂O₃, SiC, CBN, diamond) slide against the workpiece at very high local temperatures (>1500 °C in the spark zone). Tribology here governs **grinding burn, residual stress, and surface integrity**.
- **Honing, Lapping, Superfinishing**: use abrasive stones/pastes with controlled low pressure — true **boundary-lubricated micro-tribology**.
- **CMP (Chemical-Mechanical Polishing)**: combines chemical etching with abrasive slurry; a tribo-chemical process.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V \cdot T^{\,n} = C$ | **Taylor's tool-life equation** | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min); $T$ = tool life (min); $n$, $C$ = constants depending on tool-workpiece pair |
| 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{F}{N}$ | Coefficient of friction (tool–chip) | dimensionless; **0.5–2.0** in machining |
| 3 | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | Friction angle | β is angle of friction at tool–chip interface |
| 4 | $\phi = 45^\circ + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}$ | **Merchant's optimum shear angle** | α = rake angle; φ = shear angle |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi-\alpha)}$ | Chip thickness ratio | $t_1$ uncut chip thickness; $t_2$ chip thickness |
| 6 | $\eta = f\!\left(\dfrac{\eta_0 \, v}{p}\right)$ | **Stribeck curve** — friction vs. Hersey number | $\eta_0$ = dynamic viscosity; $v$ = sliding speed; $p$ = contact pressure |
| 7 | $\tau = m \cdot k$ | Constant friction factor (forming) | $m$ ≈ 0.05–0.6; $k$ = shear yield strength |
| 8 | $F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_f \, A_0 \, (1 + \mu \cot\alpha)$ | Drawing force (with friction) | $\sigma_f$ = flow stress; $A_0$ = initial area; α = die semi-angle |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2}+R_{q2}^{\,2}}}$ | Lambda ratio (lubrication regime) | $\lambda < 1$ boundary; $1 < \lambda < 3$ mixed; $\lambda > 3$ full-film |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **mathematically verifiable** and form the analytical backbone of any Module-4 KTU question. Note the use of `\cot` and `\dfrac` to keep table entries clean without breaking the markdown parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

* In **aerospace Ti-alloy machining**, cryogenic N₂ is replacing flood coolant because of environmental and tribological gains.
* In **automotive sheet-metal forming (deep drawing of advanced high-strength steels)**, lubricant choice decides whether a part draws successfully or fractures at the corner radius.
* In **silicon-wafer CMP**, tribo-chemical reactions at the nano-scale determine the planarity of next-generation microchips.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Merchant's Optimum Shear Angle Equation

We will derive the classical **Merchant shear-angle relation** starting from first principles of force equilibrium on the chip.

**Step 1 — Setup of the orthogonal cutting model.**

In orthogonal cutting the tool is straight, the cutting edge is perpendicular to the cutting velocity $V$, and the chip flows up the rake face at an angle. The shear plane is inclined at an angle $\phi$ with the cutting velocity, and the friction angle at the tool-chip interface is $\beta$ where $\tan\beta = \mu$.

**Step 2 — Forces acting on the chip.**

Three forces are in equilibrium on the free-body of the chip:

* $F_s$ — force along the shear plane (resisting shear of the workpiece material).
* $N$ — normal reaction on the tool rake face.
* $F$ — frictional force along the rake face (resisting chip flow).

**Step 3 — Closure of the force triangle.**

Applying Lami's theorem (or sines rule) to the closed triangle of $F_s$, $N$, and $F$:

$$
\frac{F_s}{\sin\!\left(90^\circ + \beta\right)} \;=\; \frac{F}{\sin\!\left(\phi + \beta - \alpha\right)} \;=\; \frac{N}{\sin\!\left(90^\circ - \phi + \alpha\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the friction force as a function of the friction angle.**

By definition, the friction angle satisfies:

$$
\tan\beta = \mu = \frac{F}{N} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F = N \tan\beta
$$

**Step 5 — Shear force in terms of friction angle and cutting geometry.**

Combining the relations from Steps 3 and 4:

$$
F_s \;=\; \frac{N \,\tan\beta \,\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\sin\!\left(90^\circ + \beta\right)}
$$

Because $\sin(90^\circ + \beta) = \cos\beta$, this simplifies to:

$$
F_s \;=\; \frac{N \,\sin\beta}{\cos\beta} \cdot \frac{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{1}
$$

So the shear force can be written cleanly as:

$$
F_s \;=\; N \,\tan\beta \,\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)
$$

**Step 6 — Work principle — minimum energy criterion.**

According to Merchant, the cutting process adopts the shear angle that **minimizes the work done** (and hence the cutting force) for a given material-removal rate. Differentiating $F_s$ with respect to $\phi$ and setting the derivative to zero:

$$
\frac{dF_s}{d\phi} \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -\sin(\phi + \beta - \alpha) \;=\; 0
$$

This means:

$$
\phi + \beta - \alpha \;=\; 90^\circ
$$

**Step 7 — Solving for the optimum shear angle.**

Rearranging the previous equation:

$$
\phi \;=\; 90^\circ - \beta + \alpha
$$

Dividing the angle expressions symmetrically (which is equivalent when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are small):

$$
\phi \;=\; 45^\circ + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

This is **Merchant's optimum shear-angle equation** — a high-yield KTU result.

### 3.2 Derivation of Chip Thickness Ratio Relation

**Step 1 — Geometric definition.**

The chip thickness ratio is defined as:

$$
r \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2}
$$

where $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness and $t_2$ = chip thickness after cut.

**Step 2 — Volume continuity (incompressible plasticity).**

Because metal is plastically incompressible during cutting, the cross-sectional area of material entering equals that leaving (per unit width):

$$
t_1 \,=\; t_2 \cdot \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the chip thickness ratio.**

Rearranging:

$$
r \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator.**

Using the cosine subtraction identity:

$$
\cos(\phi - \alpha) \;=\; \cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos\phi$:

$$
r \;=\; \frac{\tan\phi}{\cos\alpha + \tan\phi \sin\alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

Cross-multiplying and re-arranging:

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

This second KTU-high-yield equation links the **shear angle**, **rake angle**, and **chip thickness ratio**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem.** In an orthogonal turning operation, the uncut chip thickness is $t_1 = 0.30$ mm and the chip thickness is $t_2 = 0.45$ mm. The rake angle is $\alpha = +10^\circ$. Calculate (a) the chip thickness ratio, (b) the shear angle using the chip-ratio formula, and (c) the coefficient of friction if Merchant's equation gives the same shear angle.

**Solution.**

**(a)** Chip thickness ratio:

$$
r \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \frac{0.30}{0.45} \;=\; 0.667
$$

**(b)** Shear angle from chip-ratio formula:

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{r\cos\alpha}{1 - r\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.667 \times \cos 10^\circ}{1 - 0.667 \times \sin 10^\circ}
$$

Computing term by term:

$$
\cos 10^\circ \;=\; 0.9848, \qquad \sin 10^\circ \;=\; 0.1736
$$

Numerator: $0.667 \times 0.9848 = 0.6569$

Denominator: $1 - 0.667 \times 0.1736 = 1 - 0.1158 = 0.8842$

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{0.6569}{0.8842} \;=\; 0.7430
$$

$$
\phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.7430) \;=\; 36.6^\circ
$$

**(c)** Apply Merchant's equation with the same $\phi$:

$$
\phi \;=\; 45^\circ + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

$$
36.6^\circ \;=\; 45^\circ + \frac{10^\circ}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\beta}{2} \;=\; 45^\circ + 5^\circ - 36.6^\circ \;=\; 13.4^\circ \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \beta \;=\; 26.8^\circ
$$

$$
\mu \;=\; \tan\beta \;=\; \tan 26.8^\circ \;=\; 0.504
$$

**Result.** $\mu \approx 0.50$, which is typical for steel machining with a good cutting fluid.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Whenever a KTU problem gives BOTH $t_1$, $t_2$ AND asks for $\mu$, the smart approach is to compute $r \to \phi$ (geometric) and then $\phi \to \beta \to \mu$ (Merchant). This two-step path is what toppers use to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tribology Across Manufacturing

Below is a Mermaid block diagram showing how the same tribological primitives (friction, wear, lubrication, contact mechanics) manifest differently in the three manufacturing families.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MANUFACTURING TRIBOLOGY CORE]:::core
    A --> B[FRICTION PRIMITIVES]
    A --> C[WEAR PRIMITIVES]
    A --> D[LUBRICATION PRIMITIVES]
    A --> E[CONTACT MECHANICS PRIMITIVES]

    B --> F[MACHINING BRANCH]
    B --> G[FORMING BRANCH]
    B --> H[FINISHING BRANCH]

    F --> F1[Adhesion at tool chip interface]
    F --> F2[Built Up Edge BUE]
    F --> F3[Coefficient of friction 0.5 to 2.0]

    G --> G1[Coulomb friction at low pressure]
    G --> G2[Constant friction factor at high pressure]
    G --> G3[Boundary and mixed lubrication]

    H --> H1[Abrasive grit workpiece contact]
    H --> H2[Tribo chemical reactions in CMP]
    H --> H3[Micro nano scale boundary film]

    C --> C1[Diffusion wear]
    C --> C2[Adhesive wear]
    C --> C3[Abrasive wear]
    C --> C4[Oxidation wear]

    D --> D1[Cutting fluids flood mist MQL cryogenic]
    D --> D2[Forming lubricants graphite MoS2 soap glass]
    D --> D3[Finishing fluids CMP slurry abrasive paste]

    E --> E1[High contact pressure up to 2 GPa]
    E --> E2[Flash temperature 800 to 1000 C]
    E --> E3[Sliding velocity 0.5 to 3000 m per min]

    classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Tool-Chip Tribological Contact — Block Schematic

The next Mermaid block illustrates the energy and material flows at the tool–chip interface during machining, which is the single most-tested concept of Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WP[WORKPIECE MATERIAL] -- Plastic Shear --> SP[SHEAR PLANE]
    SP -- Chip Formation --> CHIP[CHIP]
    CHIP -- Sliding Contact --> RAKE[RAKE FACE OF TOOL]
    RAKE -- Friction Force F --> CHIP
    RAKE -- Normal Force N --> CHIP
    RAKE -- Heat Generation --> TZ[TOOL CHIP TEMPERATURE ZONE 800 to 1000 C]
    TZ -- Conduction --> TOOL[CUTTING TOOL]
    TZ -- Convection --> CF[CUTTING FLUID]
    CF -- Cooling 80 percent --> TZ
    CF -- Lubrication 15 percent --> RAKE
    RAKE -- Adhesion --> BUE[POSSIBLE BUILT UP EDGE]
    BUE -- Periodic Fracture --> FLANK[FLANK WEAR]
```

### 4.3 Tribological Pathways in Forming vs Finishing — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[FORGING] --> L1[Lubricant graphite MoS2 glass]
    P2[ROLLING] --> L2[Lubricant oil emulsion]
    P3[EXTRUSION] --> L3[Lubricant glass phosphate soap]
    P4[DRAWING] --> L4[Lubricant soap dry film]

    L1 --> FF1[Friction law tau equals m times k]
    L2 --> FF1
    L3 --> FF1
    L4 --> FF1

    Q1[GRINDING] --> M1[Abrasive Al2O3 SiC CBN diamond]
    Q2[HONING] --> M2[Fine abrasive stone low pressure]
    Q3[LAPPING] --> M3[Abrasive paste loose grit]
    Q4[CMP] --> M4[Slurry with chemical etchant]

    M1 --> GG1[Heat 1500 C sparks tribo film]
    M2 --> GG2[Boundary film micron scale]
    M3 --> GG3[Mixed film sub micron]
    M4 --> GG4[Tribo chemical nano scale film]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams?** Complex physical drawings (such as Merchant's force circle or Taylor's tool-life curve) are rendered as block-level functional maps. This keeps the Mermaid renderer safe while still conveying the engineering relationships that examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is Built-Up Edge (BUE) in machining? How does it affect the surface finish and tool life?

**Model Answer:**
Built-Up Edge (BUE) is a small wedge of work-material that gets welded to the cutting edge of the tool due to **adhesive seizure** at the high pressures and temperatures of the tool-chip interface. Because of adhesion, chips momentarily stick to the rake face, and the deposited material acts as a temporary cutting edge.
*Effect on surface finish:* The BUE periodically breaks off, leaving fragments welded to the freshly machined surface, producing a **rough, torn surface** with poor dimensional accuracy.
*Effect on tool life:* When a BUE fragment detaches, a small chip of tool material is also pulled out (adhesive wear), accelerating crater and flank wear.
*Control:* Use sharp tools, positive rake angles, lower cutting speeds, and effective cutting fluids to minimise adhesion. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **three** tool-wear mechanisms observed in metal cutting and state one remedy for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Wear Mechanism | Description | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasive wear** | Hard particles in the workpiece plough the tool surface | Use harder tool material (e.g., coated carbide, ceramic) |
| **Adhesive wear** | Work-material micro-welds to tool and tears off fragments | Use cutting fluid and lower cutting temperature |
| **Diffusion wear** | At high temperature, tool atoms diffuse into the chip | Use coated tools (TiN, Al₂O₃) and reduce cutting speed |
| **Oxidation wear** | Tool oxidises at high temperature, forming brittle oxides | Use oxidation-resistant coatings, reduce cutting temperature |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Module 4):**
> - Students frequently **skip the schematic / neat sketch** while discussing the tool–chip interface. KTU valuation key deducts up to **2 marks** if the diagram is missing.
> - Do **not** confuse **Coulomb friction** ($\tau = \mu p$) with **constant friction factor** ($\tau = m k$) — the second is valid only for hot/warm forming where $p$ is high.
> - Always quote the **numerical value** of friction coefficient, contact pressure, or temperature in the answer. Qualitative-only answers are penalised.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.A. (a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **tool-chip tribological interface** in orthogonal cutting. Explain the roles of **adhesion and abrasion** in tool wear. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The tool-chip tribological interface is the most critical contact in machining. A schematic (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram) shows:

1. **Tool geometry**: Cutting edge, rake face, flank face, nose radius.
2. **Workpiece**: undeformed chip thickness $t_1$, chip thickness $t_2$.
3. **Shear plane** at angle $\phi$ with the cutting velocity.
4. **Contact zone** between the chip underside and rake face — length $\ell_c$ — where friction angle $\beta$ acts.

**Role of adhesion:**
At the contact zone, asperity contact pressures exceed the yield strength of the work-material many times over. The two clean metallic surfaces form **cold-welded junctions**. The shear strength of these junctions can approach the shear strength of the *tool material* itself. As the chip slides, these junctions are sheared — sometimes within the chip (good), but sometimes within the tool (causing **crater wear**). Adhesion is the **dominant wear mechanism** at low-to-moderate cutting speeds.

**Role of abrasion:**
The chip contains hard particles — carbides, oxides, inclusions — which plough across the rake face and flank, removing small fragments of tool material by micro-cutting. Abrasion becomes dominant at **higher cutting speeds** and with **abrasive work-materials** (e.g., cast iron, fibre-reinforced composites).

**Marks distribution:** Schematic 2 marks, adhesion description 2.5 marks, abrasion description 2.5 marks. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.A. (b)** Discuss the **functions of cutting fluids** and classify them with examples. Why is cooling considered more important than lubrication at high cutting speeds? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Functions of cutting fluid:**
1. **Cooling (≈ 80 % of heat removal)** — removes heat from tool-chip zone, prolongs tool life.
2. **Lubrication (≈ 15 %)** — reduces adhesion and friction at tool-chip interface.
3. **Chip evacuation** — flushes chips away, prevents re-cutting.
4. **Corrosion protection** — protects machine and workpiece.
5. **Improved surface finish** — reduces BUE formation.

**Classification of cutting fluids:**

| Type | Composition | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight cutting oils** | Mineral oil + EP additives (S, Cl, P) | Heavy-duty turning, broaching |
| **Emulsifiable oils** | Oil-in-water emulsion (3–10 % oil) | General machining |
| **Synthetic fluids** | Chemical solutions, no mineral oil | High-speed light-duty |
| **Semi-synthetic** | Emulsion + chemical additives | CNC milling |
| **Gaseous / cryogenic** | Air, CO₂, N₂(liquid) | Ti, Inconel machining |

**Why cooling dominates at high cutting speeds:**
Heat generation in machining scales approximately as $Q \propto V^{0.7}$ to $V^{1.0}$ where $V$ is cutting speed. At high $V$, the heat input is enormous (flash temperatures 800–1000 °C). The **heat removal capacity of a fluid** depends on its specific heat, flow rate, and boiling point. Water-based fluids have very high specific heat (~4.18 kJ/kg·K) and convective heat-transfer coefficient, making them ~80 % effective as coolants. Lubricating films, in contrast, are very thin and have limited heat capacity. Hence **cooling is the primary role** of cutting fluids in high-speed machining, while lubrication dominates at low speeds (where adhesion is the main wear mode).

**Marks distribution:** Functions 2 marks, classification 2 marks, cooling vs lubrication reasoning 3 marks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **role of tribology in metal forming** processes. Compare the use of **Coulomb friction law** and **constant friction factor** for representing friction in forging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

In metal forming, large plastic deformation occurs as the workpiece slides against the die under extremely high contact pressures (1–2.5 GPa). Tribology here governs:
- **Forging force / energy** required.
- **Surface quality** of the finished part.
- **Die wear** and die life.
- **Limiting draw ratio** in deep drawing.
- **Sticking vs sliding friction** regions.

**Coulomb friction law:**
$$
\tau = \mu \, p
$$
where $\tau$ = shear stress at interface, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $p$ = local normal pressure.
- Valid when $\mu p < k$ (i.e., low pressure).
- Used in **cold forming, drawing, light rolling**.

**Constant friction factor (Schey):**
$$
\tau = m \, k
$$
where $m$ is a dimensionless friction factor (0 ≤ m ≤ 0.5–0.6), $k$ = shear yield strength of the workpiece.
- Valid when $\mu p \ge k$ (high pressure).
- Used in **hot/warm forging, heavy rolling, extrusion**.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Coulomb ($\tau = \mu p$) | Constant Factor ($\tau = m k$) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure dependence | Linear in p | Independent of p |
| Range | Low pressure, cold working | High pressure, hot working |
| Use | Drawing, light forming | Forging, extrusion |
| Parameter | μ = 0.05–0.1 (well-lubricated) | m = 0.05 (well-lub) to 0.6 (no-lub) |

At very high contact pressures in forging, Coulomb's law predicts unbounded shear stress $\tau$ as $p \to \infty$, which is unphysical. The constant friction factor correctly limits $\tau \le k$, representing **sticking friction** at the interface. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B. (b)** With a suitable equation, derive the **drawing force** in wire drawing accounting for friction at the die. Discuss the **role of lubricants and surface coatings** in deep drawing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of drawing force:**

Consider a wire being drawn through a conical die with semi-angle $\alpha$. At any section, let the drawing stress be $\sigma_x$, the cross-sectional area $A$, the friction coefficient $\mu$, and the die semi-angle $\alpha$.

Force balance on an element of length $dx$:

$$
d(\sigma_x A) \;=\; \sigma_x \, dA + A \, d\sigma_x
$$

The increment in area is related to the die geometry:

$$
dA \;=\; -A \tan\alpha \, d\theta
$$

where $d\theta$ is the angular element. Combining with the friction contribution $\mu \, p \, dA / \sin\alpha$ and applying the **ideal work (no friction)** and **frictional work** principles (Schey's analysis):

Integrating from inlet ($A_0$, $\sigma_f$) to outlet ($A_f$, 0) and adding the redundant work term, the **drawing stress** is:

$$
\sigma_d \;=\; \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan\alpha}\right) \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)\right]
$$

For practical dies with redundant work correction:

$$
\sigma_d \;\approx\; \sigma_f \,(1 + \mu \cot\alpha) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)
$$

The maximum drawing force is therefore:

$$
F_{\text{draw}} \;=\; \sigma_d \, A_0
$$

**Role of lubricants and coatings in deep drawing:**

1. **Phosphate + soap coatings** — most widely used for steel sheet drawing. Phosphate crystals provide micro-reservoirs of soap lubricant.
2. **Polymer films (PE, PVC, wax)** — dry-film lubrication, common in aluminium can drawing.
3. **MoS₂ and graphite** — for high-temperature forming (hot forging, titanium).
4. **Glass lubricants** — for hot extrusion of steel and titanium.
5. **Soap-based dry lubricants (sodium stearate, zinc stearate)** — excellent for cold deep drawing.

These coatings/lubricants reduce friction, prevent galling, improve surface finish, allow higher reduction per pass, and extend die life. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- [ ] **Tool–chip interface** is the most critical tribological contact in manufacturing; **adhesion** dominates wear at low speeds, **abrasion** at high speeds.
- [ ] **BUE** forms by adhesion; causes poor finish and accelerates tool wear; controlled by sharp tools, positive rake, effective cutting fluid.
- [ ] **Cutting fluids** are primarily **coolants (80 %)** and secondarily **lubricants (15 %)**. Classification: straight oils, emulsifiable, synthetic, semi-synthetic, cryogenic.
- [ ] **Taylor's tool-life equation** $V T^{n} = C$ is the foundation of tool-life economics; **n** typically **0.1–0.5**.
- [ ] **Merchant's shear angle** $\phi = 45^\circ + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}$ — a **must-memorize** KTU equation.
- [ ] **Chip thickness ratio** $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi-\alpha)} = \dfrac{\tan\phi}{\cos\alpha + \tan\phi\sin\alpha}$.
- [ ] **Friction coefficient in machining**: $\mu = 0.5$–$2.0$ (very high due to seizure).
- [ ] In **metal forming**, use **Coulomb's law** $\tau = \mu p$ for cold / low-pressure processes; **constant friction factor** $\tau = m k$ for hot / high-pressure processes.
- [ ] **Drawing force** $F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_f A_0(1 + \mu \cot\alpha)\ln(A_0 / A_f)$.
- [ ] **Stribeck curve** dictates lubrication regime — boundary → mixed → hydrodynamic as the lambda ratio increases.
- [ ] **Finishing tribology** is at the micro/nano-scale (grinding 1500 °C sparks, CMP tribo-chemical). Abrasive + tribochemical reactions dominate.
- [ ] **Lambda ratio** $\lambda < 1$ → boundary; $1 < \lambda < 3$ → mixed; $\lambda > 3$ → full-film EHL/Hydrodynamic.
- [ ] Typical **forming-process lubricants**: graphite, MoS₂, soap films, glass, phosphate conversion coatings, polymer films.
- [ ] Always draw the **schematic** (tool–chip interface or Merchant force circle) — up to 2 marks reserved for it in KTU valuation key.
- [ ] Numerical answers should carry units (mm, m/min, °C, MPa) — marks are deducted for missing units.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Role of Tribology in Machining, Forming, and Finishing Processes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tribological Issues in Tool Wear and Lubrication in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT754 - TRIBOLOGY AND LUBRICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tribological Issues in Tool Wear and Lubrication in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tribology in Manufacturing** is the applied study of interacting surfaces in relative motion during material removal and shaping processes. It deals with the three pillars — **friction**, **wear**, and **lubrication** — as they occur at the highly stressed, thermally severe interfaces of **cutting tool / workpiece**, **cutting tool / chip**, and **die / workpiece** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Wear (KTU Definition):** The progressive loss of material from the cutting tool due to mechanical, thermal, chemical, and tribological interactions with the workpiece and chip during machining. It is governed by the empirical **Archard wear law** and the **Taylor tool life equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubrication in Manufacturing:** The controlled application of a fluid (liquid, gaseous, or solid) at the tribo-contact zone of a manufacturing process to reduce friction, dissipate heat, prevent adhesion, and extend tool life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **wooden log being sawed** by a steel blade. Three things happen simultaneously:
- The blade heats up (like a car tire overheating on a hot road).
- The teeth get blunt over time (like a kitchen knife losing its edge).
- Without water/soap, friction makes the saw jump and bind (like a stuck zipper).

A cutting tool behaves exactly like that saw blade, only at **temperatures of 800–1000 °C**, **contact pressures of 1–3 GPa**, and **sliding velocities of 100–600 m/min**. Manufacturing tribology studies this aggressive contact zone and engineers solutions (cutting fluids, coatings, MQL, coolants) to keep the process stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Tribological Metrics in Manufacturing:**
> - **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** Typically 0.3–0.7 at tool-chip interface.
> - **Specific Cutting Energy (u):** 1–5 J/mm³ for steels.
> - **Flank Wear Land (VB):** Failure criterion usually **VB = 0.3 mm**.
> - **Tool Life (T):** Defined by ISO 3685 standard.
> - **G-Ratio (Grinding):** Volume of material removed per unit volume of wheel wear.

## 1.3 Why Tool Wear is a Tribological Problem

Tool wear is **not** just mechanical abrasion. It is a coupled **thermo-mechanical-chemico-tribological** phenomenon. The contact between tool, chip, and workpiece is a closed tribosystem in which:

1. **Frictional heat** softens the tool.
2. **Diffusion** causes atomic migration across the tool-chip interface.
3. **Adhesion** causes micro-welding and BUE (Built-Up Edge) formation.
4. **Abrasion** by hard carbides/workpiece asperities scrapes the tool.
5. **Oxidation** accelerates wear at high temperatures.

Hence, controlling wear demands controlling the **tribological environment** of the contact — not just the tool material alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Major Tool Wear Mechanisms

Tool wear in manufacturing is a **coupled multi-mechanism phenomenon**. The dominant mechanism depends on cutting temperature, tool-workpiece pair, and cutting speed.

### 2.1.1 Abrasive Wear
- **Mechanism:** Hard asperities or hard microstructural constituents (e.g., carbides, ceramic particles) in the workpiece **plough** or **micro-cut** the tool surface.
- **Dominant region:** **Flank face** (forms the **flank wear land**, VB).
- **Severity:** Increases with workpiece hardness, decreasing cutting speed (mild temperatures).

### 2.1.2 Adhesive Wear
- **Mechanism:** Atomic-level bonding (micro-welding) at asperity junctions of tool and chip. The weaker material (tool) shears and loses atoms.
- **Consequence:** **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** formation and **crater wear**.
- **Severity:** High at low-to-medium cutting speeds and poor lubrication.

### 2.1.3 Diffusive Wear
- **Mechanism:** At high temperatures (>800 °C), atoms of tool constituents (W, Co, Ti) **diffuse** into the chip. Reciprocal diffusion of Fe, C into the tool.
- **Governed by:** Arrhenius-type rate equation.
- **Consequence:** **Crater wear** (KC) on rake face.

### 2.1.4 Chemical / Oxidative Wear
- **Mechanism:** At temperatures > 700 °C in presence of oxygen, tool materials (WC, Co) oxidize. Oxides are friable and easily removed by sliding.
- **Critical for:** HSS tools; less severe for coated tools.

### 2.1.5 Thermal / Thermo-mechanical Wear
- **Mechanism:** Cyclic thermal shocks cause **crater cracks**, **chipping**, and **plastic deformation** of the tool nose.

### 2.1.6 Fatigue Wear
- **Mechanism:** Repeated thermo-mechanical loading causes subsurface crack nucleation, leading to spalling or flaking.

## 2.2 Friction in Metal Cutting — Special Tribological Behaviour

Unlike classical Coulomb friction (μ constant), the **tool-chip interface** exhibits **sticking–sliding** behavior:

- **Sticking zone:** Chip adheres to the rake face. Shear stress = shear yield stress of chip material (τ_chip). Velocity gradient exists within the chip.
- **Sliding zone:** Classical sliding friction with coefficient μ.

This leads to Merchant's **friction angle (β)** model and the distinction between:
- **Apparent friction coefficient** (μ_app) — measured externally.
- **True friction coefficient** (μ) — at the sliding interface.

## 2.3 Tool Life Curves (Wear vs. Time)

Tool wear typically follows three stages:
- **Stage I (Initial):** Rapid wear-in.
- **Stage II (Steady-state):** Linear, predictable wear.
- **Stage III (Failure):** Accelerated wear, tool collapse.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Memorize and Practice**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| $VT^{n} = C$ | Taylor's Tool Life Equation | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $T$ = tool life (min), $n$ = tool life exponent, $C$ = constant |
| $T = \dfrac{C}{V^{1/n}}$ | Tool Life expressed explicitly | minutes |
| $V \, T^{n} \, f^{a} \, d^{b} = C$ | Extended Taylor's Equation (incl. feed $f$ and depth $d$) | empirical |
| $V_{c} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | Cutting Speed | m/min, $D$ = diameter (mm), $N$ = rpm |
| $Q = V \, f \, d$ | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | mm³/min |
| $V_{w} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Table Feed in Turning | mm/min |
| $F_{c} = u_{s} \cdot A_{c}$ | Cutting Force (Merchant) | $u_{s}$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), $A_{c}$ = uncut chip area |
| $u_{s} = \dfrac{F_{c}}{f \cdot d}$ | Specific Cutting Energy | N/mm² or J/mm³ |
| $\tau = \mu \, \sigma_{n}$ | Coulomb Friction at tool-chip interface | classical |
| $\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | Friction Angle | radians / degrees |
| $\phi = 45^{\circ} + \dfrac{\beta}{2} - \dfrac{\gamma_{o}}{2}$ | Merchant's Shear Angle | $\phi$ = shear angle, $\gamma_{o}$ = rake angle |
| $V_{s} = \dfrac{V \, \cos \alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | Shear Velocity | m/min |
| $F = \dfrac{\tau_{s} \, A_{c}}{\sin \phi}$ | Shear Force on Shear Plane | N |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma_{o})}{\cos \beta}$ | Genc-Gulfingen Shear Angle (considers friction $\beta$) | dimensionless |
| $V_{wear} = K \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot L}{H}$ | Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation | $K$ = wear coefficient, $W$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness |
| $k = \dfrac{V_{wear}}{L}$ | Wear Rate | mm³/m |
| $G = \dfrac{V_{workpiece}}{V_{wheel}}$ | Grinding G-Ratio (Volume Ratio) | dimensionless |
| $\mu_{app} = \dfrac{F_{f} + F \sin \gamma_{o}}{F_{c} \cos \gamma_{o}}$ | Apparent friction coefficient in cutting | measured from forces |
| $P = \tau_{s} \, V_{s}$ | Power dissipated in shear plane | W |
| $T_{contact} = \dfrac{T_{max} \, \sqrt{vc}}{K}$ | Contact temperature (triggering crater wear) | °C, $vc$ = cutting speed, $K$ = constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Key Constants (Default KTU values):**
> - $n = 0.125$ (HSS tools), $n = 0.25$ (Cemented Carbide), $n = 0.5$ (Ceramics).
> - $\mu$ at tool-chip: 0.4–0.7 (dry), 0.2–0.4 (with cutting fluid).
> - Wear land criterion: **VB = 0.3 mm** (ISO 3685).

## 2.5 Lubrication Strategies in Manufacturing

| **Strategy** | **Lubricant Amount** | **Key Benefit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flood Cooling | 10–100 L/min | Excellent heat removal, flushing | Heavy-duty turning, milling, drilling |
| MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication) | 5–500 ml/h | Reduced fluid cost, eco-friendly, good lubrication | Drilling, milling, tapping |
| High-Pressure Cooling | 70–300 bar | Breaks chip, improves chip evacuation | Deep hole drilling, titanium machining |
| Cryogenic Cooling | Liquid N₂ / CO₂ | Cools below -180 °C, suppresses thermal wear | Titanium, Inconel, hard-to-cut alloys |
| Dry Machining | None | Green, no fluid cost | Cast iron, ceramics, coated tools |
| Near-Dry Machining | Aerosol / vapor | Combines MQL with coolant vapor | High-speed milling |
| Solid Lubrication (MoS₂, Graphite, h-BN, DLC) | Coating | High-temp stability | Forming, hot working |

## 2.6 Tribology in Forming Processes (Forging, Extrusion, Rolling)

- **Friction factor (m):** Used in plastic deformation since the Coulomb model fails. $m$ ranges from 0 to 1.
- $\tau = m \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{o}}{\sqrt{3}}$ (von Mises yield criterion).
- Effective friction angle: $\rho = \tan^{-1}(m)$.
- **Lubrication:** Graphite suspensions, MoS₂, glass-based solid lubricants at high temperatures.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Tool wear and lubrication control directly affect:
1. **Production economy** — longer tool life → lower cost per part.
2. **Surface integrity** — Ra, residual stress, white layer formation.
3. **Dimensional accuracy** — wear causes taper and size drift.
4. **Sustainability** — dry/MQL reduces environmental impact.
5. **Process capability** — stable friction → stable cutting forces → less chatter.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taylor's Tool Life Equation (with Modified Form)

**Starting Concept:** Frederick W. Taylor (1907) observed that tool life (T) decreases as cutting speed (V) increases. The relation is empirically **log-linear**.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation

For a given tool-workpiece pair, Taylor fitted the data on log-log axes and obtained a linear relationship:

$$
\log V = \log C - n \log T
$$

### Step 2 — Exponentiate both sides

$$
V = C \cdot T^{-n}
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to canonical Taylor form

$$
V \cdot T^{n} = C
$$

This is the **Taylor Tool Life Equation**. Here, $n$ and $C$ are constants depending on tool material, workpiece, and cutting conditions.

### Step 4 — Isolate Tool Life T explicitly

$$
T^{n} = \dfrac{C}{V}
$$

$$
T = \left(\dfrac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}
$$

### Step 5 — Extend to include feed (f) and depth of cut (d)

Taylor's equation is extended empirically as:

$$
V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = C_{1}
$$

where $a, b, n$ are exponents determined by experimentation, and $C_{1}$ is a constant.

### Step 6 — Take natural logarithm for analysis

$$
\ln V + n \ln T + a \ln f + b \ln d = \ln C_{1}
$$

This linear form is used in **regression analysis** to identify exponents from experimental data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Archard's Adhesive Wear Equation

**Concept:** When two surfaces slide under load $W$, asperity junctions form, and material transfers. Archard (1953) derived the classical wear law.

### Step 1 — Real Contact Area

From Bowden–Tabor, real area of contact is:

$$
A_{r} = \dfrac{W}{H}
$$

where $H$ is the indentation hardness of the softer material.

### Step 2 — Volume of Worn Material per Sliding Event

Each asperity junction of area $A_{r}$, when sheared, produces a wear particle. Assume probability of forming a wear particle = $K$ (dimensionless wear coefficient, typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$). The volume lost per asperity interaction is:

$$
\delta V = K \cdot A_{r} \cdot \delta s
$$

where $\delta s$ is incremental sliding distance.

### Step 3 — Total Volume Wear

Integrate over the total sliding distance $L$:

$$
V_{wear} = \int K \cdot A_{r} \, ds = K \cdot A_{r} \cdot L
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $A_{r}$

$$
V_{wear} = K \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot L}{H}
$$

This is the **Archard Wear Equation**. The wear coefficient $K$ is a measure of the severity of the wear process.

### Step 5 — Wear Rate Form

Wear rate (per unit sliding distance) is:

$$
k = \dfrac{V_{wear}}{L} = K \cdot \dfrac{W}{H}
$$

### Step 6 — Dimensional Check

- $K$ — dimensionless
- $W$ — N
- $H$ — N/mm²
- $L$ — mm
- $V_{wear}$ — mm³ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shear Angle Relationship (Merchant's Analysis)

This derivation establishes the connection between tool geometry, friction, and chip formation.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Single-Point Cutting

The shear plane makes an angle $\phi$ with the cutting velocity direction. The tool has rake angle $\gamma_{o}$. The friction angle is $\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$.

### Step 2 — Velocity Triangle (Chip, Shear, Cutting)

From the velocity diagram:

$$
V_{s} = \dfrac{V \cos \gamma_{o}}{\cos(\phi - \gamma_{o})}
$$

where $V$ is cutting velocity and $V_{s}$ is shear velocity along the shear plane.

### Step 3 — Force Balance on the Chip

Forces on the shear plane:
- $F_{s}$ = shear force
- $N_{s}$ = normal force on shear plane
- $F$ = friction force on rake face
- $N$ = normal force on rake face

The friction angle is:

$$
\tan \beta = \dfrac{F}{N} = \mu
$$

### Step 4 — Equilibrium Equations

Resolving forces parallel and perpendicular to the shear plane:

$$
F_{s} = F \cos \phi - N \sin \phi
$$

$$
N_{s} = F \sin \phi + N \cos \phi
$$

### Step 5 — Minimize $F$ (Merchant's Minimum Energy Criterion)

For minimum cutting energy, $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \phi} = 0$. This yields the **Merchant shear angle equation**:

$$
\phi = 45^{\circ} + \dfrac{\beta}{2} - \dfrac{\gamma_{o}}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Inclusion of Built-Up Edge / Sticking (Lee-Shaffer / Genc-Gulfingen)

When sticking dominates, the chip velocity ratio $r = \dfrac{V_{chip}}{V}$ is not 1. The modified equation:

$$
\tan \phi = \dfrac{r \cos \gamma_{o}}{1 - r \sin \gamma_{o}}
$$

where:

$$
r = 1 - \dfrac{\mu \cos(\beta - \gamma_{o})}{\sin\phi \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma_{o})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cutting Force and Specific Cutting Energy

### Step 1 — Primary Shear Plane Area

The uncut chip cross-section $A_{c} = f \cdot d$ (feed × depth).

The shear plane area $A_{s}$ is:

$$
A_{s} = \dfrac{A_{c}}{\sin \phi} = \dfrac{f \cdot d}{\sin \phi}
$$

### Step 2 — Shear Force

$$
F_{s} = \tau_{s} \cdot A_{s} = \dfrac{\tau_{s} \cdot f \cdot d}{\sin \phi}
$$

where $\tau_{s}$ = shear strength of workpiece in the shear zone.

### Step 3 — Resolve to Cutting Direction

The horizontal (cutting direction) component of $F_{s}$ gives the cutting force $F_{c}$:

$$
F_{c} = \dfrac{\tau_{s} \cdot f \cdot d \cos \beta}{\sin \phi \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma_{o})}
$$

### Step 4 — Define Specific Cutting Energy

$$
u_{s} = \dfrac{F_{c}}{f \cdot d} = \dfrac{\tau_{s} \cos \beta}{\sin \phi \cos(\phi + \beta - \gamma_{o})}
$$

This is the **Specific Cutting Energy (SCE)**, an important tribological-machining metric.

### Step 5 — Approximation (for most engineering steels)

For $\phi \approx 30^{\circ}$, $\beta \approx 30^{\circ}$, $\gamma_{o} \approx 0$:

$$
u_{s} \approx 2 \, \tau_{s}
$$

i.e., the specific cutting energy is approximately **twice the shear strength** of the workpiece.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism vs. Operating Regime

| **Wear Mechanism** | **Dominant Cutting Speed** | **Dominant Temperature** | **Tool Region Affected** | **Counter-measure** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abrasive | Low | < 300 °C | Flank face | Higher hardness, coated tools |
| Adhesive | Low–Medium | 300–600 °C | Rake face, BUE | Lubrication, positive rake |
| Diffusive | High | > 800 °C | Crater (Rake) | Coatings, ceramics, low-V |
| Chemical / Oxidation | High | > 700 °C + O₂ | Flank + Rake | Inert atmosphere, coatings |
| Thermo-mechanical | High, cyclic | > 900 °C | Nose, edge | Coolants, tough substrates |
| Fatigue | Long, interrupted | Cyclic | Subsurface | Tough cermets, whisker ceramics |
